$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdot P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the combinatorial explosion.** The conditionals $P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2)$ and $P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3)$ each require estimating probabilities over $V^2$ and $V^3$ possible histories respectively. With a vocabulary of, say, $V = 50{,}000$, this is roughly $1.25 \times 10^{14}$ parameters for the 3-gram conditional — an impossible number to estimate from any realistic corpus.

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov Assumption of order $N-1$ (here, $N=2$ for a bigram model):**

$$P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \approx P(w_3 \mid w_2)$$

$$P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3) \approx P(w_4 \mid w_3)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the bigram factorisation:**

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) \approx P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_2) \cdot P(w_4 \mid w_3)$$

**Step 5 — Generalise.** For arbitrary $n$ and order $N$, the N-gram factorisation of the joint is:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$$

This is the **fundamental equation** of N-gram language modelling. Every subsequent engineering artefact — smoothing, perplexity, backoff — operates on the terms of this product.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Building a Bigram LM from Scratch

**Corpus (training data $\mathcal{C}$):**

> *"The cat sat on the mat. The cat ate the mouse. The dog sat on the rug."*

After appending `<s>` (sentence start) and `</s>` (sentence end) tokens, the bigrams and their counts are tabulated below.

### Step 1 — Tokenise the corpus

Let $V = \{\text{The, cat, sat, on, the, mat, ate, mouse, dog, rug, </s>}\}$, giving $\vert V \vert = 11$.

### Step 2 — Tabulate bigram counts $C(w_{i-1}, w_i)$

| Bigram $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ | Count $C$ |
|---|---|
| `<s>`, The | 2 |
| `<s>`, The, **re-counted with proper case** | (see clean table below) |

Let us normalise case: lowercase the corpus and recount.

> *"the cat sat on the mat the cat ate the mouse the dog sat on the rug"*

| Bigram $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ | Count $C$ | Bigram | Count $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (the, cat) | 2 | (cat, sat) | 1 |
| (cat, ate) | 1 | (sat, on) | 2 |
| (on, the) | 2 | (the, mat) | 1 |
| (the, mouse) | 1 | (the, dog) | 1 |
| (mat, </s>) | 1 | (ate, the) | 1 |
| (mouse, </s>) | 1 | (dog, sat) | 1 |
| (rug, </s>) | 1 | (on, mat) | 0 |
| (on, rug) | 0 | (mat, the) | 0 |

### Step 3 — Compute unigram counts $C(w_{i-1})$ required as denominators

$$C(\text{the}) = 4, \quad C(\text{cat}) = 2, \quad C(\text{on}) = 2, \quad C(\text{sat}) = 2$$
$$C(\text{mat}) = 1, \quad C(\text{ate}) = 1, \quad C(\text{mouse}) = 1, \quad C(\text{dog}) = 1, \quad C(\text{rug}) = 1$$

### Step 4 — Apply MLE bigram formula $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-1})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{C(\text{the, cat})}{C(\text{the})} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5000 \\[4pt]
P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) &= \frac{C(\text{cat, sat})}{C(\text{cat})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5000 \\[4pt]
P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) &= \frac{C(\text{sat, on})}{C(\text{sat})} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0000 \\[4pt]
P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) &= \frac{C(\text{on, the})}{C(\text{on})} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0000 \\[4pt]
P(\text{mat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{C(\text{the, mat})}{C(\text{the})} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Score a novel test sentence

Test sentence: $S =$ *"the cat sat on the rug"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S) &= P(\text{the}) \cdot P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) \cdot P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) \cdot P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) \cdot P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) \cdot P(\text{rug} \mid \text{the}) \cdot P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{rug})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values (with $P(\text{rug} \mid \text{the}) = 0/4 = 0$ under MLE):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S) &= 0.30 \times 0.50 \times 0.50 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 0 \times 1.00 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The sentence "*the cat sat on the rug*" is perfectly grammatical, yet $P(S) = 0$.** This is the **zero-frequency problem** in action. The bigram *(the, rug)* was never seen in the training corpus. Without smoothing, the model assigns zero probability and the model is rendered useless for any novel N-gram.

### Step 6 — Apply Laplace Smoothing to recover a non-zero score

With $\vert V \vert = 11$ and adding $+1$ to all counts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Lap}(\text{rug} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{C(\text{the, rug}) + 1}{C(\text{the}) + V} = \frac{0 + 1}{4 + 11} = \frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667 \\[4pt]
P_{Lap}(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 11} = \frac{3}{15} = 0.2000
\end{aligned}
$$

The non-zero probability is now recovered. The full smoothed sentence probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Lap}(S) &= 0.30 \times 0.20 \times \left(\frac{1+1}{2+11}\right) \times \left(\frac{2+1}{2+11}\right) \times \left(\frac{2+1}{2+11}\right) \times 0.0667 \times \left(\frac{1+1}{1+11}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 0.30 \times 0.200 \times 0.154 \times 0.231 \times 0.231 \times 0.0667 \times 0.167 \\[4pt]
&\approx 6.21 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Perplexity of the model on this single sentence ($N = 7$ tokens)

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP(S) &= P(S)^{-1/N} = (6.21 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/7} = (1.61 \times 10^{4})^{0.1429} \approx 3.62
\end{aligned}
$$

A perplexity of ~3.6 means the model behaves as confused as a uniform choice over roughly 3–4 words at each step — a sensible result for a small toy corpus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Perplexity from Information Theory

**Step 1 — Start with the cross-entropy of the model on a test set:**

$$
H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P_{model}(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})
$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that $H(W)$ is the average number of bits needed to encode each word.**

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of perplexity as the per-word branching factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP(W) &= 2^{H(W)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})} \\[4pt]
&= \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \right)^{-1/N} = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** If a question gives you $H = 6.5$ bits, the perplexity is $2^{6.5} \approx 90.5$. The inverse — $\log_2(PP)$ — recovers entropy directly. This dual relationship is a **highly-repeatable KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an N-gram Language Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    corpusA[Raw Text Corpus] --> tokenA[Tokenizer and Case Normalisation]
    tokenA --> ngramA[N-gram Extractor N equals 1 to 5]
    ngramA --> countA[Frequency Counter Table]
    countA --> mleA[MLE Probability Estimator]
    countA --> smoothA[Smoothing Module Laplace Add-k Kneser-Ney]
    mleA --> combA[Backoff and Interpolation Combiner]
    smoothA --> combA
    combA --> storeA[Trained LM Parameters]
    storeA --> queryA[Probability Query Engine]
    queryA --> evalA[Perplexity Evaluator]
    evalA --> outA[Score or Perplexity Output]
```

## 4.2 Sliding-Window View of Bigram Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seqA["Sentence the cat sat on the mat"]
    winA["Window Position 1: the cat"]
    winB["Window Position 2: cat sat"]
    winC["Window Position 3: sat on"]
    winD["Window Position 4: on the"]
    winE["Window Position 5: the mat"]
    seqA --> winA
    seqA --> winB
    seqA --> winC
    seqA --> winD
    seqA --> winE
    winA --> bigA["Bigram: the cat  C equals 1"]
    winB --> bigB["Bigram: cat sat  C equals 1"]
    winC --> bigC["Bigram: sat on  C equals 1"]
    winD --> bigD["Bigram: on the  C equals 1"]
    winE --> bigE["Bigram: the mat  C equals 1"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Handling an Unseen N-gram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Encounter N-gram Query] --> chkA{Count greater than zero?}
    chkA -- Yes --> pathA[Return MLE probability]
    chkA -- No --> chkB{Backoff model available?}
    chkB -- Yes --> pathB[Back off to N minus 1 gram]
    chkB -- No --> chkC{Smoothing active?}
    chkC -- Yes --> pathC[Apply Laplace Add k or Kneser Ney]
    chkC -- No --> pathD[Return zero probability]
    pathB --> chkA
```

## 4.4 Smoothing Strategy Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[Raw Count Table] --> lapA[Laplace Add 1]
    rawA --> addkA[Add k 0 less than k less than 1]
    rawA --> knA[Kneser Ney Discount 0.75]
    rawA --> boA[Backoff Trigram to Bigram to Unigram]
    rawA --> intpA[Linear Interpolation Mixing Weights]
    lapA --> evalA[Perplexity Evaluation]
    addkA --> evalA
    knA --> evalA
    boA --> evalA
    intpA --> evalA
    evalA --> rankA[Lowest Perplexity Wins]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define an N-gram language model. What is the role of the Markov assumption in such a model?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An N-gram language model is a statistical model that estimates the probability of a word given a fixed window of $N-1$ preceding words, i.e. $P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$.
The **Markov assumption** restricts the history to a finite, fixed window, thereby making the model tractable by limiting the number of parameters to be estimated. **(1 mark)** — definition, **(1 mark)** — Markov assumption, **(1 mark)** — tractability/intractability of exact chain rule.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Briefly explain the zero-frequency problem in N-gram language models and how Laplace smoothing addresses it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The zero-frequency problem occurs when an N-gram appearing in the test data is **absent from the training corpus**, causing MLE to assign it a probability of **0** and making the entire sentence probability collapse to 0. **(1 mark)**
Laplace (Add-One) smoothing addresses this by adding 1 to every count: $P_{Lap}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = (C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1) / (C(w_{i-1}) + V)$, where $V$ is the vocabulary size. This guarantees that no N-gram has zero probability, and the probability mass is redistributed from seen to unseen events. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Bigram Construction & Smoothing*

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Given the training corpus:
> *"I love NLP. I love deep learning. I love NLP and deep learning."*

Construct a **bigram language model** using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**. List the distinct bigrams, their counts, and compute the probability of the test sentence: *"I love deep learning"*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply **Laplace (Add-One) smoothing** to the model obtained in part (a). Recompute the probability of the same test sentence. **Comment on the change in perplexity and the issue of probability-mass redistribution.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Normalise and add start/end tokens.** Vocabulary (lowercased, punctuated):
$V = \{\text{I, love, NLP, deep, learning, and, </s>}\}$, so $\vert V \vert = 7$.

**Step 2 — Tabulate bigram counts:**

| Bigram | Count $C$ | Bigram | Count $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (I, love) | 3 | (love, NLP) | 1 |
| (love, deep) | 1 | (NLP, </s>) | 1 |
| (deep, learning) | 2 | (learning, </s>) | 2 |
| (love, and) | 1 | (and, deep) | 1 |

**Step 3 — Compute marginal counts:**
$C(\text{I}) = 3$, $C(\text{love}) = 3$, $C(\text{NLP}) = 1$, $C(\text{deep}) = 1$, $C(\text{learning}) = 2$, $C(\text{and}) = 1$.

**Step 4 — Apply MLE for the test sentence "I love deep learning"**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{I}) &= \frac{C(\text{I})}{\sum C} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25 \\[4pt]
P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) &= \frac{3}{3} = 1.00 \\[4pt]
P(\text{deep} \mid \text{love}) &= \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \\[4pt]
P(\text{learning} \mid \text{deep}) &= \frac{2}{2} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Joint probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S) &= 0.25 \times 1.00 \times 0.333 \times 1.00 = 0.0833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Perplexity** ($N=4$):

$$
PP(S) = (0.0833)^{-1/4} = 12^{-0.25} \approx 1.86
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Apply Laplace smoothing** with $V = 7$:

$$
P_{Lap}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + 7}
$$

**Step 2 — Recompute the four required conditionals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Lap}(\text{I}) &= \frac{3 + 1}{12 + 7} = \frac{4}{19} \approx 0.2105 \\[4pt]
P_{Lap}(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) &= \frac{3 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40 \\[4pt]
P_{Lap}(\text{deep} \mid \text{love}) &= \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
P_{Lap}(\text{learning} \mid \text{deep}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{2 + 7} = \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Smoothed joint probability:**

$$
P_{Lap}(S) = 0.2105 \times 0.40 \times 0.20 \times 0.333 \approx 0.00561
$$

**Step 4 — Smoothed perplexity:**

$$
PP_{Lap}(S) = (0.00561)^{-1/4} \approx 3.66
$$

**Step 5 — Comments (mandatory for full marks):**
- The sentence probability has **decreased** from 0.0833 to 0.00561 due to the smoothing discount.
- Perplexity has **increased** from 1.86 to 3.66 — smoothing makes the model less confident (more uniform) about the data.
- The mass taken from frequent N-grams is redistributed to unseen N-grams, **eliminating zero probabilities** at the cost of over-smoothing rare events.
- Add-One is known to over-smooth for large $V$; **Kneser-Ney** is the recommended alternative for production systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Perplexity, Interpolation & Evaluation*

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **perplexity** and **cross-entropy** for an N-gram language model. State the units clearly. A test set of 1000 tokens has cross-entropy 6.5 bits/word. What is the perplexity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **diagram**, explain **linear interpolation** for combining unigram, bigram, and trigram models. Why is interpolation preferred over pure backoff in low-data regimes? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the cross-entropy** of model $M$ on sequence $W$:

$$
H_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P_M(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \quad [\text{bits/word}]
$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate** to recover the per-word branching factor (perplexity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP_M(W) &= 2^{H_M(W)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log_2 P_M(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})} \\[4pt]
&= \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P_M(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \right)^{-1/N} = P_M(w_1^N)^{-1/N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Units:** Cross-entropy is in **bits/word** (when $\log_2$ is used) or **nats/word** (when $\ln$ is used). Perplexity is **dimensionless**.

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation** with $H = 6.5$ bits/word:

$$
PP = 2^{6.5} = 2^{6} \times 2^{0.5} = 64 \times 1.4142 \approx 90.51
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** A perplexity of ~90 means the model is as confused as a uniform distribution over 90 words at every step.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define linear interpolation** with weights $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3 \geq 0$, $\sum \lambda_j = 1$:

$$
\hat{P}(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = \lambda_1 P(w_i) + \lambda_2 P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) + \lambda_3 P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})
$$

**Step 2 — Diagram (block-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uniA[Unigram P w] --> mixA[Mixing Layer weights lambda 1 lambda 2 lambda 3]
    biA[Bigram P w given w i minus 1] --> mixA
    triA[Trigram P w given w i minus 2 w i minus 1] --> mixA
    mixA --> outA[Interpolated Probability Output]
```

**Step 3 — Why interpolation > pure backoff in low-data regimes:**
- **Backoff** completely discards a higher-order model when its count is zero, so the lower-order model is used alone. In low-data settings, the higher-order model is *almost always* zero, meaning the model collapses to a low-order one.
- **Interpolation** always *combines* all orders, so the higher-order model contributes whenever it has support, and the lower-order model provides a robust fallback. This yields a **strictly smoother probability distribution** with lower perplexity on small corpora.
- Mathematically, interpolation is a **convex combination**, guaranteeing the result remains a valid probability distribution regardless of how the $\lambda_j$ are chosen (provided they sum to 1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on N-gram Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the start `<s>` and end `</s>` tokens** when tabulating bigram counts. This single omission can cascade into 2–3 wrong probabilities. *Always* pad your corpus.
> 2. **Confusing $C(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ with $C(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$** in the MLE formula. The numerator is a *joint count*, the denominator is a *marginal count of the history only*.
> 3. **Mixing log bases** in perplexity. KTU accepts $\log_2$ (bits → $2^{H}$) and $\ln$ (nats → $e^{H}$), but you must declare the base *consistently*. Switching mid-calculation costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring sentence boundaries** in the test set. Perplexity must be computed on the *entire test set*, not sentence-by-sentence, to avoid length bias.
> 5. **Writing Laplace smoothing without specifying the vocabulary size $V$**. The denominator $C(w_{i-1}) + V$ *requires* $V = \vert V \vert$, and a missing $V$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**
> - An N-gram LM approximates $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ using only the **last $N-1$ words** (Markov assumption).
> - **Chain Rule** (exact): $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ — intractable in practice.
> - **MLE** (Bigram): $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) \,/\, C(w_{i-1})$.
> - **Zero-frequency problem** → fixed by smoothing.
> - **Laplace (Add-1)**: $P = (C + 1) / (C_{prev} + V)$ — simple but over-smooths.
> - **Add-k**: same form with $k < 1$ — finer control.
> - **Kneser-Ney**: subtracts a discount $\delta \approx 0.75$, uses *continuation probability* $P_{cont}$ — state-of-the-art for N-grams.
> - **Backoff** uses lower-order model when higher-order count is 0; **Interpolation** always mixes all orders.
> - **Perplexity**: $PP = P(w_1^n)^{-1/n} = 2^{H(W)}$ — *lower is better*.
> - Higher $N$ ⇒ better fit but **worse data sparsity**; typical sweet spot is **trigrams (N=3)** in practice.
> - N-gram LMs are the **statistical baseline** against which RNNs, LSTMs, and Transformers are compared using perplexity.
> - Always use **`<s>` and `</s>` tokens** to model sentence boundaries.
> - Always specify the **base of the logarithm** in perplexity calculations (base 2 → bits, base $e$ → nats).
> - The **Markov order** and the **smoothing strategy** are the two design knobs the KTU examiner will test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes for Text Classification and Sentiment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes for Text Classification and Sentiment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Naive Bayes Classifier** is a *probabilistic supervised machine learning model* based on **Bayes' Theorem**, which is widely used in Natural Language Processing (NLP) for tasks such as **text categorization**, **spam filtering**, and **sentiment analysis**. The model is termed "naive" because it makes the **conditional independence assumption** — that every feature (word/token) in the document contributes independently to the probability of the class label, given the class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Verbatim):**
> *"Naive Bayes is a generative probabilistic classifier that applies Bayes' rule assuming strong (naive) independence among the predictor variables (words) conditioned on the class. For a document **d** represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a class $c \in C$, the classifier predicts:*
> $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \, P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$"

For **sentiment analysis**, the same formulation is applied where the class set is typically $C = \{\text{Positive}, \text{Negative}, \text{Neutral}\}$, and the document features are derived from a **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** or **TF-IDF** representation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Doctor's Quick Diagnosis"**
> Imagine a doctor who needs to predict whether a patient has *Flu* or *Allergy* based on symptoms: *sneezing, fever, runny nose, body ache*. A Naive Bayes doctor treats each symptom as an **independent clue** (even though in reality, symptoms can be correlated). She multiplies:
> *   How common Flu is in the population (prior $P(\text{Flu})$)
> *   The probability of each symptom appearing *given* Flu ($P(\text{sneezing} \mid \text{Flu})$, etc.)
> She does the same for Allergy and picks the disease with the **higher combined score**. This is exactly how Naive Bayes handles text — each word is a "symptom," each class is a "disease."

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this "naive" idea work for text?**
> Text data is *high-dimensional* and *sparse*. The independence assumption is mathematically convenient and empirically gives surprisingly strong baseline accuracy (often **85–90%** on standard sentiment benchmarks) despite being theoretically "wrong."

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Board-Students Must Memorize

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Prior Probability | $P(c)$ | Probability of class $c$ before seeing evidence |
| Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid c)$ | Probability of feature $x_i$ given class $c$ |
| Posterior | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Probability of class $c$ after seeing evidence $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Evidence | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Normalizing constant (same for all classes) |
| Vocabulary | $V$ | Set of unique words in the training corpus |
| Smoothing | $\alpha$ | Laplace / Lidstone parameter to avoid zero probability |
| Bag-of-Words | BoW | Unordered multiset representation of word counts |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Why & How

Naive Bayes classification for text follows a **6-step operational pipeline**. Each step is grounded in probability theory and is essential for solving KTU numerical problems.

**Step 1 — Corpus Preparation & Tokenization**
*   **Why:** Machines cannot process raw strings; they require structured numerical inputs.
*   **How:** The training corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(d_1, c_1), (d_2, c_2), \ldots, (d_m, c_m)\}$ is tokenized, lowercased, stopwords may be removed, and each document is converted to a vector.

**Step 2 — Build Vocabulary $V$**
*   Compile the **set of all unique tokens** appearing in the training corpus. The cardinality $|V|$ is critical for smoothing formulas.

**Step 3 — Estimate Prior Probabilities $P(c)$**
*   **Why:** Establishes the *baseline belief* about a class before observing any document.
*   **How:** Compute the relative frequency of each class label:
    $$P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$$
    where $N_c$ is the number of training documents in class $c$, and $N$ is the total number of training documents.

**Step 4 — Estimate Likelihoods $P(w \mid c)$ for Each Word**
*   **Why:** Captures the *discriminative power* of each word for each class.
*   **How (Multinomial NB — most common for text):**
    $$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c)}$$
*   **How (Bernoulli NB — for binary occurrence):**
    $$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{docs containing } w \text{ in class } c + 1}{N_c + 2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes' Theorem (Drop the Denominator)**
*   **Why:** $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $c$, so for *argmax* we can ignore it.
*   **How:** For a new document $\mathbf{x} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$:
    $$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 6 — Handle Zero Probabilities with Laplace Smoothing**
*   **Why:** A word unseen in training for a class gives $P(w \mid c) = 0$, which would zero-out the entire product.
*   **How:** Add a smoothing parameter $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 1$):
    $$P_{\text{smooth}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

**Step 7 — Predict the Class**
*   **Why:** The class with the maximum posterior is the most probable label.
*   **How:**
    $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \Big[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid c) \Big]$$
    (Logarithm is used to prevent *underflow* from multiplying many small probabilities.)

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **all formulas** you need to solve any Naive Bayes problem in the KTU 2024 University Exam. Memorize the symbols and the **denominator** structures.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Foundation of all NB variants |
| 2 | **Naive Bayes Posterior** | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | Final classification rule |
| 3 | **Log-space Posterior** | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \big[ \log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid c) \big]$ | Numerical stability |
| 4 | **Prior** | $P(c) = N_c / N$ | Class baseline probability |
| 5 | **Multinomial Likelihood (no smoothing)** | $P(w \mid c) = \text{count}(w, c) / T_c$ | Where $T_c$ = total tokens in class $c$ |
| 6 | **Multinomial Likelihood + Laplace** | $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{T_c + \vert V \vert}$ | Avoids zero-frequency problem |
| 7 | **Bernoulli NB Prior per-word** | $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } c + 1}{N_c + 2}$ | Binary occurrence model |
| 8 | **Zero-Probability Issue** | If $\text{count}(w, c) = 0 \Rightarrow P(w \mid c) = 0$ | Motivation for smoothing |
| 9 | **Posterior Normalization** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(c)\prod P(w_i \mid c)}{\sum_{c'} P(c')\prod P(w_i \mid c')}$ | Convert raw scores to probabilities |
| 10 | **Accuracy Metric** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Sentiment analysis evaluation |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Naive Bayes in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> *   **Industry Deployment:** Spam filters (Gmail, Yahoo), news article categorization (Google News), and product review sentiment (Amazon, Flipkart) historically use Multinomial Naive Bayes as a fast, low-latency baseline.
> *   **Why Engineers Prefer It:** Training is **O(N · |V|)** (linear!), inference is **O(|V|)** per document, and the model is **interpretable** — you can inspect the top "positive" and "negative" words by sorting $P(w \mid \text{positive})$ vs $P(w \mid \text{negative})$.
> *   **KTU Industrial Context:** When deploying on edge devices (low memory, no GPU), Naive Bayes is preferred over heavy deep learning models.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Multinomial Naive Bayes from Scratch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU 2024-Style 14-Mark Problem.** Work through it by hand before looking at the solution. The exact same problem appears (with slight variation) in Section 5, Part B.

#### Problem Statement
You are given the following **training corpus** for a 2-class text classification task (Sports vs. Politics). Use **Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)** to classify the test document:

> **Test Document:** `d5 = "team victory"`

**Training Data:**

| Doc ID | Text (tokenized) | Class |
|--------|------------------|-------|
| d1 | great match victory team | Sports |
| d2 | team played well final | Sports |
| d3 | election votes party campaign | Politics |
| d4 | party campaign victory polls | Politics |

---

#### Step 1 — Tokenization and Vocabulary Construction
Flatten all training documents and collect unique words:

$$V = \{\text{great, match, victory, team, played, well, final, election, votes, party, campaign, polls}\}$$

Therefore, $\vert V \vert = 12$.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute Class Priors $P(c)$

Total training documents $N = 4$.
*   $N_{\text{Sports}} = 2 \Rightarrow P(\text{Sports}) = 2/4 = 0.5$
*   $N_{\text{Politics}} = 2 \Rightarrow P(\text{Politics}) = 2/4 = 0.5$

---

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Tokens per Class

Counting **all word occurrences** (with repetition) in each class:

**Sports documents (d1 + d2):**

$$\text{words} = \{\text{great, match, victory, team, team, played, well, final}\}$$

Total tokens $T_{\text{Sports}} = 8$.

**Politics documents (d3 + d4):**

$$\text{words} = \{\text{election, votes, party, campaign, party, campaign, victory, polls}\}$$

Total tokens $T_{\text{Politics}} = 8$.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute Word Counts per Class

**Word counts in Sports** (only words in this class get non-zero count):

| Word | Count in Sports |
|------|-----------------|
| great | 1 |
| match | 1 |
| victory | 1 |
| team | 2 |
| played | 1 |
| well | 1 |
| final | 1 |
| election | 0 |
| votes | 0 |
| party | 0 |
| campaign | 0 |
| polls | 0 |

**Word counts in Politics:**

| Word | Count in Politics |
|------|-------------------|
| great | 0 |
| match | 0 |
| victory | 1 |
| team | 0 |
| played | 0 |
| well | 0 |
| final | 0 |
| election | 1 |
| votes | 1 |
| party | 2 |
| campaign | 2 |
| polls | 1 |

---

#### Step 5 — Apply Laplace Smoothing to Likelihoods

Using the formula:

$$P_{\text{Laplace}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{T_c + \vert V \vert}$$

Denominators:
*   Sports: $T_{\text{Sports}} + \vert V \vert = 8 + 12 = 20$
*   Politics: $T_{\text{Politics}} + \vert V \vert = 8 + 12 = 20$

**Required likelihoods (only for words in test document):**

For word $\text{team}$:
$$P(\text{team} \mid \text{Sports}) = \frac{2 + 1}{20} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15$$

$$P(\text{team} \mid \text{Politics}) = \frac{0 + 1}{20} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

For word $\text{victory}$:
$$P(\text{victory} \mid \text{Sports}) = \frac{1 + 1}{20} = \frac{2}{20} = 0.10$$

$$P(\text{victory} \mid \text{Politics}) = \frac{1 + 1}{20} = \frac{2}{20} = 0.10$$

---

#### Step 6 — Compute Unnormalized Posterior Scores

**For class = Sports:**

$$P(\text{Sports} \mid d5) \propto P(\text{Sports}) \cdot P(\text{team} \mid \text{Sports}) \cdot P(\text{victory} \mid \text{Sports})$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.15 \times 0.10 = 0.0075$$

**For class = Politics:**

$$P(\text{Politics} \mid d5) \propto P(\text{Politics}) \cdot P(\text{team} \mid \text{Politics}) \cdot P(\text{victory} \mid \text{Politics})$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.0025$$

---

#### Step 7 — Normalize to Obtain Probabilities

Total unnormalized mass:

$$Z = 0.0075 + 0.0025 = 0.0100$$

Normalized posteriors:

$$P(\text{Sports} \mid d5) = \frac{0.0075}{0.0100} = 0.75 \; (75\%)$$

$$P(\text{Politics} \mid d5) = \frac{0.0025}{0.0100} = 0.25 \; (25\%)$$

---

#### Step 8 — Final Prediction

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \, P(c \mid d5) = \text{Sports}$$

**Confidence = 75%** — the test document *"team victory"* is classified as **Sports**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Naive Bayes Succeeds Despite the "Naive" Assumption

| Aspect | Theoretical Concern | Empirical Reality | KTU Implication |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Conditional Independence** | Words are clearly correlated (e.g., "New" and "York") | Correlations "cancel out" between competing classes; only relative differences matter | Always mention this in viva as a *strength*, not a *weakness* |
| **Zero-Frequency Problem** | A single unseen word zeros the whole product | Solved cleanly by **Laplace / Lidstone smoothing** ($\alpha \in [0, 1]$) | Numericals **always** require you to show smoothing step |
| **Numerical Underflow** | Multiplying 1000s of small probabilities $\to 0$ | Resolved using **log-space arithmetic** | Examiners award marks for converting products to sums |
| **Class Imbalance** | Rare classes get tiny $P(c)$ | Prior balancing or **complement NB** addresses this | KTU 2024 loves asking "what if Sports had 90 docs and Politics had 10?" |
| **Feature Engineering** | BoW ignores word order | N-grams and TF-IDF often boost accuracy by 2–5% | Higher-mark questions ask for **NB + TF-IDF** vs. raw counts |

---

### 3.3 Application to Sentiment Analysis

For **sentiment analysis**, the pipeline is identical, with two key adaptations:

> [!TIP]
> **Adapting Naive Bayes for Sentiment**
> 1. **Label set:** $C = \{\text{Positive}, \text{Negative}\}$ (sometimes Neutral)
> 2. **Lexicon-aware features:** Augment BoW with **polarity tokens** (e.g., emoji, exclamation marks, negations like "not" / "never"). The negation handling rule:
> $$P(\text{not good} \mid c) \approx P(\text{NOT\_good} \mid c)$$
> i.e., the token `not_good` is treated as a single "negative-flavored" word. This recovers accuracy lost by the independence assumption.
> 3. **N-gram features:** Bigrams like *"not bad"* or *"really love"* carry sentiment that unigrams miss.
> 4. **Evaluation:** Use **accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score** on a held-out set; for imbalanced reviews, prefer **macro-F1**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Naive Bayes Text Classification Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization & Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal & Stemming]
    C --> D[Bag-of-Words Vectorizer]
    D --> E[Training Set X_train, y_train]
    E --> F[Compute Prior P of class c]
    E --> G[Compute Likelihood P of word w given c]
    F --> H[Build Naive Bayes Model]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Laplace Smoothing alpha equals 1]
    I --> J[New Test Document]
    J --> K[Tokenize and Convert to Vector]
    K --> L[Compute Posterior Score per class]
    L --> M[Argmax over classes]
    M --> N[Predicted Class Label]
    N --> O[Evaluation: Accuracy / F1 Score]
```

### 4.2 Probability Computation Flow for a Single Test Document

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Test Doc: team victory] --> T1[Tokenize into team, victory]
    T1 --> S1[Lookup P of team given Sports]
    T1 --> S2[Lookup P of victory given Sports]
    T1 --> P1[Lookup P of team given Politics]
    T1 --> P2[Lookup P of victory given Politics]
    S1 --> M1[Multiply with P of Sports]
    S2 --> M1
    P1 --> M2[Multiply with P of Politics]
    P2 --> M2
    M1 --> ARG[Argmax comparison]
    M2 --> ARG
    ARG --> RES[Output: Sports with prob 0.75]
```

### 4.3 Decision Boundary Illustration (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Document Feature Space] --> D1{Does document contain sports keywords?}
    D1 -->|Yes, high P of words like team, match, victory| R1[Lean towards Sports]
    D1 -->|Yes, high P of words like party, campaign, election| R2[Lean towards Politics]
    D1 -->|No strong discriminative words| R3[Fall back to Prior P of c]
    R1 --> F[Final Naive Bayes Score]
    R2 --> F
    R3 --> F
    F --> P[Pick class with Maximum Score]
```

### 4.4 Multinomial vs Bernoulli Naive Bayes — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MNB[Multinomial Naive Bayes]
        M1[Input: Word Count Vector] --> M2[Sum of all P of wi given c for all tokens]
        M2 --> M3[Best for word frequencies and TF-IDF]
    end
    subgraph BNB[Bernoulli Naive Bayes]
        B1[Input: Binary Vector word present or not] --> B2[Product of P of wi given c and 1 minus P of wi given c for absent words]
        B2 --> B3[Best for short documents and sentiment]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **State Bayes' Theorem and explain the term "naive" in Naive Bayes Classifier for text classification.**

**Model Answer:**
Bayes' Theorem states that for events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

In Naive Bayes, the class is $A$ and the document feature vector is $B$. The term **"naive"** refers to the simplifying assumption that **all features (words) in a document are conditionally independent given the class label** — i.e., $P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$. This assumption is unrealistic because words in natural language are correlated, yet it works well empirically for text classification.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is the zero-frequency problem in Naive Bayes? How is it solved?**

**Model Answer:**
The **zero-frequency problem** occurs when a word that appears in the test document has **never occurred in the training data of a particular class**, giving $P(w \mid c) = 0$. Since the Naive Bayes classifier multiplies all word likelihoods, a single zero will collapse the entire product to zero, making that class impossible.

It is solved by **Laplace smoothing** (add-one smoothing):

$$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{T_c + \vert V \vert}$$

This ensures no probability is ever exactly zero by adding 1 to every numerator and $\vert V \vert$ to the denominator.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** KTU 2024 allows you to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are printed below for practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Naive Bayes text classification. Discuss the role of priors, likelihoods, and Laplace smoothing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following training corpus:

| Doc | Text | Class |
|-----|------|-------|
| d1 | chinese beijing chinese | China |
| d2 | chinese chinese shanghai | China |
| d3 | tokyo japan chinese | Japan |

Classify the test document **d4 = "chinese chinese tokyo japan"** using **Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)**. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

1. **Architecture Overview** — Naive Bayes text classification has two phases: training and inference.
2. **Training Phase:**
    *   Build vocabulary $V$ from all training documents.
    *   Compute priors $P(c) = N_c / N$.
    *   Compute likelihoods $P(w \mid c)$ for every word $w \in V$ and class $c$.
3. **Inference Phase:** For a new document $\mathbf{x}$, compute:
    $$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(c) \prod_{w \in \mathbf{x}} P(w \mid c)^{count(w, \mathbf{x})}$$
4. **Role of Priors:** Encode the baseline class distribution; they dominate when the test document is short or generic.
5. **Role of Likelihoods:** Capture how discriminative each word is for each class.
6. **Role of Laplace Smoothing:** Prevents zero-frequency failures and regularizes the model.
7. **Final Prediction:** Take argmax; optionally convert to log-space for numerical stability.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:**
$V = \{\text{chinese, beijing, shanghai, tokyo, japan}\} \Rightarrow \vert V \vert = 5$

**Step 2 — Priors:**
$N = 3$, $N_{\text{China}} = 2$, $N_{\text{Japan}} = 1$

$$P(\text{China}) = 2/3, \quad P(\text{Japan}) = 1/3$$

**Step 3 — Total tokens:**
$T_{\text{China}} = 4$ (chinese, beijing, chinese, chinese, shanghai = 5 actually) → recount: d1 = chinese(1), beijing(1), chinese(1) = 3, d2 = chinese(1), chinese(1), shanghai(1) = 3. So $T_{\text{China}} = 6$.
$T_{\text{Japan}} = 3$ (tokyo, japan, chinese)

**Step 4 — Counts:**

| Word | Count(China) | Count(Japan) |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| chinese | 4 | 1 |
| beijing | 1 | 0 |
| shanghai | 1 | 0 |
| tokyo | 0 | 1 |
| japan | 0 | 1 |

**Step 5 — Smoothed Likelihoods** (denominator China: $6+5=11$; Japan: $3+5=8$):

$$P(\text{chinese} \mid \text{China}) = (4+1)/11 = 5/11$$
$$P(\text{to} \mid \text{China}) = (0+1)/11 = 1/11 \;(\text{not needed, not in test})$$
$$P(\text{tokyo} \mid \text{China}) = 1/11$$
$$P(\text{japan} \mid \text{China}) = 1/11$$

$$P(\text{chinese} \mid \text{Japan}) = (1+1)/8 = 2/8 = 1/4$$
$$P(\text{tokyo} \mid \text{Japan}) = (1+1)/8 = 2/8 = 1/4$$
$$P(\text{japan} \mid \text{Japan}) = (1+1)/8 = 1/4$$

**Step 6 — Posterior Scores for d4 = "chinese chinese tokyo japan":**

$$P(\text{China} \mid d4) \propto \frac{2}{3} \times \left(\frac{5}{11}\right)^2 \times \frac{1}{11} \times \frac{1}{11}$$

$$= \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{25}{121} \times \frac{1}{121} = \frac{50}{43923} \approx 0.001138$$

$$P(\text{Japan} \mid d4) \propto \frac{1}{3} \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{1}{16} = \frac{1}{768} \approx 0.001302$$

**Step 7 — Normalize:**
$$P(\text{Japan} \mid d4) = \frac{0.001302}{0.001302 + 0.001138} \approx 0.534$$
$$P(\text{China} \mid d4) \approx 0.466$$

**Final Prediction:** $\hat{c} = \text{Japan}$ (with ~53.4% confidence).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Multinomial Naive Bayes** and **Bernoulli Naive Bayes** with suitable formulas. When is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how Naive Bayes is used for **sentiment analysis of product reviews**. Describe a complete pipeline including preprocessing, feature extraction, training, and evaluation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Feature | Multinomial NB | Bernoulli NB |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| Feature type | **Word counts** (integer) | **Binary occurrence** (0/1) |
| Likelihood | $P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{T_c + \vert V \vert}$ | $P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } c + 1}{N_c + 2}$ |
| Considers | Frequency of words | Only presence/absence |
| Best for | Long documents, TF-IDF | Short texts, sentiment |
| Example use | News article classification | Spam detection, tweet sentiment |

Multinomial NB is preferred when word frequency matters (e.g., "great" appearing 5 times is stronger than once). Bernoulli NB is preferred for short texts and when presence/absence is more meaningful than counts.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

The complete Naive Bayes sentiment analysis pipeline for product reviews is:

1. **Data Collection:** Gather labeled reviews (Positive = 1, Negative = 0).
2. **Text Preprocessing:** Lowercasing, removal of HTML tags, tokenization, stopword removal, negation handling ("not good" → "not_good"), and stemming/lemmatization.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Convert each review to a BoW vector or TF-IDF vector. Vocabulary $V$ is constructed from training corpus.
4. **Model Training:** Estimate priors $P(\text{Positive})$, $P(\text{Negative})$ and likelihoods $P(w \mid \text{Positive})$, $P(w \mid \text{Negative})$ for all $w \in V$ with Laplace smoothing.
5. **Prediction:** For a new review $r$, compute $P(\text{Positive} \mid r) \propto P(\text{Positive}) \prod_w P(w \mid \text{Positive})^{count(w, r)}$ and pick argmax.
6. **Evaluation:** Use **accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix** on a held-out test set. For imbalanced datasets, prefer **macro-averaged F1**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight)**
> 1. **Forgetting Laplace Smoothing in the denominator:** A classic 1-mark loss. The denominator must be $T_c + \vert V \vert$, NOT just $T_c$.
> 2. **Counting unique words instead of total tokens:** $T_c$ is the *total number of word tokens* (with repetition), not $|V_c|$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Not normalizing posteriors:** If the question asks for "probability," you MUST divide by the sum. If it asks for "argmax class," normalization is optional.
> 4. **Multinomial vs Bernoulli confusion:** When a numerical problem mentions "word counts," use Multinomial. When it mentions "presence/absence" or "binary," use Bernoulli.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply log-space:** For documents with more than ~10 unique words, multiplying probabilities causes underflow. Always convert to log-sum for long documents.
> 6. **Missing the prior:** Many students compute only the likelihood product and forget to multiply by $P(c)$. This loses 1–2 marks routinely.
> 7. **Wrong vocabulary size $|V|$:** It must be unique words across the **entire training set**, not per class. This is a board-favorite trick.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Page Before the Exam)**
> *   ✅ Bayes' Theorem: $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = P(c) P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) / P(\mathbf{x})$.
> *   ✅ "Naive" = conditional independence of words given the class.
> *   ✅ Multinomial NB uses **word counts**; Bernoulli NB uses **binary occurrence**.
> *   ✅ Prior: $P(c) = N_c / N$.
> *   ✅ Likelihood (no smoothing): $P(w \mid c) = \text{count}(w, c) / T_c$.
> *   ✅ Laplace Smoothing: numerator $+1$, denominator $+ \vert V \vert$.
> *   ✅ $|V|$ = **total unique words in the entire training corpus**.
> *   ✅ $T_c$ = **total word tokens in class $c$** (count repetition!).
> *   ✅ Use **log-space** for long documents to prevent underflow.
> *   ✅ Final prediction = **argmax of posterior** (or log-posterior).
> *   ✅ For sentiment: $C = \{\text{Positive}, \text{Negative}\}$; add negation handling and bigrams.
> *   ✅ Evaluation: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Confusion Matrix.
> *   ✅ Always show **all seven steps** in numericals: vocab → priors → token counts → smoothed likelihoods → unnormalized scores → normalize → argmax.
> *   ✅ Real-world use: Spam filter, news classifier, product review sentiment, medical diagnosis.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation: Precision, Recall and F-measure, Test Sets and Cross Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation: Precision, Recall and F-measure, Test Sets and Cross Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Precision

**Precision** is the proportion of items retrieved by a classification or information extraction system that are actually *relevant* (correct). In the context of NLP evaluation, it answers the question: *"Of all the instances the system labeled as positive, how many were truly positive?"*

Formally, for a binary classification task, it is defined as:

$$\text{Precision (P)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

where **TP** stands for **True Positives** and **FP** stands for **False Positives**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** Precision is a *type-1-error-sensitive* metric. It penalizes the system for making false alarms (false positives). High precision indicates that when the system says "yes," it is usually correct.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Fishing Net

Imagine you are a fisherman casting a net into a lake to catch only **salmon**.

- You catch 10 fish in total.
- Out of those 10, only 7 are salmon; the remaining 3 are other fish (e.g., trout, carp) that you did not want.

In this analogy, the salmon you wanted are the **True Positives**, and the unwanted fish you accidentally pulled up are the **False Positives**.

Your **Precision = 7 / 10 = 0.70 or 70%**.

Precision does *not* care about the salmon that escaped your net — it only judges the *purity* of what you caught. This is why precision is also called **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** in statistics.

---

## 1.2 Recall

**Recall** (also known as **Sensitivity** or **True Positive Rate**) is the proportion of actual relevant items that were successfully retrieved by the system. It answers the complementary question: *"Of all the truly positive instances in the world, how many did the system manage to find?"*

$$\text{Recall (R)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

where **FN** stands for **False Negatives** — the relevant items the system *missed*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Recall matters in NLP:** In tasks like Named Entity Recognition (NER) for medical records or spam detection, missing a positive case (e.g., a cancer mention) is catastrophic. Therefore, recall-sensitive systems are common in safety-critical NLP applications.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnostic Test

A radiologist screens 100 patients who actually have a disease (the ground truth).

- The diagnostic test correctly flags 85 of them as sick.
- It misses 15 patients (says "healthy" when they are actually sick).

The **False Negatives** here = 15 (missed diagnoses).
**True Positives** = 85 (correctly identified).

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 \text{ (85\%)}$$

Recall measures the *coverage* of the system — its ability to find *all* positive instances, even at the cost of false alarms.

---

## 1.3 F-Measure (F-Score)

**F-Measure** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. It provides a single scalar that balances both metrics into one number, especially useful when the class distribution is imbalanced (as is common in NLP tasks like sentiment analysis or NER).

The general **F-beta** measure is defined as:

$$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{(\beta^2 \cdot P) + R}$$

The most common variant is **F1-measure**, where $\beta = 1$, giving equal weight to Precision and Recall:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harmonic Mean and not Arithmetic Mean?**  
> The harmonic mean punishes extreme values. A system with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.0 has Arithmetic Mean = 0.5 (looks decent) but Harmonic Mean = 0 (correctly reflects the system is useless). This makes F-measure a *strict* performance indicator.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Exam Student's Balance

Suppose a student scores:
- **Case A:** Math = 100/100, English = 0/100  
- **Case B:** Math = 50/100, English = 50/100

The arithmetic average in Case A is 50, but the student completely failed English.  
The **harmonic mean** correctly returns **0** for Case A and **50** for Case B — penalizing the imbalance. F-measure applies the same principle to Precision and Recall.

---

## 1.4 Test Sets

A **Test Set** (also called *held-out data* or *evaluation set*) is a portion of the labeled dataset that is **never seen by the model during training**. It is used only *once* (or a limited number of times) to estimate the generalization performance of the final model on unseen data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** The golden rule of ML/NLP evaluation is that the test set must be **completely independent** of the training set. Data leakage — even subtle leakage through preprocessing — inflates metrics artificially and causes models to fail in production.

The typical data partition ratios used in industry and academic NLP benchmarking are:

| **Split**     | **Purpose**                          | **Typical Size** |
|---------------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| Training Set  | Fit model parameters                 | 60% – 80%        |
| Validation Set (Dev Set) | Tune hyperparameters, model selection | 10% – 20%        |
| Test Set      | Final unbiased performance estimation | 10% – 20%        |

---

## 1.5 Cross-Validation

**Cross-Validation (CV)** is a statistical resampling technique used to evaluate a model's performance when the dataset is small and a single train/test split would produce high-variance estimates. The most common variant is **k-Fold Cross-Validation**.

**Procedure of k-Fold Cross-Validation:**

1. Shuffle the dataset randomly.
2. Split the dataset into **k** equal-sized (or near-equal) folds.
3. For each iteration $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$:
   - Use fold $i$ as the **test set**.
   - Use the remaining $k - 1$ folds as the **training set**.
   - Train the model and record its performance score $S_i$.
4. The final cross-validated performance is the **average** of all $k$ scores.

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV):** When $k = N$ (the total number of samples), each fold contains exactly one sample. This is computationally expensive but gives a nearly unbiased estimate.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Cooking Competition with Limited Judges

Suppose you baked 5 cookies and have only 3 judges. You cannot afford to test on all 5 cookies at once (you'd have no training data). Instead:

- **Round 1:** Train on cookies 1, 2, 3; test on cookie 4.
- **Round 2:** Train on cookies 1, 2, 4; test on cookie 5.
- **Round 3:** Train on cookies 1, 4, 5; test on cookie 2.
- (and so on, rotating which cookie is held out for testing)

You average the judge's scores from all rounds. Cross-validation works exactly like this rotation — every data point gets a chance to be the "test cookie," and the final score is the average of all rounds.

---

## 1.6 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of All Metrics

Every metric above is computed from the **Confusion Matrix**, a 2×2 table that summarizes the predictions versus the ground truth for a binary classification task.

| Predicted \ Actual | **Actual Positive** | **Actual Negative** |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Predicted Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Predicted Negative** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compute evaluation metrics" in the exam, **always start by drawing the confusion matrix first**, then plug values into the formulas. Examiners award step-marks for explicitly defining TP, FP, FN, TN before computation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving Precision and Recall from First Principles

Precision and Recall emerge directly from the **Bayesian Decision Theory** framework, where a classifier outputs a decision based on the posterior probability $P(\text{class} \mid \text{input})$. The choice of decision threshold (e.g., 0.5) determines the trade-off between the two metrics.

### Why Precision and Recall are Complementary

- **Increasing Recall** typically requires *lowering the classification threshold*, which causes the system to label more borderline cases as positive. This **decreases Precision** (more false alarms slip in).
- **Increasing Precision** typically requires *raising the threshold*, making the system more conservative. This **decreases Recall** (more genuine positives get rejected).

This tension is the famous **Precision-Recall Trade-off**, a core concept in NLP system evaluation.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of F-Measure Computation

1. **Compute Precision (P)** using the confusion matrix.
2. **Compute Recall (R)** using the confusion matrix.
3. **Verify** that both $P > 0$ and $R > 0$ — otherwise, $F_1$ is undefined (returns 0 by convention).
4. **Apply the harmonic mean formula** to get a single balanced score.

The **F-beta** parameter $\beta$ controls the relative weight:
- $\beta < 1$: Precision is weighted more heavily.
- $\beta = 1$: Equal weight (F1).
- $\beta > 1$: Recall is weighted more heavily.

For example, $\beta = 2$ (F2 measure) is commonly used in medical NLP where missing a diagnosis is more costly than a false alarm.

## 2.3 Why k-Fold Cross-Validation Reduces Variance

A single train/test split gives a **point estimate** of performance that depends heavily on which samples happened to land in the test set. By averaging over $k$ different splits, cross-validation reduces the **variance** of the estimate:

$$\text{Var}(\text{CV estimate}) \approx \frac{\text{Var}(S_i)}{k}$$

This means **larger k → lower variance**, at the cost of higher computation time. The **standard error** of the cross-validated score is:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma_{S_i}}{\sqrt{k}}$$

where $\sigma_{S_i}$ is the standard deviation of the per-fold scores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified k-Fold:** In NLP classification (especially with imbalanced classes), the **stratified** variant preserves the class ratio in every fold. This is the KTU-recommended default for text classification experiments.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **complete cheat sheet** for evaluation metrics. Memorize it for the KTU ESE.

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **What It Measures** | **Range** |
|------------|-------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Precision (P) | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Purity of positive predictions | $[0, 1]$ |
| Recall (R) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | $[0, 1]$ |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ |
| F-beta (general) | $F_{\beta} = (1+\beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | Weighted harmonic mean | $[0, 1]$ |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN}$ | Overall correctness | $[0, 1]$ |
| Specificity (TNR) | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate | $[0, 1]$ |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fall-out rate | $[0, 1]$ |
| Micro-F1 | $F_1^{\mu} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sum TP}{2 \cdot \sum TP + \sum FP + \sum FN}$ | Aggregate F1 across classes | $[0, 1]$ |
| Macro-F1 | $F_1^{M} = \dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} F_1^{(c)}$ | Unweighted mean of per-class F1 | $[0, 1]$ |
| k-Fold CV Score | $\text{CV} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$ | Mean performance across folds | depends on metric |
| Standard Error of CV | $SE = \dfrac{\sigma_{S_i}}{\sqrt{k}}$ | Uncertainty in CV estimate | $\geq 0$ |
| Leave-One-Out CV | $k = N$ (one sample per fold) | Maximum-fidelity estimate | depends on metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Micro-F1 vs Macro-F1 (NLP Multiclass Context):** In NLP tasks like NER or POS tagging, where we have many entity classes, **Macro-F1** treats all classes equally (good for rare-class evaluation), while **Micro-F1** pools all decisions globally (good for overall system performance). KTU questions on NER tagging frequently test this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Precision@K measures whether the top-K results are relevant. High precision is critical because users rarely scroll past the first page.
- **Medical NLP (Clinical NER):** Recall is paramount — missing a drug name or adverse event can harm patients.
- **Spam Filters (Gmail):** A blend of high precision (don't block legitimate mail) and high recall (block most spam) is required; F1 is the industry-standard metric.
- **Sentiment Analysis (Product Reviews):** Macro-F1 is preferred to ensure all sentiment classes (positive, negative, neutral) are evaluated fairly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Confusion Matrix to Metrics

**Problem:** An NLP sentiment classifier is tested on 200 reviews. The results are:

- 120 reviews were actually positive; the system correctly identified 100 of them.
- 80 reviews were actually negative; the system correctly identified 60 of them.
- The system misclassified 20 actual positive reviews as negative.
- The system misclassified 20 actual negative reviews as positive.

**Step 1: Construct the Confusion Matrix**

From the data:
- **TP** (predicted positive, actually positive) = 100
- **FN** (predicted negative, actually positive) = 20
- **FP** (predicted positive, actually negative) = 20
- **TN** (predicted negative, actually negative) = 60

We verify: $TP + FN + FP + TN = 100 + 20 + 20 + 60 = 200$ ✓

| Predicted \ Actual | **Actual Positive** | **Actual Negative** |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Predicted Positive** | 100 (TP) | 20 (FP) |
| **Predicted Negative** | 20 (FN) | 60 (TN) |

**Step 2: Compute Precision**

Precision is the fraction of positive predictions that are correct:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{100}{100 + 20} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333$$

**Step 3: Compute Recall**

Recall is the fraction of actual positives that were found:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{100}{100 + 20} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333$$

**Step 4: Compute F1-Score**

Apply the harmonic mean formula:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8333 \times 0.8333}{0.8333 + 0.8333}$$

**Sub-step 4a: Compute the numerator**

$$2 \cdot P \cdot R = 2 \cdot (0.8333)^2 = 2 \cdot 0.6944 = 1.3888$$

**Sub-step 4b: Compute the denominator**

$$2 \cdot (P + R) = 2 \cdot (0.8333 + 0.8333) = 2 \cdot 1.6666 = 3.3332$$

**Sub-step 4c: Final division**

$$F_1 = \frac{1.3888}{3.3332} = 0.4166 \text{ (approx)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students divide $P \cdot R$ by $(P + R)$ and forget the factor of **2** in the harmonic mean formula. Always remember: $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$, **not** $\dfrac{PR}{P+R}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Since $P = R$ in this example, $F_1$ should equal $P = R = 0.8333$. Let us re-derive using a more precise fraction.

**Corrected Sanity Derivation (using exact fractions):**

Since $P = R = \frac{100}{120} = \frac{5}{6}$ exactly,

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{(5/6) \cdot (5/6)}{(5/6) + (5/6)} = 2 \cdot \frac{25/36}{10/6} = 2 \cdot \frac{25}{36} \cdot \frac{6}{10} = 2 \cdot \frac{150}{360} = \frac{300}{360} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.8333$$

This confirms $F_1 = P = R = 0.8333$ when $P = R$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Precision-Recall Asymmetry

**Problem:** A medical NLP system screens 1000 patient notes for a rare disease. Results:
- The system flags 50 notes as positive.
- Of those 50, only 30 are truly positive (confirmed by expert review).
- In reality, 40 notes in the dataset are truly positive (but the system missed 10).

**Step 1: Identify Confusion Matrix Values**

- The system flagged 50 as positive → predicted positive count = 50.
- Of those 50, 30 were truly positive → **TP = 30**.
- The remaining 20 flagged notes are wrong → **FP = 20**.
- The system missed 10 true positives → **FN = 10**.
- True negatives = $1000 - (TP + FP + FN) = 1000 - (30 + 20 + 10) = 940$ → **TN = 940**.

| Predicted \ Actual | **Actual Positive** | **Actual Negative** |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Predicted Positive** | 30 (TP) | 20 (FP) |
| **Predicted Negative** | 10 (FN) | 940 (TN) |

**Step 2: Compute Precision**

$$P = \frac{30}{30 + 20} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Compute Recall**

$$R = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75$$

**Step 4: Compute F1**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.60 \times 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.45}{1.35} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} = 0.6667$$

**Step 5: Compute F2 (Recall-weighted)**

For medical applications, we use $\beta = 2$:

$$F_2 = (1 + 2^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{2^2 \cdot P + R} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.60 \times 0.75}{4 \cdot 0.60 + 0.75}$$

Compute numerator: $5 \cdot 0.45 = 2.25$

Compute denominator: $5 \cdot 0.45 / (2.4 + 0.75) = 2.25 / 3.15 = 0.7143$

Wait — let us re-derive step by step to avoid mistakes:

$$F_2 = 5 \cdot \frac{0.45}{2.40 + 0.75} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.45}{3.15} = 5 \cdot 0.1429 = 0.7143$$

So **F2 = 0.7143**, which is **higher than F1 = 0.6667** — this is expected because F2 weights recall more heavily, and our recall (0.75) is higher than precision (0.60).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This is exactly the kind of asymmetric calculation that earns full marks. Always show the intermediate computation of numerator and denominator separately.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — k-Fold Cross-Validation Computation

**Problem:** A Naive Bayes text classifier is evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation on a corpus of 500 documents. The F1 scores per fold are:

| Fold (i) | F1 Score (S_i) |
|----------|----------------|
| 1 | 0.82 |
| 2 | 0.78 |
| 3 | 0.85 |
| 4 | 0.80 |
| 5 | 0.75 |

**Step 1: Compute the Mean (CV Score)**

$$\bar{S} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i = \frac{0.82 + 0.78 + 0.85 + 0.80 + 0.75}{5}$$

$$\bar{S} = \frac{4.00}{5} = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Compute the Standard Deviation**

First, compute the squared deviations from the mean:

$$\begin{aligned}
(S_1 - \bar{S})^2 &= (0.82 - 0.80)^2 = 0.0004 \\
(S_2 - \bar{S})^2 &= (0.78 - 0.80)^2 = 0.0004 \\
(S_3 - \bar{S})^2 &= (0.85 - 0.80)^2 = 0.0025 \\
(S_4 - \bar{S})^2 &= (0.80 - 0.80)^2 = 0.0000 \\
(S_5 - \bar{S})^2 &= (0.75 - 0.80)^2 = 0.0025
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum (S_i - \bar{S})^2 = 0.0004 + 0.0004 + 0.0025 + 0.0000 + 0.0025 = 0.0058$$

Variance (using $n-1$ for sample standard deviation, as in research practice):

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.0058}{5 - 1} = \frac{0.0058}{4} = 0.00145$$

Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.00145} = 0.0381$$

**Step 3: Compute the Standard Error**

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{k}} = \frac{0.0381}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.0381}{2.2361} = 0.0170$$

**Step 4: Report the Final Result**

The cross-validated F1 score is reported as:

$$F_1^{CV} = 0.80 \pm 0.017$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always present cross-validation results as **mean ± standard error** (or mean ± std deviation). Examiners often award bonus marks for explicit uncertainty quantification.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

The following table maps each metric to its **best-use case** and **failure mode** — a common 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

| **Metric** | **Best Used When** | **Failure Mode (Pitfall)** |
|------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Accuracy | Classes are balanced | Misleading on imbalanced data (e.g., 99% TN can give 99% accuracy) |
| Precision | False positives are costly (e.g., spam blocking legit mail) | Ignores missed positives |
| Recall | False negatives are costly (e.g., cancer detection) | Ignores false alarms |
| F1 | You need a balanced single score | Hides asymmetric P/R performance |
| Micro-F1 | Multiclass, want global performance | Dominated by frequent classes |
| Macro-F1 | Multiclass, want rare-class fairness | Each class contributes equally regardless of size |
| k-Fold CV | Small datasets, need stable estimate | Computationally expensive for large k |
| LOOCV | Very small datasets (N < 50) | Extremely expensive; high variance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evaluation Pipeline Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete NLP evaluation workflow — from raw data to final cross-validated metric.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Labeled Corpus N samples] --> A1[Step 1 Shuffle and Stratify Data]
    A1 --> A2{Step 2 Choose Strategy}
    A2 -->|Holdout 80 20| B1[Step 3a Split Train 80 and Test 20]
    A2 -->|k Fold CV k equals 5 or 10| B2[Step 3b Partition into k Folds]
    B1 --> C1[Step 4 Train Model on Train Set]
    B2 --> C2[Step 4 Train k Times Rotating Test Fold]
    C1 --> D1[Step 5 Predict on Test Set]
    C2 --> D2[Step 5 Predict on Held Out Fold Each Iteration]
    D1 --> E1[Step 6 Build Confusion Matrix TP FP FN TN]
    D2 --> E2[Step 6 Build k Confusion Matrices]
    E1 --> F1[Step 7 Compute Precision Recall F1]
    E2 --> F2[Step 7 Average Metrics across k Folds]
    F1 --> G1[Step 8 Report Final Score]
    F2 --> G2[Step 8 Report Mean and Standard Error]
    G1 --> H1[End Final Evaluation Result]
    G2 --> H1
```

## 4.2 Precision-Recall Trade-off Schematic

The following block diagram visualizes how adjusting the decision threshold shifts the balance between Precision and Recall in a classifier (e.g., a logistic regression text classifier).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_THRESHOLD[LOW DECISION THRESSILE domain]
        T1[Threshold = 0.3] --> R1[Recall is HIGH catches most positives]
        R1 --> P1[Precision is LOW many false alarms]
    end
    subgraph MID_THRESHOLD[MODERATE THRESHOLD balanced zone]
        T2[Threshold = 0.5] --> R2[Recall and Precision are roughly equal]
        R2 --> P2[F1 score is maximized here]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_THRESHOLD[HIGH DECISION THRESHOLD conservative]
        T3[Threshold = 0.7] --> R3[Recall is LOW misses many positives]
        R3 --> P3[Precision is HIGH few false alarms]
    end
    LOW_THRESHOLD --> MID_THRESHOLD
    MID_THRESHOLD --> HIGH_THRESHOLD
```

## 4.3 k-Fold Cross-Validation Partition Diagram

The following schematic shows how a dataset of 25 samples is partitioned into 5 equal folds (5 samples each). The rotation of which fold serves as the test set is highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ITER1[ITERATION 1]
        F1A[Fold 1 TEST 5 samples]
        F1B[Fold 2 TRAIN 5 samples]
        F1C[Fold 3 TRAIN 5 samples]
        F1D[Fold 4 TRAIN 5 samples]
        F1E[Fold 5 TRAIN 5 samples]
    end
    subgraph ITER2[ITERATION 2]
        F2A[Fold 1 TRAIN]
        F2B[Fold 2 TEST]
        F2C[Fold 3 TRAIN]
        F2D[Fold 4 TRAIN]
        F2E[Fold 5 TRAIN]
    end
    subgraph ITER3[ITERATION 3]
        F3A[Fold 1 TRAIN]
        F3B[Fold 2 TRAIN]
        F3C[Fold 3 TEST]
        F3D[Fold 4 TRAIN]
        F3E[Fold 5 TRAIN]
    end
    subgraph ITER4[ITERATION 4]
        F4A[Fold 1 TRAIN]
        F4B[Fold 2 TRAIN]
        F4C[Fold 3 TRAIN]
        F4D[Fold 4 TEST]
        F4E[Fold 5 TRAIN]
    end
    subgraph ITER5[ITERATION 5]
        F5A[Fold 1 TRAIN]
        F5B[Fold 2 TRAIN]
        F5C[Fold 3 TRAIN]
        F5D[Fold 4 TRAIN]
        F5E[Fold 5 TEST]
    end
    ITER1 --> ITER2
    ITER2 --> ITER3
    ITER3 --> ITER4
    ITER4 --> ITER5
    ITER5 --> FINAL[Compute Mean and Standard Error of Scores]
```

## 4.4 Confusion Matrix to Metrics Mapping (Block Architecture)

The following block diagram shows the functional architecture mapping from raw confusion matrix entries to derived metrics — useful for KTU diagram-based questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Confusion Matrix Values] --> TP1[TP True Positive]
    INPUT --> FP1[FP False Positive]
    INPUT --> FN1[FN False Negative]
    INPUT --> TN1[TN True Negative]
    TP1 --> P1[Precision equals TP over TP plus FP]
    TP1 --> R1[Recall equals TP over TP plus FN]
    FP1 --> P1
    FN1 --> R1
    TN1 --> ACC1[Accuracy equals TP plus TN over Total]
    P1 --> F1[F1 equals 2 times P times R over P plus R]
    R1 --> F1
    F1 --> OUT[Final Scalar Evaluation Score]
    ACC1 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Precision, Recall, and F-measure. State the formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Precision (P):** The fraction of items retrieved by the system that are relevant. Formula: $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Recall (R):** The fraction of all relevant items that were successfully retrieved. Formula: $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. **(1 mark)**
- **F-measure (F1):** The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric. Formula: $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is k-fold cross-validation? Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** k-Fold Cross-Validation is a resampling technique where the dataset is divided into *k* equal folds. The model is trained *k* times, each time using a different fold as the test set and the remaining *k-1* folds as the training set. The final performance is the average of all *k* test scores. **(2 marks)**
- **Two advantages:** **(1) (i)** Reduces variance of the performance estimate compared to a single train/test split. **(ii)** Every data point is used for both training and testing, maximizing the use of limited data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module 1 questions follow a strict internal choice pattern. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are designed to test the same Course Outcomes but from different angles.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: For a binary text classification task (e.g., spam detection), a system produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 500 emails:**

| Predicted \ Actual | Spam (Positive) | Ham (Negative) |
|--------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Spam (Predicted Positive)** | 80 | 30 |
| **Ham (Predicted Negative)** | 20 | 370 |

**(a)** Calculate the Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Accuracy of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of k-fold cross-validation. How does 5-fold cross-validation differ from a single 80/20 hold-out split? What is the role of a validation (dev) set in model selection? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify Confusion Matrix Values**

From the table:
- **TP** = 80 (correctly identified spam)
- **FP** = 30 (ham wrongly flagged as spam)
- **FN** = 20 (spam missed, classified as ham)
- **TN** = 370 (correctly identified ham)

**Step 2: Compute Precision (P)** [2 marks for formula + substitution + answer]

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.7273$$

**Step 3: Compute Recall (R)** [2 marks]

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 4: Compute F1-Score** [2 marks]

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7273 \times 0.80}{0.7273 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5818}{1.5273} = \frac{1.1636}{1.5273} = 0.7619$$

**Step 5: Compute Accuracy** [1 mark]

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{80 + 370}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \text{ (90\%)}$$

**Final Answer for (a):** Precision = 0.7273, Recall = 0.80, F1 = 0.7619, Accuracy = 90%.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition of k-Fold Cross-Validation** [2 marks]:

k-Fold Cross-Validation is a statistical resampling procedure in which the original dataset of size *N* is randomly partitioned into *k* equally sized (or near-equally sized) folds. For each fold *i* ∈ {1, 2, ..., k}, the model is trained on the *k − 1* remaining folds and tested on the held-out fold *i*. The cross-validated performance is the mean of the *k* test scores:

$$CV_{score} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$$

**Comparison: 5-Fold CV vs 80/20 Hold-out** [3 marks]:

| **Aspect** | **80/20 Hold-out** | **5-Fold Cross-Validation** |
|------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Number of train/test runs | 1 | 5 |
| Data utilization | Only 80% used for training in each model | 100% of data used for both training and testing across runs |
| Variance of estimate | High (depends on a single split) | Low (averaged over 5 splits) |
| Computational cost | 1× training | 5× training |
| Bias | Higher (less data per training run) | Lower (uses 80% per training run, same as hold-out) |

The 5-Fold CV is **preferable when the dataset is small** because every sample contributes to both training and testing, leading to a more **stable and reliable** performance estimate.

**Role of Validation (Dev) Set** [2 marks]:

The validation (development) set is a held-out portion of the training data used to:
1. **Tune hyperparameters** (e.g., learning rate, regularization strength, number of layers in an NLP model).
2. **Compare candidate models** and select the best-performing architecture before final evaluation on the test set.
3. **Prevent data leakage** — by keeping the test set completely unseen until the very end, the dev set acts as a "proxy test set" during the iterative model development phase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of the confusion matrix with a neat diagram. Compute Micro-F1 and Macro-F1 for a 3-class NLP classification problem (classes: Positive, Negative, Neutral) given the following per-class counts: **(7 marks)**

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| Positive | 50 | 10 | 5 |
| Negative | 30 | 8 | 7 |
| Neutral | 20 | 5 | 3 |

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV). What are its advantages and disadvantages compared to 10-fold cross-validation? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Confusion Matrix Concept** [2 marks]:

A confusion matrix is a tabular representation that compares the **predicted class labels** (rows) with the **actual/true class labels** (columns) of a classifier. For a binary task, it is a 2×2 table with entries TP, FP, FN, TN. For multiclass tasks (like the one in this problem), it is a C×C table where *C* is the number of classes. Each cell $M_{ij}$ represents the number of samples belonging to actual class *i* that were predicted as class *j*.

**Diagram: Generic Multiclass Confusion Matrix for 3 Classes**

| Predicted \ Actual | **Positive** | **Negative** | **Neutral** |
|--------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Positive** | TP_pos (50) | FP_pos→neg (8) | FP_pos→neu (5) |
| **Negative** | FN_neg←pos (5) | TP_neg (30) | FP_neg→neu (3) |
| **Neutral** | FN_neu←pos (10) | FN_neu←neg (7) | TP_neu (20) |

(For per-class metrics, FP for a class = sum of its row excluding the diagonal; FN = sum of its column excluding the diagonal.)

**Step 1: Compute Per-Class Precision, Recall, F1** [2 marks]

For **Positive** class (TP=50, FP=10, FN=5):
$$P_{pos} = \frac{50}{50+10} = 0.8333, \quad R_{pos} = \frac{50}{50+5} = 0.9091$$
$$F1_{pos} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8333 \times 0.9091}{0.8333 + 0.9091} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7576}{1.7424} = 0.8696$$

For **Negative** class (TP=30, FP=8, FN=7):
$$P_{neg} = \frac{30}{30+8} = 0.7895, \quad R_{neg} = \frac{30}{30+7} = 0.8108$$
$$F1_{neg} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7895 \times 0.8108}{0.7895 + 0.8108} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6400}{1.6003} = 0.8000$$

For **Neutral** class (TP=20, FP=5, FN=3):
$$P_{neu} = \frac{20}{20+5} = 0.8000, \quad R_{neu} = \frac{20}{20+3} = 0.8696$$
$$F1_{neu} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8000 \times 0.8696}{0.8000 + 0.8696} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6956}{1.6696} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2: Compute Macro-F1** [1 mark]

$$F_1^{Macro} = \frac{F1_{pos} + F1_{neg} + F1_{neu}}{3} = \frac{0.8696 + 0.8000 + 0.8333}{3} = \frac{2.5029}{3} = 0.8343$$

**Step 3: Compute Micro-F1** [2 marks]

Pool all TP, FP, FN across classes:
$$\sum TP = 50 + 30 + 20 = 100$$
$$\sum FP = 10 + 8 + 5 = 23$$
$$\sum FN = 5 + 7 + 3 = 15$$

$$F_1^{Micro} = \frac{2 \cdot \sum TP}{2 \cdot \sum TP + \sum FP + \sum FN} = \frac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 100 + 23 + 15} = \frac{200}{238} = 0.8403$$

**Final Answer for (a):** Micro-F1 = 0.8403, Macro-F1 = 0.8343.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**LOOCV Explanation with Example** [3 marks]:

Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation is a special case of k-Fold Cross-Validation where **k = N** (the total number of samples in the dataset). In each iteration, the model is trained on **N − 1** samples and tested on the **single held-out sample**. This process is repeated **N** times, with each sample serving as the test point exactly once.

**Example:** Suppose a sentiment classifier is to be evaluated on a corpus of 5 reviews.
- **Iteration 1:** Train on reviews {2, 3, 4, 5}, test on review {1}.
- **Iteration 2:** Train on reviews {1, 3, 4, 5}, test on review {2}.
- **Iteration 3:** Train on reviews {1, 2, 4, 5}, test on review {3}.
- **Iteration 4:** Train on reviews {1, 2, 3, 5}, test on review {4}.
- **Iteration 5:** Train on reviews {1, 2, 3, 4}, test on review {5}.

The final LOOCV accuracy is the average accuracy across all 5 iterations.

**Advantages and Disadvantages vs 10-Fold CV** [4 marks]:

| **Aspect** | **LOOCV (k = N)** | **10-Fold CV** |
