|------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Bias | **Lowest bias** — uses N−1 samples for training in every fold | Slightly higher bias — uses 90% of data for training |
| Variance | **High variance** — the N models are highly correlated (each differs by only 1 sample) | **Lower variance** — models differ more substantially across folds |
| Computational cost | **Very high** — requires N training runs (impractical for large datasets or deep learning models) | **10× cost** — manageable for most modern NLP pipelines |
| Determinism | Fully deterministic (no random shuffling required in pure form) | Depends on the random fold split (can be mitigated with fixed seeds) |
| Best use case | Very small datasets (N < 50) | General-purpose benchmark; standard in NLP research |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy** — students often compute Accuracy when asked for Precision. Always re-read the question and match the metric to the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in F1** — F1 = 2PR/(P+R), not PR/(P+R). This single error can cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misinterpreting Micro-F1 vs Macro-F1** — Micro-F1 pools all TP/FP/FN globally; Macro-F1 averages per-class F1. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Confusing "validation set" with "test set"** — the validation (dev) set is used for hyperparameter tuning; the test set is used only once at the very end. Touching the test set during model development is a major violation.
> 5. **Reporting LOOCV without acknowledging computational cost** — always mention that LOOCV is impractical for large-scale NLP models like BERT or GPT.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precision (P)** = TP / (TP + FP) — measures *purity* of positive predictions.
- **Recall (R)** = TP / (TP + FN) — measures *coverage* of actual positives.
- **F1-Score** = 2PR / (P + R) — harmonic mean; balances P and R. Use F-beta for asymmetric weighting.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the foundational table — always draw it first in exam answers.
- **Accuracy** is misleading on imbalanced data (e.g., 99% negative class).
- **Micro-F1** pools all class decisions globally; **Macro-F1** averages per-class F1 equally.
- **Test Set** must be **completely held out** and used **only once** for final unbiased evaluation.
- **Validation (Dev) Set** is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **k-Fold Cross-Validation** rotates k-1 folds for training and 1 for testing; final score = mean across k folds.
- **Stratified k-Fold** preserves class distribution in every fold — preferred for imbalanced NLP data.
- **LOOCV** is k=N cross-validation; lowest bias but highest variance and computational cost.
- **Standard Error of CV** = σ / √k — always report CV results as mean ± SE.
- **Precision-Recall Trade-off** is governed by the decision threshold; lowering threshold increases recall but decreases precision.
- **F-beta parameter** controls P vs R weighting: β<1 favors precision, β>1 favors recall, β=1 is F1.
- **Real-world rule of thumb:** Use **Precision** when false positives are costly (spam filter), **Recall** when false negatives are costly (medical diagnosis), and **F1** when you need a single balanced score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annotating Linguistic Structures - Context-Free Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Annotating Linguistic Structures - Context-Free Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Context-Free Grammars (CFG)

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical system used in linguistics and computer science to model the syntactic structure of natural and formal languages. According to standard automata theory and the KTU 2024 NLP syllabus, a CFG is formally defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$$

Where:
- **$N$** = A finite set of *non-terminal symbols* (variables representing phrasal categories such as NP, VP, S)
- **$\Sigma$** = A finite set of *terminal symbols* (the actual words in the language; $\Sigma \cap N = \emptyset$)
- **$R$** = A finite set of *production rules* of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$
- **$S$** = A distinguished *start symbol* belonging to $N$ (typically $S$ stands for "Sentence")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A grammar is "context-free" because every production rule allows the left-hand side (LHS) non-terminal $A$ to be rewritten as $\beta$ *regardless of the surrounding context* in which $A$ appears. This context-independence is what makes CFGs tractable and widely used in parsing algorithms like CYK and Earley parsing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building with **LEGO blocks** to construct a sentence. Each LEGO piece is a grammatical category — a Noun Phrase (NP) is one block, a Verb Phrase (VP) is another, and Prepositional Phrases (PP) are smaller decorative blocks. The **rules of the grammar** are the instruction manual telling you which blocks can be attached to which.

- The **"start symbol"** $S$ is the empty baseplate on which you start building.
- **"Non-terminals"** are the partially completed LEGO structures (they still need to be broken down further).
- **"Terminals"** are the actual colored bricks you can hold — the final English words like *"the"*, *"cat"*, *"sat"*.

A **derivation** is the process of opening the instruction manual and following it step-by-step until only the final colored bricks remain. The end result — a single linear sequence of words — is the actual sentence you utter. The sequence of rules you followed forms a **parse tree**, which captures the hidden hierarchical skeleton of meaning that the flat string of words conceals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Annotation in NLP** refers to the process of adding linguistic labels (tags, constituents, dependencies) to raw text. When we *annotate linguistic structures* using a CFG, we are formally describing which syntactic groups a sentence belongs to, enabling computational systems to "understand" grammatical relationships.

## 1.3 The Role of Annotation in Linguistic Structure

In Corpus Linguistics and NLP, **linguistic annotation** is the practice of marking up text with explicit labels describing its grammatical properties. When applied to syntactic structures via CFGs, this involves:

1. **Constituency Annotation** — Identifying noun phrases, verb phrases, etc.
2. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — Labeling each word with its grammatical class.
3. **Treebank Construction** — Building large annotated corpora (e.g., Penn Treebank) where every sentence is paired with its CFG parse tree.
4. **Rule Induction** — Deriving probabilistic CFG rules from annotated data to enable statistical parsing.

This forms the bridge between abstract grammar theory and real-world language data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Context-Free Grammar

A CFG operates through **production rules** (also called *rewriting rules*). Each rule has exactly one non-terminal on the LHS. Let us dissect the components in detail.

### 2.1.1 Terminals vs Non-Terminals

| Symbol Type | Notation Convention | Example | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Terminals | Capital letters (S, NP, VP) | $NP$, $VP$, $PP$ | Placeholders that expand into other symbols |
| Terminals | Lowercase letters or quoted words | *cat*, *runs*, *"the"* | The actual lexicon — they never appear on LHS |
| Start Symbol | $S$ (always capital S by convention) | $S$ | The unique root non-terminal |

### 2.1.2 Production Rule Categories

A CFG production rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ can be classified as:

1. **Lexical Rule** — Generates a terminal word from a non-terminal.
   Example: $Noun \rightarrow \text{`cat'}$, $Verb \rightarrow \text{`runs'}$
2. **Phrasal Rule** — Generates a sequence of non-terminals.
   Example: $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$
3. **Epsilon Rule** — Generates the empty string.
   Example: $VP \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (used for pro-drop or imperative imperatives)
4. **Recursive Rule** — LHS non-terminal also appears on the RHS.
   Example: $NP \rightarrow NP\ PP$ (right-recursive) or $NP \rightarrow PP\ NP$ (left-recursive)
5. **Unit Rule** — LHS rewrites to a single non-terminal.
   Example: $Aux \rightarrow Verb$

## 2.2 Derivations: The Operational Engine of a CFG

A **derivation** is the sequence of rule applications that transforms the start symbol $S$ into a terminal string $w$. There are two standard traversal strategies:

### 2.2.1 Leftmost Derivation
At each step, we rewrite the **leftmost non-terminal** first. The notation is $S \Rightarrow_{lm} w$.

### 2.2.2 Rightmost Derivation (Canonical Derivation)
At each step, we rewrite the **rightmost non-terminal** first. The notation is $S \Rightarrow_{rm} w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Both leftmost and rightmost derivations produce the *same parse tree*, but they differ in the order in which branches are explored. The *rightmost derivation* is what powers **bottom-up parsers** (like shift-reduce), while the *leftmost derivation* powers **top-down parsers** (like recursive descent).

## 2.3 Parse Trees: The Visual Backbone of CFG Annotation

A **parse tree** (also called a *concrete syntax tree* or *phrase structure tree*) is the hierarchical representation of a derivation. It satisfies:

- The root is the start symbol $S$.
- Each internal node is a non-terminal.
- Each leaf node is a terminal (or $\varepsilon$).
- The children of any internal node $A$ form the RHS of a rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ applied at that node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Yield of a Tree:** The string obtained by reading the leaves of a parse tree from left to right is called the *yield* of the tree. The language generated by grammar $G$ is the set of all yields of all parse trees derivable from $S$.

## 2.4 Ambiguity in CFGs — A Critical KTU Topic

A grammar is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct parse tree** (or equivalently, more than one leftmost derivation). Ambiguity manifests in three primary forms in NLP:

| Ambiguity Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Lexical** | A word has multiple POS tags | *"bank"* $\rightarrow$ Noun (river bank) vs Verb (to bank money) |
| **Structural** | Multiple phrase structures for the same string | *"I saw the man with the telescope"* |
| **Attachment** | A modifier can attach to different heads | PP-attachment ambiguity in the telescope example |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams** — Memorize the table below. It encapsulates the entire mathematical machinery of CFGs.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| CFG Formal Definition | $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ | 4-tuple |
| Language Generated | $L(G) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ | Set of terminal strings derivable from $S$ |
| Production Rule | $A \rightarrow \beta,\ \text{where}\ A \in N,\ \beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$ | LHS has exactly one non-terminal |
| Chomsky Hierarchy Level | Type-2 (Context-Free) | More powerful than regular grammars (Type-3) |
| Parse Tree Leaves Concatenation | $\text{yield}(T) = w$ | Forms the sentence string |
| Tree Height | $h(T)$ | Number of edges on longest root-to-leaf path |
| Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) | $A \rightarrow BC\ \vert\ a$ | Each rule has either 2 non-terminals or 1 terminal |
| Probabilistic CFG (PCFG) | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$ | Product of rule probabilities in derivation |
| CYK Algorithm Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | Bottom-up dynamic programming for CNF grammars |
| Ambiguity Measure | $\text{Ambig}(w) =$ number of parse trees for $w$ | $\geq 2$ implies ambiguous grammar |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of CFGs in Engineering

- **Compiler Design** — CFGs form the theoretical foundation of syntax analysis in compilers (YACC, ANTLR, Bison).
- **Speech Recognition** — HMM-CFG hybrids model spoken language syntax.
- **Machine Translation** — Synchronous CFGs align source and target language structures.
- **Information Extraction** — Pattern-matching over CFG parses extracts structured data.
- **Grammar Checking Tools** — Grammarly, LanguageTool use CFG-based parsers.
- **Question Answering Systems** — Semantic parsing often leverages CFG variants (CCG, TAG).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Complete CFG Derivation of a Sentence

Consider the following simple English CFG $G_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &: S \rightarrow NP\ VP \\
R_2 &: NP \rightarrow Det\ N \\
R_3 &: VP \rightarrow V\ NP \\
R_4 &: Det \rightarrow \text{`the'} \\
R_5 &: N \rightarrow \text{`dog'} \\
R_6 &: V \rightarrow \text{`chased'} \\
R_7 &: N \rightarrow \text{`cat'}
\end{aligned}
$$

We wish to derive the sentence $w = \text{``the dog chased the cat''}$ using a **leftmost derivation**.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Leftmost Derivation

**Step 1 — Initialize from the start symbol.**

We begin with the start symbol $S$, which by definition is the root of all derivations.

$$S$$

**Step 2 — Apply rule $R_1$: $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$.**

This rule expands the entire sentence into a Noun Phrase followed by a Verb Phrase. The leftmost non-terminal is $NP$, and we are about to expand it. The application of $R_1$ replaces $S$ with $NP\ VP$.

$$S \Rightarrow NP\ VP$$

**Step 3 — Apply rule $R_2$: $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$.**

Following the leftmost derivation convention, we expand the leftmost non-terminal $NP$ into a Determiner followed by a Noun.

$$S \Rightarrow NP\ VP \Rightarrow Det\ N\ VP$$

**Step 4 — Apply rule $R_4$: $Det \rightarrow \text{`the'}$.**

The new leftmost non-terminal is $Det$. We substitute it with the terminal word *"the"*. Terminals are no longer rewritten.

$$S \Rightarrow Det\ N\ VP \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ N\ VP$$

**Step 5 — Apply rule $R_5$: $N \rightarrow \text{`dog'}$.**

The next leftmost non-terminal is $N$. We expand it to the terminal *"dog"*.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ N\ VP \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ VP$$

**Step 6 — Apply rule $R_3$: $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$.**

The only remaining non-terminal is $VP$, so we expand it into a Verb followed by a Noun Phrase.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ VP \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ V\ NP$$

**Step 7 — Apply rule $R_6$: $V \rightarrow \text{`chased'}$.**

The leftmost non-terminal is $V$. We replace it with the terminal verb *"chased"*.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ V\ NP \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ \text{`chased'}\ NP$$

**Step 8 — Apply rule $R_2$: $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$.**

We expand the inner $NP$ to another Determiner-Noun sequence.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ \text{`chased'}\ Det\ N$$

**Step 9 — Apply rule $R_4$: $Det \rightarrow \text{`the'}$.**

We replace the inner $Det$ with the terminal *"the"*.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ \text{`chased'}\ \text{`the'}\ N$$

**Step 10 — Apply rule $R_7$: $N \rightarrow \text{`cat'}$.**

The final remaining non-terminal is $N$, which we expand to the terminal *"cat"*.

$$S \Rightarrow \text{`the'}\ \text{`dog'}\ \text{`chased'}\ \text{`the'}\ \text{`cat'}$$

At this stage, the string contains only terminals, and the derivation is **complete**. The sequence of rule applications is:

$$R_1, R_2, R_4, R_5, R_3, R_6, R_2, R_4, R_7$$

### 3.1.2 Analytical Matrix for the Derivation

| Step | Rule Applied | String State | Leftmost Non-Terminal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | $S$ | $S$ |
| 1 | $R_1$ | $NP\ VP$ | $NP$ |
| 2 | $R_2$ | $Det\ N\ VP$ | $Det$ |
| 3 | $R_4$ | `the` $N\ VP$ | $N$ |
| 4 | $R_5$ | `the dog` $VP$ | $VP$ |
| 5 | $R_3$ | `the dog` $V\ NP$ | $V$ |
| 6 | $R_6$ | `the dog chased` $NP$ | $NP$ |
| 7 | $R_2$ | `the dog chased` $Det\ N$ | $Det$ |
| 8 | $R_4$ | `the dog chased the` $N$ | $N$ |
| 9 | $R_7$ | `the dog chased the cat` | None — Derivation Complete |

## 3.2 Proof Sketch: Every Regular Language is Context-Free

This is a classic KTU-style proof question. We must demonstrate that **Type-3 (Regular)** grammars are a strict subset of **Type-2 (Context-Free)** grammars.

### 3.2.1 Statement

If $L$ is a regular language, then $L$ is also a context-free language.

### 3.2.2 Proof

Let $G_{reg} = (N, \Sigma, R_{reg}, S)$ be a regular grammar. By definition, every rule in $R_{reg}$ has one of two restricted forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow aB \quad \text{(right-linear form)} \\
A &\rightarrow a \quad \text{(terminal form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $A, B \in N$ and $a \in \Sigma$.

We construct a CFG $G_{cfg} = (N, \Sigma, R_{cfg}, S)$ by setting $R_{cfg} = R_{reg}$.

Since every rule in $R_{cfg}$ has exactly one non-terminal $A$ on the LHS and a string $\beta$ on the RHS, the rules already conform to the CFG definition. Thus, $G_{cfg}$ is a valid CFG, and the language generated remains unchanged.

Therefore: $L(G_{cfg}) = L(G_{reg}) = L$, which proves the claim. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the converse fails:** Not all context-free languages are regular. For instance, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is context-free (generated by $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$) but cannot be recognized by any finite automaton or regular expression. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

## 3.3 CNF Conversion: A Worked Example

The CYK parsing algorithm requires grammars in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**, where every rule is either $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. We illustrate the conversion on a sample grammar.

### 3.3.1 Starting Grammar (Non-CNF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon \\
A &\rightarrow BcD \\
X &\rightarrow a
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 1: Eliminate $\varepsilon$-Productions

The rule $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ forces us to add "nulled" versions of all rules where $S$ appears on the RHS. We remove $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ and add variants.

After nulling: $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ is added; $A \rightarrow BcD$ is unaffected since $S$ does not appear on its RHS.

### 3.3.3 Step 2: Replace Terminals in Mixed Rules

In $S \rightarrow aSb$, the terminals $a$ and $b$ appear alongside non-terminals. Introduce new non-terminals $A_a \rightarrow a$ and $B_b \rightarrow b$:

$$S \rightarrow A_a S B_b$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3: Break Long RHS into Binary Rules

If a rule has more than two non-terminals on the RHS, break it down. For $A \rightarrow BcD$, first replace $c$:

$$A \rightarrow B C_c D \quad \text{where}\ C_c \rightarrow c$$

Then split into binary form by introducing $A_1$:

$$A \rightarrow B A_1, \quad A_1 \rightarrow C_c D$$

The final CNF grammar has all rules of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$, suitable for CYK parsing.

## 3.4 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) — Quick Derivation

A **PCFG** extends CFG by assigning a probability to each rule, such that for every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

The probability of a parse tree $T$ is the product of all rule probabilities used in the tree:

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$$

The probability of a string $w$ is the sum over all valid parse trees:

$$P(w) = \sum_{T : \text{yield}(T) = w} P(T)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For parsing disambiguation, we always select the parse tree $T^{*} = \arg\max_{T} P(T)$. This is the foundation of statistical parsing used in modern NLP systems.

**How to Read This Tree:**

- Reading the leaves left-to-right yields: *"the dog chased the cat"*.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CFG-Based Annotation Pipeline

This diagram maps the end-to-end NLP pipeline that uses CFGs to annotate linguistic structures in raw text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawText["Raw Text Input"]
    tokenizer["Tokenizer Module"]
    posTagger["POS Tagger Module"]
    cfgRules["CFG Production Rules Database"]
    parser["CFG Parser Engine"]
    parseTree["Generated Parse Tree"]
    treebank["Treebank Storage"]
    annotator["Linguistic Annotator"]
    finalOutput["Annotated Linguistic Structure"]

    rawText --> tokenizer
    tokenizer --> posTagger
    posTagger --> parser
    cfgRules --> parser
    parser --> parseTree
    parseTree --> treebank
    parseTree --> annotator
    treebank --> annotator
    annotator --> finalOutput
```

**Pipeline Walkthrough:**

1. **Raw Text Input** — Unannotated natural language text enters the system.
2. **Tokenizer Module** — Splits text into individual tokens (words, punctuation).
3. **POS Tagger Module** — Assigns a part-of-speech label to each token (NN, VB, DT, etc.).
4. **CFG Production Rules Database** — Stores the handcrafted or learned grammar rules.
5. **CFG Parser Engine** — Applies the rules to the POS-tagged sequence, building a parse tree. This is typically an Earley, CYK, or chart parser.
6. **Generated Parse Tree** — The hierarchical syntactic structure of the sentence.
7. **Treebank Storage** — Persistent storage (e.g., Penn Treebank format) for downstream training and evaluation.
8. **Linguistic Annotator** — Adds higher-level labels (e.g., semantic role, coreference) on top of the parse tree.
9. **Annotated Linguistic Structure** — The final output, ready for use in MT, QA, IE, etc.

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic 3: The Chomsky Grammar Hierarchy (Position of CFG)

```mermaid
graph TD
    type0["Type 0 Unrestricted Grammars"]
    type1["Type 1 Context Sensitive Grammars"]
    type2["Type 2 Context Free Grammars"]
    type3["Type 3 Regular Grammars"]

    type0 --> type1
    type1 --> type2
    type2 --> type3
```

**Hierarchy Explanation:**

- **Type 0 — Unrestricted:** No constraints on production rules. Most powerful; equivalent to Turing machines.
- **Type 1 — Context-Sensitive:** Productions of form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $\vert \gamma \vert > 0$.
- **Type 2 — Context-Free:** LHS restricted to a single non-terminal. *This is our focus topic.*
- **Type 3 — Regular:** LHS is a single non-terminal; RHS is restricted. Equivalent to finite-state automata.

The arrows denote **strict inclusion**: every regular language is context-free, but not every context-free language is regular. This is a frequent KTU 2024 viva question.

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic 4: Ambiguity Visualization — Classic PP-Attachment

```mermaid
graph TD
    sentA["S"]
    npA1["NP"]
    vpA["VP"]
    vA["V"]
    npA2["NP"]
    ppA["PP"]
    pA["P"]
    npA3["NP"]
    detA1["the"]
    nA1["man"]
    sawA["saw"]

    withA["with"]

    sentA --> npA1

    npA3 --> nA3

```

In the alternative parse, the PP *"with the telescope"* would attach under $VP$ instead of $NP$, yielding a different semantic interpretation: "The man used the telescope to see the girl" vs. "The man saw the girl who had the telescope." This is **structural ambiguity** — a single string with multiple parse trees.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Define a Context-Free Grammar. Explain the role of the start symbol with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal system defined by the 4-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ where $N$ is the set of non-terminals, $\Sigma$ is the set of terminals, $R$ is the set of production rules, and $S \in N$ is the distinguished **start symbol**.

The **start symbol** $S$ is the unique non-terminal from which every valid derivation begins. It represents the highest-level syntactic category — typically the complete "Sentence." All strings in the language $L(G)$ are derived by applying a sequence of production rules starting from $S$.

**Example:** For the grammar $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$, $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$, $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$, the start symbol $S$ initiates the derivation of any well-formed English sentence such as *"the cat sat on the mat"*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the start symbol with the entire language. The start symbol is **a single non-terminal**, not a sentence. The language is the *set of all terminal strings derivable from* $S$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**What is structural ambiguity in CFGs? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Structural ambiguity** occurs when a single string of words can be parsed in **more than one distinct way** by a given CFG, yielding multiple valid parse trees (i.e., multiple leftmost derivations).

**Classic Example:** The sentence *"I saw the man with the telescope"* has two parses:

1. **PP attaches to VP (Instrument Reading):** "I used the telescope to see the man."
2. **PP attaches to NP (Possessive Reading):** "I saw the man [who had the telescope]."

Both parses use the same terminal words, but the structural grouping of the prepositional phrase *"with the telescope"* differs, producing two semantically distinct interpretations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse *structural ambiguity* with *lexical ambiguity*. Lexical ambiguity concerns a single word having multiple meanings (e.g., *"bank"*); structural ambiguity concerns the same string having multiple phrase structures.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define a Context-Free Grammar formally. Explain the components $N$, $\Sigma$, $R$, and $S$ in detail with an example grammar that generates the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show the parse tree and leftmost derivation for the string $w = aabb$ using your grammar. Prove that the language generated is context-free but not regular. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Formal Definition of CFG and Example Grammar:**

A CFG is the 4-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:

- **$N$** (Non-terminals) = $\{S\}$ — a finite set of variables representing syntactic categories.
- **$\Sigma$** (Terminals) = $\{a, b\}$ — the actual alphabet symbols appearing in the generated strings.
- **$R$** (Production Rules) — a finite set of rewriting rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$.
- **$S$** (Start Symbol) = $S$ — the unique non-terminal from which derivation begins.

**Example Grammar** $G_2$ for $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &: S \rightarrow aSb \\
R_2 &: S \rightarrow ab
\end{aligned}
$$

The rule $R_1$ appends an $a$ to the left and a $b$ to the right of $S$ recursively, preserving the balance between $a$'s and $b$'s. The base case $R_2$ terminates the recursion with the smallest string $ab$.

**(b) Parse Tree and Derivation for $w = aabb$:**

**Leftmost Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSb && \text{(apply } R_1) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSbb && \text{(apply } R_1 \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow aa\ ab\ bb && \text{(apply } R_2 \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &= aabb && \text{(concatenation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree Structure:**

The parse tree for $aabb$ has $S$ at the root, with two children derived from rule $R_1$: an $a$ leaf and a child $S$, and a $b$ leaf and another child $S$ (depending on tree shape). The inner $S$ then splits again via $R_1$, and the innermost $S$ terminates via $R_2$ as $ab$.

**Proof that $L$ is Context-Free but Not Regular:**

*L is Context-Free:* By construction, $G_2$ is a valid CFG since every production has a single non-terminal $S$ on the LHS. Therefore $L = L(G_2)$ is context-free.

*L is Not Regular:* We use the **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages**. Assume for contradiction that $L$ is regular with pumping length $p$. Consider the string $s = a^{p}b^{p} \in L$. By the pumping lemma, $s = xyz$ where $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. The substring $y$ consists entirely of $a$'s (since the first $p$ characters are all $a$'s). Pumping $y$ up gives $s' = xy^{2}z = a^{p+\vert y \vert}b^{p}$, which has more $a$'s than $b$'s, so $s' \notin L$. This contradicts the pumping lemma assumption. Hence, $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When writing the parse tree, students often confuse the *order* of children. Recall that in CFG parse trees, the children of a node appear in the **same order** as the symbols on the RHS of the production rule. Mixing up left-right order will cost full marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of *derivation* and *parse tree* in CFGs. Differentiate between leftmost and rightmost derivations with a worked example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)? Convert the following grammar into CNF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAb \mid bB \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

Demonstrate each conversion step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Derivations and Parse Trees:**

A **derivation** is a sequence of rule applications that transforms the start symbol $S$ into a terminal string. Formally, a derivation is a sequence:

$$S = \gamma_0 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 \Rightarrow \gamma_2 \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow \gamma_n = w$$

where each $\Rightarrow$ corresponds to substituting a non-terminal in $\gamma_i$ with the RHS of one of its production rules.

A **parse tree** is a tree-structured representation of a derivation where:
- The root is labeled with $S$.
- Each internal node is labeled with a non-terminal.
- The children of an internal node $A$ form the RHS of the rule applied at that node.
- Each leaf is labeled with a terminal (or $\varepsilon$).

**Difference Between Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations:**

- In a **leftmost derivation**, at each step, the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is replaced.
- In a **rightmost derivation**, the **rightmost non-terminal** is replaced first.

**Worked Example with Grammar $G_1$ and $w = \text{``the dog chased the cat''}$:**

| Step | Leftmost Derivation | Rightmost Derivation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $S$ | $S$ |
| 1 | $NP\ VP$ | $NP\ VP$ |
| 2 | $Det\ N\ VP$ | $NP\ V\ NP$ |
| 3 | `the` $N\ VP$ | $NP\ V\ Det\ N$ |
| 4 | `the dog` $VP$ | $NP\ V\ Det\ \text{`cat'}$ |
| 5 | `the dog` $V\ NP$ | $NP\ V\ \text{`the'}\ \text{`cat'}$ |
| 6 | `the dog chased` $NP$ | $NP\ \text{`chased'}\ \text{`the'}\ \text{`cat'}$ |
| 7 | `the dog chased` $Det\ N$ | $Det\ N\ \text{`chased'}\ \text{`the'}\ \text{`cat'}$ |
| 8 | `the dog chased the` $N$ | `the` $N\ \text{`chased'}\ \text{`the'}\ \text{`cat'}$ |
| 9 | `the dog chased the cat` | `the dog chased the cat` |

Both derivations produce the **same parse tree**, but differ in the order of rule application.

**(b) CNF Conversion:**

**Definition:** A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production rule has one of the two forms:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (where $B, C \in N$)
- $A \rightarrow a$ (where $a \in \Sigma$)

**Original Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAb \mid bB \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Eliminate mixed RHS (replace terminals in long rules):**

Introduce new non-terminals for terminals appearing alongside other symbols. Let $C_a \rightarrow a$ and $C_b \rightarrow b$.

The rule $S \rightarrow aAb$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$. The rule $S \rightarrow bB$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_b B$.

**Step 2 — Break long RHS into binary form:**

The rule $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$ has three symbols on the RHS. Introduce $D$ to split it:

$$S \rightarrow C_a D, \quad D \rightarrow A C_b$$

**Step 3 — Final CNF Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a D \mid C_b B \\
D &\rightarrow A C_b \\
A &\rightarrow C_a A \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow C_b B \mid b \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar is in CNF: every rule has either two non-terminals on the RHS or a single terminal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to handle the **unit rules** (e.g., $A \rightarrow a$ already conforms, but check for chains like $A \rightarrow B, B \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow a$). A complete CNF conversion requires eliminating unit productions, useless symbols, and $\varepsilon$-productions, in that order. Missing any step costs significant marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CFG Annotation (Module 2)**

- **CFG Formalism:** $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ — memorize the 4-tuple and the role of each component.
- **Context-Freedom:** LHS of every rule is a *single non-terminal*, with no context dependencies.
- **Start Symbol:** Always a designated non-terminal $S$ (usually denoting "Sentence").
- **Derivations:** Sequence of rule applications: leftmost (LM) vs rightmost (RM) — both yield the *same parse tree*.
- **Parse Tree:** Root = $S$; leaves = terminals; yield = the sentence string.
- **Ambiguity:** A grammar is ambiguous if any string has $\geq 2$ parse trees. Three types: lexical, structural, attachment.
- **Chomsky Hierarchy:** Type 0 ⊃ Type 1 ⊃ Type 2 (CFG) ⊃ Type 3 (Regular).
- **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):** All rules are $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. Required for CYK algorithm.
- **CYK Algorithm:** $\mathcal{O}(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$ bottom-up dynamic programming parser for CNF grammars.
- **PCFG:** Assigns probabilities to rules; $P(T) = \prod P(R_i)$; used for statistical parsing and disambiguation.
- **Treebanks:** Annotated corpora (e.g., Penn Treebank) with CFG parse trees for training statistical parsers.
- **Real-World Use:** Compilers (YACC, ANTLR), MT, speech recognition, grammar checking, IE, QA.
- **Pumping Lemma:** Use it to prove languages are *not* regular (and thus require CFG power).
- **Annotation Workflow:** Raw Text → Tokenize → POS Tag → CFG Parse → Treebank → Higher-Level Annotation.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** CFG derivations and parse trees carry 7–14 marks; definitions and ambiguity carry 3 marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Practice drawing parse trees for at least 10–15 different sentences. The examiner awards full marks for clean, well-labeled trees with clearly visible non-terminal-to-rule mappings. Avoid writing the sentence string and tree branches ambiguously — use proper indentation and explicit labels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constituency Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constituency Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Constituency Parsing** is the process of analyzing the syntactic structure of a sentence according to a **Phrase Structure Grammar** (typically a **Context-Free Grammar, CFG**), and producing a **parse tree** (also called a *constituency tree* or *phrase structure tree*) in which each internal node represents a *constituent* (a phrase such as NP, VP, PP), and the leaves are the lexical tokens of the sentence.

Formally, given:
- A sentence $S = w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$
- A context-free grammar $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where
  - $V$ = set of non-terminal symbols (e.g., NP, VP, S)
  - $\Sigma$ = set of terminal symbols (the vocabulary)
  - $R$ = set of production rules
  - $S$ = designated start symbol

A **constituency parser** finds a sequence of derivations $S \Rightarrow^* S$ such that $S$ is generated by $G$, and exposes the structural tree representing how the non-terminals expand.

> [!NOTE] Why "Constituency"?
> The term *constituency* comes from the linguistic observation that words in a sentence group together into **constituents** — clusters of words that behave as a single syntactic unit. For example, in *"The big red dog barks"*, the words *The big red dog* collectively form a Noun Phrase (NP), and they can be moved, replaced, or tested as a unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sentence Lego Blocks" View

Imagine a sentence is a **Lego tower** built from individual bricks (words). Constituency parsing is the act of **regrouping those bricks into the official Lego sub-structures** (rectangles, wheels, base plates) that snap together in only certain valid ways.

- Each brick = a **word** (terminal)
- Each sub-structure = a **constituent / phrase** (non-terminal like NP, VP)
- The official building manual = the **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**
- The final exploded diagram = the **parse tree**

Just as you cannot legally attach a wheel directly to the roof of a Lego house, you cannot, in English, attach a Determiner (*the*) directly to a Verb (*runs*) without grouping them inside larger phrases. The CFG encodes which groupings are legal; the parser discovers them.

> [!TIP] Intuition Check
> Try this substitution test: *"The big red dog barks"* → *"It barks"*. The fact that the entire NP *The big red dog* can be replaced by the single pronoun *It* proves it is a true **constituent**. Constituency parsing automates these substitution and movement tests at scale.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Constituent** | A maximal group of words functioning as a single unit | *The big red dog* |
| **Parse Tree** | Hierarchical tree showing phrase decomposition | Root = S, leaves = words |
| **CFG** | Formal grammar where LHS is a single non-terminal | $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$ |
| **Start Symbol (S)** | The root non-terminal of the grammar | $S$ |
| **Derivation** | A sequence of rule applications generating the sentence | $S \Rightarrow NP \; VP \Rightarrow \dots$ |
| **Yield** | The string of terminal leaves of a parse tree | The original sentence |
| **Ambiguity** | A sentence having more than one valid parse | *"I saw the man with the telescope"* |

## 1.4 Where Constituency Parsing Sits in the NLP Pipeline

$$
\text{Raw Text} \rightarrow \text{Tokenization} \rightarrow \text{Part-of-Speech Tagging} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Constituency Parsing}} \rightarrow \text{Semantic Role Labeling} \rightarrow \text{Meaning}
$$

Its output (the parse tree) feeds downstream tasks such as **machine translation alignment**, **question answering**, **information extraction**, **sentiment analysis**, and **text generation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Context-Free Grammar (CFG) — The Foundation

A **Context-Free Grammar** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ with the following production:

$$
A \rightarrow \beta \qquad \text{where } A \in V \text{ and } \beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}
$$

> [!NOTE] "Context-Free" Meaning
> The rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ can be applied **regardless of the surrounding context** in which $A$ appears. The left-hand side is *always* a single non-terminal, never a string containing terminals.

### 2.1.1 Components of a CFG

| Component | Symbol | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-terminals | $V$ | Phrase categories | $\{S, NP, VP, PP, DT, NN, VB\}$ |
| Terminals | $\Sigma$ | Lexical items | $\{the, dog, cat, saw, a\}$ |
| Rules | $R$ | Production rewrites | $NP \rightarrow DT \; NN$ |
| Start symbol | $S$ | Root of every derivation | $S$ |

### 2.1.2 Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

> [!IMPORTANT] CNF is required for the CYK algorithm.
> A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every rule takes one of only two forms:
>
> 1. $A \rightarrow B \; C$ where $B, C \in V$ (binary non-terminal rule)
> 2. $A \rightarrow a$ where $a \in \Sigma$ (terminal emission rule)
>
> No $\varepsilon$-productions, no unit productions, no rules with more than 2 symbols on the RHS.

Any CFG can be **converted to CNF** in $O(|G|^2)$ using standard transformations (Chomsky 1959): binarization, unit-production removal, useless-symbol elimination.

### 2.1.3 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)

A **PCFG** extends a CFG by attaching a probability $P(\alpha \rightarrow \beta)$ to every rule such that:

$$
\sum_{\beta} P(\alpha \rightarrow \beta) = 1 \quad \forall \alpha \in V
$$

The probability of a parse tree $T$ with rule applications $r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m$ is:

$$
P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(r_i)
$$

> [!NOTE] Independence Assumption
> PCFGs assume that the probability of expanding a non-terminal is **independent of its context** (its parent, siblings, or position). This is a strong simplification — modern parsers relax it with lexicalization or neural conditioning.

## 2.2 Structural vs. Lexical Ambiguity

**Structural (syntactic) ambiguity** arises when a single string has multiple parse trees. The two classic sources:

1. **Attachment ambiguity** — Where does a modifier attach?
   *"I saw the man **with the telescope**."* — Does the telescope belong to *I* (instrument of seeing) or *the man* (possession)?
2. **Coordination ambiguity** — Which phrases are conjoined?
   *"**Old men and women**"* — are only *men* old, or both *men and women*?

## 2.3 Parsing Strategies — Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up

| Strategy | Direction | Idea | Key Issue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top-Down** | Start symbol → leaves | Expand $S$ using grammar rules; match words | Wastes effort on subtrees that do not match any word |
| **Bottom-Up** | Words → start symbol | Reduce words using grammar rules; look for $S$ | Wastes effort on subtrees that cannot lead to $S$ |
| **Chart Parsing** | Both, with memoization | Stores completed subtrees in a *chart* | $O(n^3)$ worst case; avoids redundant work |

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Tip
> If asked to compare, mention that **top-down** cannot handle **left-recursive** grammars (e.g., $NP \rightarrow NP \; PP$), whereas **bottom-up** can. Chart parsers (CYK, Earley) combine the advantages of both.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---:|:---|:---|
| CFG rule form | $A \rightarrow \beta$, $A \in V$ | Left side must be one non-terminal |
| CNF rule form | $A \rightarrow B \; C$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Binary branching only |
| PCFG rule probability | $P(\alpha \rightarrow \beta) \geq 0$ | Sums to 1 over all $\beta$ for fixed $\alpha$ |
| Parse-tree probability | $P(T) = \prod_{i} P(r_i)$ | Product over all rules used |
| Sentence probability under PCFG | $P(w_1^n) = \sum_{T:\, yield(T)=w_1^n} P(T)$ | Sum over **all** valid parse trees |
| Best parse (Viterbi CKY) | $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T)$ | Maximum-probability tree |
| Inside probability $\alpha(A,i,j)$ | $P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i^{j-1})$ | Prob. that $A$ generates span $[i,j)$ |
| Inside base case | $\alpha(A,i,i+1) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ | For terminal emission |
| Inside recursive case | $\alpha(A,i,k) = \sum_{i \leq j < k} \sum_{B,C} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(B,i,j) \cdot \alpha(C,j,k)$ | Sum over splits and binary rules |
| Sentence inside prob. | $P(w_1^n) = \alpha(S,1,n+1)$ | Equals sum over all parses |
| CYK complexity | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Polynomial in sentence length |

> [!WARNING] KTU Notation Watch
> Some textbooks index spans as $[i, j]$ inclusive; others as $[i, j)$ half-open. The choice affects the Inside base case. Always **declare your convention** in the exam to avoid losing method marks.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Constituency Parsing

Constituency parses are deployed in:

- **Machine Translation** — to re-order phrases correctly between languages with different word order (e.g., English SVO vs. Japanese SOV).
- **Question Answering** — to identify *what* is asked (the question's focus) and locate the corresponding constituent in the passage.
- **Information Extraction** — noun phrases serve as candidate entities, verb phrases as candidate relations.
- **Grammar Checking** — to detect subject-verb agreement errors, dangling modifiers, and run-on sentences.
- **Knowledge-base construction** — converting natural-language text into triple-like structures $(\text{subject}, \text{predicate}, \text{object})$ aligned with parse constituents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 CYK Algorithm (Cocke–Kasami–Younger)

The CYK algorithm decides whether a string $w_1^n$ is generated by a CFG in CNF and, for a PCFG, computes the most probable parse.

### 3.1.1 Algorithmic Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Input:** Sentence $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ and grammar $G$ in CNF.

**Output:** Parse tree(s) and probabilities.

**Initialization (length-1 cells):**
For each word position $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
$$
C[i][i+1] = \{\, A \in V \mid A \rightarrow w_i \in R \,\}
$$

**Recursion (length-$\ell$ cells, for $\ell = 2, 3, \dots, n$):**
For each span $[i, j)$ of length $\ell$:
$$
C[i][j] = C[i][j] \cup \{\, A \in V \mid \exists k, \, \exists B, C \in V \text{ with } A \rightarrow BC \in R, \, B \in C[i][k], \, C \in C[k][j] \,\}
$$

**Termination:**
If $S \in C[1][n+1]$, the sentence is grammatically valid.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — PCFG-CYK

**Sentence:** *"the dog saw a cat"* (n = 5)

**Grammar (already in CNF with probabilities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP && P = 1.0 \\
NP &\rightarrow DT \; NN && P = 0.5 \\
VP &\rightarrow VB \; NP && P = 1.0 \\
DT &\rightarrow \text{the} && P = 0.5 \\
DT &\rightarrow a && P = 0.3 \\
NN &\rightarrow dog && P = 0.3 \\
NN &\rightarrow cat && P = 0.2 \\
VB &\rightarrow saw && P = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Initialization (length-1 cells):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C[1][2] &= \{\, DT \rightarrow \text{the} \,\} = \{ DT \} \\
C[2][3] &= \{\, NN \rightarrow dog \,\} = \{ NN \} \\
C[3][4] &= \{\, VB \rightarrow saw \,\} = \{ VB \} \\
C[4][5] &= \{\, DT \rightarrow a \,\} = \{ DT \} \\
C[5][6] &= \{\, NN \rightarrow cat \,\} = \{ NN \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Length-2 cells:**

Cell $C[1][3]$ (span *"the dog"*). Split at $k = 2$:
$$
\text{Need rule } A \rightarrow B \; C \text{ with } B \in C[1][2]=\{DT\},\; C \in C[2][3]=\{NN\}.
$$
Rule $NP \rightarrow DT \; NN$ matches. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{prob}(NP, 1, 3) &= P(NP \rightarrow DT \; NN) \times P(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) \times P(NN \rightarrow \text{dog}) \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \\
&= 0.075
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
C[1][3] = \{ NP \}
$$

Cell $C[4][6]$ (span *"a cat"*). Split at $k = 5$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{prob}(NP, 4, 6) &= P(NP \rightarrow DT \; NN) \times P(DT \rightarrow a) \times P(NN \rightarrow \text{cat}) \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 \\
&= 0.030
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
C[4][6] = \{ NP \}
$$

**Step 3 — Length-3 cells:**

Cell $C[3][6]$ (span *"saw a cat"*). Possible splits: $k=4$ or $k=5$.

- Split $k=4$: need $A \rightarrow VB \; DT$. No such rule.
- Split $k=5$: need $A \rightarrow VB \; NP$. Rule $VP \rightarrow VB \; NP$ matches.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{prob}(VP, 3, 6) &= P(VP \rightarrow VB \; NP) \times P(VB \rightarrow \text{saw}) \times \text{prob}(NP, 4, 6) \\
&= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.030 \\
&= 0.030
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
C[3][6] = \{ VP \}
$$

**Step 4 — Length-4 cells:**

Cell $C[2][6]$ (span *"dog saw a cat"*). Splits $k=3,4,5$ — none yield a valid rule (NP+VP, NN+VP, NP+NP, etc., none exist). Hence $C[2][6] = \varnothing$.

Cell $C[1][5]$ (span *"the dog saw a"*). Splits $k=2,3,4$. $C[2][5]=\varnothing$, so only $k=3$ matters: need NP+VP. $C[1][3]=\{NP\}$ and $C[3][5]=\varnothing$, so no derivation.

**Step 5 — Length-5 cell (the full sentence):**

Cell $C[1][6]$ (span *"the dog saw a cat"*). Splits $k=2,3,4,5$.

- $k=2$: NP + $C[2][6]=\varnothing$. Reject.
- $k=3$: NP + VP. We have $NP \in C[1][3]$ with prob $0.075$ and $VP \in C[3][6]$ with prob $0.030$. Rule $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$ applies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{prob}(S, 1, 6) &= P(S \rightarrow NP \; VP) \times \text{prob}(NP, 1, 3) \times \text{prob}(VP, 3, 6) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.075 \times 0.030 \\
&= 0.00225
\end{aligned}
$$

- $k=4$: $C[1][4]=\varnothing$ (no rule for DT+VB). Reject.
- $k=5$: $C[1][5]=\varnothing$. Reject.

$$
C[1][6] = \{ S \}, \quad P(T^{\star}) = 0.00225
$$

**Result:** The sentence is valid; the unique best parse has probability **0.00225**.

### 3.1.3 CYK Chart Visualization

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccccc}
\text{Span} & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
1 & \cdot & \{DT\} & \{NP,\,0.075\} & \varnothing & \varnothing & \{S,\,0.00225\} \\
2 & & \cdot & \{NN\} & \varnothing & \varnothing & \varnothing \\
3 & & & \cdot & \{VB\} & \varnothing & \{VP,\,0.030\} \\
4 & & & & \cdot & \{DT\} & \{NP,\,0.030\} \\
5 & & & & & \cdot & \{NN\} \\
6 & & & & & & \cdot \\
\end{array}
$$

## 3.2 The Inside Algorithm — Derivation

The Inside algorithm computes $\alpha(A, i, j) = P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i^{j-1})$, the total probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the substring $w_i, w_{i+1}, \dots, w_{j-1}$.

### 3.2.1 Recursive Derivation from First Principles

By definition of CFG generation, the string from span $[i, j)$ can be produced by either:

**(a) A terminal rule:** $A$ emits the single word $w_i$. This is possible iff $j = i+1$ and the rule $A \rightarrow w_i$ exists, with probability $P(A \rightarrow w_i)$.

**(b) A binary rule:** $A \rightarrow BC$ where $B$ generates the left substring $[i, k)$ and $C$ generates the right substring $[k, j)$, for some split point $i \leq k < j$. The events are assumed **independent** given the rule (PCFG assumption).

Summing over all possible splits $k$ and all possible binary rules $A \rightarrow BC$ yields the recursive form below.

$$
\alpha(A, i, j) = 
\begin{cases}
\sum_{A \rightarrow w_i \in R} P(A \rightarrow w_i), & j = i+1 \\[6pt]
\displaystyle\sum_{i \leq k < j} \sum_{A \rightarrow BC \in R} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(C, k, j), & j > i+1
\end{cases}
$$

The total probability of the sentence is $\alpha(S, 1, n+1)$.

### 3.2.2 Inside Computation on the Worked Example

Reusing the PCFG from §3.1.2, we compute the same cell values — the CYK algorithm with PCFGs **is** the Inside algorithm applied to a CNF grammar. The quantities in the table are exactly $\alpha(\cdot, i, j)$.

For instance:
$$
\alpha(NP, 1, 3) = 0.075, \quad \alpha(VP, 3, 6) = 0.030, \quad \alpha(S, 1, 6) = 0.00225
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Inside vs. CYK
> - **CYK** is a *recognition* algorithm: does any parse exist? With PCFG, it returns the **best** parse (Viterbi variant) by replacing sums with max.
> - **Inside** is a *summation* algorithm: what is the **total** probability mass of *all* parses?
>
> Both share the same $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ dynamic-programming skeleton; only the combiner (sum vs. max vs. sum-of-max) differs.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Parsing Algorithms

| Algorithm | Direction | Grammar Form | Output | Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Recursive Descent** | Top-down | Any | First parse | $O(\vert G \vert \cdot n)$ avg, exponential worst | Fails on left recursion |
| **Shift-Reduce** | Bottom-up | Any | One parse | Linear per step | Used in dependency parsing |
| **Earley** | Chart, left-to-right | Any CFG | All parses | $O(n^3)$ worst, $O(n^2)$ for unambiguous | Handles left recursion |
| **CYK** | Bottom-up chart | CNF only | All/best parses | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Simplest to implement |
| **Inside** | Bottom-up chart | CNF | Total prob | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | For PCFGs |
| **Viterbi CKY** | Bottom-up chart | CNF | Best parse | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Max instead of sum |

## 3.4 Worked Derivation — Tree Probability From Scratch

Given the parse tree of *"the dog saw a cat"*:

$$
T = (S \rightarrow NP\;VP)(NP \rightarrow DT\;NN)(DT \rightarrow \text{the})(NN \rightarrow \text{dog})(VP \rightarrow VB\;NP)(VB \rightarrow \text{saw})(NP \rightarrow DT\;NN)(DT \rightarrow a)(NN \rightarrow \text{cat})
$$

The probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\;VP) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow DT\;NN) \cdot P(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) \\
&\quad \cdot P(NN \rightarrow \text{dog}) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow VB\;NP) \cdot P(VB \rightarrow \text{saw}) \\
&\quad \cdot P(NP \rightarrow DT\;NN) \cdot P(DT \rightarrow a) \cdot P(NN \rightarrow \text{cat}) \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.00225
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the Inside/CYK result — confirming the dynamic-programming recursion is correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constituency Parse Tree — Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["S"]
    NP1["NP subject"]
    VP1["VP"]
    DT1["DT: the"]
    NN1["NN: dog"]
    VBZ1["VB: saw"]
    NP2["NP object"]
    DT2["DT: a"]
    NN2["NN: cat"]
    S1 --> NP1
    S1 --> VP1
    NP1 --> DT1
    NP1 --> NN1
    VP1 --> VBZ1
    VP1 --> NP2
    NP2 --> DT2
    NP2 --> NN2
```

**How to read this tree:**

- Root `S` splits into the subject NP and the predicate VP — the universal *subject-predicate* cut.
- The subject NP further decomposes into `DT + NN`, matching the phrase *the dog*.
- The VP decomposes into `VB + NP`, matching the verb *saw* and its object NP *a cat*.
- All terminal leaves, read left-to-right, reconstruct the original sentence — this is the **yield**.

## 4.2 Attachment Ambiguity — Two Parse Trees, One Sentence

```mermaid
graph TD
    title["Ambiguity: I saw the man with the telescope"]

    title1["Parse 1: telescope = instrument of seeing"]
    S_A["S"]
    NP_A1["NP: I"]
    VP_A["VP"]
    VB_A["V: saw"]
    NP_A2["NP"]
    DT_A["DT: the"]
    NN_A["N: man"]
    PP_A["PP"]
    P_A["P: with"]
    NP_A3["NP"]
    DT_A2["DT: the"]
    NN_A2["N: telescope"]
    S_A --> NP_A1
    S_A --> VP_A
    VP_A --> VB_A
    VP_A --> NP_A2
    NP_A2 --> DT_A
    NP_A2 --> NN_A
    NP_A2 --> PP_A
    PP_A --> P_A
    PP_A --> NP_A3
    NP_A3 --> DT_A2
    NP_A3 --> NN_A2

    title2["Parse 2: telescope = possessed by the man"]
    S_B["S"]
    NP_B1["NP: I"]
    VP_B["VP"]
    VB_B["V: saw"]
    NP_B2["NP"]
    DT_B["DT: the"]
    NN_B["N: man"]
    PP_B["PP"]
    P_B["P: with"]
    NP_B3["NP"]
    DT_B2["DT: the"]
    NN_B2["N: telescope"]
    S_B --> NP_B1
    S_B --> VP_B
    VP_B --> VB_B
    VP_B --> NP_B2
    NP_B2 --> DT_B
    NP_B2 --> NN_B
    NP_B2 --> PP_B
    PP_B --> P_B
    PP_B --> NP_B3
    NP_B3 --> DT_B2
    NP_B3 --> NN_B2
```

> [!NOTE] Observation
> Both trees produce the identical yield string but differ in **structural attachment** of the PP *with the telescope*. A PCFG assigns them different probabilities; the higher-probability one is the preferred parse in a probabilistic system.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constituency Parsing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Input Sentence w1..wn"]
    TOK["Tokenization Module"]
    POS["POS Tagger Module"]
    LEARNG["Learned PCFG or Neural Model"]
    CHART["Chart Parsing Engine: CYK or Earley"]
    INSIDE["Inside Algorithm: P w1n"]
    VITERBI["Viterbi Decoder: argmax T P of T"]
    OUTTREE["Constituency Parse Tree"]
    RANK["Ranked N-best Trees"]
    DOWN["Downstream Tasks: MT, QA, IE"]

    INP --> TOK --> POS --> CHART
    LEARNG --> CHART
    CHART --> INSIDE --> DOWN
    CHART --> VITERBI --> OUTTREE --> DOWN
    VITERBI --> RANK
```

**Reading the architecture:**

- The **input pipeline** (Tokenization → POS tagging) supplies lexical evidence.
- The **chart parsing engine** consults the **learned grammar/model** to fill the $n \times n$ chart.
- Two complementary outputs flow downstream: the **Inside probability** (used for language modelling and grammar induction) and the **Viterbi best parse** (used by MT, QA, IE).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define constituency parsing. Mention any two applications. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Constituency parsing is the syntactic analysis of a sentence using a phrase-structure (context-free) grammar that decomposes the sentence into nested constituents such as NP, VP, and PP, producing a parse tree whose leaves are the words of the sentence. **(2 marks)**

Two applications: **(1 mark)**

1. **Machine Translation** — constituency trees enable accurate phrase-level re-ordering between languages with differing word order.
2. **Question Answering** — identifying the focus constituent of a question and locating the matching constituent in the answer-bearing passage.

---

### Q2. List the components of a Context-Free Grammar (CFG). What is the role of the start symbol? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A CFG is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ consisting of: **(2 marks)**

1. **$V$ — Non-terminals:** phrase categories such as S, NP, VP, PP.
2. **$\Sigma$ — Terminals:** the vocabulary (words of the language).
3. **$R$ — Production rules:** rewrites of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$.
4. **$S$ — Start symbol:** the designated root non-terminal from which all derivations begin.

**Role of start symbol (1 mark):** The start symbol $S$ is the root of every parse tree and represents the complete sentence. A string is in the language $L(G)$ iff $S \Rightarrow^* w$ for some sequence of rule applications. Without $S$, there would be no entry point for derivations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU ESE Pattern
> Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Sub-parts typically pair theory with computation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain a Context-Free Grammar with a suitable example. Discuss structural ambiguity with two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of CFG (2 marks):**
A Context-Free Grammar is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where $V$ is a finite set of non-terminals, $\Sigma$ a finite disjoint set of terminals, $R$ a finite set of rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ with $A \in V$ and $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$, and $S \in V$ is the start symbol.

**Example grammar for English fragments (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\
NP &\rightarrow DT \; NN \;\vert\; DT \; JJ \; NN \\
VP &\rightarrow VB \; NP \\
DT &\rightarrow \text{the} \;\vert\; \text{a} \\
NN &\rightarrow \text{man} \;\vert\; \text{dog} \\
VB &\rightarrow \text{saw} \;\vert\; \text{likes} \\
JJ &\rightarrow \text{big} \;\vert\; \text{little}
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar generates sentences such as *"the big dog saw a man"*.

**Structural ambiguity (3 marks):**

Structural ambiguity arises when a string admits two or more distinct parse trees. Two classic types:

1. **PP-attachment ambiguity** in *"I saw the man with the telescope"* — the PP *with the telescope* may attach to the VP (instrument of seeing) or to the object NP (possession of the man). Both readings are grammatically valid.

2. **Coordination ambiguity** in *"old men and women"* — the adjective *old* may attach only to *men* or to both *men* and *women*, yielding two parses with distinct bracketing.

---

#### (b) Explain the CYK algorithm. Apply it (with probabilities) to the sentence *"the dog saw a cat"* using the PCFG given below. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**CYK algorithm explanation (3 marks):**

The CYK (Cocke–Kasami–Younger) algorithm is a bottom-up chart-parsing algorithm for grammars in Chomsky Normal Form. It fills an $(n+1) \times (n+1)$ chart $C[i][j]$ where each cell holds the set of non-terminals that can derive the span $w_i \dots w_{j-1}$. For length-1 spans, the cell contains non-terminals with rules emitting $w_i$. For longer spans, the cell combines non-terminals from a split $(i,k)$ and $(k,j)$ using a binary rule $A \rightarrow BC$. With PCFGs, each cell stores the maximum probability of any derivation. Complexity is $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$.

**Application (4 marks):** Worked out fully in §3.1.2 above. Key results:

| Cell | Contents | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| $C[1][3]$ | NP | 0.075 |
| $C[3][6]$ | VP | 0.030 |
| $C[1][6]$ | S | **0.00225** |

The sentence is valid; the most probable parse has probability **0.00225**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Compare top-down and bottom-up parsing. Explain why chart parsing overcomes their limitations. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Top-down parsing (2 marks):** Starts from the start symbol $S$ and recursively expands non-terminals using grammar rules, attempting to match the input words left-to-right.

- *Strength:* goal-directed; never proposes structures inconsistent with the grammar root.
- *Weakness:* cannot handle **left-recursive** rules (e.g., $NP \rightarrow NP \; PP$); wastes effort on subtrees that do not match any word in the input; prone to infinite recursion.

**Bottom-up parsing (2 marks):** Starts from the input words and applies grammar rules in reverse, reducing phrases until $S$ is reached.

- *Strength:* data-driven; succeeds on left-recursive grammars; only considers subtrees that match the actual input.
- *Weakness:* wastes effort on subtrees that cannot lead to $S$ (over-generates); may build many irrelevant constituents.

**Chart parsing as the solution (3 marks):**

Chart parsing stores every successfully completed sub-structure in a **chart** (a table indexed by span). When the parser re-encounters the same span, it reuses the stored result instead of recomputing — this is **memoization**. Consequently:

- It combines the goal-direction of top-down with the data-driven coverage of bottom-up.
- Worst-case complexity is bounded at $O(n^3)$ for context-free grammars.
- Implementations include the **Earley algorithm** (works on any CFG) and **CYK** (requires CNF).

---

#### (b) Derive the Inside algorithm recurrence. Compute the inside probability of the sentence *"the dog saw a cat"* under the PCFG of Question A(b). `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The inside probability $\alpha(A, i, j)$ is defined as the total probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the span $w_i^{j-1}$.

By the law of total probability, conditioned on the rule used at the root of $A$'s derivation:

$$
\alpha(A, i, j) = \sum_{A \rightarrow \beta \in R} P(A \rightarrow \beta) \cdot P(\beta \Rightarrow^* w_i^{j-1} \mid A \rightarrow \beta)
$$

For a grammar in CNF, every rule is either terminal ($A \rightarrow a$) or binary ($A \rightarrow BC$):

- **Terminal rule ($j = i+1$):** $P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$, since the only way to derive a single word is via its terminal rule.

- **Binary rule ($j > i+1$):** $B$ must generate the left substring $[i,k)$ and $C$ the right substring $[k,j)$ for some split $k$, with independence under the PCFG assumption.

Therefore:

$$
\alpha(A, i, j) = \sum_{i \leq k < j} \sum_{A \rightarrow BC} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(C, k, j)
$$

The total sentence probability is $P(w_1^n) = \alpha(S, 1, n+1)$.

**Computation (3 marks):**

Using the grammar of Question A(b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(S, 1, 6) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\;VP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 1, 3) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 6) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.075 \times 0.030 \\
&= 0.00225
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the sentence probability under this PCFG is **0.00225**, identical to the best-parse probability because the sentence has only one valid parse in this grammar.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls
> 1. **Forgetting CNF conversion before CYK.** If the given grammar has rules like $VP \rightarrow VB \; NP \; PP$, you must first binarize them (e.g., introduce $X \rightarrow NP \; PP$ and $VP \rightarrow VB \; X$). Applying CYK directly to a non-CNF rule wastes **2-3 marks**.
> 2. **Probabilities not summing to 1 per non-terminal.** A common slip: rule probabilities are listed without verifying $\sum_\beta P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 3. **Confusing Inside (sum) with Viterbi (max).** If the question asks *"best parse"*, use max; if it asks *"total probability"*, use sum. Writing sum when max is requested loses **1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the base case in derivation questions.** Always write the base case $\alpha(A, i, i+1) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ explicitly; omitting it is worth **0.5-1 mark**.
> 5. **Off-by-one in span indexing.** Mixing inclusive/half-open indices silently corrupts every cell. Declare the convention (recommended: half-open $[i, j)$) at the start.
> 6. **Ignoring structural ambiguity in explanations.** A 7-mark question on ambiguity expects **at least two named types** (attachment, coordination, scope) with sentence examples.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Constituency parsing** produces a tree of nested phrase constituents; **dependency parsing** instead produces word-to-word head-modifier arcs (covered later in the syllabus).
- A **CFG** has 4 components $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$; rules have a *single non-terminal* on the LHS.
- **CNF** restricts rules to $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ and is mandatory for CYK.
- **PCFGs** attach probabilities to rules; the probability of a tree is the **product** of its rule probabilities (independence assumption).
- **CYK** is bottom-up, $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$, requires CNF; produces *all* parses, and with PCFGs the *best* parse via max.
- **Inside algorithm** sums over all parses; **Viterbi CKY** takes the max — same skeleton, different combiner.
- **Top-down** parsers fail on left-recursive grammars; **bottom-up** parsers are data-driven but can over-generate; **chart parsers** fix both with memoization.
- **Structural ambiguity** (PP-attachment, coordination, scope) is the central motivation for probabilistic parsers.
- The **yield** of a parse tree, read left-to-right at the leaves, must equal the input sentence.
- For a 7-mark KTU question: pair *definition* + *worked numerical example* to maximize credit.
- For a 14-mark question: structure answer as **(a) theory/derivation** and **(b) application/computation** — both halves are compulsory to attempt.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ambiguity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambiguity** in Natural Language Processing is the linguistic phenomenon wherein a single linguistic expression (a word, phrase, sentence, or discourse) admits **more than one distinct interpretation, meaning, or structural analysis** within a given language model, grammar, or contextual frame.

In formal terms, given an expression $E$ and a language model $M$, ambiguity exists if:

$$\text{Interpretations}(E, M) = \{I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n\}, \quad n \geq 2$$

Each interpretation $I_k$ is a valid parse, meaning assignment, or referent binding that satisfies the constraints of the grammar $G$ and lexicon $L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Ambiguity is treated as the *primary motivation* for disambiguation strategies — probabilistic models, lexical resources (WordNet), and statistical parsers. Students must be able to **classify, exemplify, and propose disambiguation techniques** for each ambiguity type.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Signal

Imagine a traffic signal at a four-way intersection. The green light has only **one physical state**, but it conveys:
- "Go forward" to the driver directly ahead
- "Prepare to turn left" to the driver in the left-turn lane
- "Continue your U-turn cycle" to a driver mid-maneuver

The **same signal, same moment, different correct actions** based on context. Human language behaves identically: a sentence like *"I saw the man with the telescope"* is one string, but yields two completely valid, real-world interpretations.

### 1.3 Why Ambiguity is Central to NLP

| Aspect | Impact of Ambiguity |
|---|---|
| **Parsing** | A sentence of length $n$ words can have $\mathcal{O}(c^n)$ parse trees (Catalan numbers) |
| **Machine Translation** | Word-sense errors propagate across languages |
| **Information Extraction** | Wrong subject-object binding corrupts knowledge bases |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Sarcasm and scope flip polarity |
| **Question Answering** | Anaphora errors produce wrong entities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Ambiguity Rate:** In unrestricted English text, the lexical ambiguity rate (polysemy + homonymy) typically lies in the range of **30%–43%** of running tokens, depending on the corpus and WordNet sense inventory used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Canonical Types of Ambiguity

A KTU examiner expects a clean, exhaustive taxonomy. Memorize the structure below.

#### A. Lexical Ambiguity (Word-Level)
Occurs when a **single word** has multiple meanings.

**Sub-classes:**

1. **Homonymy** — Identical form, **unrelated meanings** (different etymologies).
   - *Bank* (financial institution) vs. *Bank* (river edge).

2. **Polysemy** — Identical form, **related meanings** (same etymology).
   - *Head* (body part) vs. *Head* (department head) — both denote "top/leader."

3. **Homophony** — Identical pronunciation, different spelling/meaning.
   - *Pair / Pare / Pear*

4. **Homography** — Identical spelling, different pronunciation/meaning.
   - *Lead* (metal) /liːd/ vs. *Lead* (guide) /lɛd/

5. **Metaphoric / Figurative** — Literal vs. transferred meaning.
   - *"The boss is a bear."* (literal animal vs. aggressive person)

#### B. Syntactic (Structural / Grammatical) Ambiguity
A sentence has **more than one valid parse tree** under a given CFG.

**Sub-classes:**

1. **PP-Attachment Ambiguity** — Where does the prepositional phrase attach?
   - *"I saw the man with the telescope."* → PP attaches to *saw* or to *man*?

2. **Coordination Ambiguity** — Scope of conjunction (*and*, *or*).
   - *"old men and women"* → *old (men and women)* vs. *(old men) and women*

3. **NP-VP Scope / Garden-Path Sentences** — Locally plausible but globally wrong parse.
   - *"The horse raced past the barn fell."*

4. **Adverbial / Adjectival Attachment** — Modifies head noun or verb?
   - *"He ate the spaghetti with meatballs."* → With what? Spaghetti or eating?

#### C. Semantic Ambiguity
Sentence structure is unique, **meaning is not**.

1. **Quantifier Scope Ambiguity**
   - *"Every student read a book."* → $\forall x \exists y$ or $\exists y \forall x$?

2. **Anaphora / Reference Ambiguity**
   - *"The trophy did not fit in the suitcase **because it** was too small."* — What is *it*?

#### D. Pragmatic Ambiguity
Meaning depends on **speaker intent, context, world knowledge**.

- *"Can you pass the salt?"* — Literal ability vs. polite request.

#### E. Discourse Ambiguity
Resolution requires **multiple sentences / discourse context**.

- Pronouns referring across paragraphs; event coreference in news articles.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Disambiguation & Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the boxed formulas. They appear verbatim in numerical sub-parts of 14-mark questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sense Count (polysemy) | $\text{SC}(w) = \vert \text{Senses}(w) \vert$ | Number of WordNet senses of word $w$ |
| 2 | Lexical Ambiguity Rate | $\text{LAR} = \dfrac{\sum_{w \in C} \mathbb{1}[\text{SC}(w) \geq 2]}{\vert C \vert}$ | $C$ = corpus, $\mathbb{1}$ = indicator |
| 3 | Most-Frequent-Sense Baseline | $\text{MFS}(w) = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s)$ | Pick dominant sense |
| 4 | Naïve Bayes WSD | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s) \prod_{i} P(c_i \mid s)$ | $c_i$ = context features |
| 5 | Bayes Decision Risk | $R(\hat{s}) = \sum_{s'} L(\hat{s}, s') \, P(s' \mid c)$ | $L$ = loss function |
| 6 | WSD Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\%$ | Gold-standard evaluation |
| 7 | Parse-Tree Count (ambiguous CFG) | $T(n) \sim \mathcal{O}(c^n)$ | Catalan-like growth |
| 8 | Mutual Information (collocation) | $\text{MI}(w_1, w_2) = \log \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) P(w_2)}$ | Disambiguate compound |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** to distinguish *"Jaguar car"* from *"Jaguar animal"* in queries.
- **Clinical NLP (IBM Watson Health):** Disambiguates *"cold"* (temperature vs. common cold) in electronic health records.
- **Machine Translation (Google Translate, DeepL):** Pre-disambiguation step chooses the correct sense before generation, reducing back-translation errors by up to **18%** on low-resource pairs.
- **Legal AI (ROSS, CaseText):** Resolves *"shall"* (mandatory) vs. *"may"* (permissive) — a high-stakes disambiguation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantifier Scope Ambiguity Resolution

**Sentence:** *"Every student read a book."*

**Step 1 — Identify the two readings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reading 1 (} \forall \exists \text{):} \quad & \forall x \, [Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, [Book(y) \land Read(x,y)]] \\
\text{Reading 2 (} \exists \forall \text{):} \quad & \exists y \, [Book(y) \land \forall x \, [Student(x) \rightarrow Read(x,y)]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret each reading in plain English:**

- **Reading 1 ($\forall \exists$):** "For *every* student, there exists a *book* (possibly different) that they read." → Multiple distinct books allowed.
- **Reading 2 ($\exists \forall$):** "There is a *single* book that *every* student read." → One book, many readers.

**Step 3 — Logical contrast (truth conditions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reading 2} &\Rightarrow \text{Reading 1} \quad \text{(one book for all} \Rightarrow \text{at least one book for each)} \\
\text{Reading 1} &\not\Rightarrow \text{Reading 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So Reading 2 is **logically stronger**; if the world satisfies Reading 2, it automatically satisfies Reading 1, but not vice-versa.

**Step 4 — Disambiguation using pragmatic principle (Gricean Maxim of Quantity):**
The natural reading in most contexts is **Reading 1 ($\forall \exists$)**, because the indefinite article *"a book"* carries existential force within the scope of *"every student."* Only in a context like *"They all read the same book"* does the $\exists \forall$ reading become forced.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PP-Attachment Probability Computation

**Sentence:** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

**Step 1 — Enumerate attachment sites:**

$$
\text{Attach}_{PP} \in \{ \underbrace{VP}_{\text{to "saw"}}, \underbrace{NP}_{\text{to "man"}} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply lexicalized attachment probabilities** (from a parsed corpus such as Penn Treebank):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{PP} \rightarrow \text{VP} \mid \text{saw}) &= 0.10 \\
P(\text{PP} \rightarrow \text{NP} \mid \text{man}) &= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These probabilities reflect the empirical bias in English: instrument-PPs attach to **verbs**, while possessive/possessor-PPs attach to **nouns**. The training corpus learns that *"with a telescope"* as an instrument overwhelmingly modifies the verb *see*.

**Step 3 — Bayesian decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{a} &= \arg\max_{a \in \{VP, NP\}} P(a \mid \text{context}) \\
&= \arg\max \{ 0.10, \, 0.90 \} \\
&= NP
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting preferred interpretation:**

> *"I saw [the man [with the telescope]]"* — The man **possesses** the telescope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the sentence were *"I saw the man with the night-vision goggles in the dark forest,"* the probability shifts back toward VP-attachment because the modifier chain grows too long for a noun phrase — this is the **Right-Association** principle (attach low / late).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Lexical Ambiguity Rate Computation

**Mini-Corpus $C$** (4 words): `bank, river, bank, loan`

**Step 1 — Look up WordNet senses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SC}(\text{bank}) &= 18 \text{ (finance + river + ...)} \\
\text{SC}(\text{river}) &= 1 \\
\text{SC}(\text{loan}) &= 5 \text{ (noun: money-lending, verb: lending)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Count ambiguous tokens** ($\text{SC} \geq 2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ambiguous tokens} &= |\{w : \text{SC}(w) \geq 2\}| \\
&= |\{ \text{bank}, \text{bank}, \text{loan} \}| = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply LAR formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LAR} &= \frac{3}{|C|} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 = 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A high LAR (>50%) signals that **statistical/discriminative disambiguation** (Naïve Bayes, SVM, BERT) is essential — rule-based systems cannot scale.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Disambiguation Strategy

| Ambiguity Type | Best Classical Method | Modern Method | KTU 2-Line Answer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lexical (WSD) | Naïve Bayes / Lesk | BERT, contextual embeddings | Use sense priors + context overlap |
| Syntactic (PP-attach) | Probabilistic CFG (PCFG) | Neural CRFs, Recurrent Nets | Maximize parse probability |
| Semantic (Scope) | Montague Semantics, λ-calculus | Transformer attention maps | Apply scope-binding rules |
| Anaphora | Hobbs' algorithm, Centering | Coreference resolution (Lee et al.) | Pick nearest compatible antecedent |
| Pragmatic | Speech-act theory, Gricean Maxims | Intent classifiers (BERT) | Use discourse/world knowledge |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Ambiguity

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AMBIGUITY in NLP] --> B[Lexical]
    A --> C[Syntactic]
    A --> D[Semantic]
    A --> E[Pragmatic]
    A --> F[Discourse]

    B --> B1[Homonymy]
    B --> B2[Polysemy]
    B --> B3[Homophony]
    B --> B4[Homography]

    C --> C1[PP Attachment]
    C --> C2[Coordination Scope]
    C --> C3[Garden Path]
    C --> C4[NP VP Attachment]

    D --> D1[Quantifier Scope]
    D --> D2[Anaphora Reference]
    D --> D3[Modifier Scope]

    E --> E1[Speech Act]
    E --> E2[Presupposition]
    E --> E3[Deixis]

    F --> F1[Pronoun Resolution]
    F --> F2[Event Coreference]
```

### 4.2 Disambiguation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Raw Text Input] --> tokenizeA[Tokenizer]
    tokenizeA --> posA[POS Tagger]
    posA --> parseA[Parser / CFG]
    parseA --> detectA[Ambiguity Detector]
    detectA --> classifyA{Ambiguity Type?}

    classifyA -->|Lexical| wsdA[Word Sense Disambiguator]
    classifyA -->|Syntactic| attachA[Attachment Resolver PCFG]
    classifyA -->|Semantic| scopeA[Scope Binder]
    classifyA -->|Pragmatic| ctxA[Context Reasoner]

    wsdA --> rankA[Rank Interpretations]
    attachA --> rankA
    scopeA --> rankA
    ctxA --> rankA

    rankA --> selectA[Select Top-K]
    selectA --> outA[Disambiguated Meaning]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for PP-Attachment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Encounter PP] --> checkA{PP Headword Category}
    checkA -->|with / using / by means of| instrA[Instrument PP]
    checkA -->|of / from / in| possA[Possessive / Locative PP]
    checkA -->|to / for| benA[Dative / Beneficiary PP]
```
