```mermaid
    instrA --> verbA[Attach to Nearest Verb]
    possA --> nounA[Attach to Nearest Noun]
    benA --> verbB[Attach to Nearest Verb]

    verbA --> evalA[Compute P attach to VP]
    nounA --> evalB[Compute P attach to NP]
    verbB --> evalC[Compute P attach to VP]

    evalA --> pickA[argmax over candidates]
    evalB --> pickA
    evalC --> pickA
    pickA --> doneA[Final Tree]
```

### 4.4 Lexical Disambiguation (Lesk Algorithm) Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Doc as Document Context
    participant Word as Ambiguous Word
    participant Dict as WordNet Glosses
    participant Score as Overlap Scorer

    Doc->>Word: Identify target word + POS
    Word->>Dict: Retrieve gloss of each sense
    Dict-->>Score: Return set of gloss words per sense
    Doc->>Score: Send context bag-of-words
    Score->>Score: Compute word overlap for each sense
    Score-->>Doc: Return sense with maximum overlap
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
Define **lexical ambiguity**. Distinguish between **polysemy** and **homonymy** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lexical ambiguity arises when a single word admits more than one distinct meaning or interpretation in a given context (1 mark). **Polysemy** refers to a word having multiple *related* meanings derived from the same etymological root, e.g., *head* meaning "body part" and "leader of a department" — both evoke the notion of "topmost/foremost" (1 mark). **Homonymy** refers to words with *identical form but unrelated meanings* and different etymologies, e.g., *bank* (financial institution) vs. *bank* (edge of a river) (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
What is **PP-attachment ambiguity**? Give a sentence and list its two possible parse trees in one line each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PP-attachment ambiguity occurs when a prepositional phrase (PP) in a sentence can grammatically attach to more than one constituent — typically a verb phrase (VP) or a noun phrase (NP) (1 mark).
*Sentence:* "She hit the man with the umbrella." (1 mark)
- **Tree 1:** [VP hit [NP the man] [PP with the umbrella]] — instrument reading.
- **Tree 2:** [VP hit [NP the man [PP with the umbrella]]] — possessive reading. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Ambiguity Questions:**
> - **Common Pitfall #1:** Students write "ambiguity = having two meanings" without naming the **specific type** (lexical, syntactic, semantic, pragmatic). **Always** prefix with the type name — this is a 1-mark loss for many.
> - **Common Pitfall #2:** Confusing *polysemy* with *homonymy* in the same answer loses 1 full mark. Use a clear-cut unrelated pair for homonymy (e.g., *bat* animal vs. *bat* sports equipment).
> - **Common Pitfall #3:** In 14-mark questions with sub-part (a) and (b), students answer only one sub-part. **Both sub-parts are mandatory** unless the question explicitly grants internal choice.
> - **Common Pitfall #4:** Listing ambiguity types without **examples** caps marks at 60% of that sub-part. Always attach a one-line example per type.
> - **Common Pitfall #5:** Forgetting to mention the **disambiguation technique** corresponding to the ambiguity type. The examiner's key often has a 2-mark "method" slot.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q-Mod2-A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of lexical ambiguity** with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **WordNet** in Word Sense Disambiguation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **syntactic ambiguity**? With the help of a sentence, explain **PP-attachment ambiguity** and **coordination ambiguity**. How does a **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** help in resolving syntactic ambiguity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Types of Lexical Ambiguity & WordNet in WSD (7 marks):**

Lexical ambiguity exists at the word level when a token has multiple dictionary entries or senses. The principal subtypes are:

1. **Homonymy** — Same spelling/pronunciation, unrelated meanings.
   - *Bat* (flying mammal) vs. *bat* (sports equipment). (1 mark)
2. **Polysemy** — Same word, related meanings.
   - *Mouth* (of a river) vs. *mouth* (of a person). (1 mark)
3. **Homophony** — Same sound, different spelling.
   - *Flour / Flower*; *Pair / Pear / Pare*. (1 mark)
4. **Homography** — Same spelling, different pronunciation.
   - *Lead* (Pb) /lɛd/ vs. *Lead* (verb to guide) /liːd/. (1 mark)

**Role of WordNet in WSD (3 marks):**

WordNet is a large lexical database of English developed at Princeton, grouping words into sets of cognitive synonyms called **synsets**, each representing a distinct lexical concept. For WSD:
- Each word is mapped to one or more synsets, e.g., *bank* → `bank#1` (financial), `bank#2` (river edge), etc. (1 mark)
- Disambiguation algorithms (e.g., **Lesk algorithm**) compute the overlap between the dictionary glosses of candidate senses and the context words. The sense with maximum overlap is chosen. (1 mark)
- WordNet also encodes hypernym/hyponym and meronym relations, enabling structured knowledge-based disambiguation. (1 mark)

---

**(b) — Syntactic Ambiguity, PP-Attachment, Coordination, PCFG (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Syntactic ambiguity arises when a sentence has more than one valid parse tree under a given Context-Free Grammar (CFG), even though its surface string is unique.

**PP-Attachment Ambiguity (2 marks):**
A prepositional phrase may modify either a verb or a noun.
*Sentence:* "I shot the elephant in my pajamas."
- *Parse 1:* I shot [the elephant] [in my pajamas] — *I* am in pajamas.
- *Parse 2:* I shot [the elephant in my pajamas] — *the elephant* is in pajamas.
(The humorous reading is Parse 1, the literal reading is Parse 2.)

**Coordination Ambiguity (2 marks):**
A conjunction (*and*, *or*) can group constituents in multiple ways.
*Sentence:* "old men and women."
- *Parse 1:* [old] [men and women] — both groups are old.
- *Parse 2:* [old men] and [women] — only men are old.

**PCFG-based Resolution (2 marks):**
A PCFG augments each rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ with a probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$, all probabilities for a given $A$ summing to 1. For an ambiguous sentence with parse trees $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$:

$$
\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T_i} P(T_i) = \arg\max_{T_i} \prod_{r \in T_i} P(r)
$$

The tree with the highest joint rule probability is selected. This allows the parser to *prefer* instrument-PP attachment to verbs (because empirically $P(\text{PP} \rightarrow \text{VP} \mid \text{verb}_{\text{instrument}})$ is higher) over noun attachment, statistically resolving the ambiguity.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q-Mod2-B]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **semantic ambiguity** and **pragmatic ambiguity**. Illustrate with examples and show how **quantifier scope** and **anaphora** lead to semantic ambiguity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **approaches to Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — namely the **Lesk algorithm**, **Naïve Bayes WSD**, and **Most-Frequent-Sense (MFS) baseline**. Compare their accuracy on standard benchmarks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Semantic vs. Pragmatic Ambiguity (7 marks):**

**Semantic Ambiguity (1 mark):** Arises when a sentence, despite having a unique syntactic structure, admits more than one logical form or meaning due to the way its semantic constituents compose.

**Pragmatic Ambiguity (1 mark):** Arises when the intended meaning depends on the speaker's intent, conversational context, shared world knowledge, or speech-act type — not just the literal semantics of the words.

**Quantifier Scope Ambiguity (2.5 marks):**
*Sentence:* "Every manager likes some employee."
- $\forall \exists$ reading: For every manager, there is some (possibly different) employee they like.
- $\exists \forall$ reading: There is a single employee liked by every manager.

These are modeled in **Montague Semantics** by binding quantifiers at different levels of the syntactic tree, giving rise to distinct lambda-calculus derivations.

**Anaphoric Reference Ambiguity (2.5 marks):**
*Sentence:* "The city council refused the women a permit because **they** advocated violence."
- Pronoun *they* may refer to *the city council* or *the women*.
- Classic algorithms: **Hobbs' algorithm** (syntactic, depth-first traversal of parse tree) and **Centering Theory** (discourse-level salience ranking). Modern systems use neural coreference resolvers (e.g., Lee et al., 2017) achieving **~76% CoNLL F1**.

**Pragmatic Example (for contrast):** "It's cold in here." may be a mere **observation** (literal) or a **request** to close the window (illocutionary) — resolved only via Gricean maxims and shared context.

---

**(b) — WSD Approaches Compared (7 marks):**

**1. Most-Frequent-Sense (MFS) Baseline (2 marks):**
For each word $w$, the sense with the highest prior probability in a sense-tagged corpus (e.g., SemCor) is always selected:

$$
\hat{s}_{\text{MFS}}(w) = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s)
$$

- **Accuracy on SemEval-2/3 English all-words task:** ~**65%**.
- Surprisingly strong because sense distributions are skewed (Zipfian).

**2. Lesk Algorithm (2.5 marks):**
Dictionary-based. For each sense $s$ of word $w$, compute the overlap between the gloss (definition) of $s$ and the surrounding context $C$:

$$
\text{Score}(s) = \vert \text{Gloss}(s) \cap C \vert
$$

The sense with the highest overlap is selected.
- **Original Lesk accuracy:** ~**35–40%** (weak).
- **Enhanced Lesk** (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002) using hypernym/hyponym glosses: ~**50–55%**.

**3. Naïve Bayes WSD (2.5 marks):**
Supervised. For context features $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ (typically surrounding words, POS tags, collocations):

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(c_i \mid s)
$$

Probabilities estimated from annotated corpora (SemCor, OMSTI).
- **Accuracy:** ~**67–73%** on fine-grained all-words tasks.
- Limitation: strong independence assumption; data sparsity for rare senses.

**Comparison Summary (1 mark integrated above):** Naïve Bayes > MFS > Lesk on most tasks; modern transformer-based WSD (BERT, Gloss-BERT) pushes accuracy to **80%+** by leveraging contextual embeddings against sense definitions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ambiguity (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Ambiguity** = one expression, multiple valid interpretations; quantified as $n \geq 2$ interpretations.
- ☐ **Five types to memorize verbatim:** Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Pragmatic, Discourse.
- ☐ **Lexical sub-types:** Homonymy (unrelated), Polysemy (related), Homophony (sound), Homography (spelling).
- ☐ **Syntactic flags:** PP-attachment, coordination, garden-path sentences.
- ☐ **Semantic flags:** Quantifier scope ($\forall \exists$ vs. $\exists \forall$), anaphora resolution.
- ☐ **Pragmatic flag:** Speaker intent, speech act, Gricean maxims.
- ☐ **Key disambiguation techniques:**
  - WSD → Lesk / Naïve Bayes / MFS / BERT.
  - Syntactic → PCFG parse probability.
  - Anaphora → Hobbs / Centering / Neural coreference.
- ☐ **Formulas to memorize:**
  - LAR, Naïve Bayes WSD, MFS, PCFG parse probability.
- ☐ **WordNet** = Princeton lexical database; synsets, hypernyms, hyponyms; used in Lesk.
- ☐ **Example stock for exams:** *"I saw the man with the telescope"* (PP), *"old men and women"* (coordination), *"Every student read a book"* (scope), *"The city council refused the women a permit because they ..."* (anaphora).
- ☐ **Examiner traps:** Always name the ambiguity *type*; always give an *example*; always pair with a *disambiguation method*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CYK Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CYK Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Cocke-Younger-Kasami (CYK) algorithm** is a **bottom-up, dynamic programming parsing algorithm** used to determine whether a given string of terminal symbols belongs to the language generated by a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG) in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. It simultaneously constructs a parse tree and reports membership in **O(n³)** time, where **n** is the length of the input string.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> CYK Parsing is a **mandatory topic** in Module 2 of PECST862. Students must be able to (1) **convert a CFG to CNF**, (2) **construct the CYK parsing table** for a given string, and (3) **determine string membership** in the language. Expect direct algorithmic questions in ESE (End Semester Examination).

> [!NOTE]
> **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) Pre-requisite Definition:**
> A CFG is in CNF if every production rule is of the form:
> - **A → BC** (where A, B, C are non-terminals)
> - **A → a** (where a is a terminal)
> - **S → ε** is the only allowed ε-production, and only if S does not appear on the right side of any production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick-Stacking" Intuition

Imagine you are building a **pyramid** where each level is formed by combining **two adjacent bricks** from the level below. Each brick represents a non-terminal that "covers" a span of words.

- You first lay the **smallest bricks** (each word is tagged with which non-terminals can produce it).
- Then, you try to **stack two adjacent bricks** together to see if any non-terminal can produce the combined span.
- The pyramid is "complete" if the **apex (top brick)** is the start symbol **S**, which means the entire sentence is accepted.

This is CYK: it works **from leaves (words) up to the root (sentence)**, exactly like building a brick pyramid.

## 1.3 Why CYK Matters in NLP

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | **O(n³)** | Polynomial — efficient for moderate-length sentences |
| Space Complexity | **O(n²)** | Stores a triangular table |
| Grammar Form | **CNF only** | Requires prior grammar transformation |
| Output | Membership (Yes/No) + Parse Tree | Useful for syntax validation in NLP pipelines |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If a question does not specify that the grammar is in CNF, **the first step of your answer must be to convert it to CNF** before applying CYK. This is a frequent mark-deduction trap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-requisites: Chomsky Normal Form Conversion Steps

Before applying CYK, the CFG must be in CNF. The standard conversion involves the following high-level operations:

1. **Eliminate start symbol from RHS:** Introduce a new start symbol S′ → S if S appears on the right side of any rule.
2. **Eliminate ε-productions** (A → ε) using the Null Removal Algorithm.
3. **Eliminate unit productions** (A → B) using the Unit Production Removal Algorithm.
4. **Convert terminals in long rules:** For A → X₁X₂...Xₖ where k > 2, replace terminals with new non-terminals.
5. **Break long rules:** Convert A → B₁B₂...Bₖ (where k > 2) into a chain of binary rules using new non-terminals.

## 2.2 CYK Algorithm — Structured Logic Steps

The CYK algorithm populates a **triangular table T** of size **n × n**, where **n** is the number of tokens in the input string w = w₁w₂...wₙ.

**Step 1 — Initialize table T[i][i] (length-1 spans):**
For each token wᵢ, find all non-terminals A such that A → wᵢ is a production in G. Place these in T[i][i].

**Step 2 — Fill table T[i][j] for spans of length 2 to n:**
For span length **L = 2, 3, ..., n**:
- For each starting position **i**, define **j = i + L − 1**.
- For each split point **k** where **i ≤ k < j**:
- Compute the candidate set: **T[i][j] = { A : ∃ A → BC, B ∈ T[i][k], C ∈ T[k+1][j] }**

**Step 3 — Membership Test:**
The string w is in the language L(G) **if and only if the start symbol S is in T[1][n]**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Grammar Form | **A → BC** ∨ **A → a** | Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) |
| String Length | n =  ∣ w ∣ | Number of terminal tokens |
| Table Cell Definition | T[i][j] = { A : A ⇒* wᵢwᵢ₊₁...wⱼ } | Set of non-terminals deriving span (i, j) |
| Base Case (L=1) | T[i][i] = { A : A → wᵢ ∈ P } | Non-terminals producing the i-th token |
| Recursive Case (L≥2) | T[i][j] = ∪ₖ { A : ∃ A → BC, B ∈ T[i][k], C ∈ T[k+1][j] } | Union over all split points k |
| Time Complexity | **O(n³ · ∣P∣)** | n = string length, ∣P∣ = number of productions |
| Space Complexity | **O(n²)** | Triangular table storage |
| Acceptance Condition | **S ∈ T[1][n]** | Start symbol in top cell |
| Split Point Range | i ≤ k < j | k iterates from i to j−1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** CYK is foundational in **computational linguistics**, **compiler design** (parser generators like Python's `lark`), **bioinformatics** (RNA secondary structure prediction), and **syntactic analysis in NLP pipelines** (e.g., constituency parsing in NLTK).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete CYK Execution

**Given Grammar G (already in CNF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AB \mid BC \\
A &\rightarrow BA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow CC \mid b \\
C &\rightarrow AB \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Input String:** w = **"baaba"** (n = 5)

---

### Step 1 — Compute the base case (length-1 spans)

We look at each character and identify which non-terminals directly produce it.

- **w₁ = 'b'** : B → b → **T[1][1] = {B}**
- **w₂ = 'a'** : A → a, C → a → **T[2][2] = {A, C}**
- **w₃ = 'a'** : A → a, C → a → **T[3][3] = {A, C}**
- **w₄ = 'b'** : B → b → **T[4][4] = {B}**
- **w₅ = 'a'** : A → a, C → a → **T[5][5] = {A, C}**

---

### Step 2 — Compute length-2 spans

For each span of length 2, we split it in the middle (k = i) and look for rules A → BC where B is in the left half and C is in the right half.

**T[1][2] (covers "ba", split at k=1):**
- B ∈ T[1][1], A ∈ T[2][2] → check A → BA ✓ → Add **A**
- B ∈ T[1][1], C ∈ T[2][2] → check A → BC ✗, S → BC ✓ → Add **S**
- **T[1][2] = {A, S}**

**T[2][3] (covers "aa", split at k=2):**
- A ∈ T[2][2], A ∈ T[3][3] → check A → AA ✗, S → AA ✗, C → AA ✗ → none
- C ∈ T[2][2], A ∈ T[3][3] → check A → CA ✗, S → CA ✗, C → CA ✗ → none
- A ∈ T[2][2], C ∈ T[3][3] → check A → AC ✗, S → AC ✗, C → AC ✗ → none
- C ∈ T[2][2], C ∈ T[3][3] → check A → CC ✗, S → CC ✗, C → CC ✗, **B → CC ✓** → Add **B**
- **T[2][3] = {B}**

**T[3][4] (covers "ab", split at k=3):**
- A ∈ T[3][3], B ∈ T[4][4] → check A → AB ✗, S → AB ✗, **C → AB ✓** → Add **C**
- C ∈ T[3][3], B ∈ T[4][4] → check A → CB ✗, S → CB ✗, C → CB ✗ → none
- **T[3][4] = {C}**

**T[4][5] (covers "ba", split at k=4):**
- B ∈ T[4][4], A ∈ T[5][5] → check A → BA ✓ → Add **A**
- B ∈ T[4][4], C ∈ T[5][5] → check A → BC ✗, S → BC ✓ → Add **S**
- **T[4][5] = {A, S}**

---

### Step 3 — Compute length-3 spans

**T[1][3] (covers "baa", split at k=1 and k=2):**

- Split k=1: B ∈ T[1][1], T[2][3] = {B} → check B → BB ✗ → none
- Split k=2: T[1][2] = {A, S}, T[3][3] = {A, C}
  - A, A → check A → AA ✗, S → AA ✗, C → AA ✗ → none
  - A, C → check A → AC ✗, S → AC ✗, C → AC ✗ → none
  - S, A → check A → SA ✗, S → SA ✗, C → SA ✗ → none
  - S, C → check A → SC ✗, S → SC ✗, C → SC ✗ → none
- **T[1][3] = ∅ (empty set)**

**T[2][4] (covers "aab", split at k=2 and k=3):**
- Split k=2: T[2][2] = {A, C}, T[3][4] = {C}
  - A, C → check A → AC ✗, S → AC ✗, C → AC ✗ → none
  - C, C → check A → CC ✗, S → CC ✗, C → CC ✗, **B → CC ✓** → Add **B**
- Split k=3: T[2][3] = {B}, T[4][4] = {B}
  - B, B → check A → BB ✗, S → BB ✗, C → BB ✗ → none
- **T[2][4] = {B}**

**T[3][5] (covers "aba", split at k=3 and k=4):**
- Split k=3: T[3][3] = {A, C}, T[4][5] = {A, S}
  - A, A → check A → AA ✗ → none
  - A, S → check A → AS ✗ → none
  - C, A → check A → CA ✗ → none
  - C, S → check A → CS ✗ → none
- Split k=4: T[3][4] = {C}, T[5][5] = {A, C}
  - C, A → check A → CA ✗ → none
  - C, C → check A → CC ✗, S → CC ✗, C → CC ✗, **B → CC ✓** → Add **B**
- **T[3][5] = {B}**

---

### Step 4 — Compute length-4 spans

**T[1][4] (covers "baab", split at k=1, 2, 3):**
- Split k=1: T[1][1] = {B}, T[2][4] = {B} → check B → BB ✗ → none
- Split k=2: T[1][2] = {A, S}, T[3][4] = {C}
  - A, C → check A → AC ✗, S → AC ✗, C → AC ✗ → none
  - S, C → check A → SC ✗, S → SC ✗, C → SC ✗ → none
- Split k=3: T[1][3] = ∅, T[4][4] = {B} → empty, skip
- **T[1][4] = ∅**

**T[2][5] (covers "aaba", split at k=2, 3, 4):**
- Split k=2: T[2][2] = {A, C}, T[3][5] = {B}
  - A, B → check A → AB ✗, S → AB ✗, **C → AB ✓** → Add **C**
  - C, B → check A → CB ✗, S → CB ✗, C → CB ✗ → none
- Split k=3: T[2][3] = {B}, T[4][5] = {A, S}
  - B, A → check A → BA ✓ → Add **A**
  - B, S → check A → BS ✗ → none
- Split k=4: T[2][4] = {B}, T[5][5] = {A, C}
  - B, A → check A → BA ✓ → Add **A** (already in set)
  - B, C → check A → BC ✗, S → BC ✓ → Add **S**
- **T[2][5] = {A, C, S}**

---

### Step 5 — Compute length-5 span (the full string)

**T[1][5] (covers "baaba", split at k=1, 2, 3, 4):**
- Split k=1: T[1][1] = {B}, T[2][5] = {A, C, S}
  - B, A → check A → BA ✓ → Add **A**
  - B, C → check A → BC ✗, **S → BC ✓** → Add **S**
  - B, S → check A → BS ✗ → none
- Split k=2: T[1][2] = {A, S}, T[3][5] = {B}
  - A, B → check A → AB ✗, S → AB ✗, **C → AB ✓** → Add **C**
  - S, B → check A → SB ✗, S → SB ✗, C → SB ✗ → none
- Split k=3: T[1][3] = ∅, T[4][5] = {A, S} → empty, skip
- Split k=4: T[1][4] = ∅, T[5][5] = {A, C} → empty, skip
- **T[1][5] = {A, C, S}**

---

### Step 6 — Final Decision

> **The start symbol S ∈ T[1][5] = {A, C, S}** → **The string "baaba" IS accepted by the grammar G.**

## 3.2 Final Parsing Table (Visual Matrix)

| Span | T[1] | T[2] | T[3] | T[4] | T[5] |
|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| **Length 5** | **{A, C, S}** | — | — | — | — |
| **Length 4** | ∅ | {A, C, S} | — | — | — |
| **Length 3** | ∅ | {B} | {B} | — | — |
| **Length 2** | {A, S} | {B} | {C} | {A, S} | — |
| **Length 1** | {B} | {A, C} | {A, C} | {B} | {A, C} |
| | w₁=b | w₂=a | w₃=a | w₄=b | w₅=a |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CYK Algorithm — High-Level Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputStart([Input CFG G in CNF and String w]) --> stepA[Step A: Validate CNF Form]
    stepA --> stepB[Step B: Tokenize w into w1, w2, wn]
    stepB --> stepC[Step C: Initialize Triangular Table T of size n x n]
    stepC --> stepD[Step D: Fill Diagonal T i i using Terminal Productions]
    stepD --> stepE[Step E: For Span Length L from 2 to n]
    stepE --> stepF[Step F: For Each Starting Index i compute j = i + L - 1]
    stepF --> stepG[Step G: For Each Split Point k from i to j minus 1]
    stepG --> stepH[Step H: Look for Productions A to BC where B in T i k and C in T k+1 j]
    stepH --> stepI[Step I: Union all Matches into T i j]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step J: Increment Span Length and Repeat]
    stepJ --> stepK{All Cells Filled?}
    stepK -- No --> stepE
    stepK -- Yes --> stepL{Is Start Symbol S in T 1 n?}
    stepL -- Yes --> acceptOut([String ACCEPTED by Grammar])
    stepL -- No --> rejectOut([String REJECTED by Grammar])
    style inputStart fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff

```
