# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define CYK parsing algorithm. Why is it restricted to grammars in Chomsky Normal Form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Cocke-Younger-Kasami (CYK) algorithm** is a **bottom-up dynamic programming parsing algorithm** used to test whether a given string w can be generated by a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG) in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. It works in **O(n³)** time by systematically filling a triangular table where each cell **T[i][j]** represents the set of non-terminals that can derive the substring **wᵢwᵢ₊₁...wⱼ**.

It is restricted to CNF because the algorithm relies on the **binary nature of CNF rules** (A → BC), which allows a span to be split into exactly **two sub-spans**. This binary decomposition is the foundation of the dynamic programming recurrence. If the grammar contained longer rules (A → BCD), a single span would need to be split into **three or more** parts, complicating the table recurrence and breaking the algorithm's structure. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the time and space complexity of the CYK algorithm. Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Time Complexity: O(n³)** — The table has O(n²) cells. For each cell, we iterate over O(n) split points and check O(∣P∣) productions, giving O(n³·∣P∣). With the production set being fixed, the dominant term is **O(n³)**.
- **Space Complexity: O(n²)** — The triangular table stores sets of non-terminals for every span (i, j) where 1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n, totaling **n(n+1)/2 = O(n²)** cells.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Convert the following CFG into Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAB \mid bA \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid bB \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the CNF grammar obtained in part (a), apply the **CYK algorithm** to check whether the string **w = "aab"** is in the language.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **CYK parsing algorithm** in detail. Describe the structure of the parsing table and the recurrence relation used to fill it.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the following CNF grammar and test whether the string **"bab"** is accepted using the **CYK algorithm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AB \mid BS \\
A &\rightarrow BA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow CC \mid b \\
C &\rightarrow AB \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — CNF Conversion [7 marks]

**Step 1: Eliminate ε-productions.**
B → ε is the only ε-production. The nullable non-terminals are **B**.
For every rule containing B on the RHS, add a version with B removed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAB \mid aA \mid bA \mid b \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \mid bB \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

Also remove the ε-production itself.

**Step 2: Eliminate unit productions.**
There are no unit productions (rules of form A → B) remaining. ✓

**Step 3: Replace terminals in mixed/long rules.**
For S → aAB, introduce new non-terminal **Cₐ → a** and replace a with Cₐ:
- S → CₐAB
For A → aA, replace terminal a with Cₐ:
- A → CₐA

**Step 4: Break long rules into binary.**
For S → CₐAB (length 3), introduce **D → AB**:
- S → CₐD
For A → CₐA (length 2), this is already binary ✓
For A → bB (length 2), already binary ✓
For B → bB (length 2), already binary ✓

**Final CNF Grammar G′:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_{a}D \mid bA \mid b \\
D &\rightarrow AB \\
C_{a} &\rightarrow a \\
A &\rightarrow C_{a}A \mid a \mid bB \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — CYK Algorithm on w = "aab" [7 marks]

String: w₁=a, w₂=a, w₃=b (n=3)

**Base case (length 1):**
- T[1][1]: a → Cₐ, A → a → **{Cₐ, A}**
- T[2][2]: a → Cₐ, A → a → **{Cₐ, A}**
- T[3][3]: b → B → b, S → b, A → b → **{B, S, A}**

**Length-2 spans:**

**T[1][2] (covers "aa", k=1):** Left = T[1][1] = {Cₐ, A}, Right = T[2][2] = {Cₐ, A}
- Cₐ, Cₐ → no rule with CₐCₐ
- Cₐ, A → no rule
- A, Cₐ → no rule
- A, A → no rule
- **T[1][2] = ∅**

**T[2][3] (covers "ab", k=2):** Left = T[2][2] = {Cₐ, A}, Right = T[3][3] = {B, S, A}
- Cₐ, B → no rule
- Cₐ, S → no rule
- Cₐ, A → no rule
- A, B → check D → AB ✓ → Add **D**
- A, S → no rule
- A, A → no rule
- **T[2][3] = {D}**

**Length-3 span:**

**T[1][3] (covers "aab"):**
- Split k=1: T[1][1]={Cₐ, A}, T[2][3]={D} → check A→CₐD ✗, S→CₐD ✓ → Add **S**
- Split k=2: T[1][2]=∅ → empty, skip
- **T[1][3] = {S}**

**Final Parsing Table:**

| Span | T[1] | T[2] | T[3] |
|------|------|------|------|
| **L=3** | **{S}** | — | — |
| **L=2** | ∅ | {D} | — |
| **L=1** | {Cₐ, A} | {Cₐ, A} | {B, S, A} |
| | a | a | b |

> **Decision: S ∈ T[1][3] = {S} → String "aab" IS ACCEPTED by the grammar.** ✓

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Explanation of CYK Algorithm [7 marks]

The **CYK algorithm** is a **membership-testing algorithm** for CFGs in CNF. It uses **dynamic programming** to determine whether a string w = w₁w₂...wₙ can be derived from the start symbol S.

**Table Structure:** A **triangular table T** of size **n × n** is constructed. Each cell **T[i][j]** (where i ≤ j) contains the **set of non-terminals** that can derive the substring **wᵢwᵢ₊₁...wⱼ**.

**Filling Procedure:**
1. **Diagonal (length 1):** T[i][i] contains all non-terminals A such that A → wᵢ is a production.
2. **Upper cells (length ≥ 2):** For span (i, j) with length L = j − i + 1:
$$T[i][j] = \bigcup_{k=i}^{j-1} \{ A \mid A \rightarrow BC, B \in T[i][k], C \in T[k+1][j] \}$$
3. **Decision:** w ∈ L(G) **if and only if** S ∈ T[1][n].

The algorithm runs in **O(n³)** time and uses **O(n²)** space.

#### Part (b) — CYK on w = "bab" with given CNF grammar [7 marks]

String: w₁=b, w₂=a, w₃=b (n=3)

**Base case (length 1):**
- T[1][1]: b → B → b → **{B}**
- T[2][2]: a → A → a, C → a → **{A, C}**
- T[3][3]: b → B → b → **{B}**

**Length-2 spans:**

**T[1][2] (covers "ba", k=1):** Left={B}, Right={A, C}
- B, A → A → BA ✓ → Add **A**
- B, C → S → BC ✓ → Add **S**
- **T[1][2] = {A, S}**

**T[2][3] (covers "ab", k=2):** Left={A, C}, Right={B}
- A, B → C → AB ✓ → Add **C**
- C, B → no rule
- **T[2][3] = {C}**

**Length-3 span:**

**T[1][3] (covers "bab"):**
- Split k=1: T[1][1]={B}, T[2][3]={C} → check B → BC ✗, A → BC ✗, S → BC ✓ → Add **S**
- Split k=2: T[1][2]={A, S}, T[3][3]={B}
  - A, B → A → AB ✗, S → AB ✗, C → AB ✗ → none
  - S, B → no rule
- **T[1][3] = {S}**

**Final Parsing Table:**

| Span | T[1] | T[2] | T[3] |
|------|------|------|------|
| **L=3** | **{S}** | — | — |
| **L=2** | {A, S} | {C} | — |
| **L=1** | {B} | {A, C} | {B} |
| | b | a | b |

> **Decision: S ∈ T[1][3] = {S} → String "bab" IS ACCEPTED by the grammar.** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting CNF conversion (3-4 marks lost):** CYK strictly requires CNF. If you apply CYK to a non-CNF grammar, your parsing table will be structurally invalid and the answer is marked wrong.
> 2. **Wrong split-point iteration (2 marks lost):** The split point k iterates from **i to j−1** (inclusive), NOT from i+1 to j. Missing the inclusive lower bound is the most common error.
> 3. **Missing the union over ALL split points (3 marks lost):** A non-terminal appears in T[i][j] if it can be derived from **ANY** valid split, not just one. Students often stop at the first valid split and miss additional derivations.
> 4. **Confusing rows and columns (2 marks lost):** The table is indexed by span (start, end), not by token positions on both axes. Always label rows as "starting index" and columns as "ending index".

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CYK = Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming parser** for CFG membership testing.
- **Mandatory Pre-requisite:** The grammar MUST be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** — every rule must be of form **A → BC** or **A → a**.
- **Table Structure:** Triangular matrix of size n × n; cell **T[i][j]** stores the set of non-terminals deriving the span from position i to j.
- **Base Case:** T[i][i] = {A : A → wᵢ ∈ P} — directly from terminal-producing rules.
- **Recursive Case:** T[i][j] = **∪ₖ** {A : ∃ A → BC, B ∈ T[i][k], C ∈ T[k+1][j]} for k = i, i+1, ..., j−1.
- **Acceptance Criterion:** String is accepted **if and only if S ∈ T[1][n]**.
- **Time Complexity:** **O(n³)** (three nested loops: length, position, split).
- **Space Complexity:** **O(n²)** (triangular table).
- **Algorithm Inventors:** John Cocke, Daniel Younger, and Tadao Kasami (independently discovered around 1965-1966).
- **Key Exam Strategy:** Always **draw the empty table first**, then fill it **systematically** (length 1 → 2 → ... → n) — this earns method marks even if a later cell is wrong.
- **Memory Aid:** Think **"Pyramid up, Verify on top"** — you build the pyramid from words and check if S is at the apex.
- **Limitation:** CYK is **O(n³)** which is slower than linear parsers (e.g., LR parsers), but works for **any** CFG, making it theoretically powerful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dependency Parsing - Transition-Based Dependency Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dependency Parsing - Transition-Based Dependency Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Dependency Parsing: Transition-Based Dependency Parsing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dependency Parsing** is a syntactic analysis task in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that produces a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** — called a *dependency parse tree* — in which every word of a sentence is connected to exactly one **head** word (the *regent*) via a labeled, typed **dependency relation** (arc). The formal structure is a tuple $G = (V, A)$ where $V$ is the set of word nodes and $A \subseteq V \times L \times V$ is the set of labeled arcs.

In a dependency parse:
- Each word has **exactly one head** (except the synthetic `ROOT` token, which is the global head).
- A word is called a **dependent** (or *modifier*) of its head.
- The arc label encodes the grammatical function (e.g., `nsubj`, `dobj`, `amod`, `det`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Transition-Based Dependency Parsing** is a *deterministic, data-driven* approach that builds a parse tree incrementally by reading the input sentence left-to-right and applying a sequence of **transition actions** to a mutable configuration. It is essentially a **shift-reduce style** algorithm augmented with an arc-construction operation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Filing Cabinet" Analogy

Imagine you are a librarian sorting a stack of index cards — one card per word of a sentence — into a **family tree**:

- The **Buffer** is the conveyor belt holding the unprocessed cards in their original order.
- The **Stack** is the partial filing structure already begun.
- The **Arc Set** is the finished "parent → child" relationships you have stamped on the cards.
- At every step, you choose one of three moves:
  1. **SHIFT** — pick the next card from the conveyor belt and place it on the stack.
  2. **LEFT-ARC(label)** — declare the top card as the *parent* of the card just below it, and remove the child.
  3. **RIGHT-ARC(label)** — declare the card just below the top as the *parent* of the top card, and remove the top.

A trained **classifier (oracle)** decides which move to make. When the conveyor belt is empty and only `ROOT` remains on the stack, the family tree is complete.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology

- **Sentence length $n$** — number of tokens (excluding `ROOT`).
- **Number of arcs** $= n$ (every token has exactly one incoming head arc; `ROOT` has none).
- **Determinism** — A transition system is *deterministic* if, for every reachable configuration, at most one transition is valid.
- **Standard metrics**: **Unlabeled Attachment Score (UAS)** and **Labeled Attachment Score (LAS)**, both expressed as percentages.
- **Projective parse** — every arc can be drawn above the sentence without crossing another arc.

> [!TIP]
> KTU board questions frequently ask: *"Define HEAD and DEPENDENT with an example."* Memorize the definitions and the symbol $w_i \xrightarrow{\text{label}} w_j$ which reads as "$w_i$ is the head of $w_j$."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Transition System

A **transition-based dependency parser** is formally defined by the 4-tuple:

$$
\mathcal{T} = (C, T, c_s, C_t)
$$

where:

- $C$ = set of all possible **configurations** (states).
- $T$ = set of legal **transition actions** (moves).
- $c_s$ = unique **initial configuration** for any input sentence.
- $C_t \subseteq C$ = set of **terminal configurations** (final states).

### 2.2 Configuration Structure

A configuration $c \in C$ is a triple:

$$
c = (\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A})
$$

- $\sigma$ = **Stack** (list of word indices, top is rightmost).
- $\beta$ = **Buffer** (list of word indices, front is leftmost).
- $\mathcal{A}$ = **Arc set** — set of head-dependent labeled pairs $h \xrightarrow{\ell} d$.

### 2.3 Arc-Standard Transition System (Nivre 2003)

The most commonly tested system. Three transitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SHIFT:} \quad & (\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid b, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \\
\text{LEFT-ARC}(\ell): \quad & (\sigma \mid s_2 \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_1 \xrightarrow{\ell} s_2\}) \\
\text{RIGHT-ARC}(\ell): \quad & (\sigma \mid s_2 \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_2, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_2 \xrightarrow{\ell} s_1\})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Preconditions (very important for KTU):**
- SHIFT is allowed iff $\beta \neq \emptyset$.
- LEFT-ARC is allowed iff $\sigma$ has at least 2 elements and $s_2 \neq \text{ROOT}$.
- RIGHT-ARC is allowed iff $\sigma$ has at least 2 elements.

### 2.4 Arc-Eager Transition System (Nivre 2003 / 2004)

Adds two more transitions to enable **non-projective** parses and earlier attachment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SHIFT:} \quad & (\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid b, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \\
\text{LEFT-ARC}(\ell): \quad & (\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{b \xrightarrow{\ell} s_1\}) \\
\text{RIGHT-ARC}(\ell): \quad & (\sigma \mid s_1, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_1 \xrightarrow{\ell} b\}) \\
\text{REDUCE:} \quad & (\sigma \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **Arc-Eager**, the buffer-head $b$ participates in arcs, allowing attachment decisions before the dependent is fully shifted. This is what enables non-projective structures.

### 2.5 Why "Deterministic" Matters

For a sentence of length $n$, the Arc-Standard system has exactly $2n$ transitions to reach a terminal state (proved by induction: each transition either reduces the buffer size by 1, reduces the stack size by 1, or keeps it the same while building an arc — total is bounded by $2n+1$).

A parser is called **deterministic** because, at training time, the **static oracle** can read a gold tree and emit a unique sequence of transitions. This makes supervised learning trivial.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Meaning** | **Constraint / Value** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Sentence length (tokens excl. ROOT) | $n \geq 1$ |
| $\vert \sigma \vert + \vert \beta \vert + \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ | Conservation identity | Always equals $n + 1 + \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ updates |
| Number of arcs | Per parse tree | Exactly $n$ |
| Max transitions (Arc-Standard) | Per sentence | $2n$ |
| UAS | Unlabeled Attachment Score | $\dfrac{\text{Correct Heads}}{n} \times 100\%$ |
| LAS | Labeled Attachment Score | $\dfrac{\text{Correct Head+Label}}{n} \times 100\%$ |
| Projectivity | All arcs drawable without crossing | Def. of *projective parse* |
| ROOT rule | `ROOT` has no incoming arc | $\nexists\, h: h \xrightarrow{\ell} \text{ROOT}$ |
| Single-head rule | Each word has $\leq 1$ head | Mandatory |
| Connectedness | Every word reachable from ROOT | Mandatory |
| Acyclicity | No cycles in arc set | Mandatory for a tree |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

Transition-based parsers power real-world systems such as **spacy**, **Stanford CoreNLP**, and **Stanza**. They are linear-time ($O(n)$) and trivially parallelizable at the sentence level, making them suitable for:
- Information extraction pipelines
- Question answering (syntactic feature engineering)
- Machine translation pre-ordering
- Low-resource NLP (with cross-lingual transfer)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Arc-Standard on "She ate the cake"

We will parse the sentence $W = \langle w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4 \rangle = \langle \text{She}, \text{ate}, \text{the}, \text{cake} \rangle$ with the gold dependency tree:

$$
\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She}, \quad
\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{dobj}} \text{cake}, \quad
\text{cake} \xrightarrow{\text{det}} \text{the}, \quad
\text{ROOT} \xrightarrow{\text{root}} \text{ate}
$$

The `ROOT` token is indexed $0$. The **static oracle** (a deterministic procedure that converts a gold tree into a transition sequence) is used to derive the unique transition sequence.

### 3.2 The Static Oracle Procedure (Arc-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The oracle inspects the gold tree and, at every step, applies the following decision rule:
>
> 1. If the **front of the buffer** is the head of the **stack top** in the gold tree → **LEFT-ARC**.
> 2. Else if the **stack top** has any dependent still on the buffer → **SHIFT**.
> 3. Else → **RIGHT-ARC** to attach the stack top to its gold head.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

Let the configuration be $(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A})$ where $\sigma$ is shown as a stack with top on the right.

---

#### **Step 0 — Initial Configuration**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}],\;[\text{She}, \text{ate}, \text{the}, \text{cake}],\; \varnothing\,)
$$

Stack: `ROOT` is at the bottom. Buffer holds all 4 tokens. No arcs built.

**Oracle decision:** `ate` (stack top after a SHIFT) would be the head of `She`, so we first bring both onto the stack.
**Action:** **SHIFT**

---

#### **Step 1 — SHIFT**

Move the front of the buffer onto the stack:

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{She}],\;[\text{ate}, \text{the}, \text{cake}],\; \varnothing\,)
$$

`She` is now the top of the stack. Oracle checks: is `She` the head of anything still on the buffer? No — `She` is a leaf in the gold tree. Has `She` found its head on the stack? Yes — `ate`. But `ate` is not yet on the stack. We must SHIFT again.
**Action:** **SHIFT**

---

#### **Step 2 — SHIFT**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{She}, \text{ate}],\;[\text{the}, \text{cake}],\; \varnothing\,)
$$

Now `ate` is the stack top, and `She` is the second element. Oracle checks: does `ate` have any dependents still on the buffer? Yes — `cake` (via `dobj`). But before reaching `cake`, the gold tree has `ate → She (nsubj)`. So `She` (the second stack element) depends on `ate` (the top), which means `ate` is the head of `She`. This matches the **LEFT-ARC** pattern (top = head, second = dependent).
**Action:** **LEFT-ARC(nsubj)**

---

#### **Step 3 — LEFT-ARC(nsubj)**

Add the arc $ate \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} She$ and pop the dependent `She`:

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{ate}],\;[\text{the}, \text{cake}],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She}\,\}\,)
$$

`She` has been attached to its head and is removed from further consideration. Now `ate` is the top. Does `ate` have any dependents on the buffer? Yes — `cake` and `the` (via `the → cake`). The next word on the buffer is `the`, but `the`'s head is `cake`, not `ate`. We cannot LEFT-ARC. We must SHIFT to bring the next relevant tokens onto the stack.
**Action:** **SHIFT**

---

#### **Step 4 — SHIFT**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{ate}, \text{the}],\;[\text{cake}],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She}\,\}\,)
$$

`the` is the top. Is `the` the head of anything on the buffer? `the`'s head is `cake` (still on buffer). We can't LEFT-ARC. Is `the` a dependent of the second stack element (`ate`)? No — `the`'s gold head is `cake`, not `ate`. So we must SHIFT.
**Action:** **SHIFT**

---

#### **Step 5 — SHIFT**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{ate}, \text{the}, \text{cake}],\;[\;],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She}\,\}\,)
$$

The buffer is now empty. Stack top is `cake`, second is `the`. Oracle checks: is `cake` the head of `the`? Yes — $cake \xrightarrow{\text{det}} the$. This is the **LEFT-ARC** pattern (top `cake` = head, second `the` = dependent).
**Action:** **LEFT-ARC(det)**

---

#### **Step 6 — LEFT-ARC(det)**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{ate}, \text{cake}],\;[\;],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She},\; \text{cake} \xrightarrow{\text{det}} \text{the}\,\}\,)
$$

`the` is attached and removed. Stack top is now `cake`, second is `ate`. Oracle checks: does `cake` have any dependents on the buffer? Buffer is empty. So `cake` has found all its dependents. The gold head of `cake` is `ate` (the second stack element). So we form an arc from the second element (`ate`) to the top (`cake`) — this is **RIGHT-ARC**.
**Action:** **RIGHT-ARC(dobj)**

---

#### **Step 7 — RIGHT-ARC(dobj)**

Add the arc $ate \xrightarrow{\text{dobj}} cake$ and pop the dependent `cake`:

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}, \text{ate}],\;[\;],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She},\; \text{cake} \xrightarrow{\text{det}} \text{the},\; \text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{dobj}} \text{cake}\,\}\,)
$$

Now only `ate` remains on the stack (besides `ROOT`). Buffer is empty. `ate` has found all its dependents. The gold head of `ate` is `ROOT`. So we form a **RIGHT-ARC** from `ROOT` to `ate`.
**Action:** **RIGHT-ARC(root)**

---

#### **Step 8 — RIGHT-ARC(root)**

$$
(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A}) = (\,[\text{ROOT}],\;[\;],\; \mathcal{A} = \{\,\text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{She},\; \text{cake} \xrightarrow{\text{det}} \text{the},\; \text{ate} \xrightarrow{\text{dobj}} \text{cake},\; \text{ROOT} \xrightarrow{\text{root}} \text{ate}\,\}\,)
$$

**TERMINAL:** Stack contains only `ROOT`, buffer is empty. Parsing complete.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Complete Transition Trace

| **Step** | **Action** | **Stack $\sigma$** | **Buffer $\beta$** | **Arcs in $\mathcal{A}$** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | — (Initial) | `[ROOT]` | `[She, ate, the, cake]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 1 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, She]` | `[ate, the, cake]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 2 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, She, ate]` | `[the, cake]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 3 | LEFT-ARC(nsubj) | `[ROOT, ate]` | `[the, cake]` | `ate → She` |
| 4 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, ate, the]` | `[cake]` | `ate → She` |
| 5 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, ate, the, cake]` | `[]` | `ate → She` |
| 6 | LEFT-ARC(det) | `[ROOT, ate, cake]` | `[]` | `ate → She`, `cake → the` |
| 7 | RIGHT-ARC(dobj) | `[ROOT, ate]` | `[]` | `+ ate → cake` |
| 8 | RIGHT-ARC(root) | `[ROOT]` | `[]` | `+ ROOT → ate` |

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** Total transitions = 8 = $2n$ where $n=4$. ✓ Total arcs = 4 = $n$. ✓ Each non-ROOT token has exactly one head. ✓ ROOT has no incoming arc. ✓

### 3.5 Decision Classifier (the "Brain" of the Parser)

At inference time, a **multi-class classifier** (in modern systems, a neural network) consumes a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_c$ extracted from the current configuration $c$ and outputs a probability distribution over the legal transitions:

$$
p(t \mid c) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{W}_t \cdot \mathbf{x}_c)}{\sum_{t' \in T(c)} \exp(\mathbf{W}_{t'} \cdot \mathbf{x}_c)}
$$

The chosen transition is $\hat{t} = \arg\max_{t \in T(c)} p(t \mid c)$. Features typically include:
- Top of stack word, its POS tag, its head, its leftmost/rightmost child.
- Front of buffer word and its POS tag.
- Distance, valence, and 3rd-order features in classical systems; contextual embeddings in neural systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transition-Based Parser

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Input Sentence Tokens] --> B0[Tokenization and POS Tagging Module]
    B0 --> C0[Initialize Configuration Stack equals ROOT and Buffer equals Tokens]
    C0 --> D0[Extract Feature Vector from Current State]
    D0 --> E0[Neural Transition Classifier]
    E0 --> F0{Apply Highest Probability Legal Transition}
    F0 --> G0[Update Stack Buffer and Arc Set]
    G0 --> H0{Terminal Condition Met}
    H0 --> D0
    H0 --> I0[Output Dependency Parse Tree]
    E0 --> J0[Trained on Static Oracle Sequences]
    J0 -.trains.-> E0
```

## 4.2 Per-Step State Transition Diagram (Arc-Standard)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s0[State c: Stack Sigma Buffer Beta Arcs A]
    s1[SHIFT: move front of buffer onto stack]
    s2[LEFT-ARC label: add arc top to second-top and pop second-top]
    s3[RIGHT-ARC label: add arc second-top to top and pop top]
    s4[Terminal State: only ROOT on stack and empty buffer]
    s0 --> s1
    s0 --> s2
    s0 --> s3
    s1 --> s0
    s2 --> s0
    s3 --> s0
    s0 --> s4
```

## 4.3 Sentence-Level Pipeline View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Text] --> B1[Tokenizer]
    B1 --> C1[POS Tagger]
    C1 --> D1[Transition Parser Core]
    D1 --> E1[Dependency Graph]
    E1 --> F1[Evaluator: UAS and LAS]
    F1 --> G1[Feedback Loss to Classifier]
    G1 -.backprop.-> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first flowchart depicts the **inference pipeline** — the parser loops over the "extract features → classify → transition → check terminal" cycle until termination. The second diagram shows the **legal action space** at every state. The third diagram shows the **training signal**: UAS/LAS scores generate a loss that is back-propagated into the classifier weights.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question] — 3 Marks
**Define dependency parsing. With a suitable example, explain the concepts of HEAD and DEPENDENT.**

**Model Answer:**

Dependency parsing is the syntactic analysis of a sentence to produce a directed graph (a tree) where every word is linked to exactly one *head* word by a labeled, typed grammatical relation.

- **Head (Regent / Governor):** The word in a syntactic relationship that governs the other. It is the parent in the dependency arc.
- **Dependent (Modifier):** The word whose syntactic role is determined by the head. It is the child in the arc.

**Example:** In the sentence *"The cat sleeps"*, the dependency structure is:
- `sleeps` is the head.
- `cat` is the dependent of `sleeps` (relation: `nsubj`).
- `The` is the dependent of `cat` (relation: `det`).

Symbolically: $cat \xleftarrow{\text{nsubj}} sleeps$ and $The \xleftarrow{\text{det}} cat$.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question] — 3 Marks
**What is projectivity in dependency parsing? Distinguish between projective and non-projective dependencies with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A dependency parse is **projective** if every arc can be drawn above the sentence on a single plane without any two arcs crossing each other. Equivalently, for every arc $h \xrightarrow{} d$, all words linearly between $h$ and $d$ in the sentence are descendants of $h$ in the tree.

- **Projective example:** *"She likes red apples"* — all arcs can be drawn without crossing.
- **Non-projective example:** *"I saw a man with a telescope"* — the arc linking `man` to `with` (or to `telescope`) can cross another arc depending on the analysis chosen (e.g., in some languages with free word order).

Arc-Standard produces **only projective** trees; Arc-Eager can produce **non-projective** trees.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions on this topic almost always carry **two 7-mark sub-parts**: one theoretical and one computational (trace/example).

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Arc-Standard System Deep Dive

#### **A(a)** Explain the Arc-Standard transition system for dependency parsing in detail. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The Arc-Standard system (Nivre, 2003) is a deterministic, shift-reduce style transition system for projective dependency parsing. It is defined by:

- **Configuration:** $(\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A})$ where $\sigma$ is the stack, $\beta$ is the buffer, $\mathcal{A}$ is the arc set.
- **Initial state:** $c_s = (\,[\text{ROOT}],\;[w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n],\; \varnothing\,)$.
- **Terminal condition:** $\sigma = [\text{ROOT}]$ and $\beta = \emptyset$.

**Three transition operations:**

1. **SHIFT** — Moves the front of the buffer onto the top of the stack. Precondition: $\beta \neq \emptyset$. Formally:
$$
(\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid b, \beta, \mathcal{A})
$$

2. **LEFT-ARC($\ell$)** — Adds an arc from the stack top to the second element, then pops the second. The top is the head, the second is the dependent. Precondition: $\sigma$ has $\geq 2$ elements and the second is not ROOT. Formally:
$$
(\sigma \mid s_2 \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_1 \xrightarrow{\ell} s_2\})
$$

3. **RIGHT-ARC($\ell$)** — Adds an arc from the second element to the stack top, then pops the top. The second is the head, the top is the dependent. Formally:
$$
(\sigma \mid s_2 \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_2, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_2 \xrightarrow{\ell} s_1\})
$$

**Properties:**
- **Determinism:** A unique transition is valid at each configuration (subject to the gold tree).
- **Projectivity:** Only projective trees are produced.
- **Complexity:** $O(n)$ time per sentence; at most $2n$ transitions.
- **Static Oracle:** Given a gold tree, the oracle emits a unique transition sequence deterministically.

#### **A(b)** Trace the Arc-Standard algorithm on the sentence *"Boys play cricket"*. Show the configuration at every step. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Let the tokens be $w_1=\text{Boys}, w_2=\text{play}, w_3=\text{cricket}$. Gold tree: $play \xrightarrow{nsubj} Boys$, $play \xrightarrow{dobj} cricket$, $ROOT \xrightarrow{root} play$.

| **Step** | **Action** | **Stack $\sigma$** | **Buffer $\beta$** | **Arcs $\mathcal{A}$** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | — | `[ROOT]` | `[Boys, play, cricket]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 1 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, Boys]` | `[play, cricket]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 2 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, Boys, play]` | `[cricket]` | $\varnothing$ |
| 3 | LEFT-ARC(nsubj) | `[ROOT, play]` | `[cricket]` | `play → Boys` |
| 4 | SHIFT | `[ROOT, play, cricket]` | `[]` | `play → Boys` |
| 5 | RIGHT-ARC(dobj) | `[ROOT, play]` | `[]` | `+ play → cricket` |
| 6 | RIGHT-ARC(root) | `[ROOT]` | `[]` | `+ ROOT → play` |

**Final parse:** 3 arcs = $n$ ✓. Terminal state reached in $2n = 6$ transitions ✓.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Arc-Eager and System Comparison

#### **B(a)** Explain the Arc-Eager transition system. How does it differ from Arc-Standard? [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Arc-Eager** system (Nivre, 2003/2004) extends Arc-Standard by allowing attachment decisions earlier — the **buffer-head** can directly participate in arcs without first being shifted onto the stack. This is what enables **non-projective** parses.

**Four transition operations:**

1. **SHIFT:** Move buffer front to stack. $(\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \to (\sigma \mid b, \beta, \mathcal{A})$.

2. **LEFT-ARC($\ell$):** Add arc from buffer front $b$ to stack top $s_1$ and pop $s_1$. Precondition: $s_1$ has no head yet.
$$
(\sigma \mid s_1, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{b \xrightarrow{\ell} s_1\})
$$

3. **RIGHT-ARC($\ell$):** Add arc from stack top $s_1$ to buffer front $b$ and move $b$ to stack. Precondition: $b$ has no head yet.
$$
(\sigma \mid s_1, b \mid \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma \mid s_1 \mid b, \beta, \mathcal{A} \cup \{s_1 \xrightarrow{\ell} b\})
$$

4. **REDUCE:** Pop the stack top. Precondition: stack top already has a head.
$$
(\sigma \mid s_1, \beta, \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; (\sigma, \beta, \mathcal{A})
$$

**Key differences from Arc-Standard:**

| **Property** | **Arc-Standard** | **Arc-Eager** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of transitions | 3 | 4 (adds REDUCE) |
| Projectivity | Projective only | Allows non-projective |
| When attachment happens | After dependent is shifted | Earlier (eager) |
| Buffer-head participation | No | Yes (LEFT/RIGHT-ARC) |
| Max transitions per $n$ | $2n$ | $\leq 2n$ (often fewer) |
| Real-world usage | Common in spacy default | Used in Stanza, UDPipe |

#### **B(b)** Discuss the major challenges faced by transition-based dependency parsers. How are these addressed in modern neural systems? [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Challenges:**

1. **Error propagation** — A single wrong transition (e.g., a premature SHIFT) corrupts the entire downstream parse. Classical systems (arc-eager or arc-standard) using SVM/MaxEnt classifiers suffer from compounding errors.

2. **Feature engineering** — Classical parsers relied on hand-crafted indicator features (POS tags, word forms, distance, valence, contextual words in a window). These are sparse, language-specific, and don't generalize across domains.

3. **Out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words** — Unknown tokens degrade both the POS tagger and the transition classifier.

4. **Non-projectivity** — Classical Arc-Standard cannot handle non-projective languages (e.g., Czech, Turkish, Hindi). Arc-Eager helps but still has constraints.

5. **Greedy decisions** — No backtracking; the parser commits to a transition immediately.

**Modern neural solutions:**

- **Static oracle + structured learning:** Beam search replaces greedy decoding, reducing error propagation.
- **Dense, contextual embeddings:** BiLSTM and Transformer encoders (e.g., BERT) replace sparse hand-crafted features.
- **Graph-based hybrids:** Combine transition-based parsing with neural scoring (e.g., Dozat & Manning's stack-pointer parser).
- **Multilingual and cross-lingual transfer:** Pre-trained language models (mBERT, XLM-R) transfer knowledge to low-resource languages, mitigating OOV and feature sparsity.
- **Dynamic oracles:** Replace static oracles with dynamic ones that allow recovery from earlier mistakes during training.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing LEFT-ARC and RIGHT-ARC semantics.** In Arc-Standard, **LEFT-ARC** means the *top* of stack is the **head** and the *second* is the **dependent**. In Arc-Eager, **LEFT-ARC** means the *buffer-front* is the head and the *stack-top* is the dependent. **Always read the question carefully** — examiners frequently test this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the ROOT preconditions.** You cannot LEFT-ARC a token off the ROOT (i.e., ROOT cannot be made a dependent). Many students violate this in traces.
> 3. **Miscounting transitions.** For a sentence of length $n$, Arc-Standard takes exactly $2n$ transitions to terminate. If your trace has $2n+1$ or $2n-1$, you have an error.
> 4. **Writing "arc from $A$ to $B$" without specifying head vs. dependent.** The arrow direction matters: $A \to B$ means $A$ is the head of $B$.
> 5. **Omitting the label.** Every transition in a labeled system is parameterized by a dependency label $\ell$. Mentioning only SHIFT/LEFT/RIGHT without labels loses partial credit.
> 6. **Confusing UAS and LAS.** UAS counts correct head attachment; LAS counts correct head **and** correct label.
> 7. **Skipping the oracle reasoning.** Examiners reward explanations of *why* a transition was chosen, not just the action itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Dependency parsing** produces a tree where each word (except ROOT) has exactly one head.
- [ ] A **transition-based parser** = (Configurations, Transitions, Initial state, Terminal states).
- [ ] **Configuration = (Stack, Buffer, Arc Set).** Stack top is the rightmost element.
- [ ] **Arc-Standard** has 3 moves: SHIFT, LEFT-ARC, RIGHT-ARC. Produces only projective trees.
- [ ] **Arc-Eager** has 4 moves: SHIFT, LEFT-ARC, RIGHT-ARC, REDUCE. Can produce non-projective trees.
- [ ] **LEFT-ARC (Arc-Standard):** top is HEAD, second is DEPENDENT; pop the second.
- [ ] **RIGHT-ARC (Arc-Standard):** second is HEAD, top is DEPENDENT; pop the top.
- [ ] **ROOT rule:** `ROOT` is never a dependent; it is the global head.
- [ ] **Total arcs in a parse** = $n$ (sentence length).
- [ ] **Total transitions (Arc-Standard)** = $2n$ exactly.
- [ ] **UAS** = correct heads only; **LAS** = correct head **and** label.
- [ ] **Determinism** holds because a static oracle converts a gold tree into a unique transition sequence.
- [ ] The **decision classifier** is the "brain" — historically SVM/MaxEnt, now neural (BiLSTM, Transformer).
- [ ] **Projective parse:** all arcs can be drawn without crossing above a linear sentence layout.
- [ ] **Non-projective parse:** required for free-word-order languages (Czech, Turkish, Hindi, etc.).
- [ ] **Modern neural parsers** use beam search + contextual embeddings to mitigate error propagation.
- [ ] **Time complexity:** $O(n)$ per sentence; the parser is linear in sentence length.
- [ ] KTU questions on this topic **always** expect a worked trace on a 3–4 word sentence — practice at least 3 traces before the exam.
- [ ] Always state **preconditions** for each transition (e.g., buffer non-empty for SHIFT).
- [ ] Always **verify** at the end: stack = `[ROOT]`, buffer empty, arc count = $n$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final exam tip:** If asked to compare Arc-Standard and Arc-Eager in 14 marks, allocate **4 marks to a side-by-side table** (projectivity, transitions, time, real-world tools) and **10 marks to examples and traces**. Examiners reward visual organization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph-Based Dependency Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Graph-Based Dependency Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Graph-Based Dependency Parsing** is a global, discriminative approach to syntactic analysis in which the goal is to find the highest-scoring **directed spanning tree** over a complete graph of sentence tokens. Each potential dependency arc is assigned a score by a learned model, and parsing reduces to a combinatorial optimization problem (Maximum Spanning Tree, MST) over the set of all well-formed dependency trees.

Formally, given a sentence $S = w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ of $n$ tokens, we seek:

$$T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \; \text{score}(T \mid S)$$

where $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of valid dependency trees (acyclic, single-rooted, directed spanning trees).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST862 – Module 2):** Graph-based methods treat parsing as a search problem in a structured space, contrasting with transition-based (greedy) and constraint-based (rule-driven) approaches. They are classified as **global** and **discriminative** because the entire tree is scored jointly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Network
Imagine you are a city planner designing a road network connecting **n cities** so that every city is reachable from the *capital* (root), no loops form, and the total road cost is **minimized** (or utility **maximised**). Each pair of cities $(i, j)$ has a known "usefulness" score if we were to build a road from $i$ to $j$. The dependency parser faces the exact same problem:
- **Cities** = tokens of the sentence
- **Roads** = potential dependency arcs (head → dependent)
- **Usefulness of a road** = the model's arc score
- **Best network** = the maximum-scoring dependency tree

Just as the planner can evaluate *all* roads globally to pick the best set, a graph-based parser inspects *all* $O(n^2)$ possible arcs simultaneously, then solves a classical graph problem to deliver the optimal tree.

### 1.3 Key Terminology
- **Head** : The token that governs a dependent.
- **Dependent** : The token modified by its head.
- **Arc / Edge** : A directed dependency relation $(h, d)$ from head $h$ to dependent $d$.
- **Dependency Tree** : A directed, acyclic, single-rooted spanning tree over the sentence.
- **Projective Tree** : A tree in which every arc lies above no crossing arcs when drawn above the words.
- **Non-Projective Tree** : Contains at least one discontinuous arc (common in free-word-order languages).
- **Arc-Factored Model** : A model in which the score of a tree is the **sum** of the scores of its individual arcs.
- **MST (Maximum Spanning Tree)** : The tree with the highest total arc score in a weighted graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **arc-factored assumption** is what makes graph-based parsing tractable. Without it, scoring would have to consider interactions between multiple arcs simultaneously, leading to a much harder inference problem.

### 1.4 Why Graph-Based Parsing Matters
Graph-based parsers such as **MSTParser** (McDonald, 2005) and **ClearParser** achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on multiple languages and remain conceptually elegant because they decouple the problem into three well-studied sub-tasks: **(1)** feature engineering, **(2)** parameter learning, and **(3)** decoding via MST algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Graph-Based Parsing

**(1) Model Architecture — Arc-Factored Scoring**

Each candidate arc $(h, d)$ is scored independently by a function parameterized by a weight vector $\mathbf{w}$:

$$s(h, d) \;=\; \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)$$

The full-tree score decomposes as a sum:

$$\text{score}(T \mid S) \;=\; \sum_{(h, d) \in T} s(h, d) \;=\; \sum_{(h, d) \in T} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)$$

**(2) Parameter Learning — Online Discriminative Training**

Because exact inference over the tree space $\mathcal{T}$ is tractable, online learners such as the **Averaged Perceptron** or **Margin Infused Relaxed Algorithm (MIRA)** can be applied:

$$\mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} + \mathbf{f}(T^{*}, S) - \mathbf{f}(T^{p}, S)$$

where $T^{*}$ is the gold tree and $T^{p}$ is the parser's highest-scoring prediction under the current weights.

**(3) Decoding — Combinatorial Optimization**

For **projective** trees, decoding is performed with **Eisner's algorithm** (1996) in polynomial time $O(n^{3})$.

For **non-projective** trees, decoding is performed with the **Chu-Liu/Edmonds algorithm** in $O(n^{2})$ (or $O(n^{3})$ for higher-order models).

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Tree score | $\text{score}(T \mid S) = \sum_{(h,d) \in T} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h,d,S)$ | Arc-factored decomposition |
| 2 | Arc score | $s(h,d) = \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h,d,S)$ | Linear model over feature vector |
| 3 | Decoding (non-projective) | $T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_{(h,d) \in T} s(h,d)$ | Solved by Chu-Liu/Edmonds |
| 4 | Decoding (projective) | $T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}_{\text{proj}}} \sum_{(h,d) \in T} s(h,d)$ | Solved by Eisner's $O(n^{3})$ DP |
| 5 | Margin objective | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \vert \mathbf{w} \vert \vert^{2} + C \sum_{i} \xi_{i}$ | Structured SVM hinge loss |
| 6 | Online update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \bigl(\mathbf{f}(T^{*},S) - \mathbf{f}(T^{p},S)\bigr)$ | Perceptron-style; $\eta$ is learning rate |
| 7 | Negative log-loss (probabilistic view) | $\ell = -\log P(T^{*} \mid S) = -\log \dfrac{\exp(\text{score}(T^{*}))}{\sum_{T} \exp(\text{score}(T))}$ | Equivalent to CRF-style training |
| 8 | Cycle contraction | $w'(u, v) = w(u, v) - w(u, k^{*})$ | Used by Chu-Liu/Edmonds to break cycles |
| 9 | Root constraint | $\sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[(i, 0) \in T] = 1$ | Exactly one root node |
| 10 | Span completeness | $\sum_{d} \mathbb{1}[(h, d) \in T] = 1 \;\; \forall d \neq 0$ | Each non-root has exactly one head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Memorize equations (1), (3), (4), and (8) at minimum — these are the most frequently tested in ESE Module 2 questions.

### 2.3 Feature Templates (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature Type | Description |
|--------------|-------------|
| **Lexical** | Identity of head word, dependent word, their concatenation |
| **POS** | Part-of-speech tags of head, dependent, and their bigram |
| **Distance** | $\text{dist} = \vert h - d \vert$ and direction $\text{sign}(h-d)$ |
| **Context** | Surrounding words in a window of size $k$ (typically $k = 1$ or $2$) |
| **Valency** | Number of dependents already attached to the head |
| **Word-Class** | Brown cluster, WordNet hypernym, or embedding similarity |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Machine Translation** | Source-side reordering and projection of syntactic structure |
| **Question Answering** | Extracting subject–verb–object relations for answer retrieval |
| **Information Extraction** | Identifying entity–relation triples |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Capturing opinion-target relations via dependency paths |
| **Biomedical NLP** | Mining protein–protein interactions in scientific literature |

### 2.5 Comparison with Transition-Based Parsing

| Aspect | Graph-Based | Transition-Based |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| **Search** | Global, exhaustive | Local, greedy |
| **Features** | Limited to local arc context | Richer, history-dependent |
| **Speed** | $O(n^{2})$ to $O(n^{3})$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Accuracy** | Generally higher on long sentences | Higher on short sentences |
| **Algorithm** | MST algorithms (Chu-Liu/Edmonds, Eisner) | Arc-eager, Arc-standard, etc. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arc-Factored Decoding Objective

We start from the probabilistic interpretation. Let $P(T \mid S)$ denote the probability of a dependency tree $T$ given sentence $S$. Under the **log-linear (maximum-entropy)** framework:

$$P(T \mid S) \;=\; \frac{1}{Z(S)} \exp\!\Bigl(\sum_{(h,d) \in T} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)\Bigr)$$

where $Z(S)$ is the partition function:

$$Z(S) \;=\; \sum_{T' \in \mathcal{T}} \exp\!\Bigl(\sum_{(h,d) \in T'} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)\Bigr)$$

Because the $\arg\max$ is **invariant to multiplicative and additive constants that do not depend on $T$**, we can drop $Z(S)$ and the exp to obtain:

$$T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \; \sum_{(h,d) \in T} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)$$

This is exactly the **maximum spanning tree (MST)** problem in a complete directed graph where every edge $(h, d)$ carries weight $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)$.

### 3.2 Detailed Walk-Through: Chu-Liu/Edmonds Algorithm

We now derive the decoding algorithm step-by-step. Let $G = (V, E)$ be a complete directed graph with weight $w(i, j)$ on every arc $(i, j)$. We want the maximum-weight **rooted spanning arborescence** with root $r$.

**Step 1 — Greedy Edge Selection (Initial Forest)**

For every vertex $v \neq r$, pick the *incoming* edge of maximum weight:

$$p(v) \;=\; \arg\max_{u \in V} \; w(u, v)$$

This produces a graph $F$ consisting of one or more arborescences (a forest). Each component has either zero or one cycles.

**Step 2 — Cycle Detection**

If $F$ contains no cycle, $F$ is already the MST. Otherwise, identify every directed cycle $C$ in $F$.

**Step 3 — Cycle Contraction**

For each cycle $C$, create a super-vertex $v_{C}$. For every original edge $(u, v)$ *entering* $C$ from outside:

$$w'(v_{C}, v) \;=\; w(u, v) - w(p(v), v)$$

For every edge $(u, v)$ *leaving* $C$:

$$w'(v_{C}, v) \;=\; w(u, v)$$

These adjustments preserve the *relative weight differences* between any two candidate trees that include/exclude the cycle.

**Step 4 — Recursion**

Recursively apply Steps 1–3 on the contracted graph $G'$.

**Step 5 — Expansion**

When the recursion terminates, expand every contracted super-vertex back into its original cycle, and add to the MST all cycle edges *not* involved in the recursive MST construction (i.e., the edges of $C$ except the one "consumed" by contraction).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (3-Token Sentence)

Consider the sentence **"John saw Mary"** with tokens:

$$V = \{1:\text{John}, \; 2:\text{saw}, \; 3:\text{Mary}\}, \quad r = 2 \;(\text{root} = \text{saw})$$

Suppose a trained model produces the following arc scores $w(h, d)$:

| From \\ To | 1 (John) | 2 (saw) | 3 (Mary) |
|------------|----------|---------|----------|
| **0 (root marker)** | 0.0 | **1.2** | 0.0 |
| **1 (John)** | — | 0.3 | 0.4 |
| **2 (saw)** | **0.9** | — | **1.1** |
| **3 (Mary)** | 0.2 | 0.5 | — |

*(Entry (h, h) is undefined.)*

**Step 1 — Best incoming edge for each non-root vertex:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p(1) &= \arg\max\{w(0,1),\, w(2,1),\, w(3,1)\} \\
&= \arg\max\{0.0,\; 0.9,\; 0.2\} \;=\; 2 \\
p(3) &= \arg\max\{w(0,3),\, w(1,3),\, w(2,3)\} \\
&= \arg\max\{0.0,\; 0.4,\; 1.1\} \;=\; 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Initial forest:**

Edges selected: $(2 \to 1)$ and $(2 \to 3)$. With the root edge $(0 \to 2)$, this is already a valid tree with **no cycle**!

**Step 3 — Tree score:**

$$\text{score}(T) = w(0,2) + w(2,1) + w(2,3) = 1.2 + 0.9 + 1.1 = 3.2$$

The optimal dependency tree is therefore:

```
saw (root)
 ├── nsubj: John
 └── dobj:  Mary
```

### 3.4 Counter-Example Demonstrating Cycle Contraction

Now modify the scores so that a cycle forms:

| From \\ To | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|------------|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.0 | 1.0 | 0.0 |
| **1** | — | 0.5 | **1.5** |
| **2** | **1.4** | — | 0.6 |
| **3** | 0.2 | **1.3** | — |

**Step 1 — Best incoming edges:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p(1) &= \arg\max\{0.0,\; 1.4,\; 0.2\} \;=\; 2 \\
p(2) &= \arg\max\{1.0,\; 0.5,\; 1.3\} \;=\; 0 \\
p(3) &= \arg\max\{0.0,\; 1.5,\; 0.6\} \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle detected:** $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$? No — actually edges are $(2 \to 1)$, $(0 \to 2)$, $(1 \to 3)$, forming a tree: **no cycle**. So MST = $\{(0,2), (2,1), (1,3)\}$ with total score $1.0 + 1.4 + 1.5 = 3.9$.

**A real cycle scenario:** Suppose $p(1)=2$, $p(2)=3$, $p(3)=1$. Then the cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$ exists. Cycle edges $\{(2,1), (3,2), (1,3)\}$ have weights $\{1.4, 1.3, 1.5\}$. The cycle weight is $1.4 + 1.3 + 1.5 = 4.2$.

For every external edge entering the cycle, the **adjusted weight** is computed as in Step 3 of §3.2. For example, edge $(0, 1)$ entering at vertex $1$ with $p(1)=2$:

$$w'(C, 1) = w(0, 1) - w(p(1), 1) = 0.0 - 1.4 = -1.4$$

If this adjusted weight is the *highest* incoming weight to the contracted cycle, the recursion replaces the heaviest cycle edge with this external edge — eliminating the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** Chu-Liu/Edmonds preserves optimality because subtracting $w(p(v), v)$ from every *alternative* incoming edge of vertex $v$ inside a cycle does not change the *relative* ranking of trees that include vs. exclude that cycle.

### 3.5 Complexity Analysis

| Algorithm | Best for | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Eisner (1996) | Projective trees | $O(n^{3})$ | $O(n^{2})$ |
| Chu-Liu/Edmonds (1965/1967) | Non-projective trees | $O(n^{2})$ with Fibonacci heaps | $O(n^{2})$ |
| Tarjan's Reps | MST for dense graphs | $O(m \log n)$ | — |

For sentence length $n \leq 50$ (typical), the practical runtime is sub-millisecond.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline of Graph-Based Dependency Parsing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef feature fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#E65100
    classDef model fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
    classDef decode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef output fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#880E4F

    S0[Raw Sentence Text]:::input
    S1[Tokenizer]:::input
    S2[POS Tagger]:::input
    S3[Feature Extractor]:::feature
    S4[Arc Score Function]:::model
    S5[Complete Directed Graph G]:::model
    S6[Chu Liu Edmonds Decoder]:::decode
    S7[Eisner Decoder for Projective]:::decode
    S8[Maximum Spanning Tree T Star]:::decode
    S9[Dependency Parse Output]:::output

    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
```

### 4.2 Score Decomposition in an Arc-Factored Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef treeNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B
    classDef arcNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#E65100
    classDef scoreNode fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#33691E

    W1[Token w1]:::treeNode
    W2[Token w2]:::treeNode
    W3[Token w3]:::treeNode
    W4[Token w4]:::treeNode

    A1[Arc w1 to w2]:::arcNode
    A2[Arc w2 to w3]:::arcNode
    A3[Arc w2 to w4]:::arcNode

    S1[Score s12]:::scoreNode
    S2[Score s23]:::scoreNode
    S3[Score s24]:::scoreNode

    T[Tree Score = S1 + S2 + S3]:::scoreNode

    W1 --- A1
    A1 --- S1
    W2 --- A2
    A2 --- S2
    W2 --- A3
    A3 --- S3
    S1 --- T
    S2 --- T
    S3 --- T
```

### 4.3 Training vs. Decoding Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef trainBox fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,color:#1A237E
    classDef decodeBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef dataBox fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#33691E

    G[Gold Annotated Treebank]:::dataBox
    F[Feature Vectors]:::dataBox

    subgraph TRAINING
        direction TB
        L1[Online Perceptron or MIRA]:::trainBox
        L2[Update Weight Vector W]:::trainBox
        L3[Repeat for K Epochs]:::trainBox
    end

    subgraph DECODING
        direction TB
        D1[New Test Sentence]:::decodeBox
        D2[Score All Arcs]:::decodeBox
        D3[Run Chu Liu Edmonds or Eisner]:::decodeBox
        D4[Return Maximum Spanning Tree]:::decodeBox
    end

    G --> F
    F --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 -->|Final W| D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

### 4.4 Cycle Contraction in Chu-Liu/Edmonds

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef cycleNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C
    classDef superNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    classDef extNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1

    V1[Vertex 1]:::cycleNode
    V2[Vertex 2]:::cycleNode
    V3[Vertex 3]:::cycleNode
    VEXT[External Vertex 4]:::extNode
    VSUP[Super Vertex C123]:::superNode

    V1 -->|edge w12| V2
    V2 -->|edge w23| V3
    V3 -->|edge w31| V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V1
    V1 -.contraction.-> VSUP
    V2 -.contraction.-> VSUP
    V3 -.contraction.-> VSUP
    VEXT -->|recomputed weight| VSUP
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three primary vertices (1, 2, 3) inside the red cycle are merged into a single **super-vertex** (green). Edge weights from external nodes into the cycle are *adjusted* to preserve the optimality of the original problem, as derived in §3.2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
Define **dependency parsing**. Differentiate between **graph-based** and **transition-based** approaches in 5 lines.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dependency parsing assigns a syntactic structure to a sentence by linking each word (dependent) to a single governing word (head), forming a tree.

| Aspect | Graph-Based | Transition-Based |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Search | Global, exhaustive over $\mathcal{T}$ | Local, greedy state transitions |
| Features | Limited to local arc context | Rich history-dependent features |
| Algorithm | MST (Chu-Liu/Edmonds / Eisner) | Arc-standard, arc-eager, etc. |
| Time | $O(n^{2})$–$O(n^{3})$ | $O(n)$ |

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the **arc-factored assumption** in graph-based dependency parsing? State its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The arc-factored assumption states that the score of a complete dependency tree equals the **sum of the scores of its individual arcs**:

$$\text{score}(T \mid S) = \sum_{(h,d) \in T} \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{f}(h, d, S)$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Decoupling:** Decouples tree-level scoring from arc-level scoring, making inference tractable.
2. **Efficient decoding:** Allows exact inference using classical MST algorithms (Chu-Liu/Edmonds in $O(n^{2})$, Eisner in $O(n^{3})$).
3. **Limitation:** Cannot model interactions between sibling arcs (e.g., valency constraints, gapping).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Chu-Liu/Edmonds algorithm** for non-projective dependency parsing. Show all five algorithmic steps with a suitable diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the sentence **"Ravi ate a mango"** with tokens $\{1: \text{Ravi}, 2: \text{ate}, 3: \text{a}, 4: \text{mango}\}$ and root $r = 2$, suppose the arc-score matrix from a trained model is:

| From \\ To | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|------------|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.0 | **2.0** | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| **1** | — | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.2 |
| **2** | **1.6** | — | **1.8** | **2.1** |
| **3** | 0.1 | 0.2 | — | 0.4 |
| **4** | 0.0 | 0.7 | 0.6 | — |

Determine the **maximum-scoring dependency tree** using Chu-Liu/Edmonds. Show every step. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Problem Setting (1 mark):** A complete directed graph $G = (V, E)$ has $n$ vertices (sentence tokens) and $n(n-1)$ arcs. Every arc $(i, j)$ has weight $w(i, j)$. We seek a **rooted spanning arborescence** with maximum total weight rooted at $r$.

**2. The Five Steps (5 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Greedy Selection:** For every $v \neq r$, select the best incoming edge $p(v) = \arg\max_u w(u, v)$.
- **Step 2 — Cycle Check:** If the selected edges form an acyclic arborescence, return them as the MST.
- **Step 3 — Contraction:** For each cycle $C$, create super-vertex $v_C$ and adjust external incoming edges as $w'(v_C, v) = w(u, v) - w(p(v), v)$.
- **Step 4 — Recursion:** Apply Steps 1–3 to the contracted graph.
- **Step 5 — Expansion:** Replace each super-vertex with the original cycle, removing the edge that was "consumed" during contraction.

**3. Complexity (1 mark):** Runs in $O(n^{2})$ with Fibonacci heaps, or $O(m \log n)$ for $m$ edges in the general case.

**4. Diagram (refer to §4.4 above) — already illustrated.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Greedy incoming edge per vertex:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p(1) &= \arg\max\{w(0,1)=0.0,\; w(2,1)=1.6,\; w(3,1)=0.1,\; w(4,1)=0.0\} = 2 \\
p(3) &= \arg\max\{w(0,3)=0.0,\; w(1,3)=0.3,\; w(2,3)=1.8,\; w(4,3)=0.6\} = 2 \\
p(4) &= \arg\max\{w(0,4)=0.0,\; w(1,4)=0.2,\; w(2,4)=2.1,\; w(3,4)=0.4\} = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Forest check:** Selected edges: $(2 \to 1), (2 \to 3), (2 \to 4)$, plus the root edge $(0 \to 2)$. All share head $2$ — **no cycle forms**.

**Step 3 — Verify the tree (rooted at 2):**

```
ate (root, 2)
 ├── nsubj: Ravi (1)
 ├── det:   a    (3)
 └── dobj:  mango (4)
```

**Step 4 — Total tree score:**

$$\text{score}(T) = w(0,2) + w(2,1) + w(2,3) + w(2,4) = 2.0 + 1.6 + 1.8 + 2.1 = 7.5$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The maximum-scoring dependency tree has total score **7.5**, attaching all tokens as direct dependents of *ate*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Eisner algorithm** for projective dependency parsing. Explain its dynamic programming recursion, time complexity, and the role of complete and incomplete spans. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **MSTParser (graph-based)** with **arc-standard transition-based parser** along the following axes: scoring, decoding, feature richness, and runtime. Identify two cases where each is preferred. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Background (1 mark):** Eisner's algorithm (1996) is a **CKY-style chart parser** for projective trees. It builds the parse bottom-up using two types of spans: **complete** (a head word with all its dependents attached) and **incomplete** (a half-built span awaiting attachment from the other side).

**2. Recursion (4 marks):**

For a span $[i, j]$ where $i < j$:

$$\text{complete}(i, j) = \max_{i \leq k < j} \bigl[ \text{complete}(i, k) + \text{complete}(k+1, j) + s(j, i) \bigr]$$

For incomplete spans:

$$\text{incomplete}_{\text{left}}(i, j) = \max_{i \leq k < j} \bigl[ \text{complete}(i, k) + \text{incomplete}_{\text{right}}(k+1, j) \bigr]$$

The base case is $\text{complete}(i, i) = 0$. The optimal tree is recovered by backpointers.

**3. Complexity (1 mark):** $O(n^{3})$ time, $O(n^{2})$ space.

**4. Why it works (1 mark):** By separating complete/incomplete spans, the algorithm correctly handles **head-outward** and **head-inward** attachments and guarantees that no arc crosses another — the defining property of projectivity.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

| Axis | MSTParser (Graph-Based) | Arc-Standard (Transition-Based) |
|------|-------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Scoring** | Global — sum of arc scores over the entire tree | Local — score of the next transition |
| **Decoding** | Chu-Liu/Edmonds or Eisner | Greedy shift-reduce |
| **Features** | Restricted to local arc context | Can use rich stack/buffer history |
| **Runtime** | $O(n^{2})$–$O(n^{3})$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal under arc-factored model | Sub-optimal (greedy) |
| **Non-projectivity** | Native support (Chu-Liu/Edmonds) | Difficult to handle |

**Preferred use cases (2 marks):**
- **MSTParser preferred:** Long sentences in morphologically rich languages (e.g., Czech, Turkish) where non-projective arcs are common.
- **Arc-Standard preferred:** Real-time applications, streaming parsing, and scenarios requiring deep contextual features.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Eisner with Chu-Liu/Edmonds:** Eisner only handles *projective* trees. Writing "Eisner for non-projective" costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the root constraint:** The MST must have **exactly one** node with no incoming edge (the root). Many students forget to enforce this.
> 3. **Skipping the contraction math:** When a cycle forms, you must show the *adjusted* edge weights explicitly. Vague "we break the cycle" explanations lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the arc-factored assumption:** If you write a model where tree score is a *product* (not sum) of arc scores, the MST reduction breaks down.
> 5. **Wrong complexity:** State $O(n^{3})$ for Eisner, not $O(n^{2})$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Box — High-Yield Points to Memorize**

- [x] **Graph-based parsing = global optimization over the set of valid dependency trees.**
- [x] **Arc-factored assumption** ⇒ tree score = sum of arc scores.
- [x] **Decoding objective:** $T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_{(h,d) \in T} s(h,d)$.
- [x] **Non-projective decoder:** Chu-Liu/Edmonds in $O(n^{2})$.
- [x] **Projective decoder:** Eisner in $O(n^{3})$ with complete/incomplete spans.
- [x] **Cycle contraction rule:** $w'(v_C, v) = w(u, v) - w(p(v), v)$ for external edges into cycle.
- [x] **Training:** Online perceptron or MIRA on $(T^{*}, T^{p})$ pairs.
- [x] **Features:** Lexical, POS, distance, context, valency, word-class.
- [x] **Root constraint:** Exactly one vertex with no incoming edge.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** MT, QA, IE, sentiment analysis, biomedical NLP.
- [x] **Comparison:** Graph-based is slower but globally optimal; transition-based is faster but greedy.
- [x] **Spanning tree =** acyclic, single-rooted, directed tree covering all $n$ vertices.
- [x] **Probabilistic view:** $P(T \mid S) \propto \exp(\text{score}(T))$ — log-linear model.
- [x] **Partition function** $Z(S)$ is constant w.r.t. $T$ ⇒ dropped during $\arg\max$.
- [x] **Arc-factored limitation:** Cannot capture inter-arc interactions (valency, coordination).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
