<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Evaluation in NLP?

**Evaluation** in Natural Language Processing is the systematic process of measuring the **quality**, **correctness**, and **effectiveness** of an NLP system (such as a translator, summarizer, or language model) against a **gold-standard reference** using standardized quantitative metrics, qualitative judgments, or human assessment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Highlight):** Evaluation determines *how good* an NLP model is. Since language is inherently ambiguous, evaluation is one of the **hardest open problems** in NLP — there is no single "perfect" metric.

## 1.2 Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Evaluation

| Aspect | Intrinsic Evaluation | Extrinsic Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures internal performance of the system on an isolated sub-task | Measures the system's contribution to a larger end-task |
| **Speed** | Fast, automated | Slow, requires integration |
| **Cost** | Cheap, reproducible | Expensive, often requires human judges |
| **Example** | BLEU score of a machine translation system | Quality of translations helping a customer-support chatbot resolve tickets |
| **KTU Focus** | **Primary focus in this module** | Mentioned for completeness |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The School Exam System"

Think of NLP evaluation like grading a student:
- **Intrinsic** = A closed-book grammar test (auto-graded by answer key) → BLEU, ROUGE, Perplexity
- **Extrinsic** = Observing how the student performs in real life (job interview) → end-user satisfaction
- **Human Evaluation** = A viva voce by a professor → gold standard but expensive

## 1.4 Why is Evaluation Hard in NLP?

> [!NOTE]
> Language is **ambiguous**, **contextual**, and **culturally variable**. A perfectly grammatical sentence can be semantically wrong. Therefore, *no single automated metric correlates 100% with human judgment*.

Key Challenges:
1. **One-to-many mappings** — multiple valid outputs exist for one input.
2. **Subjectivity** — human raters may disagree (inter-annotator disagreement).
3. **Domain mismatch** — a metric tuned for news fails on tweets.
4. **Reference dependency** — most metrics need reference answers.

## 1.5 Core Evaluation Metrics (Categorized)

- **Machine Translation:** **BLEU**, **METEOR**, **TER**
- **Summarization:** **ROUGE** (ROUGE-N, ROUGE-L, ROUGE-S)
- **Language Modeling:** **Perplexity**
- **Speech Recognition:** **Word Error Rate (WER)**
- **Classification:** **Precision, Recall, F-measure, Accuracy**
- **Semantic Tasks:** **Word Embedding cosine similarity, Spearman correlation**
- **Generation (Open-ended):** **Human evaluation, BERTScore**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)

Developed by **Papineni et al. (2002)**. Measures how many n-grams of the candidate (machine output) appear in any reference translation.

### Core Idea
$$\text{BLEU} = \underbrace{BP}_{\text{Brevity Penalty}} \times \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Tokenize** the candidate and reference sentences.
2. Compute **modified n-gram precision** $p_n$ for n = 1, 2, 3, 4 (typically).
3. Each candidate n-gram is *clipped* to the maximum count it has in any single reference.
4. Multiply the exponentials of weighted log precisions.
5. Apply **Brevity Penalty (BP)** to penalize overly short outputs.

## 2.2 ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation)

Designed for **summarization** (Lin, 2004). Recall-based (not just precision).

| Variant | Formula Idea |
|---|---|
| **ROUGE-N** | N-gram recall between candidate & reference |
| **ROUGE-L** | Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) based F-measure |
| **ROUGE-S** | Skip-bigram (any pair of words in sentence order) |
| **ROUGE-W** | Weighted LCS favoring longer consecutive matches |

## 2.3 METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering)

Improves on BLEU by using **stems, synonyms, and paraphrases** (Banerjee & Lavie, 2005).

$$\text{METEOR} = F_{\text{mean}} \times (1 - \text{Penalty})$$

$$F_{\text{mean}} = \frac{10 \cdot P \cdot R}{R + 9P}$$

$$\text{Penalty} = 0.5 \times \left(\frac{\text{chunks}}{\text{unigrams\_matched}}\right)^3$$

## 2.4 Perplexity (Language Models)

For a sentence $W = w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N$:

$$PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$$

$$PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log_2 P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower perplexity = better language model.** It measures how "surprised" the model is on average.

## 2.5 Word Error Rate (WER) — Speech Recognition

$$WER = \frac{S + I + D}{N}$$

Where:
- $S$ = number of **Substitutions**
- $I$ = number of **Insertions**
- $D$ = number of **Deletions**
- $N$ = total words in reference

## 2.6 Precision, Recall, F-measure (Classification)

For binary classification with classes {Positive, Negative}:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Core Formula | Range | Best Value | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLEU** | $BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | Machine Translation |
| **ROUGE-N** | $\frac{\text{matched N-grams}}{\text{ref N-grams}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | Summarization |
| **ROUGE-L (F)** | $\frac{(1+\beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | Summarization |
| **METEOR** | $F_{mean}(1 - Penalty)$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | MT, Captioning |
| **Perplexity** | $P(W)^{-1/N}$ | $[1, \infty)$ | **Lower is better** | Language Models |
| **WER** | $(S+I+D)/N$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **Lower is better** | ASR / Speech |
| **F1** | $\frac{2 P R}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | Classification |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{Total}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Closer to 1** | Balanced Classification |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BLEU** → Used by **Google Translate**, **OpenAI** to benchmark MT systems.
- **ROUGE** → Standard metric in **DUC/TAC** summarization shared tasks.
- **Perplexity** → Used to compare **GPT-2 vs GPT-3 vs GPT-4** language models.
- **WER** → Used by **Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant, Apple Siri** ASR pipelines.
- **F1** → Used in **NER, POS tagging, Sentiment Analysis** benchmarks (CoNLL).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing BLEU Score (Unigram + Bigram)

**Reference:** `"the cat is on the mat"`
**Candidate:** `"the cat sat on mat"`

### Step 1 — Tokenize

Reference tokens: `[the, cat, is, on, the, mat]`
Candidate tokens: `[the, cat, sat, on, mat]`

### Step 2 — Compute Unigram Clipped Counts

We find the **maximum count of each unigram in any single reference**, then clip candidate counts.

| Unigram | Count in Candidate | Max count in Reference | Clipped Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| cat | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| sat | 1 | 0 | **0** |
| on | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| mat | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total candidate unigrams = 5
Total clipped unigrams = 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 = **4**

### Step 3 — Modified Unigram Precision

$$p_1 = \frac{\text{clipped matches}}{\text{total candidate unigrams}} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

### Step 4 — Bigram Computation

Candidate bigrams: `(the,cat), (cat,sat), (sat,on), (on,mat)` → **4 bigrams**
Reference bigrams: `(the,cat), (cat,is), (is,on), (on,the), (the,mat)` → **5 bigrams**

| Bigram | In Reference? | Clipped |
|---|---|---|
| (the,cat) | Yes (1) | 1 |
| (cat,sat) | No | 0 |
| (sat,on) | No | 0 |
| (on,mat) | No | 0 |

Total bigram matches = **1**

$$p_2 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

### Step 5 — Compute BLEU (with $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$, N=2)

$$\text{BLEU} = BP \times \exp\left(0.5 \log p_1 + 0.5 \log p_2\right)$$

$$\log p_1 = \log(0.8) = -0.2231$$
$$\log p_2 = \log(0.25) = -1.3863$$

$$\text{Inner} = 0.5(-0.2231) + 0.5(-1.3863) = -0.8047$$

$$\exp(-0.8047) = 0.4472$$

### Step 6 — Compute Brevity Penalty

Candidate length $c = 5$, Closest reference length $r = 6$.

Since $c < r$:

$$BP = e^{1 - r/c} = e^{1 - 6/5} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

### Step 7 — Final BLEU

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = 0.8187 \times 0.4472 = 0.3662}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice: candidate omitted `"is"` and `"the"` and added `"sat"`. BLEU heavily penalizes both missing words and incorrect words.

## 3.2 Worked Example: WER Calculation

**Reference:** `"recognize speech"`
**Hypothesis (ASR output):** `"wreck a nice beach"`

| Operation | Count |
|---|---|
| Substitutions (S) | 3 |
| Insertions (I) | 1 |
| Deletions (D) | 0 |
| N (reference length) | 2 |

$$WER = \frac{3 + 1 + 0}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.0 = 200\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> WER can **exceed 100%** when there are many insertions.

## 3.3 Worked Example: ROUGE-L F-measure

**Reference:** `R = [A, B, C, D, E, F, G]`
**Candidate:** `C = [A, B, X, Y, D, E, F, G]`

Lengths: $m = 7$ (reference), $n = 8$ (candidate)

### Step 1 — Find LCS

Longest Common Subsequence: `[A, B, D, E, F, G]` → length = 6

### Step 2 — Compute Precision and Recall

$$R_{lcs} = \frac{LCS}{m} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.857$$

$$P_{lcs} = \frac{LCS}{n} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

### Step 3 — F-measure (using $\beta = 1$, i.e., $F_1$)

$$F_{lcs} = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}} = \frac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.75}{0.857 + 0.75}$$

$$F_{lcs} = \frac{1.2857}{1.6071} \approx 0.800$$

$$\boxed{F_{lcs} = 0.80}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Perplexity

Model assigns the following probabilities to a 4-word sentence:

$$P(w_1) = 0.5, \quad P(w_2 \mid w_1) = 0.4, \quad P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) = 0.3, \quad P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3) = 0.2$$

Joint probability:

$$P(W) = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.012$$

$$PP(W) = (0.012)^{-1/4} = \frac{1}{0.012^{0.25}} = \frac{1}{0.3313} \approx 3.018$$

> [!NOTE]
> Perplexity = **3.018** — meaning the model is "as confused" as if it had to choose uniformly among ~3 words at each step.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Metric

| Task | Recommended Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Translation | **BLEU + METEOR + Human** | BLEU is fast, METEOR captures synonyms |
| Extractive Summarization | **ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L** | Recall-based suits gold summaries |
| Abstractive Summarization | **ROUGE + BERTScore + Human** | n-gram match alone is insufficient |
| Language Model | **Perplexity** | Direct measure of probability assignment |
| ASR / Speech-to-Text | **WER** | Industry standard for transcription |
| NER / POS Tagging | **F1 score** | Class imbalance needs F1, not accuracy |
| Sentence Embeddings | **Spearman correlation with human scores** | Word-level distance ≠ sentence-level |
| Dialogue / Chatbots | **Human evaluation + BLEU/ROUGE (limited)** | Open-ended generation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NLP Evaluation Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NLP System Output] --> B{Reference Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Automated Metrics Engine]
    B -- No --> D[Reference-less Evaluation]
    C --> C1[BLEU]
    C --> C2[ROUGE]
    C --> C3[METEOR]
    C --> C4[WER]
    C --> E[Quantitative Score]
    D --> D1[Perplexity]
    D --> D2[BERTScore]
    D --> D3[Diversity Metrics]
    D --> F[Intrinsic Score]
    E --> G[Human Evaluation Layer]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final Quality Verdict]
    H --> I[Reporting: Model A Beats Model B]
```

## 4.2 BLEU Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Candidate Sentence] --> A2[Tokenize into n-grams]
    B1[Reference Sentences] --> B2[Tokenize into n-grams]
    A2 --> C[Count candidate n-grams]
    B2 --> D[Count max ref n-grams per n-gram]
    C --> E[Clip counts to max ref count]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute modified precision p_n]
    F --> G[Compute weighted geometric mean]
    G --> H[Compute Brevity Penalty BP]
    H --> I[Final BLEU Score]
```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix → Precision/Recall/F1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Predicted Positive] --> A1[TP]
    A --> A2[FP]
    B[Predicted Negative] --> B1[TN]
    B --> B2[FN]
    A1 --> P[Precision = TP / TP+FP]
    A1 --> R[Recall = TP / TP+FN]
    P --> F1
    R --> F1
    F1[F1 = 2PR / P+R]
```

## 4.4 WER Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Transcript] --> C[Alignment]
    B[Hypothesis ASR Output] --> C
    C --> D1[Count Substitutions S]
    C --> D2[Count Insertions I]
    C --> D3[Count Deletions D]
    D1 --> E[WER = S+I+D / N]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Output WER Percentage]
```

## 4.5 Intrinsic vs Extrinsic — Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evaluation Type] --> B[Intrinsic]
    A --> C[Extrinsic]
    B --> B1[Standalone sub-task]
    B --> B2[Fast, automated, cheap]
    B --> B3[Example: BLEU on MT]
    C --> C1[End-to-end task]
    C --> C2[Slow, human-in-the-loop, costly]
    C --> C3[Example: Chatbot user satisfaction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation in NLP with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Intrinsic Evaluation:** Measures the internal performance of an NLP component in isolation, using automated metrics against gold-standard references. It is fast, cheap, and reproducible.
  *Example:* Computing the **BLEU score** of a machine translation system against reference translations.

- **Extrinsic Evaluation:** Measures the contribution of the NLP system to a larger end-task. It requires integration with the downstream application and is usually slow and costly.
  *Example:* Measuring user satisfaction of a customer-support chatbot whose underlying engine is a machine translation system.

> Valuation tip: 1 mark for definition of each, 0.5 for the difference, 0.5 for the example each. Total = 3.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Perplexity. Why is lower perplexity considered better for a language model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Perplexity (PP)** of a sentence $W = w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N$ is defined as the inverse probability of the sentence, normalized by length:

$$PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$$

Equivalently, $PP(W) = 2^{H(W)}$, where $H(W)$ is the cross-entropy of the model on the test data in bits.

**Why lower is better:**
- Perplexity measures the average number of choices the model is "confused" among at each step.
- A perplexity of 50 means the model is as uncertain as picking uniformly among 50 words.
- A **lower perplexity** indicates the model assigns **higher probability** to the actual test sequence → better language modeling capability.

(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for explanation.)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

#### (a) Explain the BLEU metric in detail. Discuss its formula, modified n-gram precision, and Brevity Penalty. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction to BLEU (1 mark):**
BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) was proposed by Papineni et al. (2002) to evaluate machine translation systems. It compares the candidate translation against one or more reference translations using n-gram overlap. The score lies in $[0, 1]$; higher is better.

**2. Modified N-gram Precision (3 marks):**
Given a candidate translation $C$ and references $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_M$:

- For each n-gram in $C$, count the maximum number of times it appears in any **single** reference $R_j$.
- Clip the candidate count to this maximum.
- The **modified precision** is the ratio of clipped counts to the total number of n-grams in the candidate.

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{\text{ngram}' \in C} \text{Count}(\text{ngram}')}$$

This prevents gaming the metric by repeating common words (e.g., "the the the").

**3. BLEU Formula and Brevity Penalty (3 marks):**

$$\text{BLEU} = BP \times \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

- $w_n$ = weight for n-gram order n (typically $1/N$ for uniform weights; for BLEU-4, $w_n = 0.25$).
- $N$ = maximum n-gram order (usually 4).
- $p_n$ = modified n-gram precision.

**Brevity Penalty (BP):** penalizes short candidates that achieve high precision by being too brief.

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ = length of candidate, $r$ = length of the *closest* reference.

> A short translation cannot "buy" a high BLEU by simply using only high-precision n-grams.

#### (b) Compute the BLEU score (with N=2, equal weights) for the following. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**Reference 1:** `"the cat is on the mat"`
**Reference 2:** `"there is a cat on the mat"`
**Candidate:** `"the cat the cat on mat"`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tokenize (0.5 mark):**
- Ref 1: `[the, cat, is, on, the, mat]` (length 6)
- Ref 2: `[there, is, a, cat, on, the, mat]` (length 7)
- Candidate: `[the, cat, the, cat, on, mat]` (length 6)

**Step 2 — Unigram clipped counts (1.5 marks):**

| Unigram | Cand count | Max in any Ref | Clipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 2 (Ref 1) | 2 |
| cat | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| on | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| mat | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total clipped unigrams = 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = **5**
Total candidate unigrams = **6**

$$p_1 = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333$$

**Step 3 — Bigram clipped counts (1.5 marks):**
Candidate bigrams: `(the,cat), (cat,the), (the,cat), (cat,on), (on,mat)` → 5 bigrams

| Bigram | In Ref 1? | In Ref 2? | Max | Clipped |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (the,cat) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| (cat,the) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| (the,cat) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| (cat,on) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (on,mat) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total clipped bigrams = 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 = **4**

$$p_2 = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Mean (1.5 marks):**

With $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$:

$$\text{Inner} = 0.5 \log(0.8333) + 0.5 \log(0.8)$$
$$= 0.5(-0.1823) + 0.5(-0.2231) = -0.2027$$

$$e^{-0.2027} = 0.8166$$

**Step 5 — Brevity Penalty (1 mark):**
$c = 6$, closest reference $r = 6$ (Ref 1). Since $c = r$, $BP = 1$.

**Step 6 — Final BLEU (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = 1 \times 0.8166 \approx 0.817}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`

#### (a) Explain ROUGE metrics (ROUGE-N, ROUGE-L) used for summarization evaluation. Provide the formulas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. ROUGE Overview (1 mark):**
ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) was proposed by Chin-Yew Lin (2004) for evaluating automatic summarization. It is **recall-oriented** because a good summary should capture most information from the reference.

**2. ROUGE-N (2 marks):**
Measures the overlap of n-grams between candidate and reference.

$$\text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in S} \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in S} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}$$

- ROUGE-1 = unigram overlap
- ROUGE-2 = bigram overlap
- Higher ROUGE-N → better overlap with reference.

**3. ROUGE-L (3 marks):**
Uses **Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)** which allows for word-order flexibility.

Let $X$ = reference (length $m$), $Y$ = candidate (length $n$).

$$R_{lcs} = \frac{LCS(X, Y)}{m}, \quad P_{lcs} = \frac{LCS(X, Y)}{n}$$

$$F_{lcs} = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$$

Typically $\beta$ is set high (e.g., $\beta = 8$) to favor recall, since summarization benefits more from recall than precision.

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
ROUGE does not capture **semantic similarity**; synonyms, paraphrases, and abstractive rewordings are penalized. Therefore, ROUGE is **complemented by BERTScore and human evaluation** in modern setups.

#### (b) A summarization system produces the following output. Compute ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L (with $\beta = 1$). **(7 marks)**

**Reference:** `"the prime minister announced new economic reforms yesterday"`
**Candidate:** `"prime minister announced economic reforms on monday"`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tokenize (0.5 mark):**
- Ref: `[the, prime, minister, announced, new, economic, reforms, yesterday]` (m = 8)
- Cand: `[prime, minister, announced, economic, reforms, on, monday]` (n = 7)

**Step 2 — ROUGE-1 (1.5 marks):**
Common unigrams: `{prime, minister, announced, economic, reforms}` → 5

$$\text{ROUGE-1} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625$$

**Step 3 — LCS Computation (2 marks):**
Longest Common Subsequence between ref and candidate:
`[prime, minister, announced, economic, reforms]` → length = **5**

(Note: "the" appears at the start of ref only; "new", "yesterday" appear in ref only; "on", "monday" appear in candidate only.)

**Step 4 — Recall and Precision (1.5 marks):**

$$R_{lcs} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625$$

$$P_{lcs} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143$$

**Step 5 — F-measure (1.5 marks):**

With $\beta = 1$:

$$F_{lcs} = \frac{2 \times 0.625 \times 0.7143}{0.625 + 0.7143} = \frac{0.8929}{1.3393} \approx 0.667$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\text{ROUGE-1} = 0.625, \quad \text{ROUGE-L (F}_1) \approx 0.667$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Brevity Penalty** in BLEU → lose 1–2 marks. Always state $BP$ and the condition $c \le r$.
> 2. **Using precision instead of recall** in ROUGE-N → BLEU uses clipped precision; ROUGE uses recall.
> 3. **Not clipping n-gram counts** in BLEU → a candidate full of "the the the" would otherwise get an unfair high score.
> 4. **Confusing F1 in ROUGE-L with the standard F1 formula** — the $\beta$ parameter matters: high $\beta$ = recall-weighted.
> 5. **Reporting WER as a fraction, not a percentage** — examiners expect $WER \times 100$%.
> 6. **Forgetting to take the Nth root** in perplexity — common mistake. Perplexity uses $-1/N$ exponent.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Evaluation** answers *"How good is my NLP system?"* and falls into **intrinsic** (internal metric) vs **extrinsic** (end-task) categories.
- **BLEU** is the *precision-based* n-gram metric for **machine translation**; uses **modified n-gram precision** + **Brevity Penalty (BP)**.
- **ROUGE** is the *recall-based* metric for **summarization**; variants include **ROUGE-N**, **ROUGE-L (LCS-based)**, **ROUGE-S (skip-bigram)**.
- **METEOR** improves BLEU by including **stems, synonyms, and word-order penalty (fragmentation)**.
- **Perplexity** = $P(W)^{-1/N}$ — used for **language models**; **lower is better**; can be interpreted as the effective branching factor.
- **WER** = $(S+I+D)/N$ — used for **ASR/speech recognition**; can exceed 100%.
- **F1-score** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall; used for **imbalanced classification** (NER, POS tagging).
- **Human evaluation** remains the **gold standard** for open-ended generation (chatbots, dialogue, story generation).
- For **semantic similarity** in modern NLP, **BERTScore**, **MoverScore**, and **embedding-based metrics** are increasingly used.
- **Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's $\kappa$)** measures how consistent human raters are — important in dataset construction.
- Always **state the metric's range and direction** in exams (e.g., "BLEU is in [0,1], higher is better").
- The **best practice** in KTU-style project evaluations: use **multiple metrics + human evaluation** for credibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Representations - Lexical Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Word Representations - Lexical Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lexical Semantics — Formal Definition

**Lexical Semantics** is the branch of linguistics and natural language processing (NLP) concerned with the systematic study of the **meaning of words** (lexemes) and the semantic relationships that hold between them. In NLP, it provides the foundational machinery to answer:

> *"How do we represent the meaning of a word in a form that a machine can compute, compare, and learn from?"*

A **word representation** (also called a *word embedding* or *distributional vector*) is a mathematical encoding — typically a real-valued vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ — that captures the semantic, syntactic, and contextual properties of a word based on its usage in a corpus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECST862 syllabus places Word Representations and Lexical Semantics as a core building block of Module 3. Students must be able to differentiate between **sparse (one-hot, Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF)** and **dense (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText)** representations and explain their mathematical underpinnings.

## 1.2 Word Senses and Lexical Relations

A single **lemma** (e.g., *"bank"*) may map to multiple **word senses** (*"river bank"* vs *"financial bank"*). The major lexical relations are:

| Relation | Definition | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Synonymy** | Words with nearly the same meaning | *big / large* |
| **Antonymy** | Words with opposite meaning | *hot / cold* |
| **Hyponymy / Hypernymy** | IS-A relationship | *car* is-a *vehicle* |
| **Meronymy / Holonymy** | PART-OF relationship | *wheel* part-of *car* |
| **Homonymy** | Identical spelling, different meaning | *bank* (river / finance) |

## 1.3 WordNet — A Lexical Database

**WordNet** is a large lexical database of English (Nouns, Verbs, Adjectives, Adverbs) grouped into sets of cognitive synonyms called **synsets**, each representing a distinct concept. Synsets are interlinked via conceptual-semantic and lexical relations.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coordinate System" of Words

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The City Map Analogy):**
> Imagine every word in English as a **house** scattered across an imaginary city.
> - In a **one-hot encoding**, every house stands in its own vast, isolated field — no two houses can see each other. The word *"king"* and *"queen"* are as unrelated as houses on different planets.
> - In a **dense embedding**, we redesign the city so that **houses of similar meaning (semantics)** are built **close together** on a grid. *"king"* and *"queen"* now sit in the same neighborhood, while *"banana"* sits far away.
>
> The **vector coordinates** of each word's "house" are the embeddings. The mathematical distance between coordinates (e.g., **cosine similarity**) tells us how semantically similar the words are.

## 1.5 The Distributional Hypothesis

The cornerstone idea for ALL modern word representations:

> *"A word is characterized by the company it keeps."* — **J.R. Firth (1957)**

Words that appear in similar **linguistic contexts** tend to have similar meanings. This is the *distributional hypothesis*, the engine that powers TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe, and contextual embeddings (BERT).

## 1.6 Why Word Representations Matter in NLP

| Downstream Task | Role of Word Vectors |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| Sentiment Analysis | Classify by aggregating word vectors |
| Machine Translation | Align vectors across languages |
| Information Retrieval | Rank documents by query–document similarity |
| Question Answering | Match question and passage semantics |
| Named Entity Recognition | Use vectors as features for sequence labelers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Without a robust word representation, an NLP model is essentially blind — it cannot tell the difference between *"happy"* and *"unhappy"* except by sheer memorization. Dense embeddings bring **semantic generalization**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Word Representations

Word representations are broadly classified into:

```
Word Representations
├── Sparse (Classical)
│     ├── One-Hot Encoding
│     ├── Bag-of-Words (BoW)
│     └── TF-IDF
└── Dense (Neural / Distributional)
      ├── Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013)
      │     ├── CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words)
      │     └── Skip-Gram
      ├── GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014)
      └── FastText (subword-based)
```

## 2.2 One-Hot Encoding

- Vocabulary of size $V$. Each word $w_i$ is mapped to a vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \{0,1\}^V$.
- The vector has a single `1` at the index of the word and `0` everywhere else.

**Pros:** Simple, interpretable.  
**Cons:** No notion of similarity; vocabulary size explodes; $V$ is often $\geq 10^5$.

## 2.3 Bag-of-Words (BoW)

Represents a document as a vector of word **counts** (or frequencies), ignoring grammar and word order.

$$\mathbf{d} = [c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_V]$$

where $c_i$ is the count of word $i$ in the document.

## 2.4 TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)

Tames BoW by **down-weighting common words** (e.g., *"the", "is"*) and **up-weighting discriminative words** (e.g., *"quantum", "photosynthesis"*).

### Derivation Logic

- **Term Frequency (TF):** $tf_{t,d} = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$  
  *(normalized frequency of term $t$ in document $d$)*
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** $idf_t = \log \dfrac{N}{df_t + 1}$  
  *(where $N$ is total docs and $df_t$ is docs containing term $t$)*
- **TF-IDF:** $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \times idf_t$

## 2.5 Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)

Used to find **co-occurrence statistics** between words. Given a target word $w$ and a context word $c$:

$$\text{PMI}(w, c) = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w, c)}{P(w) \cdot P(c)}$$

A common smoothing variant is **Positive PMI (PPMI):**

$$\text{PPMI}(w, c) = \max\!\left(0,\; \log_2 \dfrac{P(w, c)}{P(w) \cdot P(c)}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> PPMI is the conceptual bridge between classical sparse counts and dense embeddings. Word2Vec/GloVe are essentially *neural factorizations* of the PPMI matrix.

## 2.6 Cosine Similarity

For two vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, similarity is measured as the cosine of the angle between them:

$$\text{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\lVert \mathbf{u} \rVert_2 \cdot \lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2}$$

Range: $-1$ (opposite) to $+1$ (identical direction). For non-negative vectors (like TF-IDF), range is $[0,1]$.

## 2.7 Word2Vec — Mikolov et al. (2013)

Word2Vec produces **dense, low-dimensional** embeddings (typically $d = 50$ to $300$). Two architectures:

### (a) CBOW — Continuous Bag of Words
Predicts the **target (center) word** from a window of **context words**.

### (b) Skip-Gram
Predicts the **context words** given a **single target (center) word**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU must-know:** The Skip-Gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) objective is functionally equivalent to **factorizing a word–context PMI matrix shifted by $\log k$**, where $k$ is the number of negative samples. This is a favourite viva and 14-marker question.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| One-Hot Vector | $\mathbf{e}_i \in \{0,1\}^V$, $\sum e_{ij} = 1$ | Dimension = Vocab size |
| Term Frequency | $tf_{t,d} = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $idf_t = \log \dfrac{N}{df_t + 1}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot idf_t$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| PMI | $\text{PMI}(w,c) = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w,c)}{P(w) \cdot P(c)}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| PPMI | $\text{PPMI}(w,c) = \max\!\left(0,\; \log_2 \dfrac{P(w,c)}{P(w) \cdot P(c)}\right)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\lVert \mathbf{u} \rVert_2 \cdot \lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| SGNS Factorization | $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{c} \approx \text{PMI}(w,c) - \log k$ | — |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing): Use TF-IDF + dense embeddings for ranking.
- **Recommendation Systems**: Item embeddings analogous to word embeddings (item2vec).
- **Bioinformatics**: Protein embeddings via Word2Vec on amino-acid sequences.
- **Cybersecurity**: Log-message embeddings for anomaly detection.
- **Customer Support**: Semantic similarity for query routing and FAQ matching.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TF-IDF Calculation (Exhaustive)

**Given:** A toy corpus of $N = 3$ documents:
- $D_1$: *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $D_2$: *"the dog sat on the log"*
- $D_3$: *"cats and dogs are pets"*

**Task:** Compute the TF-IDF weight for the term *"sat"* in document $D_1$.

### Step 1 — Build the Vocabulary

Unique terms (lowercased, punctuation stripped):
$$V = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, log, cats, and, dogs, are, pets}\}$$
$$|V| = 12$$

### Step 2 — Compute Term Frequency $tf(\text{sat}, D_1)$

Term *"sat"* appears $f_{\text{sat},D_1} = 1$ time in $D_1$. Total terms in $D_1 = 6$.

$$tf_{\text{sat},D_1} = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

### Step 3 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency $idf(\text{sat})$

Number of documents containing *"sat"*: $df_{\text{sat}} = 2$ (both $D_1$ and $D_2$). Total documents $N = 3$.

$$idf_{\text{sat}} = \log \dfrac{N}{df_{\text{sat}} + 1} = \log \dfrac{3}{2+1} = \log(1) = 0$$

> **Result:** $idf_{\text{sat}} = 0$. This is interesting — *"sat"* is **not very discriminative** because it appears in 2 out of 3 documents. The `+1` inside the log is the **additive smoothing** to prevent division by zero.

### Step 4 — Compute TF-IDF

$$w_{\text{sat},D_1} = tf_{\text{sat},D_1} \times idf_{\text{sat}} = 0.1667 \times 0 = 0$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

The word *"sat"* receives a TF-IDF weight of exactly **0** because it fails to discriminate documents in this small corpus. This is the **central purpose of IDF**: penalize frequent, non-discriminative terms.

### Verification — A Discriminative Term

Now compute TF-IDF for *"mat"* in $D_1$:
- $f_{\text{mat},D_1} = 1$, $D_1$ has 6 terms ⇒ $tf_{\text{mat},D_1} = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$
- $df_{\text{mat}} = 1$ (only in $D_1$)
- $idf_{\text{mat}} = \log\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1+1}\right) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.1761$
- $w_{\text{mat},D_1} = 0.1667 \times 0.1761 \approx 0.0293$

> *"mat"* is **discriminative** (only in one document) and gets a non-zero TF-IDF weight.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cosine Similarity (Exhaustive)

**Given two TF-IDF vectors** (over a 3-word vocabulary):
$$\mathbf{u} = (0.5,\; 0.0,\; 0.8), \quad \mathbf{v} = (0.4,\; 0.2,\; 0.7)$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Dot Product

$$\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (0.5)(0.4) + (0.0)(0.2) + (0.8)(0.7) = 0.20 + 0.00 + 0.56 = 0.76$$

### Step 2 — Compute the L2 Norms

$$\lVert \mathbf{u} \rVert_2 = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.0^2 + 0.8^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 0.64} = \sqrt{0.89} \approx 0.9434$$

$$\lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2 = \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.7^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.04 + 0.49} = \sqrt{0.69} \approx 0.8307$$

### Step 3 — Compute Cosine Similarity

$$\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{0.76}{0.9434 \times 0.8307} = \dfrac{0.76}{0.7836} \approx 0.9700$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

A similarity of **0.97** is very high — the two documents are semantically nearly identical along these three dimensions. KTU students must remember: **Cosine measures orientation, not magnitude**, so it is the standard similarity metric for NLP.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PMI / PPMI (Exhaustive)

**Given:** From a corpus, we extract the following counts:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Total co-occurrences $\sum_{w,c} N(w,c)$ | $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| $N(\text{king}, \text{crown})$ | $700$ |
| $N(\text{king})$ | $5{,}000$ |
| $N(\text{crown})$ | $10{,}000$ |

### Step 1 — Compute Joint Probability

$$P(\text{king}, \text{crown}) = \dfrac{N(\text{king}, \text{crown})}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.0007$$

### Step 2 — Compute Marginal Probabilities

$$P(\text{king}) = \dfrac{5{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.005$$

$$P(\text{crown}) = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.01$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Product of Marginals

$$P(\text{king}) \cdot P(\text{crown}) = 0.005 \times 0.01 = 0.00005$$

### Step 4 — Compute PMI

$$\text{PMI}(\text{king}, \text{crown}) = \log_2 \dfrac{0.0007}{0.00005} = \log_2(14) \approx 3.807 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 5 — Compute PPMI

$$\text{PPMI}(\text{king}, \text{crown}) = \max(0,\; 3.807) = 3.807$$

> **Interpretation:** A high positive PMI of $\approx 3.81$ bits tells us *"king"* and *"crown"* co-occur **far more often than chance**, confirming strong semantic affinity — exactly what an embedding must capture.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Word2Vec Skip-Gram Objective (Exhaustive)

For a target word $w_t$ and a context word $c$ within a window of size $m$, the **Skip-Gram** model maximizes the log-probability of context words given the target:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-m \le j \le m,\; j \ne 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

The probability is modeled via **softmax** over the entire vocabulary:

$$P(c \mid w) = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{v}_c^{\top} \mathbf{v}_w\right)}{\sum_{c' \in V} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{c'}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_w\right)}$$

### Negative Sampling Refinement

Computing the softmax denominator over a vocabulary of millions is intractable. **Negative Sampling (SGNS)** approximates the objective by drawing $k$ negative samples $w_{\text{neg}}$ for every positive pair $(w,c)$:

$$L_{\text{SGNS}} = \log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_c^{\top} \mathbf{v}_w) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[\log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_w)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theory Gem:** Setting the gradient of the SGNS loss to zero yields the celebrated result:  
> $$\mathbf{v}_w \cdot \mathbf{v}_c \;=\; \text{PMI}(w, c) - \log k$$  
> Hence SGNS is mathematically equivalent to **implicitly factorizing the (shifted) PPMI matrix** of word–context co-occurrences. This is the *exact* link between classical distributional semantics and modern neural embeddings.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Representations

| Property | One-Hot | BoW | TF-IDF | Word2Vec / GloVe |
|----------|---------|-----|--------|------------------|
| Dimensionality | $V$ (high) | $V$ (high) | $V$ (high) | $d \ll V$ (e.g., 300) |
| Sparsity | $100\%$ | High | High | Dense |
| Captures Similarity | No | Weak | Weak | Yes (semantic + syntactic) |
| Context-aware | No | No | No | Window-based (yes, shallow) |
| Handles OOV | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (Yes for FastText) |
| Computationally Cheap | Yes | Yes | Yes | Costlier (training) |
| Downstream Use | Baseline | Spam filter, baseline | Information retrieval | Translation, QA, classification |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Word Representation Pipeline

This figure traces a corpus from raw text to dense embeddings, summarizing the entire Module 3 journey.

## 4.2 Architecture — CBOW vs Skip-Gram (Word2Vec)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CB["CBOW Architecture"]
        direction LR
        cb_in1[Context w t-2] --> cb_sum[Sum or Average]
        cb_in2[Context w t-1] --> cb_sum
        cb_in3[Context w t+1] --> cb_sum
        cb_in4[Context w t+2] --> cb_sum
        cb_sum --> cb_hidden[Hidden Layer of size d]
        cb_hidden --> cb_out[Output Softmax over V]
        cb_out --> cb_pred[Predicted Center Word w t]
    end

    subgraph SG["Skip-Gram Architecture"]
        direction LR
        sg_in[Input Center Word w t] --> sg_hidden2[Hidden Layer of size d]
        sg_hidden2 --> sg_out1[Predict Context w t-2]
        sg_hidden2 --> sg_out2[Predict Context w t-1]
        sg_hidden2 --> sg_out3[Predict Context w t+1]
        sg_hidden2 --> sg_out4[Predict Context w t+2]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Negative Sampling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Positive Pair: target w and true context c] --> L1[Compute log sigmoid of v_c dot v_w]
    N1[Negative Sample 1 from Unigram] --> L2[Compute log sigmoid of minus v_n1 dot v_w]
    N2[Negative Sample 2 from Unigram] --> L2
    Nk[Negative Sample k from Unigram] --> L2
    L1 --> SUM[Sum Objective]
    L2 --> SUM
    SUM --> BP[Backprop Update v_w and v_c]
    BP --> OUT[Updated Embedding Matrix]
```
