> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The standard architecture is: **Input → Embedding Lookup → Average (or Sum) → Dense (ReLU) → Softmax**. This is the canonical *Deep Average Network (DAN)* baseline for text classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Contextual Word Embeddings — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Why Static Embeddings Fail
In Word2Vec/GloVe, the embedding lookup is:
$$\mathbf{e}_{w} = \mathbf{E}[w]$$
where $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ is the embedding matrix. This is a *deterministic, context-free* lookup. The same word type $w$ always returns the same row.

**Polysemy Problem:** *"The bank of the river"* vs. *"I deposited money in the bank"* — semantically different uses, identical vectors.

### 2.1.2 ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models)
ELMo computes contextual embeddings using a **deep bi-directional LSTM** trained on a forward and backward language modelling objective.

**Forward Language Model:**
$$p(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} p(t_k \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{k-1})$$

**Backward Language Model:**
$$p(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} p(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, t_{k+2}, \dots, t_N)$$

**ELMo combines all biLM layers:**
$$\mathbf{ELMo}_k^{task} = \gamma^{task} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} \cdot \mathbf{h}_{k,j}$$
where:
- $\mathbf{h}_{k,0}$ = token embedding (context-independent)
- $\mathbf{h}_{k,j}$ for $j \geq 1$ = hidden states of biLM layer $j$
- $s_j^{task}$ = softmax-normalized layer weights (learned per task)
- $\gamma^{task}$ = scalar scaling parameter

### 2.1.3 BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)
BERT uses a **Transformer encoder** with **Masked Language Modelling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** as pre-training objectives. Context is encoded jointly using self-attention over the entire sequence.

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathrm{Transformer\_Encoder}(\mathbf{X})$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, focus on the **conceptual difference** between static and contextual, and the **ELMo layer-weighting formula**. You do not need to derive transformer self-attention.

---

## 2.2 Evaluating Vector Models — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Intrinsic Evaluation
Tests the embedding space directly against human-annotated datasets.

**(a) Word Similarity:**
- **Dataset:** WordSim-353, SimLex-999, MEN, RG-65
- **Procedure:** Compute cosine similarity between word pairs; compare ranking with human rankings using **Spearman correlation (ρ)**.

**(b) Word Analogy:**
- **Task:** Given a pair *a : b*, find $c$ such that *c : d* holds the same relation.
- **Example:** *man : king :: woman : ?*
- **Method:** Use the **3CosAdd** algorithm:
$$\mathbf{d} = \arg\max_{w \in V} \cos(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}, \mathbf{w})$$

**(c) Categorization (Clustering):**
- Group embeddings into semantic categories; measure **purity** or **Normalized Mutual Information (NMI)**.

### 2.2.2 Extrinsic Evaluation
Embeddings are used as features in downstream tasks and the task metric (accuracy, F1, BLEU) is reported.

| Task | Typical Metric |
|------|----------------|
| Named Entity Recognition (NER) | F1-score |
| Sentiment Classification | Accuracy / F1 |
| Part-of-Speech Tagging | Accuracy |
| Machine Translation | BLEU |
| Question Answering | Exact Match / F1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations do not always correlate! Improving word-similarity scores does NOT guarantee improvement on a downstream task. Always use the evaluation type aligned with the deployment goal.

---

## 2.3 Feedforward Neural Network for Text Classification — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Architecture Components

**Step 1 — Embedding Layer:** Map each token $t_i$ to $\mathbf{e}_{t_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

**Step 2 — Aggregation (Average Pooling):**
$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{t_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$
This produces a fixed-size representation regardless of input length — a *Deep Average Network (DAN)*.

**Step 3 — Hidden Layer with Nonlinearity:**
$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1), \quad \mathbf{W}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$$
where $\sigma$ is typically $\mathrm{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ or $\tanh$.

**Step 4 — Output Layer (Softmax):**
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2, \quad \mathbf{W}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times h}$$
$$\hat{y}_c = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{z})_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

### 2.3.2 Loss Function
**Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** (with one-hot ground truth $\mathbf{y}$):
$$\mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y}) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c) = -\log(\hat{y}_{c^*})$$
where $c^*$ is the true class index.

### 2.3.3 Training: Backpropagation
Compute gradients of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{W}_2, \mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2$ using the chain rule, and update via **SGD/Adam**:
$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Symbols |
|---|---------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{e}_w = f_\theta(w, c_{1:n})$ | Contextual embedding | $f_\theta$: encoder, $c_{1:n}$: context |
| 2 | $\mathbf{ELMo}_k = \gamma \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j \mathbf{h}_{k,j}$ | ELMo task-weighted combination | $\gamma$: scale, $s_j$: softmax weights |
| 3 | $\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert \, \vert\vert \mathbf{v} \vert\vert}$ | Cosine similarity | range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 4 | $\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$ | Spearman rank correlation (ties-corrected variant used in practice) | $d_i$: rank diff |
| 5 | $\mathbf{d}^* = \arg\max_w \cos(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}, \mathbf{w})$ | 3CosAdd analogy | Word2Vec analogy |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{t_i}$ | Average pooling for text → vector | DAN |
| 7 | $\mathrm{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Hidden-layer activation | Hidden layer |
| 8 | $\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Softmax over $C$ classes | Output layer |
| 9 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | Cross-entropy loss | One-hot $\mathbf{y}$ |
| 10 | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Gradient descent update | $\eta$: learning rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are **mathematically verifiable** and form the KTU 2024 high-yield set. Memorize the table — Part A questions in ESE frequently test formulas 3, 4, 6, 8, 9.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Contextual embeddings** power Google Search, BERT-based chatbots, and modern sentiment analysis at scale.
- **Vector evaluation** is standard in industrial NLP pipelines (e.g., Cohere, OpenAI evals) for A/B testing embedding models.
- **FFNN text classifiers** are the workhorse baseline in spam detection, topic labelling, and content moderation at firms like Twitter/X, Meta, and news aggregators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Similarity Used in Word-Similarity Evaluation

**Problem Setup:** Given two word embedding vectors $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \dots, u_d)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_d)$, derive a measure of their semantic similarity.

**Step 1 — Start with the vector dot product:**
$$\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i$$

**Step 2 — Recognize its dependence on magnitude.** Two vectors of identical direction but different lengths will have different dot products, even though they are semantically "the same direction". We must normalize.

**Step 3 — Compute the Euclidean norms:**
$$\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i^2}, \quad \vert\vert \mathbf{v} \vert\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the dot product by both norms:**
$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert \, \vert\vert \mathbf{v} \vert\vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_i^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the geometric meaning.** By the law of cosines, this equals $\cos \theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the two vectors. Identical vectors $\Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$; orthogonal $\Rightarrow 0$; opposite $\Rightarrow -1$.

**Step 6 — Conclude the final evaluable quantity:**
$$\boxed{\mathrm{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} u_i v_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i u_i^2 \sum_i v_i^2}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Spearman Rank Correlation for Evaluation

**Problem Setup:** Given $n$ word pairs, model rankings $r_1^{M}, \dots, r_n^{M}$ and human rankings $r_1^{H}, \dots, r_n^{H}$, derive a correlation measure.

**Step 1 — Compute the rank differences:** $d_i = r_i^{M} - r_i^{H}$.

**Step 2 — Square the differences and sum:** $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Spearman formula:**
$$\rho = 1 - \frac{6 S}{n(n^2 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{6 \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $\rho = 1$ means perfect rank agreement; $\rho = 0$ means no rank correlation; $\rho = -1$ means anti-correlation.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *simple* Spearman formula. For tied ranks, a Pearson-on-ranks correction is applied. KTU 2024 ESE expects the formula above; the tie-corrected variant is bonus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Softmax Activation

**Problem Setup:** Convert a vector of raw scores $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, \dots, z_C)$ into a probability distribution over $C$ classes, with two properties: (i) outputs are non-negative, (ii) outputs sum to 1.

**Step 1 — Apply the exponential function to ensure positivity:**
$$e^{z_c} > 0 \quad \forall c$$

**Step 2 — Normalize by the total sum to ensure probability sum = 1:**
$$\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify:** $\sum_{c=1}^{C} \hat{y}_c = \frac{\sum_c e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} = 1$. ✓

**Step 4 — Verify positivity:** $e^{z_c} > 0$ for all real $z_c$. ✓

**Step 5 — Final boxed form:**
$$\boxed{\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for Text Classification

**Problem Setup:** Given predicted distribution $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = (\hat{y}_1, \dots, \hat{y}_C)$ and one-hot ground truth $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_C)$ with $y_{c^*} = 1$, derive a loss penalizing wrong predictions.

**Step 1 — Use the principle of Maximum Likelihood Estimation.** Probability of the correct class under the model is $\hat{y}_{c^*}$.

**Step 2 — Take the negative log-likelihood:**
$$\mathcal{L} = -\log P(\text{correct class} \mid \mathbf{x}) = -\log \hat{y}_{c^*}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize using the one-hot vector:**
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$

**Step 4 — Verify edge cases:** If $\hat{y}_{c^*} \to 1$, $\mathcal{L} \to 0$ (perfect). If $\hat{y}_{c^*} \to 0$, $\mathcal{L} \to \infty$ (heavy penalty).

**Step 5 — Boxed form:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y}) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Forward Pass Through FFNN for Text Classification

**Problem Setup:** Input sentence with $N$ tokens, vocabulary $V$, embedding dimension $d$, hidden size $h$, output classes $C$.

**Step 1 — Embedding lookup:** For each token $t_i$, retrieve $\mathbf{e}_{t_i} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ from embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$.

**Step 2 — Average pooling (Deep Average Network):**
$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{t_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

**Step 3 — Hidden layer linear transform:**
$$\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{h}, \quad \mathbf{W}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$$

**Step 4 — Apply nonlinearity (ReLU):**
$$\mathbf{h} = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathbf{a}) = \max(0, \mathbf{a}) \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$$

**Step 5 — Output layer linear transform:**
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C}, \quad \mathbf{W}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times h}$$

**Step 6 — Softmax for class probabilities:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{z}) \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$$

**Step 7 — Loss computation:**
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$

**Step 8 — Full forward pass in one line:**
$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_2 \, \mathrm{ReLU}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_1 \left[\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{E}[t_i]\right] + \mathbf{b}_1\right) + \mathbf{b}_2\right)}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: 3-Class Text Classification

Suppose we have a 4-token sentence with embeddings of dimension $d=3$:
$$\mathbf{e}_{t_1} = (0.1, 0.2, 0.3), \quad \mathbf{e}_{t_2} = (0.4, 0.5, 0.6), \quad \mathbf{e}_{t_3} = (0.2, 0.1, 0.0), \quad \mathbf{e}_{t_4} = (0.3, 0.4, 0.5)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the average embedding:**
$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{4}((0.1+0.4+0.2+0.3), (0.2+0.5+0.1+0.4), (0.3+0.6+0.0+0.5)) = (0.25, 0.30, 0.35)$$

**Step 2 — Define hidden weights** (assume $h=2$):
$$\mathbf{W}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 1.0 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}_1 = (0.1, -0.2)$$

**Step 3 — Compute pre-activation:**
$$\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 = (1.0(0.25) + 0.5(0.30) - 0.5(0.35) + 0.1, \; -0.5(0.25) + 1.0(0.30) + 0.5(0.35) - 0.2)$$

$$\mathbf{a} = (0.25 + 0.15 - 0.175 + 0.1, \; -0.125 + 0.30 + 0.175 - 0.2) = (0.325, 0.150)$$

**Step 4 — Apply ReLU:**
$$\mathbf{h} = \mathrm{ReLU}(0.325, 0.150) = (0.325, 0.150)$$

**Step 5 — Define output weights** (3 classes, $C=3$):
$$\mathbf{W}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 1.0 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}_2 = (0.0, 0.0, 0.0)$$

**Step 6 — Compute logits:**
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2 = (0.325, 0.150, 0.5 \cdot 0.325 + 0.5 \cdot 0.150) = (0.325, 0.150, 0.2375)$$

**Step 7 — Apply softmax (numerically):**
$$e^{0.325} \approx 1.384, \quad e^{0.150} \approx 1.162, \quad e^{0.2375} \approx 1.268$$
$$\sum_j e^{z_j} \approx 1.384 + 1.162 + 1.268 = 3.814$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \left(\frac{1.384}{3.814}, \frac{1.162}{3.814}, \frac{1.268}{3.814}\right) \approx (0.363, 0.305, 0.332)$$

**Step 8 — If true class is 1** (one-hot $\mathbf{y}=(1,0,0)$), the loss is:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\log(0.363) \approx 1.013$$

This represents a typical FFNN forward pass for text classification.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Static vs Contextual Embeddings

| Property | Static (Word2Vec/GloVe) | Contextual (ELMo/BERT) |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Polysemy handling | ❌ Single vector per word | ✅ Vector depends on context |
| Training cost | Low | High |
| Inference speed | Fast (one lookup) | Slower (full forward pass) |
| Out-of-vocab handling | UNK token only | Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) |
| Layer-wise information | None | Multiple layers capture different linguistic info |
| Use in FFNN classifier | Yes (averaged) | Yes (pooled from top layer) |
| State-of-the-art (2024) | Largely replaced | Industry standard |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between static word embeddings (Word2Vec/GloVe) and contextual word embeddings (ELMo/BERT). Give one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Embeddings (Word2Vec/GloVe) | Contextual Embeddings (ELMo/BERT) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Vector assignment | One fixed vector per word type | Vector depends on surrounding context |
| Polysemy | Cannot distinguish different meanings of same word | Can distinguish polysemous senses |
| Architecture | Shallow (lookup table) | Deep (Bi-LSTM or Transformer) |

**Advantage of static:** Computationally efficient; simple to train and deploy for low-resource scenarios. (1 mark)

**Advantage of contextual:** Handles polysemy and captures context-dependent semantics. (1 mark)

**Key distinguishing line:** In static embeddings, $\mathbf{e}_{bank}$ is the same in *"river bank"* and *"money bank"*, but in ELMo/BERT, the vectors differ. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is cosine similarity? Why is it preferred over Euclidean distance for evaluating word embeddings?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Cosine similarity between two vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ is the cosine of the angle between them:
$$\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert \, \vert\vert \mathbf{v} \vert\vert}$$
It produces a value in $[-1, 1]$, where $1$ indicates identical direction.

**Why preferred over Euclidean distance (1.5 marks):**
1. **Magnitude invariance:** Word frequency biases embedding norms in static models. Cosine similarity ignores magnitude and only measures direction, which correlates better with semantic similarity.
2. **Bounded range $[-1, 1]$:** Easier to interpret and threshold.
3. **Standard practice:** Datasets like WordSim-353 and SimLex-999 are designed for cosine-based evaluation.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Module 3 Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of ELMo. How does it generate contextual embeddings for a word? Discuss the role of the bi-directional language model and the task-specific layer weighting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a Feedforward Neural Network for text classification. Explain the role of the embedding layer, average pooling layer, hidden layer with ReLU, and the softmax output layer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models) is a deep contextual word representation model proposed by Peters et al. (2018). Unlike static embeddings, ELMo generates a different vector for a word depending on the sentence in which it appears.

**Step 2 — Bi-directional Language Model (2 marks):**
ELMo uses a **multi-layer bi-directional LSTM** trained on a forward and backward language modeling objective.

- **Forward LM** predicts the next token: $p(t_k \mid t_1, \dots, t_{k-1})$
- **Backward LM** predicts the previous token: $p(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \dots, t_N)$

At each layer $j$, the forward and backward hidden states are concatenated:
$$\mathbf{h}_{k,j} = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{k,j} ; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{k,j}]$$

**Step 3 — Layer Combination (2 marks):**
ELMo combines all biLM layers (including the token embedding layer $j=0$) using a task-specific weighted sum:
$$\mathbf{ELMo}_k^{task} = \gamma^{task} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} \cdot \mathbf{h}_{k,j}$$
- $L$ = number of biLM layers (typically 2)
- $s_j^{task}$ = softmax-normalized layer weights, learned per task
- $\gamma^{task}$ = scalar scale parameter for the task

This means lower layers capture surface/syntactic features and upper layers capture semantic features — the model can weight them appropriately.

**Step 4 — Polysemy Resolution (1 mark):**
The word *"play"* in *"I play cricket"* (sports) and *"I play the piano"* (music) receives different ELMo vectors because the LSTM hidden states are conditioned on the full sentence.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
ELMo's contextual embeddings significantly outperform static GloVe on tasks like coreference resolution, NER, and sentiment analysis, making it a milestone model in the shift toward contextual NLP.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Architecture Overview (1 mark):**
A Feedforward Neural Network (FFNN) for text classification is a fully-connected neural network that takes a fixed-length vector representation of a text document as input and outputs class probabilities.

```
Input → Embedding → Average → Hidden (ReLU) → Softmax → Class Probabilities
```

**Step 2 — Embedding Layer (1 mark):**
Each token $t_i$ in the input sentence is mapped to a $d$-dimensional vector $\mathbf{e}_{t_i} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ via lookup in embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$. Pre-trained embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) or learned-from-scratch embeddings are both common.

**Step 3 — Average Pooling (1 mark):**
To handle variable-length sentences, the token embeddings are averaged:
$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{t_i}$$
This produces a fixed $d$-dimensional vector. (Note: max pooling is also an alternative.)

**Step 4 — Hidden Layer with ReLU (1.5 marks):**
The averaged vector is passed through a dense hidden layer:
$$\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 \quad (\mathbf{W}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d})$$
$$\mathbf{h} = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathbf{a}) = \max(0, \mathbf{a})$$
ReLU introduces non-linearity, enabling the network to learn complex decision boundaries. Sparse activation (negative values → 0) also helps reduce overfitting.

**Step 5 — Softmax Output Layer (1 mark):**
The hidden representation is mapped to class logits and normalized:
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{z})_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$
This gives a probability distribution over the $C$ classes.

**Step 6 — Training (1 mark):**
Cross-entropy loss is minimized via backpropagation:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$
Gradients are computed w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{W}_2, \mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2$ and parameters updated using SGD/Adam.

**Step 7 — Diagram Reference (0.5 marks):**
Reference the FFNN diagram in Section 4.3.

---

### Question B — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Module 3 Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation methods for vector models. Discuss word similarity (cosine + Spearman), word analogy (3CosAdd), and one downstream task evaluation in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a sentence "NLP models are powerful" with embeddings:
- $\mathbf{e}_{\text{NLP}} = (0.2, 0.1, 0.3)$
- $\mathbf{e}_{\text{models}} = (0.4, 0.3, 0.5)$
- $\mathbf{e}_{\text{are}} = (0.1, 0.1, 0.1)$
- $\mathbf{e}_{\text{powerful}} = (0.5, 0.4, 0.6)$

Compute the average embedding vector. Then, with hidden weights $\mathbf{W}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}_1 = (0, 0)$, output weights $\mathbf{W}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}_2 = (0, 0)$, compute the softmax output (assume binary classification). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction to Evaluation (1 mark):**
Evaluation of vector models assesses the quality of learned word embeddings. There are two main approaches: **intrinsic** (direct linguistic evaluation) and **extrinsic** (downstream task evaluation).

**Step 2 — Intrinsic Evaluation (1 mark):**
Intrinsic evaluation directly measures linguistic properties of the embedding space — word similarity, analogy, categorization. It is fast and does not require full NLP system training.

**Step 3 — Word Similarity with Cosine + Spearman (2 marks):**
- **Datasets:** WordSim-353, SimLex-999, MEN
- **Procedure:** For each word pair $(w_i, w_j)$, compute cosine similarity:
$$\cos(\mathbf{u}_i, \mathbf{u}_j) = \frac{\mathbf{u}_i \cdot \mathbf{u}_j}{\vert\vert \mathbf{u}_i \vert\vert \, \vert\vert \mathbf{u}_j \vert\vert}$$
- **Comparison with humans:** Rank the pairs by model cosine; rank by human scores. Compute **Spearman correlation** $\rho$:
$$\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \sum_i d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$
where $d_i$ is the rank difference for pair $i$. Higher $\rho$ = better alignment with human judgment.

**Step 4 — Word Analogy with 3CosAdd (1.5 marks):**
- **Task:** Given *a : b :: c : ?*, find the best $\mathbf{d}$.
- **Algorithm (3CosAdd by Mikolov):**
$$\mathbf{d}^* = \arg\max_{w \in V} \cos(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}, \mathbf{w})$$
- **Example:** *king - man + woman ≈ queen*. The model uses vector arithmetic in embedding space.
- **Metric:** Accuracy on standard analogy datasets (Google, MSR).

**Step 5 — Extrinsic Evaluation (1.5 marks):**
Extrinsic evaluation uses embeddings as input features for a downstream NLP task and measures task performance.

**Example — Sentiment Classification:**
1. Take pre-trained embeddings (Word2Vec or ELMo).
2. Use them as input to a text classifier (FFNN, LSTM, BERT-fine-tune).
3. Train classifier on labeled sentiment data.
4. Report **accuracy / F1-score** on held-out test set.

Other downstream tasks include NER (F1), POS tagging (accuracy), QA (Exact Match).

**Step 6 — Comparison (0.5 marks):**
Intrinsic evaluation is faster but may not correlate perfectly with downstream task performance. Extrinsic evaluation is more reliable for deployment but slower.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Average Embedding Computation (2 marks):**
Sum of embeddings:
- $x$-axis: $0.2 + 0.4 + 0.1 + 0.5 = 1.2$
- $y$-axis: $0.1 + 0.3 + 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.9$
- $z$-axis: $0.3 + 0.5 + 0.1 + 0.6 = 1.5$

$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{4}(1.2, 0.9, 1.5) = (0.3, 0.225, 0.375)$$

**Step 2 — Hidden Layer Pre-activation (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.225 \\ 0.375 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.225 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ReLU (0.5 marks):**
$$\mathbf{h} = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathbf{a}) = (0.3, 0.225) \quad \text{(all positive, no change)}$$

**Step 4 — Output Layer Logits (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.225 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 + 0.225 \\ 0 + 0.225 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.525 \\ 0.225 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Softmax Computation (1.5 marks):**
$$e^{0.525} \approx 1.6909, \quad e^{0.225} \approx 1.2523$$
$$\sum_j e^{z_j} \approx 1.6909 + 1.2523 = 2.9432$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \left(\frac{1.6909}{2.9432}, \frac{1.2523}{2.9432}\right) \approx (0.5745, 0.4255)$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
The model predicts class 1 with probability $\approx 0.5745$ and class 2 with probability $\approx 0.4255$. (Final summary 0.5 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Spearman and Pearson** formulas — Spearman uses *ranks*, not raw values.
> 2. **Forgetting the sum-to-one constraint** when writing softmax — always verify $\sum_c \hat{y}_c = 1$.
> 3. **Mixing up ELMo's $s_j$ (layer weights) with attention weights** — ELMo's weighting is a *post-hoc learned scalar combination*, not attention.
> 4. **In FFNN, forgetting to average embeddings** before feeding to dense layer — feeding variable-length sequences is invalid for FFNN.
> 5. **In numerical problems, omitting the ReLU step** — always apply nonlinearity before the next linear transform.
> 6. **Writing "ELMo uses self-attention"** — ELMo uses **bi-LSTM**, not transformers. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

**Contextual Word Embeddings**
- ✅ Static embeddings (Word2Vec/GloVe): one vector per word type → cannot handle polysemy
- ✅ Contextual embeddings (ELMo/BERT): vector is a function of surrounding context
- ✅ **ELMo** uses **bi-directional LSTM** trained with forward + backward language model objectives
- ✅ ELMo combines all layers: $\mathbf{ELMo}_k = \gamma \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j \mathbf{h}_{k,j}$
- ✅ $s_j$ are softmax-normalized layer weights; $\gamma$ is a scalar scaling parameter
- ✅ BERT uses **Transformer encoder** with Masked LM and Next Sentence Prediction
- ✅ GPT uses Transformer decoder (left-to-right)

**Evaluating Vector Models**
- ✅ **Intrinsic:** word similarity, word analogy, categorization
- ✅ **Word similarity:** cosine similarity + Spearman correlation vs. human scores
- ✅ **Word analogy:** 3CosAdd: $\mathbf{d}^* = \arg\max_w \cos(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}, \mathbf{w})$
- ✅ **Extrinsic:** downstream tasks like NER, sentiment, POS, QA
- ✅ Intrinsic and extrinsic results do not always correlate

**Feedforward Neural Networks for Text Classification**
- ✅ Architecture: Input → Embedding → Average → Dense(ReLU) → Softmax → Class Probabilities
- ✅ Average pooling: $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{t_i}$
- ✅ ReLU: $\mathrm{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$
- ✅ Softmax: $\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$
- ✅ Cross-entropy loss: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$
- ✅ Backpropagation + SGD/Adam for training
- ✅ Output of softmax is a **probability distribution** (sums to 1)

### Key Definitions to Memorize (verbatim for KTU)
1. **Contextual Word Embedding:** A vector representation of a word that is computed as a function of the surrounding sentence/sequence context, allowing the same word to have different vectors in different contexts.
2. **Cosine Similarity:** The cosine of the angle between two vectors, used to measure semantic similarity independent of magnitude.
3. **Spearman Correlation:** A non-parametric rank-based correlation measure used to compare model rankings with human rankings in word similarity evaluation.
4. **3CosAdd:** The vector arithmetic method $\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}$ used to solve word analogies in Word2Vec-style embeddings.
5. **Deep Average Network (DAN):** A feedforward text classifier that averages word embeddings before passing them through dense layers.
6. **Softmax:** An activation function that converts a vector of real numbers into a probability distribution over classes.

### Common KTU 2024 Exam Triggers
- If asked **"Why contextual embeddings?"** → discuss polysemy + dynamic representation
- If asked **"Compare intrinsic vs extrinsic"** → use the chess engine analogy
- If asked **"Derive cosine similarity"** → start from dot product, normalize by norms
- If asked **"FFNN for text"** → always show the **5 layers**: Embedding, Average, Dense, ReLU, Softmax
- If asked **"ELMo formula"** → write $\mathbf{ELMo}_k = \gamma \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j \mathbf{h}_{k,j}$ and explain every symbol

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Modelling - Recurrent Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequence Modelling - Recurrent Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence Modelling** is the class of machine learning techniques designed to process and predict outcomes on **sequential or temporal data**, where the current observation is statistically dependent on prior observations in the sequence. A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a specialised class of artificial neural network architecture that contains recurrent (cyclic) connections between hidden units, allowing information to persist across time-steps through an internal **hidden state memory vector**.

Formally, an RNN defines a parameterized mapping:

$$f_\theta : (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T) \mapsto (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T)$$

where $\theta = \{W, U, V, b, c\}$ represents the shared learnable parameters, $x_t$ is the input at time-step $t$, and $y_t$ is the corresponding output.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Storyteller"**
>
> Imagine you are reading a novel. To understand the meaning of the current sentence, you don't just look at the current sentence — you remember the **context of all previous sentences and paragraphs**. Your brain maintains a *running summary* of the story.
>
> An RNN behaves exactly like this storyteller:
> - The **input at time $t$** ($x_t$) = the new sentence being read.
> - The **hidden state** ($h_t$) = the running summary in your brain.
> - The **output** ($y_t$) = your understanding/emotion after reading that sentence.
>
> Every time new information arrives, your "summary" is **updated** (not erased), which is precisely what an RNN does with its hidden state vector $h_t$.

### 1.3 Why Traditional Feed-Forward Networks (FNNs) Fail on Sequences

| Limitation of FNN | Consequence for Sequence Data |
|---|---|
| Fixed-size input vector | Cannot handle variable-length sequences (sentences, audio) |
| No memory of past inputs | Loses temporal order — "dog bites man" $\neq$ "man bites dog" |
| Separate parameters per position | Parameter count explodes with sequence length |
| No parameter sharing | Cannot generalise to sequences of unseen length |

RNNs solve all four limitations via **parameter sharing across time-steps** and a **recurrent hidden state**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Time-step ($t$)**: Discrete index along the sequence ($t = 1, 2, \dots, T$).
> - **Hidden State ($h_t$)**: Compressed memory vector summarising all information up to time $t$.
> - **Cell State ($C_t$)**: Long-term memory used in LSTM cells.
> - **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time)**: Gradient computation algorithm for RNNs.
> - **TBPTT (Truncated BPTT)**: Variant that limits backpropagation to a fixed window to save compute.
> - **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient**: Numerical instability during BPTT where gradients shrink to 0 or blow up to $\infty$.
> - **Embedding**: Dense vector representation of a discrete token (e.g., word2vec, GloVe).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Computational Graph of an RNN

The two key design principles of an RNN are:

1. **Parameter Sharing** — The same matrices $W, U, V$ are used at every time-step.
2. **Recurrent Connection** — The hidden state $h_{t-1}$ is fed back into the network at time $t$.

For every time-step $t$, the RNN performs the following three core operations:

**Step 1: Hidden state update**

$$h_t = \phi_h(U \cdot x_t + W \cdot h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

**Step 2: Output computation**

$$\hat{y}_t = \phi_y(V \cdot h_t + b_y)$$

**Step 3: Loss evaluation (per time-step)**

$$L_t = \mathcal{L}(y_t, \hat{y}_t)$$

where the variables have the following roles:

- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ — Input vector of dimension $d$ at time $t$.
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ — Hidden state vector of dimension $H$ at time $t$.
- $U \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times d}$ — Input-to-hidden weight matrix.
- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times H}$ — Hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight matrix.
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{O \times H}$ — Hidden-to-output weight matrix.
- $b_h, b_y$ — Bias vectors for hidden and output layers.
- $\phi_h$ — Hidden activation (typically $\tanh$ or ReLU).
- $\phi_y$ — Output activation (softmax for classification, linear for regression).
- $\mathcal{L}$ — Loss function (cross-entropy, MSE, etc.).

### 2.2 Initialisation of the Hidden State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hidden state at $t=0$, i.e., $h_0$, is a **learnable parameter** in advanced models, but in the most common KTU-board formulation it is initialised as a **zero vector** $h_0 = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^H$.

### 2.3 Types of RNN Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

| Architecture | Input-Output Pattern | NLP Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **One-to-Many** | 1 input, sequence output | Image captioning |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence input, 1 output | Sentiment classification |
| **Many-to-Many (equal)** | Sequence in, sequence out (same length) | POS tagging, Named Entity Recognition |
| **Many-to-Many (encoder-decoder)** | Sequence in, sequence out (different length) | Machine Translation, Summarisation |
| **Bidirectional** | Past + future context | Speech recognition, NER |

### 2.4 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Core Logic

The total loss across a sequence of length $T$ is:

$$L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

The gradient of $L_{\text{total}}$ with respect to a parameter $W$ (or $U$, $V$) is computed by the chain rule applied *across both depth (network layers) and time*:

$$\frac{\partial L_{\text{total}}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}$$

The crux is the **Jacobian chain** $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k}$, which expands as:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

This multiplicative product is the **mathematical root cause** of vanishing and exploding gradients.

### 2.5 The Vanishing & Exploding Gradient Problem

> [!WARNING]
> The Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ is governed by the spectral radius $\rho$ of $W$ times the derivative of $\phi_h$. If $\rho > 1$ → **explosion**. If $\rho < 1$ → **vanishing**. For $\tanh$, the maximum derivative is $1$ and for long sequences the product decays to zero, making the network **unable to learn long-range dependencies**.

**Numerical illustration**: If $\rho = 0.5$ and $t - k = 100$, then $\rho^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{-31}$, essentially zero.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER REFERENCE TABLE — Memorise this for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meanings | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_t = \phi_h(Ux_t + Wh_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Forward pass of hidden state | All RNNs |
| 2 | $\hat{y}_t = \phi_y(Vh_t + b_y)$ | Output computation | All RNNs |
| 3 | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ | Total sequence loss | BPTT |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{k \leq t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t}\prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}$ | Full BPTT gradient | Backprop |
| 5 | $\rho(W) < 1 \Rightarrow$ Vanishing; $\rho(W) > 1 \Rightarrow$ Exploding | Spectral radius test | Stability |
| 6 | LSTM Forget Gate: $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Controls cell-state retention | LSTM |
| 7 | LSTM Input Gate: $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Controls new-info writing | LSTM |
| 8 | LSTM Output Gate: $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Controls cell-state exposure | LSTM |
| 9 | LSTM Cell Update: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Long-term memory update | LSTM |
| 10 | GRU Update Gate: $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Interpolates old vs new | GRU |
| 11 | GRU Reset Gate: $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Controls prior memory use | GRU |
| 12 | Bi-RNN: $\vec{h_t}, \overleftarrow{h_t}$; $h_t = [\vec{h_t};\overleftarrow{h_t}]$ | Forward + backward context | Bi-RNN |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

RNNs are foundational for:
- **Speech Recognition** (Google Assistant, Siri)
- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate, DeepL)
- **Time-series forecasting** (stock prices, weather)
- **Music generation** (Jukebox by OpenAI)
- **DNA sequence analysis** (Bioinformatics)
- **Video frame prediction** (autonomous driving perception)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Forward Pass for a 3-Time-step RNN

Consider a sequence $x_1, x_2, x_3$ with targets $y_1, y_2, y_3$. We unfold the RNN across three time-steps. Assume $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Time-step $t = 1$:**

$$a_1 = U x_1 + W h_0 + b_h = U x_1 + W \cdot 0 + b_h = U x_1 + b_h$$

$$h_1 = \tanh(a_1)$$

$$o_1 = V h_1 + b_y$$

$$\hat{y}_1 = \text{softmax}(o_1)$$

**Time-step $t = 2$:**

$$a_2 = U x_2 + W h_1 + b_h$$

$$h_2 = \tanh(a_2)$$

$$o_2 = V h_2 + b_y, \quad \hat{y}_2 = \text{softmax}(o_2)$$

**Time-step $t = 3$:**

$$a_3 = U x_3 + W h_2 + b_h$$

$$h_3 = \tanh(a_3)$$

$$o_3 = V h_3 + b_y, \quad \hat{y}_3 = \text{softmax}(o_3)$$

**Step-by-step textual explanation of the logic:**

1. The hidden state at $t=1$ depends only on the input $x_1$ and bias $b_h$ since $h_0 = 0$.
2. The hidden state at $t=2$ depends on the current input $x_2$ **and** the previous hidden state $h_1$, which encodes all information from $x_1$.
3. The hidden state at $t=3$ depends on $x_3$ **and** $h_2$, which encodes information from $x_1$ and $x_2$.
4. Hence $h_3$ contains a compressed summary of the entire input history $\{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of BPTT for Parameter $V$ (Output Matrix)

The total loss is the sum of per-step cross-entropy:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{3} L_t = -\sum_{t=1}^{3} \sum_{i} y_{t,i} \log \hat{y}_{t,i}$$

Gradient w.r.t. $V$ (no chain through earlier time-steps needed since $V$ appears only locally in $\hat{y}_t$):

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial V}$$

For each $t$:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial V} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial o_t} \cdot \frac{\partial o_t}{\partial V}$$

With softmax + cross-entropy, $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$ (the standard result). So:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial V} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t) h_t^{\top}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} (\hat{y}_t - y_t) h_t^{\top}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip**: The above compact form is the *exact* answer KTU examiners expect for the gradient of $V$. Showing the chain-rule decomposition earns full marks.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of BPTT for Parameter $W$ (Recurrent Matrix)

Here the chain rule is more involved because $W$ appears in *every* time-step.

**Step 1**: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$

**Step 2**: Focus on $\frac{\partial L_3}{\partial W}$ (the loss at $t=3$):

$$\frac{\partial L_3}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial L_3}{\partial h_3} \cdot \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial W}$$

But $h_3$ depends on $h_2$, which itself depends on $W$. So we must use the **total derivative**:

$$\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial W}\bigg|_{\text{direct}} + \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_2} \cdot \frac{\partial h_2}{\partial W}$$

**Step 3**: Generalising, we get the recursive form:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} \right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}$$

**Step 4**: Computing the local Jacobian for $\phi_h = \tanh$:

$$\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)\right) \cdot W$$

**Step 5**: Therefore the full gradient w.r.t. $W$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \left[ \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)\right) W \right] \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is the root of vanishing gradients**: The product $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)) W$ contains a sequence of matrix multiplications. If the largest singular value of $W$ is below $1$, the product decays exponentially with the time-gap $(t-k)$, causing **gradients of early time-steps to vanish**.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of LSTM Forward Pass

LSTM introduces three **gates** and a **cell state** $C_t$ to combat vanishing gradients.

**Step 1 — Forget Gate**: Decides what to discard from $C_{t-1}$.

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

**Step 2 — Input Gate & Candidate**: Decides what new information to store.

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Step 3 — Cell State Update**: Combines forget + input.

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 4 — Output Gate & Hidden State**:

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Textual explanation of the design logic:**

1. The **forget gate** $f_t \in [0,1]^H$ acts as a *learnable mask* on the previous cell state.
2. The **input gate** $i_t$ controls how much of the new candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ enters the memory.
3. The **additive update** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ uses **addition rather than multiplication**, which solves the vanishing-gradient problem.
4. The **output gate** $o_t$ decides which parts of $\tanh(C_t)$ become the visible hidden state.

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation of GRU Forward Pass

GRU merges the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$ and adds a **reset gate** $r_t$.

**Step 1 — Update Gate**:

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

**Step 2 — Reset Gate**:

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State**:

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State** (linear interpolation):

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Textual explanation:**

- $z_t$ close to $1$ → keep new candidate; close to $0$ → retain old state.
- $r_t$ close to $0$ → ignore previous state when computing candidate.
- GRU has **fewer parameters** than LSTM (3 gates vs 4 in LSTM), making it faster but slightly less expressive.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Question)

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell state $C_t$ | No | Yes | No (uses $h_t$ directly) |
| Memory mechanism | Single $h_t$ | $C_t + h_t$ | Single $h_t$ with interpolation |
| Vanishing gradient resistance | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| Parameter count | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Training speed | Fastest | Slowest | Medium |
| Performance on long sequences | Poor | Best | Good |

### 3.7 Mathematical Justification of Why LSTM Solves Vanishing Gradients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The gradient of the cell state w.r.t. its past values is:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$$

> This is a **diagonal matrix with entries in $[0,1]$** — there is no multiplication by $W$! The chain rule therefore yields:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_i)$$

> Even if some $f_i$ are small, the learnable forget gate can adapt so that the product does not decay exponentially, allowing **long-range credit assignment**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Folded (Recurrent) RNN Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Input xt"] --> nodeB["Hidden Layer with Recurrent Connection"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output yt"]
    nodeB -.->|"ht-1 fed back"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"ht passed to next step"| nodeB
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
- The curved arrow inside the hidden block represents the **self-loop** carrying $h_{t-1}$.
- The hidden block is **the same block** instantiated at every time-step — hence parameter sharing.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Unrolled RNN Across Time-Steps

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1["x1"] --> h1["h1"]
    h1 --> y1["y1"]
    x2["x2"] --> h2["h2"]
    h1 -->|"W"| h2
    h2 --> y2["y2"]
    x3["x3"] --> h3["h3"]
    h2 -->|"W"| h3
    h3 --> y3["y3"]
```

**Explanation:**
- Each column corresponds to one time-step.
- The horizontal arrows labelled $W$ show the **same recurrent matrix** being applied at every step (parameter sharing).
- $h_3$ contains a summary of $x_1, x_2, x_3$.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — LSTM Cell Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    xt["xt"] --> concat1["Concatenate ht-1 and xt"]
    ht1["ht-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> sigf["Sigmoid: Forget Gate ft"]
    concat1 -> sigi["Sigmoid: Input Gate it"]
    concat1 -> sigo["Sigmoid: Output Gate ot"]
    concat1 -> tanhc["Tanh: Candidate Cell Ct"]
    sigf -->|"ft"| multf["Multiply: ft * Ct-1"]
    sigi -->|"it"| multi["Multiply: it * Candidate"]
    tanhc --> multi
    Ctprev["Ct-1"] --> multf
    multf --> add["Add"]
    multi --> add
    add --> Ctnew["Ct: New Cell State"]
    Ctnew --> tanhC["Tanh"]
    sigo -->|"ot"| multo["Multiply: ot * tanh Ct"]
    tanhC --> multo
    multo --> htnew["ht: New Hidden State"]
    Ctnew --> Ctprev
    htnew --> ht1
```

**Explanation of the cell architecture:**
- The four parallel branches (sigmoid, sigmoid, sigmoid, tanh) compute the three gates and the candidate cell value.
- The **forget path** multiplies $f_t$ with $C_{t-1}$.
- The **input path** multiplies $i_t$ with $\tilde{C}_t$ and adds to the forget path.
- The **output path** filters $\tanh(C_t)$ through $o_t$ to produce $h_t$.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — GRU Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    xt["xt"] --> concat1["Concatenate ht-1 and xt"]
    ht1["ht-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> sigz["Sigmoid: Update Gate zt"]
    concat1 --> sigr["Sigmoid: Reset Gate rt"]
    ht1 -->|"rt * ht-1"| concat2["Concatenate rt-ht-1 and xt"]
    sigr --> concat2
    xt --> concat2
    concat2 --> tanhH["Tanh: Candidate h bar"]
    sigz -->|"zt"| interp["Interpolation"]
    ht1 -->|"1 - zt"| interp
    tanhH -->|"zt"| interp
    interp --> htnew["ht: New Hidden State"]
    htnew --> ht1
```

**Explanation:**
- $z_t$ decides the **trade-off** between the old hidden state and the new candidate.
- $r_t$ decides **how much of the past** is shown to the candidate computation.

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Bidirectional RNN Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1["x1"] --> fwd1["Forward h1"]
    x2["x2"] --> fwd2["Forward h2"]
    x3["x3"] --> fwd3["Forward h3"]
    fwd1 --> fwd2
    fwd2 --> fwd3
    x1 --> bwd1["Backward h1"]
    x2 --> bwd2["Backward h2"]
    x3 --> bwd3["Backward h3"]
    bwd3 --> bwd2
    bwd2 --> bwd1
    fwd3 --> concat["Concat forward and backward"]
    bwd1 --> concat
    concat --> output["Final Hidden State"]
```

**Explanation:**
- The **forward RNN** reads the sequence left-to-right.
- The **backward RNN** reads the sequence right-to-left.
- The two contexts are **concatened** to form the final representation — useful for tasks where both past and future matter (e.g., POS tagging, NER, machine translation encoders).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **Define a Recurrent Neural Network. How does it differ from a standard feed-forward neural network in handling sequential data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of neural networks designed to process sequential data by maintaining an internal **hidden state** $h_t$ that is updated at every time-step $t$ based on the current input $x_t$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$. The defining equation is:

$$h_t = \phi_h(U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

The key differences from a **feed-forward neural network (FNN)** are:

1. **Recurrent connections**: The hidden state $h_{t-1}$ is fed back into the network, creating a cycle that allows memory of past inputs.
2. **Parameter sharing across time**: The same weight matrices $U, W, V$ are used at every time-step, enabling the network to generalise to sequences of varying length.
3. **Variable-length input handling**: An RNN can process sequences of any length, while an FNN requires a fixed-size input vector.
4. **Temporal context**: The output $\hat{y}_t$ depends on the entire history $x_1, \dots, x_t$, whereas an FNN processes each input independently.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? Mention one solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** when gradients of the loss w.r.t. early time-step parameters become extremely small (close to zero), preventing the network from learning **long-range dependencies**.

**Mathematical cause**: The gradient involves a product of Jacobians across time:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)) \cdot W$$

If the spectral radius of $W$ is less than $1$, this product **decays exponentially** with the time-gap $(t-k)$, making the gradient of early parameters negligible.

**Solution**: Use **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks, which replace the multiplicative hidden-state update with an **additive cell-state update**:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This additive structure keeps gradients from vanishing across long time spans. (Other valid solutions: **GRU**, **gradient clipping**, **skip connections**, **better initialisation**, **orthogonal matrices**.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** *(a)* Explain the architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network with a neat diagram. Derive the forward propagation equations for an unrolled RNN over $T$ time-steps and clearly define all weight matrices and bias vectors. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm for computing the gradient of the total loss w.r.t. the recurrent weight matrix $W$. Show explicitly why the product structure of the gradient leads to the **vanishing gradient problem**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture (3 marks):** An RNN consists of an input layer, a hidden layer with **recurrent (cyclic) connections**, and an output layer. The same hidden layer is **unrolled** across $T$ time-steps, with each copy sharing the weight matrices $U, W, V$. (Refer to the unrolled diagram in Section 4.2.)

**Forward propagation equations (4 marks):** For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:

$$a_t = U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h \quad \text{(pre-activation)}$$

$$h_t = \tanh(a_t) \quad \text{(hidden state update)}$$

$$o_t = V h_t + b_y \quad \text{(output pre-activation)}$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t) \quad \text{(output probability distribution)}$$

**Definitions**:
- $U \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times d}$: input-to-hidden weights.
- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times H}$: hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weights.
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{O \times H}$: hidden-to-output weights.
- $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^H$, $b_y \in \mathbb{R}^O$: bias vectors.
- $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Total loss (1 mark):**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

**Gradient w.r.t. $V$ (1 mark — shown as comparison):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t) h_t^{\top}$$

**Gradient w.r.t. $W$ (3 marks):** Using the chain rule across time:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \left[ \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)\right) W \right] \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}$$

**Why vanishing gradients occur (2 marks):** The inner product $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)) W$ contains $t-k$ matrix multiplications. Each factor's norm is at most $\rho(W) \cdot \max(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)) \leq \rho(W)$. For a typical tanh-saturated regime, the norm of each factor is $\ll 1$, so the product decays as $\rho(W)^{(t-k)} \to 0$ exponentially. Hence the gradient contribution from distant past steps $(k \ll t)$ becomes negligible, and the network cannot learn dependencies across long time-gaps.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** *(a)* Explain the architecture of an LSTM cell with a block diagram. Derive the equations for the **forget gate**, **input gate**, **output gate**, candidate cell state, and final hidden state update. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare LSTM and GRU architectures in a tabular form. Show mathematically why the additive cell-state update of LSTM avoids the vanishing gradient problem, while the multiplicative update of a vanilla RNN does not. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**LSTM Architecture (3 marks):** An LSTM cell contains three sigmoid **gates** (forget, input, output), one **tanh candidate**, and a **cell state** $C_t$ that serves as long-term memory. The hidden state $h_t$ is the filtered, short-term view of the cell state. (Refer to Section 4.3 diagram.)

**Equations (4 marks):** Given input $x_t$ and previous state $(h_{t-1}, C_{t-1})$:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(forget gate)}$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(input gate)}$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \quad \text{(candidate cell)}$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(output gate)}$$

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \quad \text{(cell state update)}$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \quad \text{(hidden state)}$$

where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid, $\odot$ is element-wise multiplication, and $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes concatenation.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** Present the analytical comparison matrix from Section 3.6, covering number of gates, cell state, memory mechanism, vanishing-gradient resistance, parameter count, and training speed.

**Mathematical justification (4 marks):**

For **vanilla RNN**, the hidden state updates multiplicatively:

$$h_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b_h)$$

The gradient chain is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)) \cdot W$$

When $\rho(W) < 1$, this product decays exponentially with $(t-k)$, causing the **vanishing gradient problem**.

For **LSTM**, the cell state updates **additively**:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

The gradient w.r.t. past cell states is:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_i)$$

This is a product of **diagonal matrices with entries in $[0,1]$** — *no multiplication by $W$*. Even if some $f_i$ are small, the network can learn to keep $f_i$ close to 1, so the product does not vanish. This is the **mathematical reason** why LSTM can capture dependencies over hundreds of time-steps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias terms** $b_h$ and $b_y$ in the forward equations — deduct **1 mark**.
> 2. **Not specifying the activation function** $\phi_h$ (typically $\tanh$) — deduct **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $C_t$ (cell state) with $h_t$ (hidden state)** in LSTM derivations — KTU examiners treat this as a **conceptual error** (−1 mark).
> 4. **Writing BPTT gradient without the chain rule** — always show the product form $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ to earn full marks.
> 5. **Stating "LSTM solves vanishing gradients" without mathematical justification** — the additive cell-state update must be explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Confusing GRU update gate $z_t$ with forget gate** — remember $z_t$ in GRU is an **interpolation coefficient**, not a pure forget mask.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Revise in 5 Minutes Before Exam**

- ✅ An **RNN** processes sequences by maintaining a **hidden state** $h_t$ that is updated at every time-step.
- ✅ Forward pass: $h_t = \phi_h(Ux_t + Wh_{t-1} + b_h)$ and $\hat{y}_t = \phi_y(Vh_t + b_y)$.
- ✅ **Parameter sharing**: same $U, W, V$ at every time-step.
- ✅ **BPTT** uses the chain rule across time; the gradient involves a **product of Jacobians** from $k+1$ to $t$.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** arises when the spectral radius $\rho(W) < 1$, causing the Jacobian product to decay exponentially.
- ✅ **Exploding gradient** arises when $\rho(W) > 1$, causing the product to grow unboundedly (solved by **gradient clipping**).
- ✅ **LSTM** uses three gates (forget, input, output) and a cell state $C_t$ updated **additively** to combat vanishing gradients.
- ✅ **GRU** uses two gates (update, reset) — fewer parameters than LSTM, similar performance.
- ✅ **Bidirectional RNN** combines forward and backward passes for tasks needing full-sequence context.
- ✅ The total sequence loss is $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ — sum, not product.
- ✅ In LSTM, the gradient of cell state w.r.t. its past is $\prod \text{diag}(f_i)$ — **no $W$ multiplication**, hence no vanishing.
- ✅ **Embedding layer** is typically the first layer of an NLP RNN, converting token IDs to dense vectors.
- ✅ KTU expects you to **draw the unrolled RNN diagram** for any 7-mark architecture question.
- ✅ KTU expects the **BPTT derivation with full chain rule** for full marks on backprop questions.
- ✅ Remember to mention **orthogonal initialisation** of $W$ as a partial remedy for vanishing/exploding gradients.
- ✅ **Teacher forcing** is a training technique where ground-truth $y_{t-1}$ is fed as input at time $t$ (often used in seq2seq training).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNNs as Language Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RNNs as Language Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Recurrent Neural Network Language Model (RNN-LM)** is a neural probabilistic language model that estimates the joint probability distribution over sequences of words by factorizing it into a product of conditional probabilities, where each conditional probability is computed using a recurrent hidden state that summarizes the history of all previous tokens in the sequence.

Formally, for a token sequence $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})$$

The recurrent architecture parameterizes each conditional term $P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})$ using a hidden state vector $h_t$ that is recursively updated at every time step $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RNNs as Language Models form the foundational bridge between classical N-gram models and modern transformer-based (attention-based) language models. Mastering this topic is mandatory before studying Seq2Seq, LSTMs, GRUs, and pre-trained representations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Storyteller with Short-Term Memory"

Imagine a person listening to a story being read aloud. They cannot remember every single word that was spoken since the beginning (long-term memory loss), but they maintain a **running mental summary** of what has happened so far. At each new word, they:

1. **Listen** to the next word (input $x_t$).
2. **Update** their mental summary (hidden state $h_t$).
3. **Predict** the most likely next word in the story (output $y_t$).

This is exactly how an RNN-LM operates — the hidden state $h_t$ acts as a **compressed, lossy memory** of all prior context, allowing the network to make predictions one token at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from N-grams:** Traditional N-gram models (e.g., trigrams) can only "see" a fixed window of $N-1$ previous words. RNN-LMs, in theory, can leverage **arbitrarily long histories** through their recurrent hidden state — a major advantage that motivated the shift toward neural language modeling.

### 1.3 Why RNNs for Language Modeling?

| Property | N-gram Models | RNN Language Models |
|---|---|---|
| Context window | Fixed ($N-1$) | Theoretically unbounded |
| Parameter sharing across positions | No (each n-gram has its own count) | Yes (shared weights $W, U, V$) |
| Handles unseen n-grams | Poorly (smoothing required) | Naturally generalizes via embeddings |
| Sparsity of data | Severe (curse of dimensionality) | Distributed representations |
| Sequential inference speed | Fast | Slower (cannot parallelize across time) |

### 1.4 Standard Evaluation Metric: Perplexity

The standard evaluation metric for any language model (including RNN-LMs) is **Perplexity (PPL)**. Lower perplexity indicates a better language model.

$$\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T)^{-1/T}$$

For an RNN-LM, perplexity is computed as the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy loss) over the test corpus.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always quote the formula in exponential form: $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} -\log P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})\right)$. This is the most frequently tested 2-mark formula in NLP papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of an RNN-LM

The end-to-end operation of a Recurrent Neural Network Language Model follows these structured steps:

1. **Input Encoding (Embedding Lookup):** Each word $w_t$ at time step $t$ is converted into a dense, real-valued vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$ via an embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d_e}$, where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size and $d_e$ is the embedding dimension.
2. **Hidden State Update:** The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ and the current input embedding $x_t$ are combined through an affine transformation followed by a non-linear activation (typically $\tanh$ or ReLU):
   $$h_t = f_h(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b_h)$$
3. **Output Projection:** The hidden state $h_t$ is projected to a vector of vocabulary-sized logits $o_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$:
   $$o_t = U h_t + b_o$$
4. **Softmax Normalization:** The logits are converted to a probability distribution over the next word using the softmax function:
   $$P(w_{t+1} = k \mid w_{1:t}) = \frac{\exp(o_t^{(k)})}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(o_t^{(j)})}$$
5. **Loss Computation:** Cross-entropy loss is computed between predicted distribution and the actual next word.
6. **Parameter Update via Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT):** Gradients flow backward through the unrolled network across all $T$ time steps.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Recurrence

The "Why": At every time step $t$, the model must base its prediction on *all* prior words $w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}$. Without recurrence, the network would require a separate input layer of fixed size for each context length — infeasible for real text. Recurrence allows the *same* set of parameters $(W_h, W_x, U)$ to be reused at every time step, theoretically capturing dependencies of any length while keeping the parameter count fixed.

### 2.3 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — The Core Training Algorithm

BPTT is the gradient-based learning algorithm for RNNs. It works by:
1. **Unrolling** the recurrent network across all $T$ time steps (creating a "deep" feedforward network with shared weights).
2. **Computing** the total loss as a sum of losses at every time step: $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$.
3. **Propagating** the gradient of $L$ with respect to each parameter *backward* through time, applying the chain rule at every step.
4. **Accumulating** the gradients from each time step before performing a single parameter update.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** You must clearly distinguish between **Truncated BPTT** (used in practice, gradient flow is cut off after $k$ steps to control compute) and **Full BPTT** (theoretically correct, computationally expensive).

### 2.4 The Vanishing & Exploding Gradient Problem

A critical limitation of vanilla RNN-LMs. When BPTT propagates gradients across many time steps, the gradient of the loss with respect to early hidden states involves repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight matrix $W_h$.

For an activation like $\tanh$, repeated multiplication causes the gradient to either:
- **Vanish** (shrink toward zero) — making it impossible to learn long-range dependencies.
- **Explode** (grow unboundedly) — causing numerical instability (NaN losses).

**Standard Mitigation Strategies:**
- Gradient clipping (rescales gradient when $\Vert g \Vert > $ threshold).
- Gated architectures: **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** and **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**.
- Orthogonal initialization of $W_h$.
- Layer normalization.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})$ | Joint probability factorization | Foundation of autoregressive generation |
| 2 | $h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b_h)$ | Hidden state update | Core RNN recurrence |
| 3 | $o_t = U h_t + b_o$ | Output logits projection | Maps hidden state to vocabulary space |
| 4 | $P(w_{t+1} \mid h_t) = \text{softmax}(o_t)$ | Next-word distribution | Produces probability over vocabulary |
| 5 | $L_t = -\sum_{k=1}^{\vert V \vert} y_t^{(k)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(k)}$ | Cross-entropy loss at step $t$ | Training objective (one-hot target) |
| 6 | $L = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ | Average cross-entropy over sequence | Aggregate loss for full sequence |
| 7 | $\text{PPL} = \exp(L)$ | Perplexity | Standard LM evaluation metric |
| 8 | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_h} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_h}$ | BPTT gradient for $W_h$ | Captures contributions from all past steps |
| 9 | $h_t^{\text{LSTM}} = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$ | LSTM hidden output | Mitigates vanishing gradient |
| 10 | $g \leftarrow \frac{g \cdot \tau}{\Vert g \Vert}$ if $\Vert g \Vert > \tau$ | Gradient clipping rule | Prevents exploding gradients |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

RNN-LMs (and their descendants LSTMs/GRUs) power a wide range of industrial applications:
- **Smart keyboards & autocomplete** (predictive text on mobile devices).
- **Speech recognition** acoustic rescoring.
- **Machine translation** (pre-transformer era; backbone of Google Translate circa 2016).
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** language correction.
- **Music generation** (treating notes as a vocabulary).
- **Time-series forecasting** (same recurrence principle applies).
- **DNA sequence modeling** in bioinformatics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass of an RNN-LM (Full Mathematical Unrolling)

Consider a vocabulary $V = \{ \text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{on}, \text{mat} \}$ with $\vert V \vert = 5$, embedding dimension $d_e = 3$, hidden dimension $d_h = 4$.

We process the input sentence: "the cat sat on mat" token-by-token.

**Step 1 — Initial Hidden State:**
At $t = 0$, before observing any token, the hidden state is initialized to a zero vector:

$$h_0 = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Token to Embedding:**
At time step $t = 1$, the input word is $w_1 = \text{"the"}$. The one-hot vector $e_{\text{the}} \in \mathbb{R}^{5}$ is looked up against the embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 3}$:

$$x_1 = E^\top e_{\text{the}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Hidden State Update at $t = 1$:**

$$h_1 = \tanh\!\left(W_h h_0 + W_x x_1 + b_h\right)$$

Since $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$, the first term vanishes, leaving:

$$h_1 = \tanh\!\left(W_x x_1 + b_h\right)$$

where $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$, $W_x \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 3}$, $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$.

**Step 4 — Output Projection at $t = 1$:**

$$o_1 = U h_1 + b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{5}$$

where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 4}$ and $b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{5}$.

**Step 5 — Softmax Probability:**

$$P(w_2 \mid w_1) = \text{softmax}(o_1) = \frac{\exp(o_1)}{\sum_{j=1}^{5} \exp(o_1^{(j)})}$$

**Step 6 — Loss at $t = 1$:**

$$L_1 = -\log P(w_2 = \text{"cat"} \mid w_1 = \text{"the"})$$

**Step 7 — Recursion for $t = 2, 3, \ldots, T$:**
The same process repeats, with the crucial difference that $h_{t-1}$ is no longer zero, so both $W_h h_{t-1}$ and $W_x x_t$ contribute to $h_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 &= \tanh(W_h h_1 + W_x x_2 + b_h) \\
h_3 &= \tanh(W_h h_2 + W_x x_3 + b_h) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
h_T &= \tanh(W_h h_{T-1} + W_x x_T + b_h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Aggregate Loss:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_{t+1} \mid w_{1:t})$$

This completes the forward pass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

We derive the gradient of the total loss with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $W_h$. The derivation is the heart of how RNN-LMs learn.

**Step 1 — Loss Decomposition:**
By the chain rule, the gradient of total loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ with respect to $W_h$ is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_h} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_h}$$

**Step 2 — Expressing $L_t$ in Terms of $W_h$:**
At time step $t$, $L_t$ depends on $h_t$, which depends on $W_h$ through the most recent recurrence. But $h_t$ also depends on $h_{t-1}$, which itself depends on $W_h$, and so on back to $h_1$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_h} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_h}$$

**Step 3 — Recursive Jacobian $\partial h_t / \partial h_k$:**
By chain rule applied across the hidden state sequence:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Single-Step Jacobian:**
For the standard RNN cell, the single-step Jacobian is:

$$\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)\right) W_h^\top$$

where $a_i = W_h h_{i-1} + W_x x_i + b_h$ is the pre-activation.

**Step 5 — Full BPTT Gradient Expression:**

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_h} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \left[\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)\right) W_h^\top \right] \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_h}$$

**Step 6 — Why Vanishing Gradients Occur:**
The product $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_i)) W_h^\top$ involves multiplication of $t - k$ matrices. If the spectral radius of $W_h$ is less than 1, the product shrinks exponentially — **the gradient vanishes**. If greater than 1, it explodes.

This is the **fundamental reason** LSTMs and GRUs were invented: their gated memory cells keep this product bounded near unity.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Cross-Entropy Loss

Suppose at some time step $t$, the RNN-LM produces the following softmax output for a vocabulary of size 5:

$$\hat{y} = [0.05,\ 0.10,\ 0.60,\ 0.15,\ 0.10]$$

The true target is the third word, represented as the one-hot vector:

$$y = [0,\ 0,\ 1,\ 0,\ 0]$$

**Step 1 — Cross-Entropy:**

$$L_t = -\sum_{k=1}^{5} y^{(k)} \log \hat{y}^{(k)} = -(0 + 0 + 1 \cdot \log(0.60) + 0 + 0) = -\log(0.60)$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation:**

$$L_t = -(-0.5108) = 0.5108$$

**Step 3 — Perplexity Contribution:**

$$\text{PPL}_t = \exp(L_t) = \exp(0.5108) \approx 1.6665$$

**Interpretation:** At this single time step, the model is "as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among ~1.67 equally likely options." Good language models achieve perplexities in the 20–100 range on benchmark corpora; state-of-the-art transformers reach single-digit to low-teens.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RNN-LM Unrolled Architecture (Time-Expanded View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["x_t-1: word embed"] --> hh1["h_t-1: hidden state"]
    hh1 --> hh2["h_t: hidden state"]
    inp2["x_t: word embed"] --> hh2
    hh2 --> hh3["h_t+1: hidden state"]
    inp3["x_t+1: word embed"] --> hh3
    hh1 --> out1["o_t-1: output logits"]
    hh2 --> out2["o_t: output logits"]
    hh3 --> out3["o_t+1: output logits"]
    out1 --> sm1["softmax P"]
    out2 --> sm2["softmax P"]
    out3 --> sm3["softmax P"]
    hh1 -.-> hh2
    hh2 -.-> hh3
    inp1 --> hh1
    inp2 --> hh2
    inp3 --> hh3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each box $h_t$ depends on its own input $x_t$ and the *previous* hidden state $h_{t-1}$ (shown by the dotted arrows looping horizontally). The output $o_t$ at time $t$ is the prediction for the *next* word $w_{t+1}$, hence it is offset by one position.

---

### 4.2 RNN-LM Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Token Sequence w1, w2, ..., wT] --> B[Embedding Matrix E]
    B --> C[Embedding Vectors x1, x2, ..., xT]
    C --> D[RNN Cell: hidden state update]
    E[Previous Hidden State h_t-1] --> D
    D --> F[Current Hidden State h_t]
    F --> G[Output Projection U]
    G --> H[Logits over Vocabulary]
    H --> I[Softmax Layer]
    I --> J[Probability Distribution P w_t+1 given w1 to t]
    J --> K[Cross-Entropy Loss Computation]
    K --> L[BPTT: Backprop Through Time]
    L --> M[Parameter Update: W_h, W_x, U, b]
    M -.updates.-> D
    M -.updates.-> G
    M -.updates.-> B
```

---

### 4.3 BPTT Gradient Flow Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L_T: loss at step T] --> H3[h_T gradient]
    H3 --> H2[h_T-1 gradient]
    H2 --> H1[h_T-2 gradient]
    H1 --> H0[h_T-3 gradient]
    L2[L_T-1] --> H2
    L3[L_T-2] --> H1
    L4[L_T-3] --> H0
    H0 --> W[Accumulated Gradient for W_h]
    H1 --> W
    H2 --> W
    H3 --> W
    W --> UPD[Single SGD / Adam Update Step]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Interpretation:** At every time step, a new loss $L_t$ is generated, and its gradient must be propagated *backward* through all earlier hidden states. The gradients from all time steps are **summed** to form a single update to the shared parameter $W_h$. This is the essence of BPTT.

---

### 4.4 Comparison: N-gram vs RNN-LM Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NGRAM[N-gram Model]
        N1[w_t-2] --> N3[P next word]
        N2[w_t-1] --> N3
    end
    subgraph RNNLM[RNN Language Model]
        R1[w_1] --> R4[h_t: summary of ALL past context]
        R2[w_2] --> R4
        R3[w_t-1] --> R4
        R4 --> R5[P next word]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The N-gram can only "see" a fixed window, while the RNN aggregates information from the *entire* history into the hidden state $h_t$. This is the central architectural advantage of RNN-LMs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Recurrent Neural Network Language Model. How does it differ from a traditional N-gram language model in terms of context handling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An RNN-LM is a neural network that models the probability of a word sequence by maintaining a recurrent hidden state $h_t$ that encodes the history of all previous words. It factorizes the joint probability as $P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})$, with each conditional probability computed via a softmax over the vocabulary using the hidden state.
- **Difference in Context Handling (1.5 marks):** N-gram models condition only on the previous $N-1$ words (fixed context window), suffering from data sparsity and an inability to generalize beyond seen contexts. RNN-LMs use a recurrent hidden state that theoretically encodes arbitrarily long histories with a fixed parameter set, enabling better generalization through distributed word representations (embeddings).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is perplexity? Why is it the standard evaluation metric for language models?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Perplexity is the exponential of the average cross-entropy loss over a test corpus: $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} -\log P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})\right)$. It can be interpreted as the effective number of choices the model is uncertain among at each time step.
- **Why Standard (1.5 marks):**
  1. It is **length-independent**, unlike raw loss.
  2. It is **comparable across corpora and vocabularies**.
  3. It is **monotonically related** to cross-entropy, so minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing perplexity.
  4. It has an intuitive probabilistic interpretation (inverse geometric mean of probabilities).

---
