> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with **internal choice** (a + b sub-parts of 7 marks each).
> - **Cognitive Levels Tested:** L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze) — Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
> - **Course Outcomes (CO) Mapped:** CO3 — *Apply deep learning architectures for sequence modeling tasks.*

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Stacked RNN. Mention one advantage over a single-layer RNN.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Stacked RNN (Deep RNN)** is a recurrent neural network architecture in which multiple RNN layers are arranged sequentially, with the **output hidden states of one layer serving as the input to the next layer** at the same time step. Mathematically, the recurrence at layer $\ell$ is:

$$h^{(\ell)}_t = \phi\!\left( W^{(\ell)}_{hh} h^{(\ell)}_{t-1} + W^{(\ell)}_{h^{(\ell-1)}h} h^{(\ell-1)}_t + b^{(\ell)}_h \right)$$

**Advantage (1 mark):** It enables the network to learn a **hierarchy of temporal features** — lower layers capture short-term local patterns, while higher layers capture long-term, high-level abstractions. This significantly increases the model's representational capacity compared to a shallow single-layer RNN.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the definition with formula, 1 mark for the architectural description, 1 mark for the advantage.)*

---

### **Question 2: What is a Bidirectional RNN? Why can't it be used for real-time streaming applications?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN)** is an RNN architecture that processes a sequence in **both forward and backward directions** using two independent hidden state sequences, $\overrightarrow{h}_t$ (forward) and $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ (backward), which are combined (typically concatenated) to produce the final output:

$$y_t = W_{hy} \left[ \overrightarrow{h}_t \,\,;\,\, \overleftarrow{h}_t \right] + b_y$$

The backward hidden state is computed by processing the sequence in reverse:

$$\overleftarrow{h}_t = \phi\!\left( \overleftarrow{W}_{hh} \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overleftarrow{b}_h \right)$$

**Why not for streaming (1 mark):** The backward pass requires access to the **entire future of the sequence** up to time $T$ to compute $\overleftarrow{h}_t$. In a real-time streaming scenario, future inputs are not yet available, making the backward pass impossible. Hence, Bi-RNNs are restricted to **offline, full-sequence tasks** such as NER, POS tagging, and machine translation.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the combination formula, 1 mark for the streaming limitation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Both Q-A and Q-B must be of comparable difficulty and cover the same module. The student answers **either** Q-A **or** Q-B. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Stacked RNN with Mathematical Derivation** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a **Stacked RNN** with a neat diagram. Derive the layer-wise forward propagation equations for a 3-layer Stacked RNN and explain how information flows both **horizontally (across time)** and **vertically (across layers)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a Stacked Bi-LSTM with $L = 2$ layers, hidden size $d_h = 128$, input dimension $d_x = 64$, and output dimension $d_y = 10$, compute the **total number of trainable parameters**. State clearly any assumptions made. Discuss why stacking helps in capturing long-range dependencies in NLP tasks.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Architectural Description (2 marks)**

A Stacked RNN consists of $L$ RNN layers arranged vertically. Each layer $\ell$ has its own hidden state $h^{(\ell)}_t$ and its own set of parameters $W^{(\ell)}_{hh}, W^{(\ell)}_{h^{(\ell-1)}h}, b^{(\ell)}_h$. The **output hidden state sequence** of layer $\ell$ is fed as the **input sequence** to layer $\ell+1$ at the corresponding time steps.

For $L=3$ layers:
- Layer 1: Takes raw input $x_t$.
- Layer 2: Takes $h^{(1)}_t$ as input.
- Layer 3: Takes $h^{(2)}_t$ as input.

**Step 2: Derivation of Forward Propagation (3 marks)**

Initialize: $h^{(\ell)}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ for $\ell = 1, 2, 3$.

**Layer 1 (Bottom):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(1)}_t &= \phi\!\left( W^{(1)}_{xh} x_t + W^{(1)}_{hh} h^{(1)}_{t-1} + b^{(1)}_h \right), \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Layer 2 (Middle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(2)}_t &= \phi\!\left( W^{(2)}_{h^{(1)}h} h^{(1)}_t + W^{(2)}_{hh} h^{(2)}_{t-1} + b^{(2)}_h \right), \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Layer 3 (Top):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(3)}_t &= \phi\!\left( W^{(3)}_{h^{(2)}h} h^{(2)}_t + W^{(3)}_{hh} h^{(3)}_{t-1} + b^{(3)}_h \right), \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output (Sequence Labeling):**

$$y_t = W_{hy} h^{(3)}_t + b_y$$

**Step 3: Information Flow Explanation (2 marks)**

- **Horizontal flow (within a layer):** At time $t$, layer $\ell$'s hidden state $h^{(\ell)}_t$ depends on $h^{(\ell)}_{t-1}$ — the hidden state at the *previous time step* in the *same layer*. This captures **temporal dependencies**.
- **Vertical flow (across layers):** At time $t$, layer $\ell$'s input is $h^{(\ell-1)}_t$ — the hidden state at the *same time step* in the *previous layer*. This captures **hierarchical feature abstraction**.
- **Combined effect:** The model learns both **fine-grained temporal patterns** (lower layers) and **coarse-grained semantic patterns** (upper layers).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: State Assumptions (1 mark)**

- Bi-LSTM has 2 layers ($L=2$), each with forward and backward directions.
- All LSTMs have hidden size $d_h = 128$.
- Input dim $d_x = 64$, output dim $d_y = 10$.
- An LSTM cell has **4 gates** (input, forget, output, candidate), each with weights of size $d_h \times d_{\text{in}}$ and $d_h \times d_h$, plus bias.
- Output is a linear layer (not part of LSTM) projecting $2 d_h = 256 \to d_y = 10$.

**Step 2: Layer 1 Parameter Count (2 marks)**

- Per direction: $4 \times [d_h \cdot d_x + d_h \cdot d_h + d_h] = 4 \times [128 \cdot 64 + 128^2 + 128]$
  $= 4 \times [8192 + 16384 + 128] = 4 \times 24704 = 98{,}816$
- Bi-directional Layer 1: $2 \times 98{,}816 = 197{,}632$

**Step 3: Layer 2 Parameter Count (2 marks)**

- Input dim to Layer 2 = $2 d_h = 256$ (output of Bi Layer 1).
- Per direction: $4 \times [d_h \cdot 2d_h + d_h \cdot d_h + d_h] = 4 \times [128 \cdot 256 + 128^2 + 128]$
  $= 4 \times [32768 + 16384 + 128] = 4 \times 49280 = 197{,}120$
- Bi-directional Layer 2: $2 \times 197{,}120 = 394{,}240$

**Step 4: Output Projection (1 mark)**

- Linear layer: $2 d_h \times d_y + d_y = 256 \times 10 + 10 = 2570$

**Step 5: Total Parameters (1 mark)**

$$
\theta_{\text{total}} = 197{,}632 + 394{,}240 + 2570 = 594{,}442 \text{ parameters}
$$

**Discussion: Why Stacking Helps (extra — for full marks, 1 mark allocated above covers this)**

Stacking allows the model to learn a **hierarchy of temporal features**. For NLP:
- Layer 1 captures character-level or sub-word patterns.
- Layer 2 captures word-level and phrase-level semantics.
- This hierarchical abstraction is essential for modeling long-range dependencies, where direct connections across hundreds of time steps are otherwise difficult to learn due to vanishing gradients.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Bidirectional RNN with Mathematical Derivation** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a **Bidirectional RNN** with a neat diagram. Derive the forward and backward pass equations clearly, and discuss how the outputs are combined. Provide two **real-world NLP tasks** where Bi-RNNs are essential.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **Stacked RNN** and **Bidirectional RNN** architectures in terms of: (i) direction of information flow, (ii) parameter count, (iii) computational graph structure, (iv) suitability for streaming vs. offline tasks, and (v) ability to handle long-range dependencies. Justify each point with one sentence.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Architecture Description (1.5 marks)**

A Bi-RNN has **two parallel sub-networks**:
- A **forward RNN** ($\overrightarrow{\text{RNN}}$) processes the sequence from $t = 1$ to $T$.
- A **backward RNN** ($\overleftarrow{\text{RNN}}$) processes the sequence from $t = T$ down to $1$.
- The two sub-networks have **independent weights** but operate on the **same input sequence** $x_t$.
- The two hidden states at each time step are **combined** (concatenation is most common) and projected to produce the output.

**Step 2: Forward Pass Derivation (1.5 marks)**

Initialized with $\overrightarrow{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\overrightarrow{h}_t = \phi\!\left( \overrightarrow{W}_{hh} \overrightarrow{h}_{t-1} + \overrightarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overrightarrow{b}_h \right), \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$$

**Step 3: Backward Pass Derivation (1.5 marks)**

Initialized with $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$, processed in reverse time:

$$\overleftarrow{h}_t = \phi\!\left( \overleftarrow{W}_{hh} \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overleftarrow{b}_h \right), \quad t = T, T-1, \ldots, 1$$

**Step 4: Output Combination (1 mark)**

The two hidden states are concatenated and projected:

$$y_t = W_{hy} \left[ \overrightarrow{h}_t \,\,;\,\, \overleftarrow{h}_t \right] + b_y$$

where $\left[ \overrightarrow{h}_t \,\,;\,\, \overleftarrow{h}_t \right] \in \mathbb{R}^{2 d_h}$.

**Step 5: Two Real-World NLP Tasks (1.5 marks)**

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** To classify the entity type of the word *"Apple"* in *"Apple announced its earnings..."*, the model benefits from knowing that *"earnings"* (a future word) follows — confirming a corporate entity. Bi-RNNs provide this future context.
2. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging:** Disambiguating *"book"* as a noun vs. verb requires both preceding (*"the"*) and following (*"is thick"*) context. Bi-RNNs combine both directions for accurate tagging.
3. *(Alternative valid answer: Machine Translation — encoder must read source bidirectionally; Speech Recognition — phoneme boundaries need future audio.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| # | Aspect | Stacked RNN | Bidirectional RNN | Justification (1 sentence each) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direction of information flow** | Single direction (forward), but vertically deep | Bidirectional, but only one layer deep (unless stacked) | Stacked adds *depth* in feature hierarchy; Bi-RNN adds *context width*. |
| 2 | **Parameter count** | $L \times$ per-layer cost | $2 \times$ per-layer cost (one extra direction) | For $L=2$ stacked vs. Bi, parameters are roughly equal but distributed differently. |
| 3 | **Computational graph structure** | Sequential across time *and* layers (deeper backprop) | Sequential across time, but forward and backward passes are parallelizable | Stacked has a longer backpropagation path, making vanishing gradients more severe. |
| 4 | **Streaming suitability** | Suitable (can be used in streaming) | Not suitable (requires full future context) | Bi-RNN's backward pass is undefined when future inputs are not yet available. |
| 5 | **Long-range dependencies** | Better with depth (hierarchical compression) | Better with full-context encoding (no future truncation) | Stacking helps *within* the past direction; Bi-RNN helps *across* both directions. |

*(Examiner's note: Award 1.4 marks per row, with deductions for missing justifications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Hotspots)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Stacked with Bidirectional:** Students often write "stacked" when they mean "bidirectional" and vice versa. **Stacked = depth (vertical layers), Bidirectional = direction (forward + backward passes).** This is the #1 conceptual error — **lose up to 2 marks** per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the boundary conditions:** Examiners specifically test $\overrightarrow{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ and $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$. **Omitting these costs 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong input dimension for stacked layers:** Students frequently write the input to layer 2 as $x_t$ instead of $h^{(1)}_t$. **This is a critical formula error — 2-3 marks lost.**
>
> 4. **Parameter count errors in LSTM vs. RNN:** When computing parameters, students often use the **simple RNN** formula ($d_h(d_x + d_h) + d_h$) for LSTM cells. Remember: **LSTM has 4 gates × (input weights + recurrent weights + bias)**. **Up to 3 marks lost.**
>
> 5. **Saying Bi-RNN is "two RNNs in series":** Bi-RNN has **two parallel RNNs** (not sequential), both operating on the same input. **1 mark lost for this misconception.**
>
> 6. **Drawing diagrams without arrows:** A stacked RNN diagram without vertical arrows (layer-to-layer) and horizontal arrows (time recurrence) will lose diagram marks. **1 mark lost.**
>
> 7. **Forgetting the "offline" constraint for Bi-RNN:** When asked "Why not for real-time?" — students must explicitly state the **backward pass needs future inputs**, not just vaguely say "it uses two directions."
>
> 8. **Mixing up $\overrightarrow{h}_t$ and $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ indexing:** Forward: $\overrightarrow{h}_{t-1} \to \overrightarrow{h}_t$. Backward: $\overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} \to \overleftarrow{h}_t$. Many students write the backward pass as $\overleftarrow{h}_{t-1}$, which is **incorrect**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Stacked RNN** = **vertical depth** (multiple layers); recurrence within each layer; output of layer $\ell$ is input to layer $\ell+1$.
- [ ] **Bidirectional RNN** = **horizontal width** (forward + backward passes); two independent hidden state sequences.
- [ ] **Stacked RNN forward equation:** $h^{(\ell)}_t = \phi(W^{(\ell)}_{hh} h^{(\ell)}_{t-1} + W^{(\ell)}_{h^{(\ell-1)}h} h^{(\ell-1)}_t + b^{(\ell)}_h)$.
- [ ] **Bi-RNN forward:** $\overrightarrow{h}_t = \phi(\overrightarrow{W}_{hh} \overrightarrow{h}_{t-1} + \overrightarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overrightarrow{b}_h)$.
- [ ] **Bi-RNN backward:** $\overleftarrow{h}_t = \phi(\overleftarrow{W}_{hh} \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overleftarrow{b}_h)$.
- [ ] **Bi-RNN output:** $y_t = W_{hy} [\overrightarrow{h}_t; \overleftarrow{h}_t] + b_y$ — concatenation is the **default** combination.
- [ ] **Boundary conditions:** $\overrightarrow{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$ (or learned).
- [ ] **Initializations** are usually zero vectors, but some implementations use **learned initial states**.
- [ ] **Stacked RNN** captures **hierarchical temporal abstractions** (low-level → high-level).
- [ ] **Bi-RNN** captures **bidirectional context** (past + future).
- [ ] **Bi-RNN output dimension** at top layer = **$2 d_h$** (concatenation) or **$d_h$** (addition).
- [ ] **Parameter count** for stacked Bi-LSTM (single layer, single direction) = $4 [d_h(d_x + d_h) + d_h]$.
- [ ] **Parameter count** for stacked Bi-LSTM total scales as **$\mathcal{O}(L \cdot d_h^2)$** linearly in depth.
- [ ] **Bi-RNN cannot be used for streaming/real-time** — requires full future sequence.
- [ ] **Stacked RNN can be used for streaming** — no future dependency in standard form.
- [ ] **Most common pairing in practice:** **Stacked Bi-LSTM/GRU** — combines both advantages.
- [ ] **Activation function for hidden state:** $\tanh$ (default for vanilla RNN), sigmoid for gates (LSTM/GRU).
- [ ] **Training:** Stacked and Bi-RNNs are both trained via **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, with stacking increasing the effective depth and Bi-RNN doubling the parameter count.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** NER, POS tagging, machine translation (encoder), speech recognition, sentiment analysis, music generation.
- [ ] **Vanishing gradient concern:** Stacking *increases* the BPTT path length, exacerbating vanishing gradients. Solutions: use **LSTM/GRU cells**, **skip connections**, or **gradient clipping**.
- [ ] **Stacking with LSTM/GRU is the standard** in practice — vanilla RNNs are rarely stacked more than 2 layers deep.
- [ ] **Bidirectional + Stacked = "Deep Bi-RNN"** — the most common production architecture for sequence labeling pre-Transformer era.
- [ ] **Default output extraction:**
  - Sequence classification → use final hidden state $h_T$.
  - Sequence labeling → use hidden state at every time step $h_t$ (all $t$).
  - Encoder-decoder → use full sequence and pass to attention/decoder.
- [ ] **Order of operations in frameworks:** `Bidirectional(LSTM(units=128, return_sequences=True))(input)` — note that `Bidirectional` **wraps** the RNN cell.
- [ ] **In PyTorch:** `nn.RNN(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers=L, bidirectional=True)` — both depth and bidirectionality in one call.

---

### 📌 Final Exam Tips (KTU Board-Specific)

1. **Always draw the unrolled diagram** in 14-mark questions — this is worth **2-3 marks** by itself.
2. **Label all arrows** clearly: time direction, layer direction, weight matrices.
3. **State boundary conditions explicitly** ($\overrightarrow{h}_0, \overleftarrow{h}_{T+1}$).
4. **Mention practical use cases** — examiners reward real-world relevance.
5. **Use standard notation** consistently: $h^{(\ell)}_t$ for stacked, $\overrightarrow{h}_t$ / $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ for Bi-RNN.
6. **For parameter count questions**, state **assumptions first** (e.g., "LSTM has 4 gates, biases included, no peephole connections").
7. **Differentiate clearly** between "depth" (stacked) and "direction" (bidirectional) in any comparison.

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Stacked and Bidirectional RNN Architectures*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECST862 Natural Language Processing*
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol*

topics.title: Recursive Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recursive Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)** is a type of deep neural network architecture specifically engineered to process data with **hierarchical, tree-structured, or recursive topology**. Unlike standard feedforward networks (which operate on fixed-size input vectors) and unlike Recurrent Neural Networks (which process sequential/linear data), RecNNs exploit the **compositional structure of language** by recursively applying the same set of weights over an input parsed into a syntactic tree (typically a constituency or dependency parse tree).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Recursive Neural Networks are foundational to *Compositional Distributional Semantics*, the linguistic principle that the meaning of a phrase is a function of the meanings of its constituent words and the rules used to combine them (Frege's Principle of Compositionality).

Formally, given two child vectors $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ representing contiguous sub-phrases, a RecNN computes a parent representation $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ as:

$$\mathbf{p} = f\left(W \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{bias}\right)$$

where $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2d}$ is the shared weight matrix, $\mathbf{bias} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the bias vector, and $f(\cdot)$ is a non-linear activation function (typically $\tanh$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The LEGO Block Analogy:**
> Imagine you are assembling a LEGO castle. You don't build it by laying every single 1x1 brick in a straight line. Instead, you first build small sub-components (a wall, a tower), then combine those sub-components into larger ones (a gate, a battlement), and finally merge those into the complete castle.
>
> A **Recursive Neural Network does exactly this with language.** It first computes the meaning of small phrases (e.g., "very good"), then merges them into larger phrases (e.g., "not very good"), and continues up the syntactic tree until it forms a vector for the entire sentence. Each merge uses the **same rule book** ($W$ matrix), just as a single brick-design is used repeatedly in the LEGO castle.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features

| Feature | Recursive NN (RecNN) | Recurrent NN (RNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Tree-structured | Linear chain (sequence) |
| **Input Type** | Hierarchical/parsed text | Sequential time-series |
| **Combine Function** | Applied to two children simultaneously | Applied to current + previous state |
| **Key Use Case** | Sentiment classification, parse-tree scoring | Language modeling, machine translation |
| **Computation Time** | $O(\log n)$ (parallelizable across tree depth) | $O(n)$ sequential |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** Students often confuse "Recursive" with "Recurrent". The two words are not synonymous. **Recurrent = time loops, Recursive = tree composition.**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Hyperparameters

In a KTU exam context, the following are the **default values** you should commit to memory:
- **Activation Function:** $\tanh(x)$ (range $[-1, 1]$), or **ReLU** in modern variants
- **Vector Dimensionality ($d$):** Typically **25 to 50** for word vectors, **30** for hidden parent nodes (as per Socher et al., 2011 - the foundational paper)
- **Loss Function:** Euclidean distance for reconstruction; **Cross-Entropy** for classification tasks
- **Learning Rate ($\eta$):** Often **0.01 to 0.1**
- **Regularization ($\lambda$):** L2 norm penalty, typically **$10^{-4}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Structured Logic Steps

A Recursive Neural Network operates through three tightly-coupled stages:

**Step 1: Input Preparation (Word Embedding)**
- Every word $w_i$ in the sentence is converted into a $d$-dimensional dense vector $\mathbf{x}_i$ using a pre-trained embedding (e.g., word2vec, GloVe).
- These embeddings serve as the **leaves** of the computation tree.

**Step 2: Bottom-Up Tree Composition (The "Why" and "How")**
- **Why?** Because language is inherently hierarchical. The phrase "the cat" forms a meaningful unit before it interacts with the verb "sat".
- **How?** The algorithm parses the sentence into a binary constituency tree (e.g., using the Stanford Parser). Then, starting from the leaves, the RecNN applies the *same* weight matrix $W$ at every internal node to merge sibling representations.
- The merged parent vector is then ready to be combined with its sibling in the next higher level of the tree.

**Step 3: Top-Down Labeling (Classification at Root)**
- At the root node, the final parent vector $\mathbf{p}_{root}$ represents the **entire sentence**.
- A softmax classifier (e.g., for sentiment: positive/negative/neutral) is applied to $\mathbf{p}_{root}$ to produce the final prediction.
- The error is then propagated downward through the tree using **Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)**.

### 2.2 The Three Classical Variants of Recursive Neural Networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** The following three variants are frequently asked in 14-mark questions. Know the matrix dimensions, the *what changed*, and the *why it changed*.

#### 2.2.1 Standard Recursive Neural Network (Plain RecNN)
- Uses a single weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2d}$.
- **Limitation:** Cannot capture sufficient interaction between left and right children. The interaction is bottlenecked through the non-linearity.

#### 2.2.2 Matrix-Vector Recursive Neural Network (MV-RNN)
- Each word is represented by **both** a vector $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$.
- The composition rule becomes:
$$\mathbf{p} = f\left(A \cdot \mathbf{b} + B \cdot \mathbf{a}\right)$$
- **Why it changed:** Different words act as *operators* on their siblings. A matrix captures how one word modifies the meaning of another (e.g., adjectives modifying nouns).
- **Drawback:** Very high parameter count — one matrix per word in the vocabulary.

#### 2.2.3 Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN)
- Replaces the bilinear interaction with a **tensor** $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d \times 2d}$ (a 3-way array).
$$\mathbf{p} = f\left(\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}^T V \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix} + W \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}\right)$$
- **Why it changed:** Captures multiplicative interactions between children more explicitly, allowing the model to learn complex compositional semantics (e.g., negation: "not good" flips sentiment).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RecNN Composition** | $\mathbf{p} = f(W[\mathbf{a};\mathbf{b}] + \mathbf{b})$ | Basic parent node computation | $W: d \times 2d$ |
| 2 | **MV-RNN Composition** | $\mathbf{p} = f(A\mathbf{b} + B\mathbf{a})$ | Matrix-vector interaction | $A,B: d \times d$ |
| 3 | **RNTN Composition** | $\mathbf{p} = f(\mathbf{x}^T V \mathbf{x} + W\mathbf{x})$ | Tensor-based bilinear | $V: 2d \times 2d \times d$ |
| 4 | **Softmax Classifier** | $y = \text{softmax}(U \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{b}_s)$ | Output class probability | $U: c \times d$ |
| 5 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Loss for classification | Scalar |
| 6 | **Activation** | $f(x) = \tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | Non-linearity | Scalar |
| 7 | **BTS Gradient (example)** | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{\text{nodes}} \delta_n \cdot [\mathbf{a}_n; \mathbf{b}_n]^T$ | Sum over all tree nodes | $d \times 2d$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive Neural Networks have powered several landmark NLP systems:
- **Sentiment Analysis (Socher et al., 2011-2013, Stanford):** Movie review classification with phrase-level polarity detection.
- **Paraphrase Detection:** Identifying whether two sentences convey the same meaning by comparing their root vectors.
- **Natural Language Inference (SNLI):** Determining entailment, contradiction, or neutral relation between sentence pairs.
- **Relation Extraction (Tai et al., 2015):** Classifying semantic relations between entities (e.g., "lives in", "works for") along dependency tree paths.
- **Semantic Compositionality Research:** Foundation for modern Tree-LSTMs and graph-based transformers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Forward Pass in a Recursive Neural Network

**Problem Setup:**
Consider the sentence *"The cat sat"*, which has the constituency parse tree:

```
         ROOT
        /    \
      NP      VP
     / \     /  \
  The  cat  sat  .
```

We are given word embeddings (leaves):
- $\mathbf{x}_{\text{The}} = [0.1, 0.2]^T$
- $\mathbf{x}_{\text{cat}} = [0.3, 0.5]^T$
- $\mathbf{x}_{\text{sat}} = [0.6, 0.1]^T$

Let the weight matrix be:
$$W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.5 & 0.1 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Bias is assumed zero for simplicity. Activation $f = \tanh$.

**Step 1: Compute the NP node (The + cat)**

The concatenated child vector is:
$$[\mathbf{x}_{\text{The}}; \mathbf{x}_{\text{cat}}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now multiply by $W$:
$$W \cdot [\mathbf{x}_{\text{The}}; \mathbf{x}_{\text{cat}}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.5 & 0.1 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1 computation:
$$(0.2)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.3)(0.3) + (0.2)(0.5)$$
$$= 0.02 + 0.02 + 0.09 + 0.10 = 0.23$$

Row 2 computation:
$$(0.4)(0.1) + (0.5)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.5)$$
$$= 0.04 + 0.10 + 0.03 + 0.15 = 0.32$$

Apply $\tanh$ activation:
$$\mathbf{p}_{NP} = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 0.23 \\ 0.32 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{e^{0.23} - e^{-0.23}}{e^{0.23} + e^{-0.23}} \\ \frac{e^{0.32} - e^{-0.32}}{e^{0.32} + e^{-0.32}} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing exponentials:
- $e^{0.23} \approx 1.2586$, $e^{-0.23} \approx 0.7945$
- $e^{0.32} \approx 1.3771$, $e^{-0.32} \approx 0.7261$

Tanh values:
- $\tanh(0.23) = \frac{1.2586 - 0.7945}{1.2586 + 0.7945} = \frac{0.4641}{2.0531} \approx 0.2260$
- $\tanh(0.32) = \frac{1.3771 - 0.7261}{1.3771 + 0.7261} = \frac{0.6510}{2.1032} \approx 0.3095$

Therefore:
$$\mathbf{p}_{NP} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2260 \\ 0.3095 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the VP node (sat + .)**

We treat the period as a leaf with embedding $\mathbf{x}_{.} = [0.05, 0.05]^T$.

$$[\mathbf{x}_{\text{sat}}; \mathbf{x}_{.}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.1 \\ 0.05 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $W$:
$$W \cdot [\mathbf{x}_{\text{sat}}; \mathbf{x}_{.}] = \begin{bmatrix} (0.2)(0.6) + (0.1)(0.1) + (0.3)(0.05) + (0.2)(0.05) \\ (0.4)(0.6) + (0.5)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.05) + (0.3)(0.05) \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1:
$$= 0.12 + 0.01 + 0.015 + 0.01 = 0.155$$

Row 2:
$$= 0.24 + 0.05 + 0.005 + 0.015 = 0.31$$

Apply $\tanh$:
- $\tanh(0.155) \approx 0.1538$
- $\tanh(0.31) \approx 0.3012$

$$\mathbf{p}_{VP} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1538 \\ 0.3012 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute the ROOT node (NP + VP)**

$$[\mathbf{p}_{NP}; \mathbf{p}_{VP}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2260 \\ 0.3095 \\ 0.1538 \\ 0.3012 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $W$:
$$W \cdot [\mathbf{p}_{NP}; \mathbf{p}_{VP}] = \begin{bmatrix} (0.2)(0.2260) + (0.1)(0.3095) + (0.3)(0.1538) + (0.2)(0.3012) \\ (0.4)(0.2260) + (0.5)(0.3095) + (0.1)(0.1538) + (0.3)(0.3012) \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1:
$$= 0.0452 + 0.03095 + 0.04614 + 0.06024 = 0.18253$$

Row 2:
$$= 0.0904 + 0.15475 + 0.01538 + 0.09036 = 0.35089$$

Apply $\tanh$:
- $\tanh(0.18253) \approx 0.1805$
- $\tanh(0.35089) \approx 0.3377$

$$\mathbf{p}_{ROOT} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1805 \\ 0.3377 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Classification at the Root**

Suppose we have a 3-class sentiment softmax classifier with:
$$U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \\ 0.4 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}_s = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute logits:
$$\mathbf{z} = U \cdot \mathbf{p}_{ROOT} + \mathbf{b}_s = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5(0.1805) + 0.3(0.3377) + 0.1 \\ 0.2(0.1805) + 0.6(0.3377) + 0.0 \\ 0.4(0.1805) + 0.1(0.3377) - 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.09025 + 0.10131 + 0.1 \\ 0.03610 + 0.20262 + 0.0 \\ 0.07220 + 0.03377 - 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.29156 \\ 0.23872 \\ 0.00597 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply softmax: $\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$

Exponentials:
- $e^{0.29156} \approx 1.3385$
- $e^{0.23872} \approx 1.2697$
- $e^{0.00597} \approx 1.0060$

Sum = $1.3385 + 1.2697 + 1.0060 = 3.6142$

Probabilities:
$$\hat{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3703 \\ 0.3513 \\ 0.2784 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The model predicts Class 1 (e.g., Positive) with **37.03%** probability, Class 2 (Neutral) with **35.13%**, and Class 3 (Negative) with **27.84%**. The verdict is Positive (slight edge).

### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)

The error at the root node must be backpropagated to every internal node. For a node $n$ with children $c_L$ and $c_R$, the gradient of the loss $L$ w.r.t. $W$ is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{n \in \text{internal}} \delta^{(n)} \cdot [\mathbf{c}_L^{(n)}; \mathbf{c}_R^{(n)}]^T$$

where $\delta^{(n)}$ is the "error signal" at node $n$, computed recursively as:

$$\delta^{(n)} = \left( \sum_{k \in \text{children of } n} W^T_{\text{slice}} \cdot \delta^{(k)} \right) \odot f'(\mathbf{p}_n)$$

Here $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product, and $W^T_{\text{slice}}$ picks the appropriate columns of $W$ corresponding to which child the error came from.

The total gradient is the **sum over all nodes** in the tree (a structural analog of summation in standard backpropagation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Through Structure" and not "Through Time"?** Because the same matrix $W$ is reused at every node, the gradient computation must *sum* contributions across the entire tree topology — this is unique to RecNNs and is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Standard RecNN | MV-RNN | RNTN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parameter Count** | $O(d^2)$ | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot d^2)$ | $O(d^3)$ |
| **Compositional Power** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Negation Handling** | Poor | Moderate | Strong |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Slow (large $\vert V \vert$) | Moderate |
| **Memory Footprint** | Small | Very Large | Medium |
| **Best Use Case** | Baseline experiments | Operator semantics | Sentiment with negation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Recursive Neural Network

This diagram illustrates the **bottom-up compositional flow** of information in a RecNN applied to the sentence *"The cat sat"*. Each internal node applies the same weight matrix $W$ to its two children.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Leaf: The (vector)"] --> N1["NP node: f(W concat a b)"]
    B["Leaf: cat (vector)"] --> N1
    N1 --> N3["ROOT node: f(W concat a b)"]
    C["Leaf: sat (vector)"] --> N2["VP node: f(W concat a b)"]
    D["Leaf: period (vector)"] --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4["Softmax Classifier: Output Class"]
    N4 --> N5["Prediction: Positive 37 percent"]
    style N1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style N2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style N3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style N4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RecNN Training Pipeline

The following block diagram maps the **end-to-end training loop** of a Recursive Neural Network, including the tree construction, forward pass, error computation, and BTS.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S["Input Sentence"] --> P1["Stanford Parser / Constituency Parser"]
    P1 --> P2["Binary Parse Tree"]
    P2 --> F1["Word Embedding Lookup"]
    F1 --> F2["Recursive Forward Pass over Tree"]
    F2 --> F3["Root Vector Extraction"]
    F3 --> F4["Softmax Classifier"]
    F4 --> F5["Predicted Class Probabilities"]
    F5 --> C1["Compute Loss Cross Entropy"]
    C1 --> C2["BTS Backpropagation Through Structure"]
    C2 --> C3["Gradient Descent Update on W and U"]
    C3 --> F2
    style F2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style C2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style C3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow: Plain RecNN vs. MV-RNN vs. RNTN

This diagram compares the **composition mechanism** of the three classical RecNN variants at a single node.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Child vector a"] --> M1
    R1["Child vector b"] --> M1
    M1["Plain RecNN: p = tanh W concat a b"] --> O1["Parent p"]
    L2["Child vector a + Matrix A"] --> M2
    R2["Child vector b + Matrix B"] --> M2
    M2["MV-RNN: p = tanh A b + B a"] --> O2["Parent p"]
    L3["Child vector a"] --> M3
    R3["Child vector b"] --> M3
    M3["RNTN: p = tanh tensor product plus W concat a b"] --> O3["Parent p"]
    style M1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style M2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style M3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

### 4.4 Computational Complexity Tree

The following schematic illustrates the **parallelizability advantage** of RecNNs: while RNNs have $O(n)$ sequential depth, RecNNs have $O(\log n)$ tree depth, meaning the GPU can compute all nodes at a given level in parallel.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Level 0: Leaves (n nodes)"] --> L2a["Level 1: n/2 parents"]
    L1 --> L2b["Level 1: n/2 parents"]
    L2a --> L3a["Level 2: n/4 parents"]
    L2b --> L3b["Level 2: n/4 parents"]
    L3a --> L4["Level log n: Root (1 node)"]
    L3b --> L4
    style L1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style L4 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#cc6600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This logarithmic depth is a major reason researchers were excited about RecNNs in the early 2010s — they offered a path to handle long sentences efficiently, before Transformers made this advantage less central.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Recursive Neural Network. How is it different from a Recurrent Neural Network?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)** is a neural architecture that processes structured inputs by recursively applying the same set of weights to combine representations in a hierarchical (tree) topology. It is built on the principle of *compositionality* — the meaning of a phrase is computed from the meanings of its constituent sub-phrases and the syntactic rules combining them.

| Aspect | Recursive NN | Recurrent NN |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Tree-structured | Linear sequence |
| **Recursion** | Over hierarchical structure | Over time steps |
| **Best Input** | Parsed sentences | Time-series / raw sequences |
| **Parallelism** | Parallel across tree depth | Sequential |

RecNNs require a **precomputed parse tree**, whereas RNNs do not.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for distinction + 1 for parse tree dependency)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List and briefly explain any two variants of Recursive Neural Networks.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Matrix-Vector Recursive Neural Network (MV-RNN):**
Each word is represented as a pair $(\mathbf{a}, A)$ where $\mathbf{a}$ is a vector (the word's meaning) and $A$ is a matrix (how the word modifies other words). Composition is:
$$\mathbf{p} = \tanh(A \mathbf{b} + B \mathbf{a})$$
The matrix captures **operator semantics** — e.g., the word "very" acts as an intensifier.

**2. Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN):**
Uses a 3-way tensor $V$ to explicitly model **bilinear interactions** between child vectors:
$$\mathbf{p} = \tanh\left(\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}^T V \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix} + W \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}\right)$$
This is highly effective at capturing **negation and contrast** (e.g., "good" vs "not good").

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks per variant)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Likely Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and working of a standard Recursive Neural Network. Derive the forward pass equation and show how the root node vector is used for sentence classification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN). How does it overcome the limitations of the standard RecNN?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Standard RecNN Architecture and Working:**

**Architecture:** A RecNN consists of:
1. **Word Embedding Layer:** Maps each word to a $d$-dimensional vector.
2. **Composition Function:** A weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2d}$ and bias $\mathbf{b}$, shared across all internal tree nodes.
3. **Classifier:** A softmax layer at the root node for class prediction.

**Working (Forward Pass):**
Given two child vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ at any internal node:

$$\mathbf{p} = f(W[\mathbf{a}; \mathbf{b}] + \mathbf{b})$$

where $[\mathbf{a}; \mathbf{b}]$ denotes vector concatenation, and $f = \tanh$.

**Process:**
- Step 1: Parse the sentence into a binary constituency tree.
- Step 2: Assign word embeddings to leaves.
- Step 3: Bottom-up: for each internal node, apply the composition rule.
- Step 4: At root $\mathbf{p}_{root}$, apply classifier:
$$\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(U \mathbf{p}_{root} + \mathbf{b}_s)$$

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):** The parse tree of "The cat sat" shows the bottom-up flow from leaves → NP node → VP node → ROOT → classifier.

**Sentence Classification:** The root vector $\mathbf{p}_{root} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ encodes the **entire sentence meaning**. A simple linear layer + softmax projects it to class probabilities. For example, in 5-class sentiment (1-5 stars), $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}^5$.

*(7 marks: 2 architecture + 3 forward pass + 2 classification)*

**(b) RNTN Working and Advantages:**

**Working of RNTN:**

At every internal node of the parse tree, RNTN applies the following composition rule:

$$\mathbf{p} = f\left(\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}^T V^{[1:d]} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix} + W \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{b}\right)$$

where $V \in \mathbb{R}^{2d \times 2d \times d}$ is a **3-way tensor** that captures pairwise interactions between dimensions of the children. The expression $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}^T V \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a} \\ \mathbf{b} \end{bmatrix}$ computes a $d$-dimensional vector where each element is a bilinear form.

**Why RNTN Overcomes Standard RecNN Limitations:**

1. **Captures Negation:** Standard RecNN struggles with "not good" vs "good" because the negation effect is bottlenecked through a single non-linearity. RNTN's tensor can learn that certain dimensions should *flip sign* under negation.

2. **Multiplicative Interactions:** Standard RecNN only models additive combinations ($\mathbf{W}\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}$). RNTN adds bilinear terms ($\mathbf{a}^T V \mathbf{b}$) which model how features *interact* rather than just add.

3. **Compositional Power:** Theoretically, the RNTN is a **universal approximator** over trees, meaning it can model any compositional function up to the given dimensionality.

4. **Better Sentiment Performance:** On the Stanford Sentiment Treebank, RNTN achieves ~80% accuracy on fine-grained 5-class sentiment, while standard RecNN achieves ~75%.

*(7 marks: 3 working + 4 limitations overcome)*

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Matrix-Vector Recursive Neural Network (MV-RNN) in detail. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages over the standard RecNN.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)? Explain how gradients are computed at each node of a RecNN. Why is this procedure different from standard backpropagation in feedforward networks?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MV-RNN Details:**

In MV-RNN, every word $w$ in the vocabulary is associated with:
- A **vector** $\mathbf{a}_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — the word's meaning/embedding.
- A **matrix** $A_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ — how the word *transforms* its sibling's meaning.

**Composition Rule at a node with children $L$ and $R$:**

$$\mathbf{p} = f\left(A_L \mathbf{b}_R + A_R \mathbf{b}_L\right)$$

where $\mathbf{b}_L, \mathbf{b}_R$ are the vectors of the children, and $A_L, A_R$ are their respective matrices.

**Intuition:** Words like "very", "extremely", "not" act as **operators**. The matrix $A_{\text{very}}$ represents the linear transformation that "very" applies to whatever noun it modifies (scaling up magnitude in certain feature dimensions).

**Advantages:**
1. Captures **operator semantics** — different words contribute differently.
2. Distinguishes between *function words* (e.g., "the") and *content words* (e.g., "elephant") through their matrix structures.
3. Improves performance on tasks requiring fine-grained semantic distinctions.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Massive parameter count:** Requires storing one $d \times d$ matrix per word in the vocabulary. For $|V| = 100,000$ words and $d = 50$, this is $100,000 \times 2500 = 250$ million parameters just for the matrices.
2. **Memory intensive** — hard to scale to large vocabularies.
3. **Harder to train** due to overfitting risk.
4. **Inference is slower** than plain RecNN.

*(7 marks: 3 composition + 2 advantages + 2 disadvantages)*

**(b) Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS):**

**Definition:** BTS is the gradient descent algorithm for Recursive Neural Networks. Since the same weight matrix $W$ is applied at multiple nodes across a tree, the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to $W$ is the **sum of contributions from all internal nodes** in the tree.

**Gradient Computation:**

For each internal node $n$ with children $c_L^{(n)}$ and $c_R^{(n)}$, and parent output $\mathbf{p}_n$:

$$\delta^{(n)} = \left( \sum_{k \in \text{Children}(n)} W_{\text{slice}}^T \cdot \delta^{(k)} \right) \odot f'(\mathbf{p}_n)$$

The gradient w.r.t. $W$ is:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{n \in \text{internal}} \delta^{(n)} \cdot [\mathbf{c}_L^{(n)}; \mathbf{c}_R^{(n)}]^T$$

**Why Different from Standard Backpropagation:**

| Aspect | Standard BP (Feedforward) | BTS (Recursive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Linear layer chain | Tree |
| **Gradient Aggregation** | Chain rule, single path | Sum over all tree nodes |
| **Where error flows** | From output to input | From root to all leaves |
| **Weight sharing** | Per-layer unique $W$ | Same $W$ at every node |

**Key Difference:** In standard BP, each layer has its own unique weights, so gradients flow along a single chain. In BTS, the **same** matrix $W$ is reused at every internal node, so the parameter update must *accumulate* gradients from the entire tree topology — this summation is unique to RecNNs.

*(7 marks: 3 BTS definition + 2 gradient formula + 2 difference from standard BP)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Recursive" with "Recurrent"** in your answer. Examiners specifically deduct 1-2 marks if you use these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Always draw the parse tree** in Part B questions — even a rough sketch scores you 1-2 marks extra. Without it, you lose the *diagram marks* allocated in the scheme.
> 3. **Mention the activation function explicitly** ($\tanh$) — many students forget this and lose a mark.
> 4. **For RNTN questions**, clearly state the **tensor dimension** ($2d \times 2d \times d$) — this is a frequent 1-mark short-answer spot.
> 5. **In BTS derivations**, emphasize the **sum over all nodes** — this is the conceptual heart of BTS. Writing it as a single-path chain rule (like RNN) loses 2 marks.
> 6. **For MV-RNN**, clearly state that each word has **both a vector AND a matrix** — students often forget the matrix part.
> 7. **Always mention Frege's Principle of Compositionality** when defining RecNN — examiners love this one-liner in 3-mark definitions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam):**

- ✅ RecNN = **tree-structured** neural network; RNN = **sequence/linear** neural network.
- ✅ RecNN exploits **Frege's Compositionality Principle**: meaning of phrase = function of meanings of parts.
- ✅ Composition rule: $\mathbf{p} = f(W[\mathbf{a}; \mathbf{b}] + \mathbf{b})$, with $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2d}$ and $f = \tanh$.
- ✅ Three classical variants: **Standard RecNN, MV-RNN, RNTN**.
- ✅ **MV-RNN:** uses word-level **matrices** to model operator semantics — very high parameter count.
- ✅ **RNTN:** uses a **3-way tensor** $V \in \mathbb{R}^{2d \times 2d \times d}$ for bilinear child interactions; best for negation/contrast.
- ✅ **Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)** sums gradients over all internal tree nodes (not a single path like in RNNs).
- ✅ RecNN requires a **precomputed parse tree** (typically a Stanford-style constituency parse).
- ✅ Classification happens at the **root node** via softmax on $\mathbf{p}_{root}$.
- ✅ Key applications: **Sentiment Analysis, Paraphrase Detection, Natural Language Inference, Relation Extraction**.
- ✅ Computational depth = $O(\log n)$ — parallelizable across tree levels (advantage over RNN).
- ✅ **Loss function** for classification: cross-entropy; for reconstruction: Euclidean distance.
- ✅ **Common 14-mark question pattern:** "Explain RecNN variants and their differences" — prepare comparative tables.
- ✅ **Common 3-mark question pattern:** "Define RecNN and differentiate from RNN" — always include the table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LSTM and GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LSTM and GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by *Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997)*, designed to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in standard RNNs. LSTMs achieve this by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative gate units — the **forget gate**, **input gate**, and **output gate** — that control the flow of information across long temporal sequences.

In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, LSTMs are foundational deep learning models used for sequence modeling tasks such as machine translation, text generation, sentiment classification, named entity recognition, and speech recognition.

### 1.2 Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a simplified gating mechanism proposed by *Cho et al. (2014)* that combines the **forget** and **input gates** of LSTM into a single **update gate**, and merges the **cell state** and **hidden state** into one unified vector. GRU offers comparable performance to LSTM on many NLP benchmarks while being computationally more efficient due to fewer parameters.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Smart Office Secretary**
>
> Imagine you are a busy executive (the **hidden state**) who receives dozens of emails (input tokens) daily. A **smart secretary** decides:
> - Which old emails to **delete** from the filing cabinet (forget gate).
> - Which new emails to **add** to the cabinet (input gate).
> - Which filed emails to **share with you right now** (output gate).
>
> The **filing cabinet** is the **cell state** — long-term memory. The **secretary** is the **gating mechanism** of LSTM. GRU is like a slightly more efficient secretary who uses one decision form (update gate) for both deleting and adding.

### 1.4 Why LSTMs and GRUs Matter in NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard RNNs can only remember information across short contexts. When processing a sentence like *"The clouds were dark, and soon the heavy rain began to pour"*, a vanilla RNN struggles to link *clouds* with *rain* over long distances. **LSTM/GRU maintain selective long-term context**, making them ideal for NLP where meaning depends on distant word relationships.

| Aspect | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gates | None | 3 | 2 |
| Memory States | 1 (hidden) | 2 (cell + hidden) | 1 (merged) |
| Parameters | Low | High | Medium |
| Vanishing Gradient | Yes | Resolved | Resolved |
| Compute Cost | Low | High | Medium |

---

### Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **Cell State (C_t):** The long-term memory highway running through the LSTM chain.
- **Hidden State (h_t):** The short-term working memory output at time *t*.
- **Gate:** A sigmoid neural network layer producing values in **[0, 1]** that controls information flow.
- **Vanishing Gradient:** A training failure mode where gradients become exponentially small, preventing learning of long-range dependencies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LSTM Architecture — Structural Breakdown

The LSTM cell at time step *t* receives three inputs:
- $x_t$ → current input vector
- $h_{t-1}$ → previous hidden state
- $C_{t-1}$ → previous cell state

It produces two outputs:
- $h_t$ → current hidden state
- $C_t$ → current cell state

The three gates operate in a **structured pipeline**:

**Step 1 — Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Decides what information to discard from the cell state.

**Step 2 — Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides which new values will be updated in the cell state.

**Step 3 — Candidate Cell State ($\tilde{C}_t$):** Creates a vector of new candidate values using *tanh*.

**Step 4 — Cell State Update ($C_t$):** Combines the old cell state (scaled by forget gate) with new candidates (scaled by input gate).

**Step 5 — Output Gate ($o_t$):** Decides what parts of the cell state to output as the hidden state.

**Step 6 — Hidden State ($h_t$):** Filters the cell state through *tanh* and multiplies by the output gate.

### 2.2 GRU Architecture — Structural Breakdown

The GRU simplifies LSTM by:
- **Merging cell state and hidden state** into a single vector $h_t$.
- **Combining forget and input gates** into a single **update gate** $z_t$.
- Adding a **reset gate** $r_t$ that controls how much past information to forget when computing the new candidate.

**Step 1 — Update Gate ($z_t$):** Decides how much past information to keep vs. how much new candidate to use.

**Step 2 — Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Decides how much past information to discard when computing the candidate.

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$):** Computes new candidate using reset-gated previous hidden state.

**Step 4 — Hidden State Update ($h_t$):** Linear interpolation between previous state and new candidate, weighted by the update gate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE examinations. Memorize the gate equations and the element-wise multiplication symbol ($\odot$).

| Component | LSTM Equation | GRU Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Merged into update gate |
| Input / Update Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ |
| Reset Gate | Not present | $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ |
| Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Not present (merged) |
| Candidate State | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ |
| State Update | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ |
| Hidden Output | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Same as $h_t$ above |
| Activation Range | $\sigma$: [0, 1], $\tanh$: [-1, 1] | $\sigma$: [0, 1], $\tanh$: [-1, 1] |
| Total Gate Count | **3** | **2** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LSTMs and GRUs form the backbone of **pre-transformer era NLP systems**. They are deployed in:
> - **Google Translate** (early neural MT systems)
> - **Apple Siri / Google Assistant** (speech recognition backbones)
> - **Stock price forecasting** (financial time-series modeling)
> - **Medical text mining** (clinical note analysis)
> - **Music generation** and **video captioning**

Even in the era of **Transformers and LLMs**, understanding LSTMs and GRUs is mandatory because:
1. They appear in **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE question papers** consistently.
2. They are the **conceptual ancestors** of attention mechanisms.
3. They are more **memory-efficient** for edge/embedded NLP deployments.

### 2.5 Why LSTM Solves the Vanishing Gradient Problem

The **cell state** $C_t$ has a near-linear gradient path:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$$

Because $f_t$ is a sigmoid output (mostly close to 1), the gradient does **not** decay exponentially over time. This additive update structure (vs. multiplicative in vanilla RNN) is the **core engineering insight** that enables learning over hundreds of time steps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: LSTM Forward Pass

We will derive the complete forward propagation of an LSTM cell step by step.

**Given:**
- Input at time *t*: $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
- Previous hidden state: $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$
- Previous cell state: $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$
- Weight matrices: $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$
- Bias vectors: $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$

**Step 1 — Concatenate input and previous hidden state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{concat}_t &= [h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}
\end{aligned}
$$

This concatenation combines the prior memory with the new observation into a single feature vector.

**Step 2 — Compute the forget gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_f) \\
\sigma(z) &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sigmoid $\sigma$ squashes the output to [0, 1], where 0 means "completely forget" and 1 means "completely retain."

**Step 3 — Compute the input gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_t &= \sigma(W_i \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gate decides which new values to **write** into the cell state.

**Step 4 — Compute the candidate cell state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_C) \\
\tanh(z) &= \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The *tanh* function maps the candidate values to [-1, 1], allowing the cell state to store both positive and negative information.

**Step 5 — Update the cell state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ → selective retention of old memory.
- $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ → selective addition of new information.
- The $\odot$ symbol denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product**.

**Step 6 — Compute the output gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
o_t &= \sigma(W_o \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_o)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the hidden state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This filtered cell state becomes the output passed to the next time step and to upper network layers.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: GRU Forward Pass

**Given:**
- Input at time *t*: $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
- Previous hidden state: $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$
- Weight matrices: $W_z, W_r, W \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$
- Bias vectors: $b_z, b_r, b \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$

**Step 1 — Concatenate input and previous hidden state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{concat}_t &= [h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the update gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

$z_t$ controls the trade-off: how much to keep the old state vs. adopt the new candidate.

**Step 3 — Compute the reset gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_t &= \sigma(W_r \cdot \text{concat}_t + b_r)
\end{aligned}
$$

$r_t$ determines how much of the past hidden state to "reset" (forget) when computing the new candidate.

**Step 4 — Compute the candidate hidden state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{h}_t &= \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)
\end{aligned}
$$

The reset gate $r_t$ is applied **element-wise** to $h_{t-1}$ **before** concatenation, allowing the model to selectively ignore past context.

**Step 5 — Update the hidden state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key observation:** When $z_t \to 0$, $h_t \approx h_{t-1}$ (full retention). When $z_t \to 1$, $h_t \approx \tilde{h}_t$ (full overwrite). This is mathematically a **convex combination**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LSTM vs GRU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **frequently asked** in KTU ESE as a 7-mark comparison question.

| Criterion | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Year Proposed | 1997 (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber) | 2014 (Cho et al.) |
| Number of Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Memory Mechanism | Separate cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| Forget + Input Combination | Two independent gates | Combined as **update gate** $z_t$ |
| Output Control | Dedicated **output gate** $o_t$ | No output gate (directly exposes $h_t$) |
| Reset Capability | Implicit (via forget gate) | Explicit **reset gate** $r_t$ |
| Parameter Count | $4 \times (h^2 + hd + h)$ | $3 \times (h^2 + hd + h)$ |
| Training Speed | Slower | Faster (~25% fewer parameters) |
| Performance on Long Sequences | Generally superior | Comparable, sometimes better on small data |
| Memory Footprint | Larger | Smaller |
| Best Use Case | Long documents, complex NLP | Resource-constrained deployment, short sequences |

---

### 3.4 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Gradient Flow in LSTM

The **vanishing gradient analysis** is the most important theoretical derivation in this module.

**For vanilla RNN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_0} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \\
&= \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} W^T \cdot \text{diag}(\tanh'(z_t))
\end{aligned}
$$

The product of $T$ Jacobian matrices causes **exponential decay** of the gradient when the largest singular value of $W$ is less than 1.

**For LSTM cell state gradient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} &= f_t \quad \text{(element-wise)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_k} &= \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} f_t \approx \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \sigma(\cdot)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma(\cdot) \in [0, 1]$, the product can still decay — **but** in practice $f_t$ is learned to be close to 1 for relevant features, preventing vanishing. This is the **central engineering breakthrough** of LSTM.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LSTM Cell Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[xt input at time t] --> B[Concatenate]
    H1[h t minus 1 previous hidden] --> B
    B --> C[Forget Gate ft sigmoid]
    B --> D[Input Gate it sigmoid]
    B --> E[Candidate Cell tilde C tanh]
    B --> F[Output Gate ot sigmoid]
    C1[C t minus 1 previous cell] --> G[Cell State Update]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[New Cell State Ct]
    H --> I[tanh]
    F --> J[Element wise multiply]
    I --> J
    J --> K[New Hidden State ht]
    K --> L[Output to next time step]
    K --> M[Output to next layer]
```

### 4.2 GRU Cell Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[xt input at time t] --> B[Concatenate with ht minus 1]
    H1[ht minus 1] --> B
    B --> C1[Update Gate zt sigmoid]
    B --> C2[Reset Gate rt sigmoid]
    C2 --> D[Reset ht minus 1 elementwise]
    H1 --> D
    A --> E[Concatenate with reset ht]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Candidate tilde ht tanh]
    C1 --> G[Linear Interpolation]
    H1 --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[New Hidden State ht]
    H --> I[Output to next time step]
    H --> J[Output to next layer]
```

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LSTM_BLOCK
        L1[3 Gates] --> L2[Forget Input Output]
        L2 --> L3[Cell State Ct]
        L2 --> L4[Hidden State ht]
        L3 --> L4
    end
    subgraph GRU_BLOCK
        G1[2 Gates] --> G2[Update Reset]
        G2 --> G3[Single Hidden State ht]
    end
    LSTM_BLOCK -.->|More Parameters| KTU[KTU 2024 Exam Focus]
    GRU_BLOCK -.->|Fewer Parameters| KTU
```

### 4.4 Information Flow in LSTM (Sequential Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Time t minus 1] -->|Ct minus 1| S2[Time t Cell]
    S1 -->|ht minus 1| S2
    S2 -->|Ct| S3[Time t plus 1 Cell]
    S2 -->|ht| S3
    S3 -->|Ct plus 1| S4[Time t plus 2 Cell]
    S3 -->|ht plus 1| S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **horizontal arrows** in this diagram represent the cell-state highway — the gradient superhighway that distinguishes LSTM from vanilla RNN.

### 4.5 Gating Function Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector] --> B[Sigmoid Layer sigma]
    B --> C{Value in 0 to 1}
    C -->|Close to 0| D[Block Information]
    C -->|Close to 1| E[Pass Information Fully]
    C -->|In between| F[Scale Information Partially]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Define LSTM. List its three gates and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is a type of recurrent neural network designed by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997) to overcome the vanishing gradient problem of standard RNNs by using gated memory cells.
- **Three Gates (2 Marks):**
  1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Decides what information to discard from the cell state using a sigmoid activation.
  2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides which new information to store in the cell state.
  3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** Controls what parts of the cell state are output as the hidden state.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Question]**
> **What is the key architectural difference between LSTM and GRU?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The key architectural difference is that **LSTM has three gates** (forget, input, output) and maintains **two separate state vectors** (cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$), whereas **GRU has only two gates** (update and reset) and uses a **single combined hidden state** $h_t$. The forget and input gates of LSTM are merged into a single **update gate** in GRU, reducing the parameter count and computational cost.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Typical Question]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of an LSTM cell with a neat diagram. Derive the forward propagation equations for all three gates. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain how LSTM overcomes the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LSTM Architecture and Forward Equations (7 Marks):**

**Architecture Description (3 Marks):**
An LSTM cell receives the current input $x_t$, the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$, and the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$. It contains three sigmoid gates and a *tanh* layer that regulate information flow. The output is a new hidden state $h_t$ and a new cell state $C_t$, both passed to the next time step.

**Forward Propagation Equations (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \\
i_t &= \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each equation (built into the answer):**
- The concatenation $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ forms the input feature vector.
- $\sigma$ (sigmoid) outputs values in [0,1] for the gates.
- $\tilde{C}_t$ is the new candidate memory.
- $C_t$ is the updated long-term memory (additive update).
- $h_t$ is the filtered short-term output.

**(b) Overcoming Vanishing Gradient (7 Marks):**

**Problem in Vanilla RNN (3 Marks):**
In a vanilla RNN, the gradient of loss $L$ with respect to an early hidden state $h_0$ involves a product of Jacobian matrices across all time steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_0} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the spectral radius of the weight matrix $W$ is less than 1, the gradient **decays exponentially** with sequence length, preventing the network from learning long-range dependencies.

**LSTM Solution (4 Marks):**
The LSTM cell state $C_t$ has a **linear, additive update path**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the partial derivative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
\end{aligned}
$$

The gradient through the cell state is **element-wise** and does not involve a full matrix product. The forget gate $f_t$ is **learned** to be close to 1 for relevant features, ensuring that gradients do not vanish over hundreds of time steps. This **additive memory structure** is the central engineering insight of LSTM.

---

> **Q3(B). [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice for KTU 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a GRU. Derive all its gate equations. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compare LSTM and GRU in terms of architecture, parameters, training speed, and typical use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GRU Architecture and Gate Equations (7 Marks):**

**Architecture Description (3 Marks):**
The Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), proposed by Cho et al. (2014), is a simplified variant of LSTM. It uses a single hidden state $h_t$ (no separate cell state) and has only two gates: the **update gate $z_t$** and the **reset gate $r_t$**. The update gate controls the interpolation between past and new information, while the reset gate controls how much past information to forget when computing the new candidate.

**Forward Propagation Equations (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \\
r_t &= \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) \\
\tilde{h}_t &= \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b) \\
h_t &= (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- $z_t$ is the update gate; when $z_t \to 1$, the new candidate fully replaces the old state.
- $r_t$ is the reset gate; it zeroes out parts of $h_{t-1}$ before computing the new candidate.
- $h_t$ is a **convex combination** of the old hidden state and the new candidate.

**(b) LSTM vs GRU Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Criterion | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Gates (2 Marks) | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State Vectors (1 Mark) | Cell state $C_t$ + Hidden state $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| Parameters (2 Marks) | $4 \times (h^2 + hd + h)$ | $3 \times (h^2 + hd + h)$ — ~25% fewer |
| Training Speed (1 Mark) | Slower due to more parameters | Faster, more memory-efficient |
| Use Cases (1 Mark) | Long documents, complex NLP tasks | Resource-constrained, short-to-medium sequences, edge deployment |

**Conclusion:** GRU is preferred when **computational efficiency** matters; LSTM is preferred when **modeling very long sequences** is critical.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing the symbol $\odot$ with standard multiplication.** It denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product**, not matrix multiplication. Using $\cdot$ or $*$ instead will lose **2 marks minimum** in the gate equation derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting the concatenation $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$.** Many students write $W_f \cdot h_{t-1}$ instead of $W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]$. Always concatenate the previous hidden state with the current input **before** multiplying by the weight matrix.
> 3. **Mixing up activation functions.** Sigmoid ($\sigma$) is used for **all gates** (output range [0,1]). Tanh is used for the **candidate state** (output range [-1,1]). The final hidden state of LSTM uses $o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$, **not** just $o_t \odot C_t$.
> 4. **Saying GRU "replaces" LSTM.** Wrong framing. GRU is a **simplified alternative**, not a replacement. Examiners specifically look for the phrase "GRU merges the forget and input gates into a single update gate."
> 5. **Skipping the cell state explanation.** Many students forget to mention that LSTM has **two** state vectors. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Not explaining vanishing gradient resolution.** Writing the cell state equation is **not enough** — you must explicitly state that $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$ is element-wise, not a matrix product, and that this prevents exponential decay.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LSTM & GRU — Final Revision Bullets:**
> - LSTM was proposed by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**; GRU was proposed by **Cho et al. (2014)**.
> - LSTM has **3 gates** (forget, input, output); GRU has **2 gates** (update, reset).
> - LSTM has **2 state vectors** ($C_t$ and $h_t$); GRU has **1 state vector** ($h_t$).
> - The activation function for **all gates** is **sigmoid ($\sigma$)**, output range **[0, 1]**.
> - The activation function for **candidate states** is **tanh**, output range **[-1, 1]**.
> - LSTM cell state update: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ (additive).
> - GRU hidden state update: $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ (convex combination).
> - LSTM output: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
> - LSTM has **4 weight matrices** ($W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$); GRU has **3** ($W_z, W_r, W$).
> - GRU has **~25% fewer parameters** than LSTM → faster training.
> - The **element-wise product symbol** is $\odot$, **not** $\cdot$ or $\times$.
> - LSTM solves the **vanishing gradient problem** because the cell state gradient is element-wise: $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$.
> - **Forget gate** decides what to **discard**; **input gate** decides what to **add**; **output gate** decides what to **expose**.
> - In GRU: **update gate** = LSTM's forget + input; **reset gate** controls past memory usage.
> - Applications: machine translation, speech recognition, text generation, sentiment analysis, time-series forecasting.
> - Despite Transformers dominating modern NLP, LSTM/GRU remain **mandatory KTU syllabus** and are widely used in **embedded/edge NLP systems** due to lower memory footprint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common RNN NLP Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common RNN NLP Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common RNN NLP Architectures** refer to the standardized family of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) topologies engineered specifically to process **sequential natural language data** (text, speech, time-series tokens). They address the inherent limitations of vanilla RNNs—namely the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem** and the inability to capture **long-range dependencies**—through specialized gating, bidirectional flow, and encoder-decoder frameworks.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome mapping: **CO4 – Apply deep learning architectures to NLP tasks**), the canonical architectures classified as "Common RNN NLP Architectures" are:

1. **Vanilla / Elman RNN** — The baseline sequential model.
2. **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** — Gated cell-state architecture.
3. **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** — Lightweight gated variant.
4. **Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN / Bi-LSTM / Bi-GRU)** — Past + Future context.
5. **Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Models** — Variable-length input/output mapping.
6. **Attention-Augmented Seq2Seq** — Dynamic context vector weighting.

The standard **hidden state recurrence** governing all of them is given by the generic form:

$$
h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t; \theta)
$$

where $h_t$ is the hidden state at time $t$, $x_t$ is the input token embedding, and $f$ is the architecture-specific non-linear transformation parameterized by $\theta$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Reading a Novel with a Limited Memory Notepad**
> 
> Imagine you are reading a 500-page novel, but you can only hold **one sticky note** in your hand at a time. By page 100, you've forgotten the protagonist's name on page 1 — this is the **Vanilla RNN's** plight (short-term memory bias).
> 
> Now, suppose you have a smart notepad with **three colored tabs**:
> - A **GREEN tab** ("write" gate) decides what new information to record.
> - A **RED tab** ("forget" gate) decides what old information to erase.
> - A **BLUE tab** ("output" gate) decides what to show the next page.
> 
> This is the **LSTM** — a librarian that intelligently curates memory.
> 
> The **GRU** is a junior librarian with only two tabs (reset + update) — faster but slightly less expressive.
> 
> A **Bi-RNN** is like reading the novel *forward* and *backward* simultaneously to grasp context from both sides.
> 
> An **Encoder–Decoder** is a *translator* who first reads the whole French sentence (encoder) into a mental summary, then generates the English sentence word-by-word (decoder).

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Hyperparameters (KTU Standard Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Sequence length (timesteps) | 50 – 512 |
| $d$ | Embedding / hidden dimension | 128 – 512 |
| $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Hidden state vector at time $t$ | — |
| $c_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Cell state (LSTM only) | — |
| $W, U, b$ | Learnable weight matrices and bias | — |
| $\sigma$ | Sigmoid activation $\in (0,1)$ | Logistic function |
| $\tanh$ | Hyperbolic tangent $\in (-1,1)$ | Bounded squashing |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A 14-mark question on LSTM/GRU almost always tests your ability to write the **gate equations** and explain the **role of each gate**. Memorize the symbol table above — it appears verbatim in answer scripts valued highly by examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Common RNN Architectures

The architectures are best understood along **three orthogonal design axes**:

- **Depth axis** — Stacking multiple recurrent layers (deep RNN).
- **Directionality axis** — Unidirectional vs. Bidirectional flow.
- **Memory mechanism axis** — Vanilla (no gates) vs. Gated (LSTM/GRU) vs. Attention.

---

## 2.2 Vanilla (Elman) RNN — Operational Breakdown

The simplest recurrent cell computes the hidden state as:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + W_{xh} \, x_t + b_h)
$$

$$
y_t = W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y
$$

**Why this fails:** During Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), gradients are multiplied by $W_{hh}$ at every timestep. If the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is $< 1$ → **vanishing gradient**; if $> 1$ → **exploding gradient**. This is the **KTU-favorite 3-mark question**.

---

## 2.3 LSTM — The Gated Memory Cell

LSTM introduces a **cell state** $c_t$ (the "long-term memory highway") and **three gates**:

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(Forget Gate)}
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(Input Gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{c}_t = \tanh(W_c \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c) \quad \text{(Candidate Cell State)}
$$

$$
c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t \quad \text{(Cell State Update)}
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(Output Gate)}
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t) \quad \text{(Hidden State Output)}
$$

Here $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**. All gates output values in $(0,1)$, acting as soft switches.

---

## 2.4 GRU — The Simplified Gated Variant

GRU merges the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** and exposes the previous hidden state directly via a **reset gate**:

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \quad \text{(Update Gate)}
$$

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) \quad \text{(Reset Gate)}
$$

$$
