\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h) \quad \text{(Candidate Hidden State)}
$$

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \quad \text{(Hidden State Update)}
$$

**GRU vs LSTM:** GRU has **~25% fewer parameters** (2 gates vs 3, no separate cell state), trains faster, and often matches LSTM on small/medium datasets.

---

## 2.5 Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN)

Two independent RNNs process the sequence in opposite directions; their hidden states are concatenated:

$$
\vec{h}_t = \text{RNN}_{\text{fwd}}(x_t, \vec{h}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{h}_t = \text{RNN}_{\text{bwd}}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1})
$$

$$
h_t = [\vec{h}_t \, ; \, \overleftarrow{h}_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Bi-RNN requires the **entire input sequence upfront**. It is therefore **incompatible with real-time streaming or autoregressive generation**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark distinction.

---

## 2.6 Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture

Used for **variable-length-to-variable-length** tasks (machine translation, summarization, QA).

**Encoder phase:**

$$
h_t^{\text{enc}} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{enc}}(x_t, h_{t-1}^{\text{enc}}), \quad t = 1 \dots T_x
$$

The final hidden state $h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$ is the **context vector** $c$ — a fixed-size bottleneck summarizing the entire source.

**Decoder phase:**

$$
h_t^{\text{dec}} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{dec}}(y_{t-1}, h_{t-1}^{\text{dec}}, c)
$$

$$
y_t = \text{softmax}(W_s \, h_t^{\text{dec}} + b_s)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Bottleneck problem:** When $T_x$ is large (e.g., a long document), the single context vector $c$ cannot retain all information. This motivated the **Attention Mechanism**, which lets the decoder attend to *all* encoder states $\lbrace h_1^{\text{enc}}, \dots, h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}} \rbrace$ dynamically.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Architecture | # Gates | Key Equations | Parameter Count (per cell) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | 0 | $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$ | $d(d + \vert V\vert + 1)$ | Toy tasks, short sequences |
| LSTM | 3 | $f_t, i_t, o_t$ gates; $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ | $4 \times d(d + \vert V\vert + 1)$ | Long text, MT, speech |
| GRU | 2 | $z_t, r_t$ gates; $h_t = (1 - z_t) h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ | $3 \times d(d + \vert V\vert + 1)$ | Faster training, small data |
| Bi-RNN/LSTM/GRU | Same as base | $h_t = [\vec{h}_t ; \overleftarrow{h}_t]$ | $2 \times$ base | POS tagging, NER (offline) |
| Encoder–Decoder | — | $c = h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$; $y_t = \text{softmax}(W_s h_t^{\text{dec}})$ | Sum of enc + dec | MT, summarization |
| Seq2Seq + Attention | — | $\alpha_t = \text{softmax}(e_t)$; $c_t = \sum \alpha_{t,i} h_i^{\text{enc}}$ | + Attention params | Modern NMT baseline |

> Where $\vert V\vert$ = vocabulary size of input embedding, $d$ = hidden dimension.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vanishing Gradient in Vanilla RNN

We derive why vanilla RNNs fail on long sequences. Consider the loss $\mathcal{L}$ for a single timestep $t$, and the gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to hidden state $h_{t_0}$ (some earlier timestep):

**Step 1 — Chain rule expansion across $T$ timesteps:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t_0}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \prod_{k=t_0+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand each Jacobian term** (using the $\tanh$ recurrence):

$$
\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} = \text{diag}\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(W_{hh} h_{k-1} + W_{xh} x_k)\bigr) \cdot W_{hh}
$$

**Step 3 — Bounding each factor:** Since $\tanh' \in (0,1]$ and $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert$ has spectral radius $\rho$, each Jacobian is bounded by $\rho$. Hence:

$$
\biggl\Vert \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \biggr\Vert \leq \rho
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric product across $T$ steps:**

$$
\biggl\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t_0}} \biggr\Vert \leq \rho^{(t - t_0)} \cdot \Vert \mathcal{L}'_t \Vert
$$

**Step 5 — Limiting behavior:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \rho < 1 \;&\Rightarrow\; \rho^{(t - t_0)} \to 0 \text{ as } (t - t_0) \to \infty \quad \text{(Vanishing Gradient)} \\
\text{If } \rho > 1 \;&\Rightarrow\; \rho^{(t - t_0)} \to \infty \text{ as } (t - t_0) \to \infty \quad \text{(Exploding Gradient)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LSTM's fix:** The cell-state update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ introduces an **additive path** (the $+ i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ term) that bypasses the multiplicative Jacobian chain, enabling gradient flow over hundreds of steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Attention Score (Bahdanau Style)

The additive attention mechanism computes a dynamic context vector $c_t$ for decoder step $t$:

**Step 1 — Compute alignment scores** $e_{t,i}$ between decoder state $s_{t-1}$ and each encoder state $h_i^{\text{enc}}$:

$$
e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a \, s_{t-1} + U_a \, h_i^{\text{enc}})
$$

**Step 2 — Softmax-normalize across all encoder positions** to obtain attention weights:

$$
\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,j})}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted sum of encoder states** produces the time-specific context:

$$
c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} \, h_i^{\text{enc}}
$$

**Step 4 — Decoder consumes the context** alongside previous output:

$$
s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, [y_{t-1} ; c_t])
$$

$$
y_t = \text{softmax}(W_s \, s_t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "derive the attention mechanism", always start with the **alignment score** $e_{t,i}$ and explicitly state that the softmax ensures $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$, making it a valid probability distribution over source positions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU | Bi-LSTM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Long-term dependency | Poor | Excellent | Very Good | Excellent |
| Gates | 0 | 3 (f, i, o) | 2 (z, r) | 3 (f, i, o) × 2 |
| Parameters | $d^2$ | $4d^2$ | $3d^2$ | $8d^2$ |
| Training speed | Fast | Slow | Medium | Slowest |
| Real-time use | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Sequence length sweet spot | $< 30$ | $50 – 500$ | $50 – 300$ | $50 – 500$ |
| Exploding gradient risk | High | Low | Low | Low |
| KTU exam frequency | Medium | **Highest** | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Family Tree of Common RNN Architectures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Recurrent Neural Networks] --> B[Vanilla Elman RNN]
    A --> C[Gated Architectures]
    A --> D[Structural Variants]
    B --> B1[Single hidden state]
    B --> B2[BPTT suffers vanishing gradient]
    C --> E[LSTM]
    C --> F[GRU]
    E --> E1[Forget Gate]
    E --> E2[Input Gate]
    E --> E3[Output Gate]
    E --> E4[Cell State c_t]
    F --> F1[Update Gate]
    F --> F2[Reset Gate]
    D --> G[Bidirectional RNN]
    D --> H[Deep Stacked RNN]
    D --> I[Encoder Decoder Seq2Seq]
    G --> G1[Forward pass]
    G --> G2[Backward pass]
    G --> G3[Concatenation of states]
    I --> J[Context Vector Bottleneck]
    I --> K[Attention Mechanism]
    K --> K1[Bahdanau Additive]
    K --> K2[Luong Dot Product]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid: LSTM Cell Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Input x_t] --> C1[Concat with h_t-1]
    H[Previous Hidden h_t-1] --> C1
    C1 --> FG[Forget Gate f_t sigmoid]
    C1 --> IG[Input Gate i_t sigmoid]
    C1 --> OG[Output Gate o_t sigmoid]
    C1 --> CC[Candidate Cell tanh]
    CCV[Previous Cell c_t-1] --> M1[Multiply]
    FG --> M1
    M1 --> ADD[Add]
    IG --> MUL[Multiply]
    CC --> MUL
    MUL --> ADD
    ADD --> NC[New Cell c_t]
    NC --> T1[tanh]
    OG --> M2[Multiply]
    T1 --> M2
    M2 --> NH[New Hidden h_t]
    NC --> CN[Carry c_t to next step]
    NH --> HN[Carry h_t to next step]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid: Seq2Seq Encoder-Decoder with Attention Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Source Token x1] --> E1[Encoder LSTM 1]
    S2[Source Token x2] --> E2[Encoder LSTM 2]
    S3[Source Token x3] --> E3[Encoder LSTM 3]
    E1 --> H1[h1 enc]
    E2 --> H2[h2 enc]
    E3 --> H3[h3 enc]
    H1 --> ATT[Attention Layer]
    H2 --> ATT
    H3 --> ATT
    ATT --> CT[Dynamic Context c_t]
    D0[Decoder Initial s0] --> D1[Decoder LSTM step 1]
    CT --> D1
    Y0[Start token] --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Decoder LSTM step 2]
    CT --> D2
    D2 --> D3[Decoder LSTM step 3]
    CT --> D3
    D1 --> O1[Output y1]
    D2 --> O2[Output y2]
    D3 --> O3[Output y3]
    O1 --> SOFT1[Softmax]
    O2 --> SOFT2[Softmax]
    O3 --> SOFT3[Softmax]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Bi-LSTM for Sequence Labeling)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Sentence Tokens] --> EMB[Embedding Layer d=300]
    EMB --> FW[Forward LSTM Layer]
    EMB --> BW[Backward LSTM Layer]
    FW --> CON[Concatenation Layer 2d]
    BW --> CON
    CON --> DENSE[Dense Hidden Layer ReLU]
    DENSE --> DROP[Dropout 0.5]
    DROP --> OUT[Softmax per token]
    OUT --> TAG[POS / NER Tags]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Bi-LSTM + Softmax is the canonical architecture for **POS Tagging** and **Named Entity Recognition** (Module 4 outcomes). If asked to "design an architecture for sequence labeling", this is the answer template worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an LSTM cell. How does it overcome the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs?*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

- An LSTM cell contains three gates — **forget** $f_t$, **input** $i_t$, and **output** $o_t$ — plus a **cell state** $c_t$ and **hidden state** $h_t$.
- The cell state acts as a memory highway; the forget gate $f_t$ decides what to erase from $c_{t-1}$, the input gate $i_t$ decides what new information $\tilde{c}_t$ to write.
- **Vanishing gradient fix:** The cell-state update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ provides an **additive residual path**, so gradients during BPTT flow through the additive branch without being multiplied by $W$ repeatedly, preserving long-range signal.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*Compare GRU and LSTM. Mention any two advantages of GRU over LSTM.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 (f, i, o) | 2 (z, r) |
| Cell state | Separate $c_t$ | None (merged with $h_t$) |
| Parameters | $4d^2$ | $3d^2$ |

**Two advantages of GRU over LSTM:**
1. **Fewer parameters** (≈25% less) → faster training, less memory, suited for small datasets.
2. **Simpler architecture** → easier to implement and tune; often matches LSTM performance on shorter sequences.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Note:** Module 4 of PECST862 typically carries an internal choice. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions, each with 7-mark sub-parts, exactly matching the KTU valuation pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) architecture for machine translation in detail. Show how a context vector is generated and used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the bottleneck problem in Seq2Seq? Explain the Bahdanau attention mechanism with its mathematical formulation, and show how it solves the bottleneck. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution A(a): Encoder–Decoder Architecture

**Step 1 — Definition:** The Encoder–Decoder model maps a variable-length source sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_{T_x})$ to a variable-length target sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_{T_y})$, where typically $T_x \neq T_y$.

**Step 2 — Encoder phase:** An LSTM (or GRU) processes the source tokens one by one:

$$
h_t^{\text{enc}} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{enc}}(x_t, h_{t-1}^{\text{enc}}), \quad t = 1, 2, \dots, T_x
$$

**Step 3 — Context vector construction:** The final encoder hidden state encapsulates the entire source:

$$
c = h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}} \in \mathbb{R}^d
$$

**Step 4 — Decoder phase:** The decoder LSTM generates target tokens auto-regressively, conditioned on the context vector $c$ and previously generated tokens:

$$
h_t^{\text{dec}} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{dec}}(y_{t-1}, h_{t-1}^{\text{dec}}, c)
$$

$$
y_t = \text{softmax}\bigl(W_s \, h_t^{\text{dec}} + b_s\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Training:** Cross-entropy loss between predicted $\hat{y}_t$ and gold $y_t^*$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 6 — Inference:** Greedy decoding or Beam Search selects tokens until the `<EOS>` symbol is produced.

---

#### Solution A(b): Bahdanau Attention Mechanism

**Step 1 — Bottleneck Problem:** When $T_x$ is large, the single vector $c$ cannot retain fine-grained alignment between source and target. Performance degrades sharply for long sentences.

**Step 2 — Bahdanau's Idea:** Instead of a single static $c$, compute a **time-varying context** $c_t$ for each decoder step $t$, allowing soft alignment to all encoder positions.

**Step 3 — Alignment score:**

$$
e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a \, s_{t-1} + U_a \, h_i^{\text{enc}})
$$

where $s_{t-1}$ is the previous decoder hidden state, and $h_i^{\text{enc}}$ is the $i$-th encoder state.

**Step 4 — Attention weights via softmax:**

$$
\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,j})}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic context vector:**

$$
c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} \, h_i^{\text{enc}}
$$

**Step 6 — Decoder update with attention:**

$$
s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, [y_{t-1} ; c_t])
$$

**Step 7 — How it solves the bottleneck:** The decoder now has direct access to **all** encoder states through $c_t$, weighted by learned relevance. Long sentences are no longer crushed into a single vector — alignment is dynamic and fine-grained, yielding substantial BLEU improvements in NMT.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the vanishing gradient problem in a vanilla RNN using the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) formulation. State the role of LSTM cell state in mitigating this. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain Bidirectional LSTM with a diagram. Why is Bi-LSTM unsuitable for real-time applications? List two NLP tasks where Bi-LSTM gives state-of-the-art performance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution B(a): Vanishing Gradient Derivation

**Step 1 — Loss gradient with respect to a distant hidden state $h_{t_0}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t_0}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \prod_{k=t_0+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian of the $\tanh$ recurrence:**

$$
\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} = \text{diag}\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)\bigr) \cdot W_{hh}^{\top}
$$

**Step 3 — Norm bound** with spectral radius $\rho = \sigma_{\max}(W_{hh})$:

$$
\biggl\Vert \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \biggr\Vert \leq \rho
$$

**Step 4 — Product over $T = t - t_0$ steps:**

$$
\biggl\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t_0}} \biggr\Vert \leq \rho^T \, \Vert \mathcal{L}'_t \Vert
$$

**Step 5 — Limiting case:**

$$
\rho < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \rho^T \to 0 \text{ exponentially as } T \uparrow \quad \text{(Vanishing)}
$$

$$
\rho > 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \rho^T \to \infty \text{ exponentially as } T \uparrow \quad \text{(Exploding)}
$$

**Step 6 — LSTM's mitigation:** The LSTM cell state $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ creates an **additive** gradient pathway: $\partial c_t / \partial c_{t-1} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ — a diagonal matrix of forget-gate values, **not** multiplied by $W_{hh}$. With $f_t$ near 1, gradients flow unimpeded across hundreds of timesteps.

---

#### Solution B(b): Bidirectional LSTM

**Step 1 — Architecture:** Two independent LSTM layers process the same input in opposite directions.

**Step 2 — Forward pass:**

$$
\vec{h}_t = \text{LSTM}_{\text{fwd}}(x_t, \vec{h}_{t-1}), \quad t = 1 \dots T
$$

**Step 3 — Backward pass:**

$$
\overleftarrow{h}_t = \text{LSTM}_{\text{bwd}}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1}), \quad t = T \dots 1
$$

**Step 4 — Concatenation:**

$$
h_t = [\vec{h}_t \, ; \, \overleftarrow{h}_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}
$$

**Step 5 — Why unsuitable for real-time applications:** Bi-LSTM requires the **complete input sequence** to compute $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ (it needs future tokens $x_{t+1}, \dots, x_T$). Therefore it cannot be used for **streaming, online, or autoregressive** inference.

**Step 6 — Two SOTA NLP tasks for Bi-LSTM:**
1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Each token is classified using left + right context.
2. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — Tagging benefits from both preceding and following words.
   *(Additional: Speech Recognition, Chunking, Word Sense Disambiguation.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark pitfalls costing students 2–4 marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the softmax in attention weights.** Examiners explicitly check $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$. If you write $c_t = \sum \alpha_{t,i} h_i^{\text{enc}}$ without showing the softmax, you lose 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Conflating cell state and hidden state in LSTM.** They are *different* vectors: $c_t$ is long-term memory, $h_t$ is the working/output state. Mixing them up is a recurring deduction.
> 
> 3. **Drawing unidirectional RNN diagrams when Bi-RNN is asked.** A Bi-RNN diagram **must** show two arrows in opposite directions and a concatenation block.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the vanishing gradient derivation.** A direct statement like "LSTM fixes vanishing gradient" without the $\rho^T$ bound is incomplete — examiners expect the BPTT chain rule expansion.
> 
> 5. **Confusing GRU update gate with LSTM output gate.** GRU has only **update ($z$)** and **reset ($r$)** — no separate output gate. Writing $o_t$ for GRU will be marked wrong.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Common RNN NLP Architectures**

- ☐ **Vanilla RNN** recurrence: $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$; suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients over long sequences.
- ☐ **LSTM** has 3 gates — **Forget ($f_t$)**, **Input ($i_t$)**, **Output ($o_t$)** — and a separate **cell state $c_t$**. Equation: $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$.
- ☐ **GRU** has 2 gates — **Update ($z_t$)** and **Reset ($r_t$)** — no separate cell state. Equation: $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.
- ☐ **LSTM mitigates vanishing gradient** through the **additive cell-state path** that bypasses repeated multiplication by $W_{hh}$.
- ☐ **Bi-RNN / Bi-LSTM** concatenates forward and backward hidden states: $h_t = [\vec{h}_t ; \overleftarrow{h}_t]$; requires full sequence — **not real-time**.
- ☐ **Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq)** uses a fixed-size **context vector $c = h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$**; suitable for variable-length translation/summarization.
- ☐ **Bahdanau Attention** computes alignment scores $e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i^{\text{enc}})$, then $\alpha_{t,i} = \text{softmax}(e_{t,i})$, then $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i^{\text{enc}}$.
- ☐ **Luong Attention** variants: **dot**, **general**, **concat** — score $e_{t,i} = h_i^{\text{enc}\top} W \, s_t$ (general) is the most cited.
- ☐ **GRU vs LSTM** — GRU: fewer parameters, faster, often comparable performance; LSTM: more expressive, better on very long sequences.
- ☐ **KTU hot topics** — gate equations (must be memorized), vanishing gradient derivation, Bi-RNN real-time limitation, attention softmax constraint, encoder–decoder bottleneck problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder-Decoder Model with RNNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encoder-Decoder Model with RNNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

The **Encoder-Decoder Model with RNNs**, also widely known as the **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) model**, is a neural network architecture designed to map a variable-length input sequence to a variable-length output sequence. It was formally introduced by Sutskever, Vinyals, and Le (2014) and Cho et al. (2014) and forms the foundational backbone of modern Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and many other NLP applications.

The architecture is composed of two coupled Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block**
> - **Encoder RNN:** A recurrent network that compresses an input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ into a fixed-dimensional latent representation called the **Context Vector** (also referred to as the *Thought Vector* or *Sentence Embedding*).
> - **Decoder RNN:** A recurrent network that generates the output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y})$ one token at a time, conditioned entirely on the context vector produced by the encoder.

## 1.2 The Engineering Problem It Solves

Traditional RNNs and MLPs require **fixed-size** inputs and outputs. However, real-world NLP tasks inherently deal with **variable-length** sequences.

- An English sentence can be of length 5 words; its French translation can be of length 7 words.
- A document can be summarized into 2 sentences or 20 sentences.
- Question: *"How is the weather?"* → Answer: *"Sunny"* (length 1) vs. Answer: *"It is currently sunny with mild winds."* (length 7).

The Seq2Seq model elegantly decouples the input length from the output length.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Translator

Imagine you are a **human translator** sitting in a soundproof booth. A speaker delivers a sentence in **Hindi**:

> *"मुझे कल दिल्ली जाना है"*

To translate this to English, you do **not** translate word-by-word simultaneously. Instead, you:

1. **LISTEN** to the entire sentence (Encoder Phase).
2. Build a **mental understanding / summary** of the meaning in your brain (Context Vector).
3. **SPEAK** the English translation one word at a time, where each spoken word depends on what you understood and what you said previously (Decoder Phase).

> *"I have to go to Delhi tomorrow."*

The "mental summary" is the **Context Vector**, and the process of "speaking word-by-word based on prior words" is the **autoregressive** nature of the decoder RNN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The Context Vector $\mathbf{c}$ is the **only information pathway** between the encoder and decoder in the basic Seq2Seq model. All meaning of the source sentence must be compressed into this single fixed-size vector.

## 1.4 The Standard Architectural Pipeline

The complete pipeline can be summarized as:

$$\text{Input Tokens} \rightarrow \text{Embedding Layer} \rightarrow \text{Encoder RNN} \rightarrow \text{Context Vector } \mathbf{c} \rightarrow \text{Decoder RNN} \rightarrow \text{Output Tokens}$$

In this module, we will deeply analyze each stage, its mathematical formulation, and its limitations (which motivate the **Bahdanau Attention** mechanism studied next in Module 4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Stage by Stage

The Seq2Seq model operates in three well-defined stages. Let us dissect each one rigorously.

### Stage A: The Embedding Layer

Raw words are discrete symbols. They must be projected into a continuous **dense vector space** of dimension $d_{emb}$.

- Input token $x_t \in \mathbb{V}_{\text{src}}$ (source vocabulary)
- Output embedding $\mathbf{e}_t = E \cdot x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{emb}}$

where $E \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{emb} \times \vert \mathbb{V}_{\text{src}} \vert}$ is the **embedding matrix** (a trainable parameter).

### Stage B: The Encoder RNN

The encoder processes the embedded input sequence **left-to-right** and updates its hidden state at every time step.

The standard update rule for an **LSTM cell** (the most common choice due to vanishing gradient resistance) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{i}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{i} [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\bigr) \quad &\text{(Input gate)} \\
\mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{f} [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\bigr) \quad &\text{(Forget gate)} \\
\mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{o} [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\bigr) \quad &\text{(Output gate)} \\
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t &= \tanh\bigl(W_{c} [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c\bigr) \quad &\text{(Candidate memory)} \\
\mathbf{c}_t &= \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t \quad &\text{(Cell state update)} \\
\mathbf{h}_t &= \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t) \quad &\text{(Hidden state output)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a vanilla **RNN**, the update simplifies to $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(W_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$. The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts both formulations.

### Stage C: The Context Vector

After processing all $T_x$ tokens, the encoder summarizes the entire input sequence into a single vector. The most common aggregation is:

$$\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$$

i.e., the **final hidden state** of the encoder is taken as the context vector. In LSTM-based encoders, often the final cell state $\mathbf{c}_{T_x}$ is also used.

### Stage D: The Decoder RNN (Autoregressive Generation)

The decoder is initialized with $\mathbf{s}_0 = \mathbf{c}$ and generates the output sequence **one token at a time**, conditioned on:
1. The context vector $\mathbf{c}$
2. The previous hidden state $\mathbf{s}_{t-1}$
3. The previously generated token $y_{t-1}$

The update and output equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{s}_t &= f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c}) \\
\hat{y}_t &= \text{softmax}\bigl(W_{s} \mathbf{s}_t + \mathbf{b}_s\bigr) \\
y_t &= \arg\max(\hat{y}_t) \quad \text{(during inference)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The decoder also uses an **\<SOS\>** (Start of Sequence) token as $y_0$ to begin generation, and stops upon producing an **\<EOS\>** (End of Sequence) token.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Component | Mathematical Expression | Dimensions / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Token Embedding | $\mathbf{e}_t = E \cdot x_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_{emb}}$ | Converts discrete symbols to dense vectors |
| 2 | Encoder Hidden Update (vanilla RNN) | $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + W_{xh}\mathbf{e}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_{h}}$ | Sequential feature extraction |
| 3 | Context Vector | $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_{h}}$ | Compressed source sentence representation |
| 4 | Decoder Hidden Update | $\mathbf{s}_t = f(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_{h}}$ | Autoregressive target sequence generation |
| 5 | Output Distribution | $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_s \mathbf{s}_t + \mathbf{b}_s)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathbb{V}_{\text{tgt}} \vert}$ | Probability over target vocabulary |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = - \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log \hat{y}_t[y_t^{\star}]$ | Scalar (nats) | Training objective for sequence prediction |
| 7 | Total Sequence Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}$ | Scalar | Batch-level loss for gradient descent |
| 8 | BPTT Gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W}$ | Scalar sum | Backpropagation through time across $T$ steps |
| 9 | Greedy Decoding | $y_t^{\star} = \arg\max_{y} \; P(y \vert y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$ | Token ID | Fast inference strategy |
| 10 | Beam Search Width | $k \in \{1, 5, 10\}$ (typical values) | Integer | Trades accuracy for inference cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Note the dimensions carefully. If the encoder hidden size is $d_h = 256$ and input embedding size is $d_{emb} = 128$, then $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{256 \times 128}$ and $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{256 \times 256}$. Mixing dimensions is a common error that costs marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

The encoder-decoder RNN architecture powers:

- **Neural Machine Translation (NMT):** Google Translate (pre-Transformer era), OpenNMT.
- **Abstractive Text Summarization:** Rush et al. (2015) — feed attention-based decoder.
- **Speech Recognition:** Mapping acoustic frame sequences to phoneme/word sequences.
- **Image Captioning:** CNN as encoder, RNN as decoder (Vinyals et al., 2015).
- **Chatbots / Dialogue Systems:** Mapping user utterance → system response.
- **Code Generation:** Mapping natural language intent → source code tokens.

## 2.4 The Critical Limitation — The Information Bottleneck

The **single fixed-size context vector** $\mathbf{c}$ must encode the **entire meaning** of the source sentence. For long sentences (say, 50+ words), this creates a severe **information bottleneck**.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Bottleneck Problem:** Empirical results (Bahdanau et al., 2015) show that vanilla encoder-decoder performance **degrades sharply** as source sentence length increases. For sentences longer than ~30 words, BLEU scores drop precipitously because $\mathbf{c}$ cannot retain all necessary details. This directly motivates the **Attention Mechanism** — covered as the next topic in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Forward Pass of the Encoder — Exhaustive Derivation

**Given:** Input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3) = (\text{“I”}, \text{“love”}, \text{“NLP”})$, embedding dimension $d_{emb}=4$, hidden dimension $d_h=3$, using a vanilla RNN.

**Step 1: Convert tokens to embeddings.**

We look up the embedding matrix $E$ to obtain $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \mathbf{e}_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$. Assume the lookups yield:

$$\mathbf{e}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \\ 0.7 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{e}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 1.0 \\ 1.1 \\ 1.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Initialize hidden state.**

The encoder is typically initialized to a zero vector:

$$\mathbf{h}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute first hidden state ($t=1$).**

Assume $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$ and $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ and $\mathbf{b}_h \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ are randomly initialized. For a vanilla RNN:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{h}_1 &= \tanh\bigl(W_{hh} \mathbf{h}_0 + W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\bigr) \\
&= \tanh\bigl(\mathbf{0} + W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\bigr) \\
&= \tanh\bigl(W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_1 + \mathbf{b}_h\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ is critical — the term $W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_0$ vanishes, and only the embedding contributes.

**Step 4: Compute subsequent hidden states ($t=2, 3$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{h}_2 &= \tanh\bigl(W_{hh} \mathbf{h}_1 + W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_2 + \mathbf{b}_h\bigr) \\
\mathbf{h}_3 &= \tanh\bigl(W_{hh} \mathbf{h}_2 + W_{xh} \mathbf{e}_3 + \mathbf{b}_h\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Derive the context vector.**

The final hidden state becomes the context vector:

$$\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_3 = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$$

> **Logic Note:** $\mathbf{c}$ is a function of all preceding hidden states by induction, so it carries information about every token in the input sequence. However, due to the recurrent compression, information from $x_1$ is **more weakly represented** in $\mathbf{c}$ than information from $x_3$.

## 3.2 Forward Pass of the Decoder — Exhaustive Derivation

**Given:** Context vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$, target sequence prefix $y_0 = \langle \text{SOS}\rangle$.

**Step 1: Initialize decoder hidden state.**

$$\mathbf{s}_0 = \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$$

**Step 2: Embed the start token.**

The decoder also has an embedding matrix $E_{\text{dec}}$. Let $\tilde{\mathbf{e}}_0 = E_{\text{dec}} \cdot y_0$.

**Step 3: First decoder step ($t=1$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{s}_1 &= \tanh\bigl(W_{sh} \mathbf{s}_0 + W_{yh} \tilde{\mathbf{e}}_0 + W_{ch} \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{b}_s\bigr) \\
\hat{\mathbf{y}}_1 &= \text{softmax}\bigl(W_{os} \mathbf{s}_1 + \mathbf{b}_o\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $W_{ch}$ is a learnable projection that allows $\mathbf{c}$ to be re-injected at **every** decoder step (a common architectural variant called *context conditioning*).

**Step 4: Sample the first output token.**

$$y_1 = \arg\max_i \; \hat{\mathbf{y}}_1[i]$$

**Step 5: Iterate until \<EOS\>.**

The decoder continues this loop for $T_y$ steps, using its **own previous output** as the next input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{s}_t &= \tanh\bigl(W_{sh} \mathbf{s}_{t-1} + W_{yh} \tilde{\mathbf{e}}_{t-1} + W_{ch} \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{b}_s\bigr) \\
\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t &= \text{softmax}\bigl(W_{os} \mathbf{s}_t + \mathbf{b}_o\bigr) \\
y_t &= \arg\max_i \; \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[i]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Loss Function Derivation — Cross-Entropy Across the Sequence

**Given:** Ground-truth target sequence $y^{\star} = (y_1^{\star}, y_2^{\star}, \ldots, y_{T_y}^{\star})$, predicted distributions $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$.

**Step 1: Per-token cross-entropy loss.**

The cross-entropy between the predicted distribution $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$ and the one-hot ground truth $y_t^{\star}$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}_t = - \sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathbb{V}_{\text{tgt}} \vert} \mathbb{1}[i = y_t^{\star}] \cdot \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[i] = -\log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[y_t^{\star}]$$

**Step 2: Sequence-level loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \mathcal{L}_t = - \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[y_t^{\star}]$$

**Step 3: Mini-batch loss.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}$$

**Step 4: Gradient computation via BPTT.**

The gradient w.r.t. the output projection $W_{os}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{os}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{os}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \bigl(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t - \mathbf{1}_{y_t^{\star}}\bigr) \mathbf{s}_t^{\top}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the classic softmax + cross-entropy gradient. The error at step $t$ flows **backward in time** through the decoder hidden states (BPTT) and then **across the context vector** into the encoder, updating all parameters via gradient descent.

## 3.4 Teacher Forcing — Analytical Justification

**Problem:** During training, if the decoder makes an early mistake (e.g., predicts wrong token at $t=1$), the error compounds because the wrong token is fed back as input at $t=2$, leading to **exposure bias**.

**Solution — Teacher Forcing:**

With probability $p_{\text{TF}}$ (typically 1.0 early in training, annealed later), replace the decoder's predicted input with the **ground-truth token**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\mathbf{e}}_{t-1} &= 
\begin{cases}
E_{\text{dec}} \cdot y_{t-1}^{\star} & \text{with probability } p_{\text{TF}} \\
E_{\text{dec}} \cdot y_{t-1} & \text{with probability } 1 - p_{\text{TF}}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

This stabilizes training and accelerates convergence.

## 3.5 Inference Strategies — Greedy vs. Beam Search

### Greedy Decoding (Default)

$$y_t^{\star} = \arg\max_{y} \; P(y \vert y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Drawback:** Locally optimal ≠ globally optimal. A high-probability early token may force a low-probability tail.

### Beam Search (KTU High-Yield)

Maintain top-$k$ partial hypotheses at each step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{B}_t &= \text{Top-}k_{(y_1, \ldots, y_t)} \Bigl( \sum_{j=1}^{t} \log P(y_j \vert y_{<j}, \mathbf{c}) \Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $k=1$, beam search reduces to greedy decoding. For $k=5$ or $k=10$, it provides significantly better translation quality at the cost of $k \times$ inference time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know:** For Part B 14-mark questions, if asked to compare inference strategies, you must mention (a) computational cost, (b) risk of repetition loops in greedy decoding, (c) the **length normalization** issue in beam search (longer sequences have lower joint probability, so a length penalty $\alpha$ is applied: $\text{score} = \frac{\log P}{T_y^{\alpha}}$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Unrolled Encoder-Decoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE SEQUENCE INPUT"]
        x1["X1 : TOKEN 1"]
        x2["X2 : TOKEN 2"]
        x3["X3 : TOKEN 3"]

    subgraph EMB["EMBEDDING LAYER"]

        e2["E2 EMB VECTOR"]

        h2["H2 HIDDEN STATE"]

    end

    subgraph MID["LATENT REPRESENTATION"]
        ctx["C CONTEXT VECTOR THOUGHT VECTOR"]

    end

    x1 --> e1 --> h1
    x2 --> e2 --> h2

    h1 --> h2
    h2 --> h3

    style ctx fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style MID fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram above shows the **unrolled** computational graph. Let us also depict the **training-time vs. inference-time** flow, since they differ in a critical way.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ENCODER FORWARD PASS"] --> B["COMPUTE CONTEXT VECTOR C"]
    B --> C{"TRAINING OR INFERENCE"}

    C -->|TRAINING| D["DECODER INPUT: GROUND TRUTH Y_STAR_T_MINUS_1"]
    D --> E["DECODER FORWARD STEP T"]
    E --> F["CROSS ENTROPY LOSS L_T"]
    F --> G["BPTT BACKPROP THROUGH DECODER AND ENCODER"]
    G --> H["ADAM OR SGD WEIGHT UPDATE"]

    C -->|INFERENCE| I["DECODER INPUT: PREVIOUS PREDICTION Y_T_MINUS_1"]
    I --> J["DECODER FORWARD STEP T"]
    J --> K["SOFTMAX OVER VOCABULARY"]
    K --> L["ARGMAX OR BEAM SEARCH"]
    L --> M{"PREDICTED EOS?"}
    M -->|NO| I
    M -->|YES| N["FINAL OUTPUT SEQUENCE"]

    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Information Bottleneck Visualization

The critical limitation of the basic Seq2Seq model is best understood visually.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LONG INPUT SENTENCE 50 TOKENS"] --> B["ENCODER RNN COMPRESSES SEQUENTIALLY"]
    B --> C["FIXED SIZE VECTOR C DIM 256 OR 512"]
    C --> D["DECODER RNN EXPANDS INTO OUTPUT"]
    D --> E["TRANSLATED SENTENCE"]

    C -.->|"BOTTLENECK: LOSES INFO FROM EARLY TOKENS"| F["DEGRADED PERFORMANCE ON LONG SENTENCES"]

    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The red box ($\mathbf{c}$) is the **single point of failure** for long sequences. The red dashed arrow explicitly highlights the information loss. In the KTU exam, drawing this bottleneck diagram earns extra credit in 14-mark questions because it demonstrates deep understanding of the model's limitations — and it naturally sets up the attention mechanism.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**What is a context vector in the encoder-decoder architecture, and what is its role?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$ is a fixed-dimensional vector that summarizes the entire input sequence, produced by the encoder RNN as its final hidden state: $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$.

**Role:** (1 mark) It serves as the **only information bridge** between the encoder and the decoder. (1 mark) The decoder uses $\mathbf{c}$ as its initial hidden state and as a conditioning input at every generation step to produce the output sequence. (1 mark)

> **Valuation Note:** A common mistake is to say "context vector is the input to the encoder" — it is the **output of the encoder**, not the input. This alone costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**List any two advantages of using LSTM cells over vanilla RNN cells in the encoder-decoder model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Mitigates vanishing gradient problem:** LSTMs use gated memory cells to preserve long-range dependencies over many time steps, which is critical for encoding long sentences. (1.5 marks)
2. **Selective memory via forget/input gates:** LSTMs can **forget irrelevant information** and **retain salient information** across time steps, producing a richer context vector $\mathbf{c}$. (1.5 marks)

> **Valuation Note:** Generic answers like "LSTMs are better" fetch 0 marks. Specific gate-level reasoning is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **encoder-decoder architecture** for sequence-to-sequence learning. Clearly label the encoder RNN, decoder RNN, context vector, and the flow of information. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **cross-entropy loss function** used to train this model, and explain how **teacher forcing** stabilizes training. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Define the architecture.** (1 mark)

The encoder-decoder (Seq2Seq) model maps a variable-length input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ to a variable-length output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \ldots, y_{T_y})$. It consists of two RNNs — an **encoder** and a **decoder** — coupled by a **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$.

**Step 2: Describe the encoder.** (1.5 marks)

The encoder RNN reads the input tokens one by one, updating its hidden state:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, x_t)$$

After processing all $T_x$ tokens, the final hidden state becomes the context vector:

$$\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$$

**Step 3: Describe the decoder.** (1.5 marks)

The decoder is initialized with $\mathbf{s}_0 = \mathbf{c}$ and generates output tokens autoregressively:

$$\mathbf{s}_t = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_s \mathbf{s}_t + \mathbf{b}_s)$$

It starts with a special \<SOS\> token and stops when it generates \<EOS\>.

**Step 4: Draw the diagram and label it.** (2 marks)

[Student should draw a diagram similar to Section 4.1 with both RNNs unrolled, the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ in the middle, and arrows showing information flow from encoder to decoder.]

**Step 5: State the application.** (1 mark)

This architecture is foundational for **Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**, e.g., translating English to French, where source and target sentence lengths differ.

---

### **Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Define per-token loss.** (1.5 marks)

The cross-entropy between the predicted distribution $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$ and the ground-truth one-hot vector $\mathbf{1}_{y_t^{\star}}$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}_t = - \sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathbb{V} \vert} \mathbb{1}[i = y_t^{\star}] \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[i] = -\log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[y_t^{\star}]$$

**Step 2: Sum across time steps.** (1.5 marks)

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \mathcal{L}_t = - \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[y_t^{\star}]$$

**Step 3: Compute gradients via BPTT.** (1.5 marks)

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{os}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{os}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \bigl(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t - \mathbf{1}_{y_t^{\star}}\bigr) \mathbf{s}_t^{\top}$$

This gradient flows **backward in time** through the decoder states and then through the context vector into the encoder, updating all parameters jointly.

**Step 4: Explain teacher forcing.** (1.5 marks)

During training, the decoder's input at step $t$ is **not** its own prediction $y_{t-1}$ (which may be wrong), but rather the **ground-truth token** $y_{t-1}^{\star}$. This prevents error accumulation early in training. Formally:

$$y_{t-1}^{\text{input}} = 
\begin{cases}
y_{t-1}^{\star} & \text{(teacher forcing)} \\
\arg\max \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} & \text{(free-running)}
\end{cases}$$

A common practice is to use teacher forcing with probability $p_{\text{TF}} = 1.0$ initially and **anneal** it gradually to $0.5$ as training progresses.

**Step 5: Mention exposure bias.** (1 mark)

Teacher forcing, however, creates an **exposure bias**: the model never sees its own mistakes during training. At inference time, this mismatch can degrade performance, motivating techniques like **scheduled sampling**.

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B — Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **information bottleneck problem** in the basic encoder-decoder model. How does it affect performance on long sentences, and what architectural remedy addresses it? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **greedy decoding** and **beam search** for inference in the encoder-decoder model. Show the mathematical formulation of beam search with beam width $k$ and discuss the role of **length normalization**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Define the bottleneck.** (2 marks)

In the basic Seq2Seq model, the entire source sentence must be compressed into a **single fixed-size context vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$, regardless of the input length $T_x$. Mathematically, $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x} = f(\mathbf{h}_{T_x - 1}, x_{T_x})$ recursively depends on all prior tokens, but the **information capacity** is bounded by $d_h$ (typically 256 or 512).

**Step 2: Quantify the limitation.** (2 marks)

For a source sentence of $T_x = 50$ tokens, the encoder must encode 50 words of meaning into 256–512 floating-point numbers. The information loss follows roughly $\mathcal{O}(T_x / d_h)$. Empirical studies (Cho et al., 2014; Bahdanau et al., 2015) show BLEU score degradation of **5–10 points** when $T_x$ increases from 20 to 50.

**Step 3: Explain gradient difficulties.** (1.5 marks)

Longer sentences also produce **deeper backpropagation chains**, exacerbating vanishing/exploding gradients even with LSTM cells, and making the encoder's final state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ increasingly dominated by the **most recent tokens**, forgetting early ones.

**Step 4: Propose the remedy.** (1.5 marks)

The architectural remedy is the **Attention Mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Instead of a single context vector, the decoder dynamically computes a **weighted sum of all encoder hidden states** $\{\mathbf{h}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_{T_x}\}$ at every decoding step, with weights (alignment scores) learned via a feedforward network. This eliminates the bottleneck and allows the decoder to "look back" at any source position.

---

### **Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Define greedy decoding.** (1.5 marks)

Greedy decoding selects the highest-probability token at each step:

$$y_t^{\star} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathbb{V}} \; P(y \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Advantages:** Fast (single forward pass per step), simple to implement. **Disadvantages:** Locally optimal ≠ globally optimal. Early mistakes propagate, and the decoder may fall into repetition loops (e.g., "the the the...").

**Step 2: Define beam search.** (2 marks)

Beam search maintains the **top-$k$ partial hypotheses** at each step. At decoding step $t$, it expands each of the $k$ hypotheses by appending every token in the vocabulary, scores the $k \cdot \vert \mathbb{V} \vert$ candidates by cumulative log-probability, and retains the top $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(y_1, \ldots, y_t) &= \sum_{j=1}^{t} \log P(y_j \mid y_{<j}, \mathbf{c}) \\
\mathcal{B}_t &= \text{Top-}k \bigl\{ \text{Score}(y_1, \ldots, y_t) \bigr\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Discuss complexity and tradeoff.** (1.5 marks)

- **Time complexity:** $O(k \cdot T_y \cdot \vert \mathbb{V} \vert)$ versus $O(T_y \cdot \vert \mathbb{V} \vert)$ for greedy.
- **Empirical gain:** Beam width $k=5$ typically improves BLEU by 1–2 points over greedy.
- **Diminishing returns:** $k=10$ rarely justifies the cost; $k=1$ is greedy decoding.

**Step 4: Explain length normalization.** (2 marks)

Raw cumulative log-probability **always decreases** with sequence length (summing more negative terms). This biases beam search toward shorter sequences. The fix is **length normalization** with penalty $\alpha$ (Wu et al., 2016):

$$\text{Normalized Score} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{t} \log P(y_j \mid y_{<j}, \mathbf{c})}{t^{\alpha}}$$

Common choices: $\alpha = 0.5$ or $\alpha = 0.6$. The Google NMT paper uses a more sophisticated **coverage penalty** to discourage under-/over-translation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse the context vector with the embedding.** The embedding is the dense vector for **one** token; the context vector summarizes **all** tokens. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do not write "the encoder predicts the output."** The encoder only produces $\mathbf{c}$; the decoder generates the output tokens.
> 3. **Always show dimensions in formula derivations.** A derivation with no dimensions is considered incomplete.
> 4. **In beam search questions,** never write "beam search finds the best sequence" — it finds a *good* sequence under the constraint of width $k$, and is not guaranteed to be globally optimal (which is NP-hard).
> 5. **For teacher forcing,** explicitly state the probability $p_{\text{TF}}$ and mention exposure bias as the drawback. Generic answers lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Encoder-Decoder Model with RNNs**

- ✅ **Architecture:** Two RNNs (encoder + decoder) coupled by a context vector $\mathbf{c}$.
- ✅ **Context Vector:** $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ (final encoder hidden state); the **sole information bridge**.
- ✅ **Encoder:** Reads $\mathbf{x}$ left-to-right, updates $\mathbf{h}_t = f(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, x_t)$.
- ✅ **Decoder:** Autoregressive; $\mathbf{s}_t = f(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$; outputs via $\text{softmax}(W_s \mathbf{s}_t)$.
- ✅ **Special Tokens:** \<SOS\> to start, \<EOS\> to end generation.
- ✅ **Loss:** Per-token cross-entropy summed across the target sequence: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t[y_t^{\star}]$.
- ✅ **Training Trick:** **Teacher Forcing** — feed ground truth $y_{t-1}^{\star}$ instead of model prediction during training.
- ✅ **Backprop:** **BPTT** through decoder, then across $\mathbf{c}$ into encoder.
- ✅ **Inference — Greedy:** $\arg\max$ at each step; fast but suboptimal.
- ✅ **Inference — Beam Search:** Maintain top-$k$ hypotheses; better quality, $k \times$ cost.
- ✅ **Length Normalization:** Divide cumulative log-probability by $t^{\alpha}$ to prevent short-sequence bias.
- ✅ **Key Limitation:** **Information bottleneck** — fixed-size $\mathbf{c}$ cannot encode long sentences well.
- ✅ **Architectural Remedy:** **Attention Mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2015) — the natural next topic in Module 4.
- ✅ **Real-World Use:** NMT, summarization, captioning, dialogue systems, speech recognition.
- ✅ **Cell Choice:** LSTM or GRU preferred over vanilla RNN to mitigate vanishing gradients on long sequences.
- ✅ **Embedding Matrix $E$:** Trainable; dimensions $d_{emb} \times \vert \mathbb{V} \vert$; separate matrices for source and target.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attention Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Attention Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention Model (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Attention Model** in Natural Language Processing is a neural network mechanism that dynamically computes a weighted combination of input representations, where the weights (called *attention weights* or *alignment scores*) are learned as a function of a query against a set of key–value pairs. Formally, given a query $\mathbf{Q}$, keys $\mathbf{K}$, and values $\mathbf{V}$, attention is defined as:
> $$\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$
> where $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the key vectors. This forms the mathematical backbone of the **Transformer architecture** (Vaswani et al., 2017).

In KTU Module 4 terminology, the attention paradigm replaces fixed-length context vectors used in traditional encoder–decoder RNN/LSTM seq2seq models, allowing the decoder to "look back" at *all* encoder hidden states with variable focus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spotlight in a Dark Library**
>
> Imagine you are a student writing an essay (the *decoder*) and the entire library is full of books (the *encoder output*). Instead of carrying the whole library in your head (the *bottleneck problem*), you hold a **flashlight (the attention mechanism)**. You sweep the flashlight across the shelves, and the brightness you shine on each book depends on how relevant that book is to the sentence you are currently writing. Highly relevant books are illuminated brightly (high attention weight), irrelevant books stay in the dark (low weight). The bright books collectively influence what you write next.
>
> - **Query (Q)** = the essay sentence you are currently drafting ("What is the capital of France?")
> - **Key (K)** = the index card / topic label of each book in the library
> - **Value (V)** = the actual content of each book
> - **Attention weight** = how brightly the flashlight shines on each book

This solves the fundamental limitation of vanilla seq2seq models where a *single* fixed-size context vector had to compress an entire input sentence.

## 1.3 Why Attention Was Introduced (Motivation)

1. **Bottleneck Problem:** Traditional seq2seq models collapse the entire source sentence into one fixed-size context vector — information loss for long sentences.
2. **Long-Range Dependency Decay:** In RNNs, gradients vanish over long sequences; attention provides direct (O(1)) paths between any two positions.
3. **Parallelization:** Attention computes all pairwise relationships simultaneously, unlike sequential RNN unrolling.
4. **Interpretability:** Attention weights can be visualized to understand *what the model is looking at* — a feature heavily tested in KTU questions.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist (KTU High-Frequency)

| Symbol | Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{Q}$ | Query | Vector representing "what we are looking for" |
| $\mathbf{K}$ | Key | Vector representing "what each input position offers" |
| $\mathbf{V}$ | Value | Vector containing the actual content to be aggregated |
| $d_k$ | Key dimension | Dimensionality used for scaling |
| $d_v$ | Value dimension | Dimensionality of value vectors |
| $\alpha_{ij}$ | Attention weight | Scalar importance of position $j$ for query at position $i$ |
| $h$ | Number of heads | Parallel attention subspaces (multi-head) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of an Attention Model

An attention model operates through three sequential stages:

### Stage 1 — Projections
The input hidden states $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ are linearly projected into three distinct spaces using **learnable weight matrices** $\mathbf{W}^Q, \mathbf{W}^K, \mathbf{W}^V$:

$$\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^V$$

> **Why three separate projections?** Decoupling the role of "being searched" (key) from "carrying content" (value) gives the model expressive flexibility. The same vector can act differently in different roles.

### Stage 2 — Compatibility Scoring
For every query–key pair, a **compatibility score** is computed. The most common scoring function in KTU syllabus is **Scaled Dot-Product**:

$$e_{ij} = \frac{\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor prevents the softmax from saturating (entering regions of extremely small gradient) when $d_k$ is large.

### Stage 3 — Softmax Normalization & Aggregation
Scores are normalized into a probability distribution via softmax, then used to weight the value vectors:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\exp(e_{ik})}, \quad \mathbf{c}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} \mathbf{v}_j$$

## 2.2 Types of Attention (KTU Exam Critical)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Bahdanau (Additive) Attention** | $e_{ij} = \mathbf{v}_a^\top \tanh(\mathbf{W}_a[\mathbf{h}_i; \mathbf{s}_j])$ | Early seq2seq NMT, RNN decoders |
| **Luong (Multiplicative) Attention** | $e_{ij} = \mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{W}_a \mathbf{s}_j$ | Faster dot-product form |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $e_{ij} = \frac{\mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | Transformer standard |
| **Self-Attention** | Q, K, V all come from the *same* sequence | Capturing intra-sentence dependencies |
| **Cross-Attention** | Q from decoder, K/V from encoder | Encoder–decoder bridging |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | $h$ parallel attention computations concatenated | Capturing diverse relationships |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Self-Attention** = Q, K, V sourced from the same input $\mathbf{X}$ (used in encoder and decoder of Transformer).
> - **Cross-Attention** = Q from one source (e.g., decoder), K and V from another (e.g., encoder output). Used in the decoder's second sub-layer.

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention (MHA) — The Transformer Core

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Instead of one flashlight with one beam (single-head), you now have **8 flashlights**, each with a different colored lens. Each lens (head) focuses on a different *type* of relationship — one head might track syntactic subject–verb agreement, another might track coreference, another semantic similarity. The colored beams are then combined for the final output.

The mathematical formulation:

$$\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)$$

The output projection matrix $\mathbf{W}^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$ integrates the parallel heads.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$ | Core scaled dot-product attention |
| 2 | $d_k = d_{\text{model}} / h$ | Per-head dimension (in Multi-Head) |
| 3 | $\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_k \exp(e_{ik})}$ | Softmax normalization |
| 4 | $e_{ij} = \mathbf{v}_a^\top \tanh(\mathbf{W}_a[\mathbf{h}_i; \mathbf{s}_j])$ | Bahdanau additive scoring |
| 5 | $e_{ij} = \mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{W} \mathbf{s}_j$ | Luong general (multiplicative) |
| 6 | $e_{ij} = \mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{s}_j$ | Luong dot (simplest multiplicative) |
| 7 | $\text{MHA}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$ | Multi-head concatenation |
| 8 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)$ | Per-head computation |
| 9 | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise feed-forward (in Transformer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Dimensions:**
> - All vectors $\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}, \mathbf{v}$ have dimension $d_k$ (or $d_v$ for values).
> - In Transformer-base: $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$, so $d_k = d_v = 64$.
> - $\sqrt{d_k}$ is a dimensionless scaling constant (no unit).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate, DeepL): Replaced RNN seq2seq since 2017.
- **Document Summarization**: Long-document transformers (Longformer, BigBird) use sparse attention variants.
- **Speech Recognition**: Whisper, Conformer models.
- **Vision Transformers (ViT)**: Image patches as tokens.
- **Code Generation** (Copilot, Code Llama): Self-attention over code tokens.
- **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**: Cross-attention between query and retrieved documents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention From First Principles

> **Problem Setup:** Given a sequence of $n$ input tokens with embeddings $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, derive the scaled dot-product attention output $\mathbf{c}_i$ for query position $i$.

### Step 1 — Define Learnable Projection Matrices
We introduce three weight matrices that the model will learn during training:

$$\mathbf{W}^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \quad \mathbf{W}^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \quad \mathbf{W}^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$$

These are the only attention-specific learnable parameters introduced at this layer.

### Step 2 — Project the Inputs to Q, K, V Spaces

$$\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{k}_j = \mathbf{x}_j \mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{x}_j \mathbf{W}^V$$

Each input token is transformed into three semantically distinct role-specific vectors.

### Step 3 — Compute Raw Compatibility Scores
For query $i$ against key $j$:

$$e_{ij} = \mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j = \mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j$$

In matrix form across all positions:

$$\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Scaling Factor $\sqrt{d_k}$

$$\hat{e}_{ij} = \frac{e_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Why scale?** If $\mathbf{q}_i, \mathbf{k}_j$ are components of zero-mean unit-variance vectors, their dot product has variance $d_k$. Large dot-product magnitudes push softmax into saturation (gradient $\to 0$), so we divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to restore unit variance. This stabilizes training.

### Step 5 — Softmax Normalization (Row-wise)

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\hat{e}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\hat{e}_{ik})}$$

Equivalently, in matrix form:

$$\mathbf{A} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

Each row of $\mathbf{A}$ sums to **1**, forming a valid probability distribution over input positions for each query.

### Step 6 — Weighted Sum of Values

$$\mathbf{c}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} \mathbf{v}_j$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Expression

$$\boxed{\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}}$$

**Complexity Analysis:**

$$\text{Time} = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_k), \quad \text{Space} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$$

This quadratic complexity in sequence length is the **key bottleneck** of vanilla attention — the reason for sparse and linear-attention variants.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Mini Attention)

> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** Compute attention for 3 input tokens with $d_k = 2$.

Given raw scores $\mathbf{E} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $d_k = 4$.

### Step 1 — Scale

$$\hat{\mathbf{E}} = \frac{\mathbf{E}}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{\mathbf{E}}{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 0.0 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 1.5 & 1.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 & 1.0 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Row-wise Softmax (Row 1)

Row 1 unnormalized: $e^{1.0}, e^{0.0}, e^{0.5} = 2.7183, 1.0000, 1.6487$

Sum $= 5.3670$

$$\alpha_1 = \left( \frac{2.7183}{5.3670}, \frac{1.0000}{5.3670}, \frac{1.6487}{5.3670} \right) = (0.5065, 0.1863, 0.3072)$$

### Step 3 — Verify Row Sum

$$0.5065 + 0.1863 + 0.3072 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
Token 1 attends most strongly to itself (50.65%) and to token 3 (30.72%), with weak attention to token 2 (18.63%).

## 3.3 Derivation: Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ is the Correct Scaling Constant

Assume $\mathbf{q}_i$ and $\mathbf{k}_j$ are independent random vectors with each component drawn from $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Then:

$$\mathbb{E}[q_{ik}] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(q_{ik}) = 1$$

The dot product is:

$$e_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{d_k} q_{ik} \cdot k_{jk}$$

By linearity:

$$\mathbb{E}[e_{ij}] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(e_{ij}) = \sum_{k=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_{ik})\text{Var}(k_{jk}) = d_k$$

To restore unit variance:

$$\hat{e}_{ij} = \frac{e_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Var}(\hat{e}_{ij}) = 1$$

This is the **rigorous justification** for the $\sqrt{d_k}$ denominator — a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Multi-Head Attention Complexity Derivation

For a model with embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$ and $h$ heads:

$$d_k = d_v = \frac{d_{\text{model}}}{h}$$

**Per-head computation cost:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_k)$
**Total MHA cost:** $h \cdot \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_k) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{\text{model}})$

Crucially, the **total cost of MHA is the same** as a single-head attention with full $d_{\text{model}}$ dimensions — but the model gains representational diversity through parallel subspaces.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Attention Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputSeq[Input Sequence X] --> ProjQ[Linear Projection WQ]
    InputSeq --> ProjK[Linear Projection WK]
    InputSeq --> ProjV[Linear Projection WV]
    ProjQ --> QMat[Q Matrix]
    ProjK --> KMat[K Matrix]
    ProjV --> VMat[V Matrix]
    QMat --> Score[QK Transpose / sqrt dk]
    KMat --> Score
    Score --> Softmax[Softmax Normalization]
    Softmax --> AttnWeights[Attention Weights A]
    VMat --> WeightedSum[Weighted Sum with A]
    AttnWeights --> WeightedSum
    WeightedSum --> Output[Context Vector Output C]
    Output --> FFN[Feed Forward + Residual]
    FFN --> FinalOut[Final Output]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Head Attention Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input X] --> Split[Split into h Heads]
    Split --> H1[Head 1: Attention Q1 K1 V1]
    Split --> H2[Head 2: Attention Q2 K2 V2]
    Split --> H3[Head 3: Attention Q3 K3 V3]
    Split --> Hn[Head h: Attention Qh Kh Vh]
    H1 --> Concat[Concatenate all heads]
    H2 --> Concat
    H3 --> Concat
    Hn --> Concat
    Concat --> WO[Output Projection WO]
    WO --> Out[MHA Output]
```

## 4.3 Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SelfAttention["Self-Attention Block (Encoder)"]
        X1[Input Embeddings X] --> Q1[Q from X]
        X1 --> K1[K from X]
        X1 --> V1[V from X]
        Q1 --> SA[Attention Computation]
        K1 --> SA
        V1 --> SA
        SA --> SAOut[Self Context]
    end

    subgraph CrossAttention["Cross-Attention Block (Decoder)"]
        X2[Decoder State] --> Q2[Q from Decoder]
        Y2[Encoder Output] --> K2[K from Encoder]
        Y2 --> V2[V from Encoder]
        Q2 --> CA[Attention Computation]
        K2 --> CA
        V2 --> CA
        CA --> CAOut[Cross Context]
    end
```

## 4.4 Transformer Block — Where Attention Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In[Input Tokens] --> Emb[Token + Positional Embedding]
    Emb --> MHA1[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    MHA1 --> AddNorm1[Add and Norm]
    AddNorm1 --> FFN1[Feed Forward Network]
    FFN1 --> AddNorm2[Add and Norm]
    AddNorm2 --> DecoderIn[Pass to Decoder]

    DecoderIn --> MaskedMHA[Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    MaskedMHA --> AddNorm3[Add and Norm]
    AddNorm3 --> CrossMHA[Cross Multi-Head Attention]
    CrossMHA --> AddNorm4[Add and Norm]
    AddNorm4 --> FFN2[Feed Forward Network]
    FFN2 --> AddNorm5[Add and Norm]
    AddNorm5 --> Linear[Linear + Softmax]
    Linear --> Probs[Output Probabilities]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** In the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), there are $N = 6$ stacked encoder blocks and $N = 6$ stacked decoder blocks. Each sub-layer is wrapped with **residual connection** followed by **layer normalization**: $\text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the attention mechanism in NLP. How does it overcome the bottleneck problem of traditional encoder–decoder models?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An attention mechanism is a neural component that allows a decoder to dynamically focus on relevant parts of the encoder's output sequence by computing a weighted combination of encoder hidden states, with weights (attention scores) derived from a compatibility function between the decoder's current state (query) and each encoder state (keys).
>
> In traditional seq2seq models, the entire source sentence is compressed into a single fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c}$, which becomes an information bottleneck, especially for long sentences. Attention overcomes this by allowing the decoder to access **all** encoder hidden states at every decoding step, computing a context vector $\mathbf{c}_i$ specific to each output position $i$. This eliminates information loss and enables direct modeling of long-range dependencies.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of bottleneck resolution.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Self-Attention and Cross-Attention with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
> |---|---|---|
> | Source of Q, K, V | All from the **same** sequence | Q from one sequence, K & V from **another** |
> | Purpose | Capture intra-sequence dependencies | Bridge two different sequences |
> | Example (Transformer) | Encoder's first sub-layer, Decoder's masked sub-layer | Decoder's second sub-layer (Q from decoder, K/V from encoder) |
> | Use case in NMT | Understanding source sentence structure | Aligning target word generation with source words |
>
> Example: In the sentence "The **animal** didn't cross the street because **it** was tired", self-attention helps the model link "**it**" to "**animal**" within the same sequence. Cross-attention would help a translation model align an English word with its French counterpart.

*(Marking scheme: 1.5 marks for table, 1.5 marks for examples.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Scaled Dot-Product Attention mechanism. Derive the expression for the attention output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Multi-Head Attention? Explain its working with a suitable diagram. How does it differ from single-head attention in terms of representational capacity? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Diagram Description (2 marks):** The standard scaled dot-product attention flow has three inputs Q, K, V. The Q and K matrices are multiplied (with $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling) and passed through a (optional) Mask, then a Softmax to produce attention weights A. A is multiplied with V to yield the output.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Given input embeddings $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ and learnable projections:

$$\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^V$$

The compatibility score matrix is:

$$\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ for gradient stability:

$$\hat{\mathbf{E}} = \frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\mathbf{A} = \text{softmax}(\hat{\mathbf{E}})$$

Compute the final context matrix as a weighted sum of values:

$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Definition (2 marks):** Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs $h$ parallel scaled dot-product attention operations, each with its own learned projection matrices, then concatenates and projects the results:

$$\text{MHA}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)$$

**Working (3 marks):** With $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ and $h = 8$, each head operates on $d_k = d_v = 64$ dimensional projections. The 8 heads jointly attend to information from 8 different representation subspaces. The output projection $\mathbf{W}^O \in \mathbb{R}^{512 \times 512}$ integrates the heads.

**Difference (2 marks):** Single-head attention constrains the model to a single attention distribution per query, limiting its ability to jointly attend to multiple positions or feature types. Multi-head attention allows the model to capture **diverse relationships simultaneously** (e.g., syntactic, semantic, positional) in different subspaces, yielding significantly richer representations at equivalent computational cost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain Bahdanau Attention and Luong Attention. Compare their scoring functions mathematically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of positional encoding in Transformer-based attention models. Why can't pure attention models handle word order inherently? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Bahdanau (Additive) Attention (3 marks):** Introduced in 2015 for NMT. The alignment score uses a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer:

$$e_{ij} = \mathbf{v}_a^\top \tanh(\mathbf{W}_a[\mathbf{h}_i^{\text{enc}}; \mathbf{h}_j^{\text{dec}}])$$

where $\mathbf{h}_i^{\text{enc}}$ is the encoder hidden state at position $i$, $\mathbf{h}_j^{\text{dec}}$ is the decoder hidden state at position $j$, and $\mathbf{v}_a, \mathbf{W}_a$ are learnable parameters. The concatenation $[\cdot; \cdot]$ is followed by a non-linear $\tanh$, making it an **additive** score.

**Luong (Multiplicative) Attention (3 marks):** Introduced in 2015 as a faster alternative. Three variants exist:
- **Dot:** $e_{ij} = \mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{s}_j$
- **General:** $e_{ij} = \mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{W}_a \mathbf{s}_j$
- **Concat:** Same as Bahdanau but with different parameterization

**Comparison (1 mark):**
- Bahdanau uses **concatenation + tanh + dot with $\mathbf{v}_a$** → additive, more parameters, more expressive.
- Luong uses **direct dot product** → multiplicative, fewer parameters, faster, but requires $\dim(\mathbf{h}) = \dim(\mathbf{s})$.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**The Problem (3 marks):** Pure self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — it treats input as a set, not a sequence. For any permutation $\pi$ of input tokens, $\text{Attention}(\mathbf{X}) = \text{Attention}(\pi(\mathbf{X}))$ because the QK$^T$ computation is symmetric. This means "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" would produce identical attention outputs, which is unacceptable for language.

**Positional Encoding Solution (4 marks):** Vaswani et al. inject positional information using fixed sinusoidal encodings added to the input embeddings:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$

where $pos$ is the token position and $i$ is the dimension index. These encodings are added to the token embeddings $\mathbf{x}_{pos}$ before being fed into attention:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{pos} = \mathbf{x}_{pos} + PE_{(pos)}$$

This breaks permutation symmetry and allows the model to distinguish "dog bites man" from "man bites dog". Alternatives include **learned positional embeddings** (used in BERT) and **relative positional encodings** (used in T5, Transformer-XL).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor** in scaled dot-product attention: This is a 2-mark deduction if missed in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V sources in self vs cross attention**: A frequent conceptual error. Self-attention has all three from the same input; cross-attention has K, V from a different source.
> 3. **Not stating the purpose of $\sqrt{d_k}$**: Examiners explicitly want *"prevents softmax saturation by controlling variance to 1"*.
> 4. **Forgetting to include residual connections** in Transformer block diagrams — partial mark loss.
> 5. **Omitting the masking operation** in decoder self-attention — important for causal/autoregressive generation.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Attention** computes a weighted sum of value vectors, with weights derived from query–key compatibility.
- ✅ **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula: $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top / \sqrt{d_k})\mathbf{V}$.
- ✅ **$\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling** prevents softmax saturation by normalizing dot-product variance to 1.
- ✅ **Self-Attention** uses Q, K, V from the same input; **Cross-Attention** uses K, V from a different source.
- ✅ **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel attention heads, each on $d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$ dimensions, then concatenates and projects.
- ✅ **Bahdanau = additive** (tanh + concatenation), **Luong = multiplicative** (dot product).
- ✅ **Positional encoding** is mandatory for Transformers because attention is permutation-invariant.
- ✅ **Complexity** of attention is $\mathcal{O}(n^2 d)$ — quadratic in sequence length (the main scalability bottleneck).
- ✅ **MHA total cost** equals single-head attention cost but with richer representations.
- ✅ **Residual + LayerNorm** wraps every sub-layer in the Transformer.
- ✅ **Masked attention** in decoder prevents attending to future tokens (autoregressive property).
- ✅ **Softmax properties** used: row-stochastic attention matrix, each row sums to 1.
- ✅ **Attention weights are interpretable** — visualization reveals model focus (used in BERTology, probing studies).
- ✅ **Applications**: NMT, summarization, QA, speech, vision (ViT), code generation, RAG systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
