topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformers, NLP Applications - Machine Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transformers — Formal Academic Definition

The **Transformer** is a deep learning architecture introduced in the seminal 2017 paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al.). It relies entirely on a mechanism called **self-attention** to compute representations of its input and output, completely eliminating recurrence and convolution. The Transformer is a **sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq)** model that maps an input sequence of symbols to an output sequence of symbols using stacked **encoder** and **decoder** layers, each composed of multi-head attention and position-wise feed-forward sub-layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Transformer is the cornerstone architecture of modern NLP. Every state-of-the-art model — BERT, GPT, T5, RoBERTa, MarianMT, and NLLB — is built directly on Transformer blocks. For KTU exams, focus on self-attention, multi-head attention, positional encoding, and the encoder–decoder structure.

In the context of KTU's outcome-based education, this topic maps to the following course outcomes:

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|-------|----------------|---------------------------|
| CO4 | Apply transformer-based models for sequence-to-sequence NLP tasks | Apply / Analyze |
| CO5 | Evaluate NLP applications like Machine Translation, summarization, QA | Evaluate |

---

## 1.2 Machine Translation (MT) — Formal Definition

**Machine Translation (MT)** is the sub-field of computational linguistics and NLP that investigates the use of software to translate text or speech from one natural language (the **source language**) to another (the **target language**). Modern Neural Machine Translation (NMT) systems — particularly those built on the Transformer — have effectively replaced older Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) and Rule-Based Machine Translation (RBMT) systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key MT Paradigms (Chronological):**
> 1. **Rule-Based MT (RBMT):** Uses hand-crafted linguistic rules.
> 2. **Statistical MT (SMT):** Learns translation probabilities from aligned corpora (e.g., IBM Models, Moses).
> 3. **Neural MT (NMT):** End-to-end deep learning (RNN/LSTM encoder–decoder, then Transformer).
> 4. **Large Language Model MT (LLM-MT):** Prompt-based translation using GPT-4, PaLM, NLLB.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

### 🎯 Analogy 1: The Transformer as a Conference Translator Booth

Imagine you are a translator sitting in a soundproof booth at a United Nations conference. Six diplomats are speaking simultaneously in different languages, but you must understand them all to produce a coherent English summary. 

- You **don't** listen to them in sequence (no recurrence).
- You **don't** look at fixed local windows (no convolution).
- Instead, you **simultaneously pay different levels of attention** to each diplomat — more to the one making a strong claim, less to the one whispering — and at any moment, you "weigh" each speaker's voice against every other.

That simultaneous, learned reweighting is **self-attention**. The conference room is your input sequence; the English summary is your output sequence.

### 🎯 Analogy 2: Self-Attention as a Highlight-and-Look-Up Operation

Consider reading the sentence: *"The animal didn't cross the street because **it** was too tired."* To understand what **"it"** refers to, your brain doesn't read left-to-right sequentially. It instantly "looks back" at "**animal**" and assigns a high relevance score. Self-attention does precisely this — it computes, for every word, a weighted sum over **all other words** in the sentence, where the weights are learned.

### 🎯 Analogy 3: Machine Translation as Cross-Lingual Storytelling

Think of MT as a multilingual librarian. You describe a story in Malayalam; the librarian listens, builds a mental **"meaning map"** (the encoder's output), and then re-tells the story in English one word at a time, occasionally **glancing back** at the meaning map to make sure the details match (cross-attention in the decoder).

---

## 1.4 Why Transformers Revolutionized NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three core advantages of Transformers over RNNs/LSTMs:**
> 1. **Parallelization:** Self-attention computes all positions simultaneously, unlike sequential RNNs.
> 2. **Long-Range Dependencies:** Direct connections between any two positions bypass the vanishing-gradient bottleneck.
> 3. **Scalability:** Performance scales predictably with model size, data, and compute — the basis of modern LLMs.

Standard KTU metrics you must memorize:

- **BLEU Score:** A precision-based metric for MT evaluation, range **0 to 1** (often shown as 0–100).
- **ROUGE Score:** Recall-based metric, range **0 to 1**, common in summarization.
- **Perplexity (PPL):** Measures how well a probability model predicts samples; lower is better.
- **Model dimension $d_{\text{model}}$** in the original paper: **512**.
- **Number of heads $h$** in the original paper: **8**.
- **Encoder/Decoder blocks** in the original paper: **6 each** (12 total).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer Architecture — High-Level Overview

The original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of two halves:

| Component | Role | Input → Output |
|-----------|------|----------------|
| **Encoder** (×N) | Reads & encodes source sequence into continuous representations | Source tokens → contextual embeddings |
| **Decoder** (×N) | Generates target sequence auto-regressively, one token at a time | Previously generated tokens + encoder output → next token |

Each encoder layer has two sub-layers:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention**
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**

Each decoder layer has three sub-layers:
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** (so position $i$ cannot attend to positions $>i$)
2. **Multi-Head Cross-Attention** (decoder attends to encoder output)
3. **Position-wise FFN**

Every sub-layer is wrapped with a **Residual Connection** and **Layer Normalization** (the "Add & Norm" block).

---

## 2.2 Self-Attention: The Core Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Intuition

For each token in the sequence, we want to compute a new representation that is a **weighted mixture** of all other tokens, where the weights are computed dynamically based on pairwise token similarity.

### 2.2.2 The Three Projections: Query, Key, Value

Each input embedding $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$ is projected into three vectors using three learned weight matrices $\mathbf{W}^Q, \mathbf{W}^K, \mathbf{W}^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$ (for single-head attention, $d_k = d_{\text{model}}$):

$$
\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{k}_i = \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{W}^V
$$

**Analogy:** A "Query" is like a Google search query, a "Key" is the title/description of a webpage, and a "Value" is the actual content. The Query–Key dot product measures how relevant each Value is.

### 2.2.3 Scaled Dot-Product Attention

For a sequence of length $n$, we stack all queries into matrix $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, keys into $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, and values into $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$. The attention output is:

$$
\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \mathbf{V}
$$

**Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** As $d_k$ grows, the dot products $\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j$ grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients. The scaling factor keeps the variance of the dot products at $\approx 1$, stabilizing training.

### 2.2.4 Masked Self-Attention (Decoder)

In the decoder, to prevent information leakage from future tokens during training, we apply a **causal mask** by setting all entries in the upper triangle (above the diagonal) of the $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}$ matrix to $-\infty$ before the softmax:

$$
\text{MaskedAttn}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + \mathbf{M}\right) \mathbf{V}
$$

where $\mathbf{M}_{ij} = 0$ if $j \leq i$, and $\mathbf{M}_{ij} = -\infty$ if $j > i$.

---

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention

A single attention head can only focus on one type of relationship at a time. **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel attention "heads," each with its own learned projections, then concatenates and re-projects:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\,\mathbf{W}^O
$$

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)
$$

with $\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{W}_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $\mathbf{W}_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$, and $\mathbf{W}^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$. Typically $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple heads?** Different heads can specialize: one head may track syntactic dependencies (subject–verb), another tracks coreference (pronoun–antecedent), another tracks positional proximity. The ensemble yields richer representations.

---

## 2.4 Positional Encoding

Because self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** (it treats input as a set, not a sequence), we must inject information about token order. **Positional Encoding** adds a vector $\mathbf{P}_i$ to the input embedding $\mathbf{x}_i$ at position $i$. The original paper uses fixed sinusoidal encodings:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

where $pos$ is the position and $i$ indexes the dimension. Modern variants (BERT, RoBERTa) use **learned absolute** positional embeddings, while models like T5 and RoPE-based LLMs use **rotary** or **relative** positional encodings.

---

## 2.5 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)

Each encoder/decoder layer contains a position-wise FFN applied independently to each position:

$$
\text{FFN}(\mathbf{x}) = \max(0,\, \mathbf{x}\mathbf{W}_1 + \mathbf{b}_1)\,\mathbf{W}_2 + \mathbf{b}_2
$$

This is a two-layer MLP with ReLU. The inner dimension is typically $d_{\text{ff}} = 4 d_{\text{model}} = 2048$ in the base model.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} / \sqrt{d_k})\,\mathbf{V}$ | Core attention operation |
| 2 | Scaling factor | $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ | Prevents softmax saturation |
| 3 | Q / K / V projection | $\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{W}^Q$ etc. | Learned linear projections |
| 4 | Multi-Head Concat | $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$ | Combine heads |
| 5 | Per-head dimension | $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$ | $d_{\text{model}}=512, h=8 \Rightarrow d_k=64$ |
| 6 | Positional Encoding (sin) | $\sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Even dimensions |
| 7 | Positional Encoding (cos) | $\cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Odd dimensions |
| 8 | FFN | $\max(0, \mathbf{x}\mathbf{W}_1+\mathbf{b}_1)\mathbf{W}_2+\mathbf{b}_2$ | Position-wise non-linearity |
| 9 | Residual + LayerNorm | $\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x} + \text{Sublayer}(\mathbf{x}))$ | "Add & Norm" |
| 10 | BLEU Score | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | MT evaluation metric |
| 11 | Brevity Penalty | $BP = \min(1, e^{1-r/c})$ | Penalizes too-short translations |
| 12 | Perplexity | $PPL = 2^{H(p)} = \exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log p_\theta(w_i))$ | Language model quality |
| 13 | Cross-Entropy Loss (MT) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log p_\theta(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$ | NMT training objective |
| 14 | Causal Mask | $\mathbf{M}_{ij} = -\infty$ for $j > i$ | Decoder self-attention |
| 15 | Token embedding + position | $\mathbf{e}_i = \text{Embed}(w_i) + \mathbf{P}_i$ | Input representation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are almost guaranteed to be asked to **write the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula** or the **Positional Encoding formula**. Memorize them in the exact LaTeX form. KTU examiners award full marks only for correct subscripts and the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Transformer-Based Application | Example Model |
|--------|------------------------------|---------------|
| Machine Translation | Google Translate, DeepL | NLLB-200, MarianMT |
| Text Summarization | News/article TLDRs | BART, T5, PEGASUS |
| Question Answering | Search engines, chatbots | BERT, RoBERTa |
| Code Generation | GitHub Copilot | Codex, Code Llama |
| Speech Translation | Real-time voice translation | Whisper + NLLB pipeline |
| Document Translation | PDF/DOC multilingual rendering | NLLB, Google Doc translate |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention Output

### Step 1 — Setup
We have a sequence of $n$ tokens with input embeddings $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$. For one attention head, we project to query, key, and value matrices:

$$
\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^V
$$

with $\mathbf{W}^Q, \mathbf{W}^K, \mathbf{W}^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$.

**Explanation:** Each row of $\mathbf{X}$ is one token's embedding. Multiplying by the learned weight matrices produces three "views" of the same token — query, key, value — each capturing a different role in the attention computation.

### Step 2 — Compute Pairwise Similarities
Take the dot product of every query with every key:

$$
\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}, \qquad S_{ij} = \mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j
$$

**Explanation:** $S_{ij}$ is the raw, unnormalized similarity between token $i$ and token $j$. A large positive value means token $i$'s "query" matches token $j$'s "key" strongly — i.e., token $i$ should attend to token $j$.

### Step 3 — Scale to Control Variance
Assume components of $\mathbf{q}_i$ and $\mathbf{k}_j$ are independent random variables with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Then $\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j = \sum_{m=1}^{d_k} q_{im}\,k_{jm}$ has:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j] = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j) = d_k \sigma^4
$$

Thus, the standard deviation grows as $\sqrt{d_k}$. To restore unit variance, we divide:

$$
\mathbf{S}_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Explanation:** Without scaling, large $d_k$ pushes softmax inputs into saturation regions where the gradient is vanishingly small, halting learning.

### Step 4 — Softmax Normalization Row-wise
Apply softmax along each row of $\mathbf{S}_{\text{scaled}}$ to get attention weights $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{\text{scaled},ij})}{\sum_{m=1}^{n} \exp(S_{\text{scaled},im})}
$$

**Explanation:** Each row of $\mathbf{A}$ is a probability distribution over positions — how much token $i$ attends to each other token. By construction, $\sum_j A_{ij} = 1$ and $A_{ij} \geq 0$.

### Step 5 — Weighted Sum with Values
The final output is the weighted sum of value vectors:

$$
\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}, \qquad \mathbf{z}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\,\mathbf{v}_j
$$

**Explanation:** The new representation $\mathbf{z}_i$ of token $i$ is a convex combination of all value vectors in the sequence, weighted by how relevant each one is to token $i$'s query.

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Head Attention Output Dimension

### Step 1 — Per-head dimensions
For $h$ heads, we set $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$. Each head produces output $\mathbf{Z}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.

### Step 2 — Concatenation

$$
\mathbf{Z}_{\text{concat}} = \text{Concat}(\mathbf{Z}_1, \mathbf{Z}_2, \dots, \mathbf{Z}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h d_v}
$$

### Step 3 — Output projection
Multiply by the learned output matrix $\mathbf{W}^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$:

$$
\mathbf{Z}_{\text{multi}} = \mathbf{Z}_{\text{concat}}\mathbf{W}^O \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,\mathbf{W}^O\;}
$$

**Explanation:** The $\mathbf{W}^O$ projection allows the model to learn how to mix information from the different heads, since each head has specialized on a different relationship type.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Positional Encoding Closed Form

### Step 1 — Frequency schedule
Define the wavelength $\lambda_i = 2\pi \cdot 10000^{2i / d_{\text{model}}}$. Then:

$$
PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot pos}{\lambda_i}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot pos}{\lambda_i}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Verify relative position property
Using the angle-addition identity, the encoding at position $pos+k$ can be expressed as a linear function of the encoding at position $pos$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i)} &= \sin\!\left(\frac{(pos+k)\,\omega_i}{1}\right) = \sin(pos\,\omega_i + k\,\omega_i) \\
&= \sin(pos\,\omega_i)\cos(k\,\omega_i) + \cos(pos\,\omega_i)\sin(k\,\omega_i) \\
&= PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} \cdot \cos(k\,\omega_i) + PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} \cdot \sin(k\,\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This shows the encoding of $pos+k$ is a rotation (in 2D) of the encoding of $pos$ by an angle $k\,\omega_i$. Thus, the model can easily learn to attend to **relative** offsets $k$, not just absolute positions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Encoder vs. Decoder Attention

| Property | Encoder Self-Attention | Decoder Masked Self-Attention | Decoder Cross-Attention |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Q source** | previous encoder layer output | previous decoder layer output | previous decoder layer output |
| **K, V source** | previous encoder layer output | previous decoder layer output (masked) | encoder final output |
| **Causal mask** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (upper triangular $-\infty$) | ❌ No |
| **Sees future tokens?** | Yes (bidirectional) | No (autoregressive) | Yes (encoder is fully visible) |
| **Role** | Build contextual source representations | Track what has been generated so far | Align target generation with source |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Mini Self-Attention Calculation

Suppose we have 3 tokens with $d_k = 2$ and the following $\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}$ (for simplicity, assume these are given):

$$
\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}$

$$
\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation:** Row $i$, column $j$ is $\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j$, the raw similarity.

### Step 2 — Scale by $1/\sqrt{d_k} = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$

$$
\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 1.414 & 1.414 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Softmax row-wise (numerically stable)
Row 1: $[\,0.707,\ 0,\ 0.707\,]$ → after softmax $\approx [0.422, 0.155, 0.422]$
Row 2: $[\,0,\ 0.707,\ 0.707\,]$ → $\approx [0.155,\ 0.422,\ 0.422]$
Row 3: $[\,0.707,\ 1.414,\ 1.414\,]$ → $\approx [0.156,\ 0.422,\ 0.422]$

### Step 4 — Multiply by $\mathbf{V}$

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.422 & 0.155 & 0.422 \\ 0.155 & 0.422 & 0.422 \\ 0.156 & 0.422 & 0.422 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1: $[\,0.422\cdot 1 + 0.155\cdot 0 + 0.422\cdot 1,\ 0.422\cdot 0 + 0.155\cdot 1 + 0.422\cdot 1\,] = [0.844,\ 0.577]$
Row 2: $[\,0.155 + 0 + 0.422,\ 0 + 0.422 + 0.422\,] = [0.577,\ 0.844]$
Row 3: $[\,0.156 + 0 + 0.422,\ 0 + 0.422 + 0.422\,] = [0.578,\ 0.844]$

**Final output:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.844 & 0.577 \\ 0.577 & 0.844 \\ 0.578 & 0.844 \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each row of the output is a context-aware reweighting of the value vectors. Notice token 1 and token 3 produce nearly identical outputs (both attended to token 3 strongly) — this is how attention captures semantic similarity.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Transformer vs. RNN/LSTM for MT

| Property | RNN/LSTM Encoder–Decoder | Transformer (Base) |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Computation pattern** | Sequential (left-to-right) | Fully parallel |
| **Long-range dependency** | Degrades with length | Constant path length $O(1)$ |
| **Training time** | Slow (no parallelism) | Fast (GPU-friendly) |
| **Translation quality (BLEU)** | 25–30 on WMT'14 En–De | 28.4 on WMT'14 En–De (base) |
| **Parameters** | ~65M–380M | Base: 65M, Big: 213M |
| **Inference latency** | $O(n)$ sequential | $O(1)$ per layer, but $O(n^2)$ memory for attention |
| **Memory for attention** | N/A | $O(n^2 \cdot h)$ |
| **Best for low-resource MT** | Limited | Excellent via transfer learning |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformer High-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full encoder–decoder Transformer architecture as introduced in Vaswani et al. (2017), which is the canonical answer for KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcIn["Source Tokens: I love NLP"] --> srcEmb["Input Embedding"]
    srcEmb --> srcPos["+ Positional Encoding"]
    srcPos --> enc1["Encoder Block 1"]
    enc1 --> enc2["Encoder Block 2"]
    enc2 --> encN["Encoder Block N (6 in base)"]
    encN --> encOut["Encoder Output Representations"]

    tgtIn["Target Tokens: shift right"] --> tgtEmb["Output Embedding"]
    tgtEmb --> tgtPos["+ Positional Encoding"]
    tgtPos --> dec1["Decoder Block 1"]
    dec1 --> dec2["Decoder Block 2"]
    dec2 --> decN["Decoder Block N (6 in base)"]
    encOut --> dec1
    encOut --> dec2
    encOut --> decN
    decN --> linear["Linear Projection to Vocabulary"]
    linear --> softmax["Softmax over Vocabulary"]
    softmax --> outTok["Output Token: j'aime la PNL"]
    outTok --> tgtIn
```

---

## 4.2 Inside One Encoder Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xIn["Input x"] --> mha["Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
    mha --> add1["Add and Norm"]
    xIn --> add1
    add1 --> ffn["Feed-Forward Network FFN"]
    ffn --> add2["Add and Norm"]
    add1 --> add2
    add2 --> xOut["Output of Encoder Block"]
```

**Explanation:** The residual connection from input to the first "Add & Norm" is the critical "Add" operation: $\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x} + \text{MultiHeadAttn}(\mathbf{x}))$. This allows gradients to flow directly through the network, mitigating vanishing gradients in very deep Transformers.

---

## 4.3 Inside One Decoder Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    yIn["Target Input y (shifted)"] --> mmha["Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
    mmha --> add1["Add and Norm 1"]
    yIn --> add1
    add1 --> mca["Multi-Head Cross-Attention with Encoder Output"]
    mca --> add2["Add and Norm 2"]
    add1 --> add2
    encIn["Encoder Output K, V"] --> mca
    add2 --> ffn["Feed-Forward Network"]
    ffn --> add3["Add and Norm 3"]
    add2 --> add3
    add3 --> yOut["Output of Decoder Block"]
```

**Explanation:** The cross-attention sub-layer is where translation alignment happens. Queries come from the decoder, Keys and Values come from the encoder — the decoder "looks at" the source sentence to decide what to generate next.

---

## 4.4 Self-Attention Mechanism — Query / Key / Value Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    x["Input Embedding x_i"] --> wq["Multiply by W^Q"]
    x --> wk["Multiply by W^K"]
    x --> wv["Multiply by W^V"]
    wq --> q["Query q_i"]
    wk --> k["Key k_j (for all j)"]
    wv --> v["Value v_j (for all j)"]
    q --> dot["q_i dot k_j for all j"]
    k --> dot
    dot --> scale["Scale by 1 over sqrt d_k"]
    scale --> sm["Softmax to get attention weights alpha_ij"]
    sm --> wsum["Weighted sum over v_j"]
    v --> wsum
    wsum --> z["Output z_i"]
```

**Explanation:** This is the canonical "Scaled Dot-Product Attention" diagram. For KTU, you can redraw this in the exam room — the three projections (Q, K, V), the scaled dot product, the softmax, and the weighted sum are the four mandatory steps.

---

## 4.5 Machine Translation Pipeline (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcText["Source Text e.g. Malayalam sentence"] --> tok["Subword Tokenization BPE or SentencePiece"]
    tok --> emb["Token + Positional Embedding"]
    emb --> enc["Transformer Encoder N x blocks"]
    enc --> ctx["Contextual Representations"]
    ctx --> dec["Transformer Decoder N x blocks auto-regressive"]
    dec --> prob["Linear + Softmax over target vocabulary"]
    prob --> tokOut["Predicted Subword Token"]
    tokOut --> dec2{"EOS reached?"}
    dec2 -->|No| dec
    dec2 -->|Yes| detok["Detokenize Subwords to Target Text"]
    detok --> tgtText["Translated Text e.g. English sentence"]
```

**Explanation:** MT is auto-regressive at inference time: the decoder generates one token at a time, feeds it back as input, and stops when an end-of-sequence (EOS) token is produced. Beam search or top-$k$ sampling is typically used to explore the output space.

---

## 4.6 Multi-Head Attention — Parallel Heads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    x["Input x"] --> h1["Head 1: Attn Q1 K1 V1"]
    x --> h2["Head 2: Attn Q2 K2 V2"]
    x --> h3["Head 3: Attn Q3 K3 V3"]
    x --> hN["Head h: Attn Qh Kh Vh"]
    h1 --> cat["Concatenate"]
    h2 --> cat
    h3 --> cat
    hN --> cat
    cat --> wo["Multiply by W^O"]
    wo --> out["Multi-Head Output"]
```

**Explanation:** Each head uses different learned projection matrices $\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{W}_i^V$. In the original paper, $h=8$ and $d_k = d_v = 64$, giving each head a $64$-dimensional subspace to specialize in.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks. Answers should be concise — roughly 3 to 4 sentences plus a formula or diagram. Always define the term first, then explain the mechanism.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the concept of self-attention in Transformers. How does it differ from traditional attention mechanisms used in RNN-based encoder–decoder models?**

**Model Answer:**

Self-attention is a mechanism in the Transformer architecture that computes a representation of a sequence by relating every position to every other position in the same sequence. For each token, three projections — **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, and **Value (V)** — are learned. The output for a token is a weighted sum of all value vectors, where weights are computed as the softmax of scaled dot products $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} / \sqrt{d_k}$.

Unlike RNN-based attention (Bahdanau/Luong), which computes a single context vector per source position over a sequential hidden state, **self-attention processes the entire sequence in parallel** and models **all pairwise token interactions simultaneously** with constant path length. This eliminates sequential dependency, enables full GPU parallelism, and removes the vanishing-gradient bottleneck for long-range dependencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (3-mark questions):** Do NOT write more than the allotted length. KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, and 1 mark for the difference. Going off-topic on RNN vs. Transformer will not earn extra marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Positional Encoding in a Transformer? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

**Positional Encoding** is a vector added to each token embedding to inject information about the position of that token in the sequence, since self-attention is permutation-equivariant. The original Transformer uses fixed sinusoidal encodings:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

It is **necessary** because the self-attention operation $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})\mathbf{V}$ treats the input as a **set** rather than a sequence — without positional information, the sentence "the cat sat on the mat" would be indistinguishable from any permutation of its words. The sinusoidal form also has the elegant property that $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$, allowing the model to learn relative offsets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has 14 marks, usually split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks. KTU mandates an **internal choice**: you must answer either OR question. Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the Transformer model introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017). Describe the roles of the encoder and decoder, and explain Multi-Head Self-Attention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Scaled Dot-Product Attention mechanism with its mathematical formulation. Why is the scaling factor $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ used? Show how Multi-Head Attention is computed from it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Transformer architecture consists of an **encoder stack** and a **decoder stack**, each containing $N=6$ identical layers in the base model. The encoder processes the source sequence and produces continuous representations; the decoder auto-regressively generates the target sequence one token at a time, attending both to its own previous outputs and to the encoder's output.

**Encoder block** (×2 sub-layers):
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** sub-layer.

Each sub-layer is wrapped with a **residual connection** and **Layer Normalization** (Add & Norm): $\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x} + \text{Sublayer}(\mathbf{x}))$.

**Decoder block** (×3 sub-layers):
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** (causal mask prevents attending to future tokens).
2. **Multi-Head Cross-Attention** (Q from decoder, K and V from encoder).
3. **Position-wise FFN.**

**Role of Multi-Head Self-Attention:** It projects the input into $h$ different representation subspaces (heads), computes scaled dot-product attention in each, concatenates, and projects back. This allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions** — for example, one head may capture syntactic dependencies while another captures coreference.

#### Model Solution for (b):

For a sequence of length $n$ with projected matrices $\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}$:

$$
\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}
$$

**Steps:**
1. Compute pairwise dot products $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
2. Scale by $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep variance of dot products $\approx 1$.
3. Apply row-wise softmax to get attention weights (each row sums to 1).
4. Multiply by $\mathbf{V}$ to get the weighted-sum output $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.

**Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** If $\mathbf{q}_i, \mathbf{k}_j$ have components with variance $\sigma^2$, then $\text{Var}(\mathbf{q}_i \cdot \mathbf{k}_j) = d_k \sigma^4$. For large $d_k$ (e.g., 64), raw dot products are large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into saturation regions where its gradient is exponentially small. Scaling restores unit variance, stabilizes gradients, and enables reliable training.

**Multi-Head Attention:**

$$
\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,\mathbf{W}^O
$$

with $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)$, where $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is Machine Translation? Compare Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) and Neural Machine Translation (NMT) approaches. Explain how the Transformer has become the dominant architecture for NMT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the BLEU evaluation metric for Machine Translation. How does the Brevity Penalty address the problem of short translations? Mention two other MT evaluation metrics with one line each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Machine Translation (MT)** is the automated translation of text from one natural language (source) to another (target) using software. It is a core NLP application with research dating back to the 1950s.

**SMT vs. NMT:**

| Aspect | Statistical MT (SMT) | Neural MT (NMT) |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Modeling | Probabilistic phrase tables + language models | End-to-end neural network |
| Pipeline | Many sub-components (aligner, LM, re-ranker) | Single differentiable model |
| Training data | Aligned parallel corpora | Same; can leverage monolingual data |
| Quality (BLEU) | SOTA ~30 | SOTA 40+ (Transformer) |
| Long sentences | Quality degrades | Handles well (esp. Transformer) |
| Interpretability | Higher (inspectable phrases) | Lower (black-box) |
| Examples | Moses, Google Translate (pre-2016) | Google Translate (post-2016), DeepL |

**Why Transformer dominates NMT:** The Transformer enables **full parallelism** during training (unlike sequential RNNs), handles **long-range dependencies** via direct attention, and **scales effectively** with data and compute. Models like NLLB-200, MarianMT, and Google Translate's production system are Transformer-based, achieving state-of-the-art BLEU and human-eval parity on high-resource language pairs.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)** is a precision-based metric that measures n-gram overlap between a candidate translation and one or more reference translations. It is computed as:

$$
\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)
$$

where $p_n$ is the **modified n-gram precision** (n-grams in the candidate that also appear in any reference, clipped by maximum reference count), $w_n = 1/N$ (typically $N=4$), and $BP$ is the **Brevity Penalty**.

**Brevity Penalty (BP):**

$$
BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}
$$

where $c$ is the candidate length and $r$ is the closest reference length. The penalty **exponentially penalizes candidate translations that are shorter than the reference**, preventing the system from gaming a high BLEU score by emitting only a few high-precision words. For example, a candidate of length 5 against a reference of length 20 gets $BP = e^{1-4} \approx 0.018$, effectively zeroing the score.

**Other MT evaluation metrics:**
- **ROUGE** — Recall-oriented metric commonly used in summarization, but variants like ROUGE-L are also applied to MT.
- **METEOR** — Considers synonyms, stemming, and word order, and correlates better with human judgment than BLEU.
- **chrF** — Character n-gram F-score; robust for morphologically rich languages like Malayalam.
- **TER (Translation Edit Rate)** — Measures the number of edits required to change a candidate into a reference (lower is better).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in Transformer / MT questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** in the attention formula — KTU examiners check this *very* strictly. 1 full mark is docked.
> 2. **Confusing Q/K/V sources** between encoder self-attention (Q=K=V from encoder), decoder masked self-attention (Q=K=V from decoder), and decoder cross-attention (Q from decoder, K=V from encoder). This is a 14-mark question's most commonly failed sub-part.
> 3. **Writing the BLEU formula without the Brevity Penalty** — the BP is mandatory; without it, BLEU is not BLEU.
> 4. **Drawing the Transformer diagram without labeling N=6 blocks** or without showing "Add & Norm" — the residual connections and layer norm are worth separate marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Positional Encoding for even vs. odd indices** — the sin goes to even, cos goes to odd. Getting this swapped costs full marks on the formula sub-part.
> 6. **Saying "positional encoding gives the model a sense of order"** without writing the formula — vague answers get partial credit at best. KTU 2024 expects the exact LaTeX expression.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — read this the night before the exam:**

- ✅ **Transformer = Encoder + Decoder**, both stacks of $N=6$ identical blocks in the base model.
- ✅ **Self-attention** projects input to **Query, Key, Value** and computes $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})\mathbf{V}$.
- ✅ **Scaling factor** is $1/\sqrt{d_k}$, used to prevent softmax saturation; *never* omit it.
- ✅ **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel heads with $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$, concatenates, and projects with $\mathbf{W}^O$.
- ✅ **Positional Encoding** is sinusoidal: $\sin$ for even indices, $\cos$ for odd indices, with wavelength $10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}$.
- ✅ **Encoder self-attention** is **bidirectional** (no mask).
- ✅ **Decoder self-attention** is **masked** (causal, upper-triangular $-\infty$).
- ✅ **Decoder cross-attention** uses **Q from decoder, K and V from encoder** — this is the "alignment" sub-layer for MT.
- ✅ **Residual + LayerNorm** wraps every sub-layer: $\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x} + \text{Sublayer}(\mathbf{x}))$.
- ✅ **FFN** is a position-wise 2-layer MLP with ReLU and inner dimension $4 d_{\text{model}}$.
- ✅ **Machine Translation** = translating source language to target language automatically. Modern NMT is Transformer-based.
- ✅ **BLEU** = $BP \cdot \exp(\sum w_n \log p_n)$, with **Brevity Penalty** $BP = e^{1 - r/c}$ for short candidates.
- ✅ **BLEU range:** 0 to 1 (or 0 to 100). Higher is better.
- ✅ **Perplexity** = $\exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log p_\theta(w_i))$; lower is better. Used for language models.
- ✅ **MT evaluation metrics to remember:** BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, chrF, TER.
- ✅ **Original Transformer paper metrics:** $d_{\text{model}}=512$, $h=8$, $d_k=64$, $d_{\text{ff}}=2048$, $N=6$, parameters $\approx 65$M (base).
- ✅ **Original paper BLEU on WMT'14 En–De:** 28.4 (base) / 28.5 (big model in original); later Transformer-big variants exceed 29.
- ✅ **NMT advantages over SMT:** end-to-end differentiable, handles long sentences, leverages monolingual data via back-translation, parallel training.
- ✅ **NMT disadvantages:** opaque (black-box), data-hungry for low-resource languages, hallucination in low-resource settings.
- ✅ **Causal mask** prevents information leakage from future tokens during training; absent in inference (it is built into the architecture).
- ✅ **Decoder is auto-regressive** at inference: each generated token is fed back as input for the next step until EOS.
- ✅ **Beam search** (typical width 4–8) is used at inference to find high-probability translations; greedy decoding is faster but lower quality.
- ✅ **Transfer learning** in NMT: pre-train on high-resource pairs (e.g., En–De), fine-tune on low-resource pairs (e.g., Malayalam–English) — the basis of **IndicTrans2**, **NLLB-200**.
- ✅ **Subword tokenization** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece, Unigram) is mandatory in modern NMT to handle open vocabularies and rare words.

> **End of PECST862 — Module 4 Notes. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Question Answering and Information Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Question Answering and Information Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Information Retrieval (IR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Information Retrieval (IR)* is the science of searching for information in documents, searching for documents themselves, searching for metadata which describes documents, or searching within databases, whether relational stand-alone databases or hypertextually networked databases such as the World Wide Web. The goal of an IR system is to **find relevant documents** to a user query from a large corpus, ranked by estimated relevance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of IR like a **massive library without a librarian**. You walk in and shout a question ("books about black holes"). The system scans millions of books, ranks them by how well they match your query, and hands you a stack ordered from "most likely useful" to "barely related." Modern search engines like Google and Bing are massive industrial-scale IR systems. The challenge: documents are unstructured text, and relevance is subjective.

**Core IR Sub-tasks:**
- **Indexing** – Pre-processing the corpus into a fast searchable structure (inverted index).
- **Retrieval** – Matching a user query against the index.
- **Ranking** – Scoring and ordering results by estimated relevance.
- **Evaluation** – Measuring how well the system retrieves what users actually want.

---

### 1.2 Question Answering (QA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Question Answering (QA)* is a sub-field of NLP and IR concerned with building systems that automatically answer questions posed by humans in natural language. Unlike IR which returns *documents*, QA aims to return *precise answers* (a sentence, phrase, or entity) — making it the "next level" beyond document retrieval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** If IR is like a **library that returns whole books**, QA is like a **research assistant who actually reads the book and tells you the exact answer**. You ask *"What is the capital of France?"* and the assistant says *"Paris"* — no need to scan 10 documents. The progression is clear: **Search → Document Retrieval → Passage Retrieval → Exact Answer Extraction**.

**Key QA Taxonomy (KTU syllabus focus):**

| QA Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Factoid QA** | Returns a specific fact (entity, date, number) | "When was Einstein born?" |
| **Non-Factoid / Opinion** | Requires reasoning, summarization, opinion | "Why is the sky blue?" |
| **Closed-Domain** | Restricted to a specific domain (medicine, sports) | Medical QA systems |
| **Open-Domain** | Any topic, web-scale | IBM Watson, DrQA |
| **Extractive** | Answer is a span in a given passage | SQuAD-style tasks |
| **Abstractive / Generative** | Answer is generated, not extracted | ChatGPT, T5, BART |
| **Knowledge-Base QA (KBQA)** | Answers are pulled from structured triples (subject–predicate–object) | Freebase / Wikidata queries |
| **Reading Comprehension (RC)** | Answer is found by "reading" a given passage | SQuAD, RACE |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask to **contrast IR with QA** in 3-mark questions. Memorize: *IR returns ranked documents; QA returns direct answers. IR answers "what documents talk about X?" while QA answers "what is X?"*

---

### 1.3 Why IR and QA Matter Together

Modern QA systems almost always use IR as the **front-end retriever** and a **neural reader** as the back-end. This is the dominant pipeline:
1. **Retriever (IR module)** → narrows millions of docs to top-k passages.
2. **Reader (QA module)** → reads the passages and extracts/generates the answer.
This is the **Retriever-Reader (or "open-domain QA")** paradigm — a high-yield KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IR Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Document Pre-processing**
- Tokenization → split text into words/tokens.
- Stopword removal → discard common words (a, the, of).
- Stemming / Lemmatization → reduce words to root form (running → run).
- Lowercasing, punctuation removal.

**Step 2: Inverted Index Construction**
The **inverted index** is the backbone of any search engine. For each unique term in the vocabulary, store a posting list of documents where it appears.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "inverted"?** A normal index maps *Document → words it contains*. An **inverted index** flips this: maps *Word → list of documents containing it*. This makes query lookup O(1) for each query term.

**Step 3: Query Processing** – Apply the same pre-processing to the user query.

**Step 4: Retrieval & Ranking** – Use a scoring model to rank matched documents (see formulas below).

**Step 5: Result Presentation** – Return top-k ranked documents, often with snippets.

---

### 2.2 Classical Retrieval Models

#### 2.2.1 Boolean Retrieval Model
Treats queries as Boolean expressions (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`). No ranking — just match or no match. Used in early systems (e.g., legal databases).

#### 2.2.2 Vector Space Model (VSM) — Salton, 1971
Documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional space (one dimension per term). Relevance = **cosine similarity** between query and document vectors.

#### 2.2.3 Probabilistic Model (BM25)
The de-facto industry standard for ranking. Combines term frequency, document length, and inverse document frequency in a tuned probabilistic framework.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULAS — memorize for Part A and Part B derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (raw)** | $\text{TF}(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term $t$ in doc $d$ |
| 2 | **Inverse Document Frequency** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | $N$ = total docs, $\text{df}(t)$ = docs containing $t$ |
| 3 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$ | Combines local + global importance |
| 4 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{q}\cdot\vec{d}}{\vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert}$ | Measures angle between query and doc vectors |
| 5 | **BM25 Score** | $\sum_{t \in Q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f_{t,D}(k_1+1)}{f_{t,D} + k_1\!\left(1 - b + b\dfrac{\vert D\vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$ | $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$ typical |
| 6 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Of retrieved, how many are relevant? |
| 7 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Of all relevant, how many retrieved? |
| 8 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 9 | **Mean Average Precision (MAP)** | $\text{MAP} = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q\vert}\sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q\vert}\text{AP}(q)$ | $\text{AP} = \dfrac{1}{R_q}\sum_{k=1}^{n}P(k)\cdot \text{rel}(k)$ |
| 10 | **NDCG (Normalized DCG)** | $\text{NDCG}_p = \dfrac{\text{DCG}_p}{\text{IDCG}_p}$ | $\text{DCG}_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\dfrac{\text{rel}_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ |
| 11 | **Jaccard Similarity (for sets)** | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B\vert}{\vert A \cup B\vert}$ | Used in set-based retrieval |
| 12 | **Robertson–Sparck Jones IDF (BM25)** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)$ | $n_t$ = number of docs containing $t$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $\vert x \vert$ in formula tables. This breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. The above table is the **safe form** expected in board answers.

---

### 2.4 The QA Architecture (Pipeline Model)

A traditional **modular QA pipeline** has four stages:

1. **Question Analysis** – Determine question type (who/what/where/when/why/how), expected answer type (PERSON, DATE, LOCATION), focus word, and constraints.
2. **Document/Passage Retrieval** – Use IR to fetch candidate passages.
3. **Answer Extraction** – Match candidate answer against question type using patterns, NER, or neural span prediction.
4. **Answer Validation / Ranking** – Cross-check with multiple sources; pick best confidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **End-to-End (Neural) QA** skips explicit modules — a single transformer (BERT, RoBERTa) takes (question + passage) and outputs an answer span. This is the modern SOTA approach in SQuAD-style benchmarks.

---

### 2.5 Knowledge-Base QA (KBQA)

QA systems may answer by querying a **structured knowledge base** (KB) of facts stored as triples:
$$\text{subject} \to \text{predicate} \to \text{object}$$
Example: `(Marie_Curie, born_in, Warsaw)`.

The question *"Where was Marie Curie born?"* is parsed into a logical form (SPARQL, lambda-DCS, or neural query graph) and matched to the KB.

**Common KBs:** Freebase, Wikidata, DBpedia, YAGO.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of IR/QA |
|---|---|
| **Web Search (Google, Bing)** | Massive-scale IR with neural ranking (BERT-based) |
| **Voice Assistants (Siri, Alexa)** | ASR → IR → QA → TTS pipeline |
| **Enterprise Search (Elasticsearch)** | TF-IDF / BM25 / dense vector retrieval |
| **Medical Decision Support** | Closed-domain QA over PubMed |
| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Product search and FAQ-based QA bots |
| **Legal Tech** | Boolean IR over case law databases |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — TF-IDF Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:** This exact type of 7-mark "compute TF-IDF and rank documents" question appears frequently in KTU Module-4 assessments.

**Problem Setup.** Consider a tiny corpus of 3 documents and a query. Compute TF-IDF scores and rank the documents using cosine similarity.

**Corpus:**
- $D_1$ = *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $D_2$ = *"the dog sat on the rug"*
- $D_3$ = *"cats and dogs are pets"*

**Query** $Q$ = *"cat dog"*

**Step 1: Vocabulary & Pre-processing.**
After lowercasing and tokenization, vocabulary = {the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug, cats, and, are, pets}. Counted frequencies:

| Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $\text{df}(t)$ | $N$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 3 |
| cat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| sat | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 3 |
| on | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 3 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| rug | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| cats | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| are | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| pets | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |

**Step 2: Compute IDF for each term.** Use $\text{IDF}(t) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ (base 10 is conventional; some texts use natural log).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDF}(\text{the}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{cat}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{sat}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{on}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{mat}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{dog}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{rug}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{cats}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{and}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{are}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{IDF}(\text{pets}) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right) \approx 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: TF-IDF vectors for query and each document.** Query $Q$ = {cat, dog} → $f_{\text{cat},Q} = 1$, $f_{\text{dog},Q} = 1$, all others zero.

$$
\vec{Q} = \bigl[\,0,\ 0.4771,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0\,\bigr]
$$

**Document vectors** (only including non-zero entries, full 11-D):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_1} &= \bigl[\,0.3522,\ 0.4771,\ 0.1761,\ 0.1761,\ 0.4771,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0\,\bigr] \\
\vec{D_2} &= \bigl[\,0.3522,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0.4771,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0\,\bigr] \\
\vec{D_3} &= \bigl[\,0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.4771,\ 0.4771,\ 0.4771,\ 0.4771\,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cosine similarity for each (Q, D).**

$$
\cos(Q,D_i) = \dfrac{\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_i}}{\vert\vert\vec{Q}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{D_i}\vert\vert}
$$

**Norm of Q:** $\vert\vert\vec{Q}\vert\vert = \sqrt{0.4771^2 + 0.1761^2} = \sqrt{0.2276 + 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.2586} \approx 0.5085$.

**Compute $\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_1}$:** Only overlapping non-zero dimensions are *cat* and *dog*. In $D_1$, cat = 0.4771, dog = 0.

$$
\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_1} = (0.4771)(0.4771) + (0.1761)(0) = 0.2276
$$

$$
\vert\vert\vec{D_1}\vert\vert = \sqrt{0.3522^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.1240 + 0.2276 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.6412} \approx 0.8007
$$

$$
\cos(Q,D_1) = \dfrac{0.2276}{0.5085 \times 0.8007} = \dfrac{0.2276}{0.4072} \approx 0.5591
$$

**Compute $\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_2}$:** In $D_2$, cat = 0, dog = 0.1761.

$$
\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_2} = (0.4771)(0) + (0.1761)(0.1761) = 0.0310
$$

$$
\vert\vert\vec{D_2}\vert\vert = \sqrt{0.3522^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.1240 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.4446} \approx 0.6668
$$

$$
\cos(Q,D_2) = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.5085 \times 0.6668} = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.3391} \approx 0.0914
$$

**Compute $\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_3}$:** In $D_3$, cat = 0, dog = 0.1761.

$$
\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_3} = (0.4771)(0) + (0.1761)(0.1761) = 0.0310
$$

$$
\vert\vert\vec{D_3}\vert\vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.2276 \times 4} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.9104} = \sqrt{0.9414} \approx 0.9703
$$

$$
\cos(Q,D_3) = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.5085 \times 0.9703} = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.4934} \approx 0.0629
$$

**Step 5: Final Ranking.**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | **0.5591** |
| 2 | $D_2$ | 0.0914 |
| 3 | $D_3$ | 0.0629 |

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $D_1$ is the top-ranked document because it contains "cat" (high IDF, distinctive) and is short (less diluted). $D_3$ ranks lowest because, even though it mentions "dog", it is a longer document and "dog" is a less distinctive term than "cat" in this corpus. This matches KTU's expected "reasoning component" in 7-mark questions.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Precision, Recall, F1

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:** Standard 3-7 mark KTU question.

**Scenario:** An IR system returns **8 documents** for a query. Out of these, **6 are relevant**. The corpus contains **10 relevant documents in total**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TP} &= 6 \quad (\text{retrieved and relevant}) \\
\text{FP} &= 8 - 6 = 2 \quad (\text{retrieved but not relevant}) \\
\text{FN} &= 10 - 6 = 4 \quad (\text{relevant but not retrieved}) \\
\text{TN} &= \text{rest of corpus (not needed here)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{6}{6+2} = \dfrac{6}{8} = 0.75
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{6}{6+4} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.60
$$

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} = \dfrac{0.90}{1.35} = 0.667
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Precision and Recall almost always trade off. Increasing k (number of retrieved documents) usually increases Recall but decreases Precision. The **F-measure** with parameter $\beta$ allows weighting one over the other: $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2)\dfrac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$. $\beta=1$ → balanced; $\beta>1$ → favors Recall; $\beta<1$ → favors Precision.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — BM25 Score Calculation

**Problem.** For a small corpus of **3 documents** with average document length $\text{avgdl} = 50$ words, and parameters $k_1 = 1.5$, $b = 0.75$, query $Q = \{q_1, q_2\}$. Compute BM25 scores.

| Doc | $|D|$ | $f(q_1,D)$ | $f(q_2,D)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | 40 | 3 | 1 |
| $D_2$ | 60 | 1 | 2 |
| $D_3$ | 50 | 2 | 0 |

Assume $\text{IDF}(q_1) = 0.5$, $\text{IDF}(q_2) = 1.2$.

**Compute BM25 for $D_1$ (with $|D| = 40$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length norm}_1 &= 1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{40}{50} = 0.25 + 0.60 = 0.85 \\
\text{Term } q_1 \text{ contrib} &= 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{3(1.5+1)}{3 + 1.5(0.85)} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{7.5}{4.275} = 0.5 \cdot 1.7544 = 0.8772 \\
\text{Term } q_2 \text{ contrib} &= 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{1(2.5)}{1 + 1.5(0.85)} = 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{2.5}{2.275} = 1.2 \cdot 1.0989 = 1.3187 \\
\text{BM25}(D_1) &= 0.8772 + 1.3187 = 2.1959
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute BM25 for $D_2$ (with $|D| = 60$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length norm}_2 &= 0.25 + 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{60}{50} = 0.25 + 0.90 = 1.15 \\
\text{Term } q_1 \text{ contrib} &= 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{1(2.5)}{1 + 1.5(1.15)} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{2.5}{2.725} = 0.5 \cdot 0.9174 = 0.4587 \\
\text{Term } q_2 \text{ contrib} &= 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{2(2.5)}{2 + 1.5(1.15)} = 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{5.0}{3.725} = 1.2 \cdot 1.3423 = 1.6107 \\
\text{BM25}(D_2) &= 0.4587 + 1.6107 = 2.0694
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute BM25 for $D_3$ (with $|D| = 50$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length norm}_3 &= 0.25 + 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{50}{50} = 1.00 \\
\text{Term } q_1 \text{ contrib} &= 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{2(2.5)}{2 + 1.5(1.00)} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{5.0}{3.5} = 0.5 \cdot 1.4286 = 0.7143 \\
\text{Term } q_2 \text{ contrib} &= 0 \quad (\text{since } f(q_2, D_3) = 0) \\
\text{BM25}(D_3) &= 0.7143
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Ranking:** $D_1$ (2.196) > $D_2$ (2.069) > $D_3$ (0.714).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition check:** $D_1$ ranks highest because it has both a high $f(q_1)$ *and* is shorter than average (length normalization boosts shorter docs). $D_2$ ranks second but is penalized for being long. $D_3$ ranks lowest because it lacks $q_2$ entirely.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Boolean IR | Vector Space (TF-IDF + Cosine) | BM25 | Neural (DPR / BERT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Matching** | Exact (set-based) | Soft (vector angle) | Soft (probabilistic) | Semantic (embedding) |
| **Ranking** | No ranking | Yes (cosine) | Yes (BM25 score) | Yes (dot product) |
| **Handles synonyms** | ❌ No | ❌ Poor | ❌ Poor | ✅ Yes |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Fast | Fast | Slower |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | High | Low |
| **Industry use** | Legal, legacy | Lucene classic | Elasticsearch, default | Modern search, RAG |
| **KTU typical weight** | 3 marks (definition) | 7 marks (compute) | 7 marks (compute) | 5 marks (discuss) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classical IR Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Document Corpus] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal]
    C --> D[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    D --> E[Inverted Index Builder]
    E --> F[(Inverted Index Storage)]
    G[User Query] --> H[Query Pre-processor]
    H --> I[Matcher over Inverted Index]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Scoring Engine TF-IDF or BM25]
    J --> K[Ranked Document List]
    K --> L[User]

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    class A,G input
    class B,C,D,E,H,I,J process
    class F storage
    class K,L output
```

### 4.2 Modular QA Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[User Question] --> Q1[Question Analysis Module]
    Q1 --> Q2[Detect Question Type: Who, What, When, Where, Why, How]
    Q1 --> Q3[Identify Expected Answer Type: PERSON, DATE, LOCATION]
    Q1 --> Q4[Extract Focus Word and Constraints]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    R1[Passage Retrieval IR Module] --> R2[Inverted Index Lookup]
    R2 --> R3[Top K Candidate Passages]
    R3 --> E1[Answer Extraction Module]
    E1 --> E2[Pattern Matching and Named Entity Recognition]
    E1 --> E3[Neural Span Prediction BERT or BiLSTM]
    E2 --> V1
    E3 --> V1
    V1[Answer Validation and Ranking] --> V2[Confidence Scoring and Cross Source Check]
    V2 --> A1[Final Answer to User]

    classDef qbox fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef rbox fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef ebox fill:#e6fcf5,stroke:#20c997,color:#000
    classDef vbox fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#9333ea,color:#000
    class Q,Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 qbox
    class R1,R2,R3 rbox
    class E1,E2,E3 ebox
    class V1,V2,A1 vbox
```

### 4.3 Modern Retriever-Reader (Open-Domain QA) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Question] --> B[Question Encoder BERT or DPR]
    C[Document Corpus] --> D[Dense Passage Retriever DPR or BM25]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Top K Passages, e.g. K equals 100]
    E --> F[Reader Model BERT or RoBERTa]
    A --> F
    F --> G[Span Start and End Predictions]
    G --> H[Answer Span Extracted]
    H --> I[Returned to User]

    classDef enc fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef ret fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef read fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    class A,B,C enc
    class D,E ret
    class F,G read
    class H,I out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway from the diagrams:** The classical IR pipeline is **document-centric**; the modular QA pipeline is **answer-centric** with explicit question analysis; and modern open-domain QA merges both via dense retrieval (semantic) followed by neural reading. KTU examiners expect students to identify the *responsibility* of each block when asked to "explain the QA architecture."

graph TD
