## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Information Retrieval (IR) and Question Answering (QA) systems.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Information Retrieval | Question Answering |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Find *relevant documents* | Find *precise answers* |
| **Output** | Ranked list of documents | Exact span / entity / sentence |
| **Query** | Often keyword-based | Natural language question |
| **User effort** | User reads results to find answer | System returns the answer directly |
| **Example** | Google search results page | "Paris" returned for "Capital of France?" |
| **Pipeline** | Indexing + retrieval + ranking | QA pipeline: question analysis, retrieval, extraction, validation |

IR is the **foundation** of QA — most QA systems use an IR-style retriever as a front-end component. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of an inverted index in Information Retrieval.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in the corpus to the list (posting list) of documents in which the term appears. It is the backbone of fast text search.

**Structure example:**
```
Term          →  Posting List
cat           →  [D1, D5, D12, ...]
dog           →  [D2, D3, D5, ...]
```

**Key roles:**
1. **Fast lookup** — query terms are looked up in O(1) per term.
2. **Phrase queries** — positional information enables "exact phrase" matching.
3. **Ranking** — supports term-frequency and TF-IDF computations.
4. **Scalability** — used in Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr for web-scale search.

Construction steps: tokenization → stopword removal → stemming → sort terms → store postings. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (PECST862):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. The two sub-parts may be (a) a derivation/computation and (b) a descriptive question — this is the most common KTU pattern for Module 4.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Given the following three documents and a query, compute the **TF-IDF vectors** and rank the documents using **cosine similarity**. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

- $D_1$ = *"information retrieval is the science of search"*
- $D_2$ = *"natural language processing includes question answering"*
- $D_3$ = *"search engines use information retrieval systems"*
- Query $Q$ = *"information retrieval search"*

**(b)** Describe the **Vector Space Model** of Information Retrieval with its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Vocabulary** (after lowercasing, removing stopwords "is", "the", "of", "a", "an"):
{information, retrieval, science, search, natural, language, processing, includes, question, answering, engines, use, systems}

**Step 2: Term frequencies and document frequencies:**

| Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $N=3$ | $\text{df}$ | $\text{IDF} = \log(N/\text{df})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| information | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.1761 |
| retrieval | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.1761 |
| science | 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| search | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.1761 |
| natural | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| language | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| processing | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| includes | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| question | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| answering | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| engines | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| use | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |
| systems | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 0.4771 |

**Step 3: TF-IDF vectors for Q, D1, D2, D3** (only non-zero entries shown):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{Q} &= \{(\text{information}, 0.1761),\ (\text{retrieval}, 0.1761),\ (\text{search}, 0.1761)\} \\
\vec{D_1} &= \{(\text{information}, 0.1761),\ (\text{retrieval}, 0.1761),\ (\text{science}, 0.4771),\ (\text{search}, 0.1761)\} \\
\vec{D_2} &= \{(\text{natural}, 0.4771),\ (\text{language}, 0.4771),\ (\text{processing}, 0.4771),\ (\text{includes}, 0.4771),\ (\text{question}, 0.4771),\ (\text{answering}, 0.4771)\} \\
\vec{D_3} &= \{(\text{information}, 0.1761),\ (\text{retrieval}, 0.1761),\ (\text{search}, 0.1761),\ (\text{engines}, 0.4771),\ (\text{use}, 0.4771),\ (\text{systems}, 0.4771)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cosine similarities.**

$\vert\vert\vec{Q}\vert\vert = \sqrt{3 \times 0.1761^2} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.0930} = 0.3050$

$\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_1} = 3 \times 0.1761^2 = 0.0930$ (dot product over 3 common terms)

$\vert\vert\vec{D_1}\vert\vert = \sqrt{3 \times 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0930 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.3206} = 0.5662$

$\cos(Q,D_1) = \dfrac{0.0930}{0.3050 \times 0.5662} = \dfrac{0.0930}{0.1727} = 0.5385$

$\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_2} = 0$ (no common terms) → $\cos(Q,D_2) = 0$

$\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{D_3} = 3 \times 0.1761^2 = 0.0930$ (3 common terms: information, retrieval, search)

$\vert\vert\vec{D_3}\vert\vert = \sqrt{3 \times 0.1761^2 + 3 \times 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0930 + 0.6828} = \sqrt{0.7758} = 0.8808$

$\cos(Q,D_3) = \dfrac{0.0930}{0.3050 \times 0.8808} = \dfrac{0.0930}{0.2686} = 0.3463$

**Step 5: Final Ranking.**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | **0.5385** |
| 2 | $D_3$ | 0.3463 |
| 3 | $D_2$ | 0.0000 |

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $D_1$ is ranked highest because all 3 query terms appear in it and it is the shortest document. $D_3$ shares all 3 query terms but is longer, diluting its score. $D_2$ has no query term overlap and is correctly ranked last.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Vector Space Model (VSM)** — Gerard Salton, 1971.

- **Idea:** Represent each document $d$ and each query $q$ as a vector in $|V|$-dimensional space, where $V$ is the vocabulary.
- **Components:** $i$-th component = weight of term $i$ (e.g., TF-IDF).
- **Relevance** = similarity between $q$ and $d$, measured by **cosine similarity**.

$$
\text{sim}(q, d) = \cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{q}\cdot\vec{d}}{\vert\vert\vec{q}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{d}\vert\vert}
$$

**Advantages:**
1. Continuous ranking (not just match/no-match like Boolean).
2. Partial matches are naturally handled.
3. Easy to extend (new weighting schemes, normalization).
4. Foundation for modern IR; used in Lucene/Elasticsearch.

**Limitations:**
1. Assumes term independence ("bag of words" — loses word order).
2. Cannot handle synonyms or semantic similarity ("car" ≠ "automobile").
3. High dimensionality; sparse vectors.
4. Sensitive to document length — requires length normalization.
5. Cannot represent phrase-level meaning.

**Why important for QA:** VSM is the basis of passage retrieval in nearly all QA systems. The retriever-uses-cosine-similarity paradigm remains dominant even in modern dense retrieval (where vectors come from neural encoders). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **BM25 ranking algorithm** with its complete formula. List the parameters and discuss their effect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **architecture of a typical Question Answering system** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **closed-domain** and **open-domain QA**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**BM25 (Best Matching 25)** — Robertson, Walker, et al. (1990s). It is the de-facto ranking function used by **Elasticsearch, Solr, and Lucene** (default in many systems).

**Complete formula for document $D$ and query $Q$:**

$$
\text{BM25}(D, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \dfrac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert D\vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

where:
- $q_i$ = the $i$-th query term.
- $f(q_i, D)$ = frequency of $q_i$ in document $D$.
- $\vert D\vert$ = length of document $D$ (in words).
- $\text{avgdl}$ = average document length in the corpus.
- $k_1$ = term-frequency saturation parameter (typical: **1.2 to 2.0**).
- $b$ = document-length normalization parameter (typical: **0.75**).

**IDF used in BM25 (Robertson–Sparck Jones form):**

$$
\text{IDF}(q_i) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{N - n(q_i) + 0.5}{n(q_i) + 0.5} + 1\right)
$$

where $N$ = total documents, $n(q_i)$ = number of documents containing $q_i$.

**Effect of parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect | Increase value → | Decrease value → |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k_1$ | TF saturation | More weight to repeated terms | More linear in TF |
| $b$ | Length normalization | Penalize long docs more | Less length penalty |

- $b = 0$ → no length normalization.
- $b = 1$ → full length normalization.
- $k_1 = 0$ → ignores term frequency entirely (binary model).

**Why BM25 dominates industry:**
1. Empirically outperforms raw TF-IDF on most corpora.
2. Two tunable parameters give flexibility.
3. Fast, interpretable, and theoretically grounded in probabilistic IR.
4. Works well as the first-stage retriever in modern RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture of a typical QA system — 4 modules:**

1. **Question Analysis** — Parses the question. Identifies:
   - **Question type:** who / what / when / where / why / how.
   - **Expected answer type (focus):** PERSON, LOCATION, DATE, NUMBER, REASON.
   - **Keywords** for retrieval.
2. **Passage / Document Retrieval** — Uses IR (Boolean, TF-IDF, BM25, or dense retrieval via DPR/BERT) to fetch top-$k$ candidate passages from the corpus.
3. **Answer Extraction** — Three sub-approaches:
   - **Pattern-based** — Regular expressions, NER, hand-crafted rules.
   - **Statistical** — Train a classifier (SVM, logistic regression) on candidate answers.
   - **Neural** — BERT / RoBERTa span prediction (start and end token probabilities) — modern SOTA.
4. **Answer Validation / Ranking** — Cross-check answer across multiple passages, score confidence, return the best.

**Closed-Domain vs Open-Domain QA:**

| Aspect | Closed-Domain | Open-Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Specific domain (medicine, finance) | Any topic |
| **Knowledge source** | Curated KB or in-domain corpus | Web, Wikipedia, large corpus |
| **Difficulty** | Lower (restricted vocabulary) | Higher (vocabulary, ambiguity) |
| **Examples** | Medical diagnosis bots, legal assistants | IBM Watson, DrQA, GPT-based assistants |
| **Accuracy** | Generally higher (narrow scope) | Generally lower (broad scope) |
| **Data** | Annotated in-domain data required | Can use unsupervised or web-scale data |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common 7-mark errors):**
> 1. **Confusing BM25's IDF with the classical $\log(N/\text{df})$ IDF** — BM25 uses the *Robertson–Sparck Jones* smoothed form with the $+1$ in the log; classical IR uses the simpler form. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to define $b$ and $k_1$** — the question says "list the parameters and discuss their effect." Vague answers lose marks.
> 3. **Drawing the QA diagram with no labels** — examiners expect labelled arrows showing data flow.
> 4. **Saying "open-domain is harder" without justification** — always mention *vocabulary explosion, ambiguity, larger candidate space*.
> 5. **Mixing up precision and recall formulas** — a classic 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

- **IR** = find relevant **documents**; **QA** = find precise **answers**.
- **Inverted index** = term → posting list; backbone of search engines.
- **TF-IDF** = local importance × global rarity; $w_{t,d} = f_{t,d} \cdot \log(N/\text{df}(t))$.
- **Cosine similarity** measures the angle (not magnitude) between query and document vectors — preferred over dot product.
- **BM25** = probabilistic ranking with **two parameters**: $k_1$ (TF saturation) and $b$ (length normalization, default 0.75).
- **Precision** = TP/(TP+FP); **Recall** = TP/(TP+FN); **F1** = harmonic mean of P and R.
- **MAP** = mean of Average Precision across queries; rewards early retrieval of relevant docs.
- **NDCG** = graded relevance metric; uses $\log_2(i+1)$ discount.
- **QA types:** factoid vs non-factoid; closed vs open; extractive vs abstractive; text-based vs KBQA.
- **Modern QA architecture** = Retriever (IR) + Reader (BERT/span-prediction); the **open-domain QA** paradigm.
- **KBQA** = answer from structured triples; e.g., Freebase `(Marie_Curie, born_in, Warsaw)`.
- **SQuAD** = standard extractive QA benchmark; BERT-based models dominated SQuAD 1.1/2.0.
- **Key knowledge bases for QA:** Freebase, Wikidata, DBpedia, YAGO.
- **DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval)** = neural IR using BERT encoders; uses dot-product similarity in dense space.
- **Always state IDF formula correctly:** use $\log(N/\text{df}(t))$ in classical IR, and the smoothed form for BM25.
- **For cosine similarity in tables**: use `\vert` or `\mid` — never `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table.
- **KTU signature question patterns:** (i) compute TF-IDF + cosine, (ii) compute BM25, (iii) draw and label QA architecture, (iv) compare IR vs QA, (v) discuss precision/recall trade-off, (vi) explain KBQA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Large Language Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST862 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Large Language Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Language Model (LLM):** A Large Language Model is a deep neural network — typically built upon the **Transformer architecture** — trained on massive corpora of textual (and increasingly multimodal) data using **self-supervised learning** objectives, possessing parameter counts in the order of **hundreds of millions to hundreds of billions**, and exhibiting **emergent capabilities** such as in-context learning, instruction following, chain-of-thought reasoning, and zero/few-shot generalization.

In the KTU 2024 PECST862 syllabus, LLMs are positioned as the culmination of the statistical and neural NLP pipeline (covered in earlier modules), where the scale of **parameters ($\mathcal{P}$)**, **training tokens ($\mathcal{T}$)**, and **compute ($\mathcal{C}$)** collectively produce qualitatively new linguistic competencies that are absent in smaller models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 introduces LLMs as a paradigm shift — from *task-specific* models (POS taggers, parsers, NER) to *general-purpose foundation models* that unify multiple NLP tasks under a single generative interface.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lifelong Reader" Intuition

Imagine a person who has spent **30 years reading every book, every Wikipedia article, every research paper, every legal contract, and every Reddit thread** ever written. They have not memorized these texts word-for-word, but they have absorbed:

- The **statistical regularities** of language (grammar, collocations, idioms).
- The **factual knowledge** of the world (geography, science, history).
- The **reasoning patterns** of human thought (cause-effect, analogies, deduction).
- The **stylistic fingerprints** of different authors and domains.

Now, when you ask this person a question, they do not "look it up" — they **generate** an answer by predicting the most probable sequence of words, informed by decades of internalized patterns.

> A Large Language Model is the **computational incarnation of this lifelong reader**.

| Component of the Analogy | Computational Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Decades of reading | Pre-training on $\sim$Trillion tokens |
| Internalized patterns | Model weights $\theta$ (parameters) |
| Answering a question | Autoregressive decoding |
| Adjusting to a new domain | Fine-tuning / RLHF |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Descriptors of an LLM:**
> - **Parameters ($\mathcal{P}$):** Number of trainable weights — ranges from $10^7$ (small) to $10^{12}$ (frontier).
> - **Tokens ($\mathcal{T}$):** Sub-word units consumed during pre-training (1 token $\approx$ 0.75 words for English).
> - **Compute ($\mathcal{C}$):** Measured in **FLOPs** (Floating Point Operations) — often $10^{23}$ to $10^{25}$ for frontier models.
> - **Context Window ($L$):** Maximum sequence length the model can attend over (e.g., 2K, 8K, 128K, 1M tokens).
> - **Perplexity (PPL):** Exponential of average cross-entropy loss — lower is better.
> - **Vocabulary Size ($V$):** Number of unique tokens in the tokenizer (typically 32K–256K).

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Motivation

The journey to LLMs evolved through three epochs:

1. **Statistical Era (Pre-2013):** N-gram models, HMMs — limited to surface statistics.
2. **Neural Era (2013–2017):** Word2Vec, RNNs, LSTMs — semantic embeddings but sequential bottleneck.
3. **Transformer Era (2017–Present):** Vaswani et al.'s *Attention is All You Need* (2017) introduced **parallelizable self-attention**, enabling training on web-scale data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of LLM Success:**
> 1. **Architecture:** Transformer (parallel, scalable, long-range).
> 2. **Data:** Unsupervised web-scale corpora (Common Crawl, Wikipedia, books, code).
> 3. **Compute:** GPU/TPU clusters enabling training at scale.

---

## 1.5 Why LLMs Matter in the KTU NLP Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From the KTU 2024 Course Outcome (**CO4**) perspective, students must be able to **analyze the architecture, training paradigm, and capabilities of modern foundation models** — bridging classical NLP theory with the generative AI revolution. This module directly maps to *Apply*, *Analyze*, and *Evaluate* levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer Backbone — Theoretical Foundation

Every modern LLM is built on the **Transformer** block. We decompose it into its core sub-modules:

### 2.1.1 Self-Attention Mechanism

Self-attention allows each token to dynamically weigh the importance of all other tokens in the sequence.

Given an input sequence represented as a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$ (where $n$ is sequence length and $d_{model}$ is the embedding dimension), the Transformer projects $X$ into three matrices using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is then computed as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** The dot products grow in magnitude with $d_k$, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients (the "saturating softmax" problem). Scaling restores stable gradient flow — a critical detail often tested in KTU exams.

### 2.1.2 Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

Instead of performing a single attention function, the model projects $Q, K, V$ into $h$ subspaces, applies attention in parallel, and concatenates:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W_i^Q, X W_i^K, X W_i^V)$$

> **Why multi-head?** Different heads learn to attend to **different linguistic phenomena** — one head may track syntactic dependencies, another may focus on coreference, another on positional proximity.

### 2.1.3 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)

Each Transformer block contains a position-wise FFN applied independently to each position:

$$\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$$

This is typically a **two-layer MLP with ReLU/GELU activation**, expanding to $4 \times d_{model}$ in the hidden layer.

### 2.1.4 Residual Connections & Layer Normalization

Each sub-layer is wrapped with a residual connection and layer norm:

$$x_{\text{out}} = \text{LayerNorm}\big(x + \text{Sublayer}(x)\big)$$

This stabilizes training of very deep stacks (96, 128, or even 200+ layers in frontier LLMs).

### 2.1.5 Positional Encoding

Since self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**, positional information must be injected. Two dominant strategies:

| Strategy | Formula / Mechanism | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Absolute Sinusoidal** | $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$ | Original Transformer |
| **Learned Absolute** | Trainable embedding $P \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ | BERT, GPT-2 |
| **RoPE** (Rotary) | Rotates $Q, K$ by position-dependent angles | LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX |
| **ALiBi** (Attention bias) | Adds linearly decaying bias to attention scores | BLOOM, MPT |

---

## 2.2 The Two Architectural Families of LLMs

| Family | Attention Pattern | Representative Models | Best For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Encoder-only** (Bidirectional) | Full self-attention (no masking) | BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa | Classification, NER, embeddings |
| **Decoder-only** (Autoregressive) | Masked self-attention (causal) | GPT-3/4, LLaMA, Mistral, PaLM | Text generation, chat, reasoning |
| **Encoder-Decoder** (Seq2Seq) | Bidirectional encoder + causal decoder | T5, BART, FLAN-T5 | Translation, summarization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Decoder-only LLMs dominate today's landscape because **causal language modeling** (predict the next token) is the simplest and most scalable self-supervised objective, and it unifies all NLP tasks into a single "prompt → completion" interface.

---

## 2.3 Pre-Training Objectives

### 2.3.1 Causal Language Modeling (CLM)

Given a sequence $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, the model is trained to maximize:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CLM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

This is the objective used by all GPT-style decoder-only models.

### 2.3.2 Masked Language Modeling (MLM)

A fraction (typically 15%) of tokens are replaced with `[MASK]`, and the model predicts them from bidirectional context:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}})$$

This is the BERT objective.

### 2.3.3 Denoising / Span Corruption (T5 Objective)

Random spans of tokens are replaced with sentinel tokens, and the model must generate the original spans:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{T5}}(\theta) = -\sum_{s \in \text{spans}} \log P_\theta(s \mid \text{input with sentinels})$$

---

## 2.4 The Three-Stage Training Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern LLMs are not trained in a single pass. They go through three distinct stages:**

### Stage 1 — Pre-Training
- **Data:** Web-scale text (Common Crawl, books, code, scientific papers).
- **Objective:** Causal LM on raw text.
- **Cost:** Millions of GPU-hours; hundreds of millions to billions of tokens.
- **Output:** A *base model* with broad linguistic/world knowledge but poor instruction-following.

### Stage 2 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
- **Data:** Hand-crafted or curated (prompt, ideal response) pairs.
- **Objective:** Same CLM loss, restricted to high-quality demonstrations.
- **Output:** An *instruction-tuned model* that responds helpfully to user queries.

### Stage 3 — Alignment via RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)
- **Data:** Human preference rankings over multiple model outputs.
- **Method:** A *reward model* $r_\phi$ is trained on pairwise comparisons; then **PPO** (Proximal Policy Optimization) fine-tunes the LLM to maximize expected reward while staying close to the SFT model via a KL-divergence penalty.
- **Output:** An *aligned model* that is helpful, harmless, and honest.

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RLHF}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \big[ r_\phi(x, y) \big] + \beta \, \text{KL}\big(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot \mid x)\big)$$

---

## 2.5 Emergent Abilities & Scaling Laws

### 2.5.1 Emergent Abilities

Emergent abilities are capabilities that are **absent in small models but appear in large models** — typically at certain threshold scales. Documented by Wei et al. (2022), these include:

- In-context learning (learning from examples in the prompt).
- Multi-step reasoning (chain-of-thought).
- Instruction following.
- Code generation from natural language descriptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Point:** Emergence suggests that LLMs are not merely "scaled-up n-grams" — they undergo **qualitative phase transitions** in capability, analogous to water becoming ice at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

### 2.5.2 Scaling Laws (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022)

Performance (measured as cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L}$) follows a **power-law relationship** with three variables:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{T}) = \mathcal{L}_0 + \left(\frac{\mathcal{P}_c}{\mathcal{P}}\right)^{\alpha_\mathcal{P}} + \left(\frac{\mathcal{T}_c}{\mathcal{T}}\right)^{\alpha_\mathcal{T}}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ = number of parameters
- $\mathcal{T}$ = number of training tokens
- $\alpha_\mathcal{P}, \alpha_\mathcal{T} \approx 0.07$ empirically
- $\mathcal{P}_c, \mathcal{T}_c$ are constants

**Hoffmann et al. (Chinchilla Law):** For compute-optimal training, parameters and tokens should scale **in equal proportion**:

$$\mathcal{P}^{*} \approx \mathcal{T}^{*} / 20$$

That is, for every parameter, the model should see approximately **20 tokens** during training.

---

## 2.6 In-Context Learning (ICL) — The "Learning Without Training" Phenomenon

A defining LLM capability: the model can perform a **new task** at inference time using only **examples placed in the prompt** — without any gradient updates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICL is NOT gradient descent.** The model weights remain frozen. The "learning" happens through the model's attention mechanism over the demonstration tokens — effectively a form of **implicit Bayesian inference** over the task distribution.

Three regimes:

| Regime | Prompt Structure | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero-shot** | `Task: Translate to French. Sentence: Hello.` | No examples |
| **One-shot** | 1 demonstration + query | 1 example |
| **Few-shot** | $k$ demonstrations + query | $k=3, 5, 32$ examples |

---

## 2.7 Decoding Strategies

During inference, the model produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each step. The decoding strategy determines how the next token is sampled:

| Strategy | Formula / Rule | Character |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Greedy** | $\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t})$ | Deterministic, repetitive |
| **Top-$k$ Sampling** | Sample from top $k$ highest-probability tokens | Diverse, controlled |
| **Nucleus (Top-$p$)** | Sample from smallest set whose cumulative prob $\geq p$ | Adaptive diversity |
| **Temperature** | $P'(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / \tau)}{\sum_u \exp(z_u / \tau)}$ | $\tau < 1$ sharpens, $\tau > 1$ flattens |
| **Beam Search** | Maintain top-$B$ partial sequences | High-probability, less diverse |

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every key equation** an LLM-aware NLP student must know for the KTU 2024 board exam.

| Concept | Equation | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ | Core of Transformer |
| Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA} = \text{Concat}(h_1,\dots,h_h) W^O$ | $h$ parallel heads |
| Positional Encoding (sinusoidal) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d})$ | Original Transformer |
| Causal LM Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ | GPT objective |
| MLM Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}})$ | BERT objective |
| KL Divergence (RLHF penalty) | $\text{KL}(\pi_\theta \| \pi_{\text{SFT}})$ | Keeps aligned model close to SFT |
| Scaling Law (power) | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_0 + a \mathcal{P}^{-\alpha} + b \mathcal{T}^{-\beta}$ | Kaplan/Chinchilla |
| Chinchilla Optimal Ratio | $\mathcal{P}^{*} \approx \mathcal{T}^{*} / 20$ | Compute-optimal training |
| Temperature Sampling | $P'(v) = \exp(z_v/\tau) / \sum_u \exp(z_u/\tau)$ | $\tau \in (0,\infty)$ |
| Perplexity | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\big(-\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \log P(x_i)\big)$ | Lower = better |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $H(P, Q) = -\sum_x P(x) \log Q(x)$ | Foundation of LM training |
| BPE Merges | $V_{\text{merge}} = V_{\text{base}} + N_{\text{merges}}$ | Sub-word tokenization |
| Rotary Position Embedding | $q'_m = R_{m\theta} q_m$ | Rotates $Q,K$ by position |
| GELU Activation | $\text{GELU}(x) \approx 0.5x(1 + \tanh(\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715x^3)))$ | Standard in modern LLMs |
| SwiGLU FFN | $\text{SwiGLU}(x) = (\text{SiLU}(xW_1) \otimes xW_3)) W_2$ | Used in LLaMA, PaLM |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | LLM Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Software Engineering** | GitHub Copilot, Cursor, code review automation |
| **Healthcare** | Clinical note summarization, medical Q&A, drug discovery |
| **Legal** | Contract analysis, case-law retrieval, legal drafting |
| **Education** | Personalized tutoring, automated grading, content generation |
| **Customer Service** | Conversational agents, ticket classification, sentiment analysis |
| **Scientific Research** | Literature mining, hypothesis generation, code for experiments |
| **Creative Industries** | Script writing, marketing copy, game NPC dialogue |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Self-Attention to Multi-Head Attention

We now provide a **line-by-line derivation** showing how multi-head attention is constructed from the basic self-attention primitive, as this is a frequently asked derivation in KTU NLP modules.

---

**Step 1 — Input Representation.**

Let the input sequence consist of $n$ tokens, each represented as a $d_{\text{model}}$-dimensional embedding vector. We stack these into the matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^\top \\ x_2^\top \\ \vdots \\ x_n^\top \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each row $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times d_{\text{model}}}$ is the embedding of the $i$-th token, typically the sum of token embedding and positional embedding.

---

**Step 2 — Project to Query, Key, Value Spaces.**

For each of the $h$ attention heads, we have learned projection matrices. For head $i$:

$$Q_i = X W_i^Q, \quad K_i = X W_i^K, \quad V_i = X W_i^V$$

where $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$ and typically $d_k = d_{\text{model}} / h$.

**Conversion logic:** Each head learns to project the shared input into a *different representation subspace*, allowing it to capture different relational patterns.

---

**Step 3 — Scaled Dot-Product Attention Per Head.**

For each head $i$:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q_i K_i^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V_i$$

**Conversion logic:** The product $Q_i K_i^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the *attention score matrix* — entry $(a, b)$ measures how much token $a$ should attend to token $b$. The softmax normalizes rows to a valid probability distribution. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents gradient vanishing as $d_k$ grows.

---

**Step 4 — Concatenate All Heads.**

$$\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_k} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Concatenation along the feature dimension restores the original $d_{\text{model}}$ width by stacking $h$ blocks of width $d_k$.

---

**Step 5 — Final Output Projection.**

$$\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$ is a learned output projection.

$$\boxed{\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \big[\text{head}_1; \text{head}_2; \dots; \text{head}_h\big] W^O}$$

**Conversion logic:** The output projection mixes information across heads and maps back to the model's residual stream dimension.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for Causal Language Modeling

We derive why cross-entropy is the natural training objective for LLMs.

---

**Step 1 — Autoregressive Factorization.**

By the chain rule of probability, the joint distribution over a sequence factors as:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Conversion logic:** This factorization is the foundation of *autoregressive generation* — the model predicts one token at a time, conditioned on all previous tokens.

---

**Step 2 — Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL).**

To train the model, we minimize the negative log-probability of the observed training sequence:

$$\text{NLL} = -\log P(x_1, \dots, x_n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Conversion logic:** The log converts a product of small probabilities into a sum, avoiding numerical underflow. The negation turns *maximization of likelihood* into *minimization of loss*.

---

**Step 3 — Equivalence to Cross-Entropy.**

For a single position $i$, the model's output is a categorical distribution $P_\theta(\cdot \mid x_{<i})$ over vocabulary $V$. The true distribution is a *one-hot* vector $\mathbf{1}_{x_i}$. The cross-entropy between true and predicted distribution is:

$$H(\mathbf{1}_{x_i}, P_\theta) = -\sum_{v \in V} \mathbf{1}_{x_i}[v] \cdot \log P_\theta(v \mid x_{<i}) = -\log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

Averaging over all positions gives the **mean cross-entropy loss**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Conversion logic:** Cross-entropy is mathematically identical to NLL under one-hot targets. This is why the LLM training objective is universally described as "cross-entropy minimization."

---

**Step 4 — Connection to Perplexity.**

Perplexity is defined as the exponential of the per-token cross-entropy:

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** Perplexity can be interpreted as the *effective branching factor* — the average number of tokens the model is "confused among" at each step. A PPL of 50 means the model behaves as if choosing uniformly among 50 plausible next tokens.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RLHF Objective from First Principles

We derive the full RLHF loss step by step, as this is an **increasingly important KTU 2024 exam topic** given its industrial relevance.

---

**Step 1 — Reward Model Training.**

Given a prompt $x$ and two model responses $y_a, y_b$, a human annotator indicates a preference $y_a \succ y_b$. The **Bradley-Terry model** gives the probability of this preference:

$$P(y_a \succ y_b \mid x) = \frac{\exp(r_\phi(x, y_a))}{\exp(r_\phi(x, y_a)) + \exp(r_\phi(x, y_b))} = \sigma\big(r_\phi(x, y_a) - r_\phi(x, y_b)\big)$$

The reward model $r_\phi$ is trained by minimizing the negative log-likelihood of observed preferences:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_a, y_b, \text{pref}) \sim \mathcal{D}} \big[ \log \sigma\big(r_\phi(x, y_a) - r_\phi(x, y_b)\big) \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_a \succ y_b] + \log \sigma\big(r_\phi(x, y_b) - r_\phi(x, y_a)\big) \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_b \succ y_a] \big]$$

**Conversion logic:** This is a binary classification loss — the reward model learns to assign *higher scalar scores* to responses humans prefer.

---

**Step 2 — RL Fine-Tuning Objective.**

The LLM policy $\pi_\theta$ is fine-tuned to maximize expected reward, with a KL-divergence penalty to prevent drifting too far from the SFT model:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RL}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{prompt}},\, y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)} \big[ r_\phi(x, y) \big] + \beta \, \text{KL}\big(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot \mid x)\big)$$

**Conversion logic:**
- The first term **maximizes reward** (written as a loss, hence the negative sign).
- The second term **penalizes deviation** from the SFT model, preventing the policy from "reward hacking" by producing degenerate high-reward outputs.
- $\beta$ is a hyperparameter controlling the strength of the KL constraint.

---

**Step 3 — PPO Surrogate Objective (in practice).**

In practice, **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)** is used to optimize $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RL}}$ stably. The clipped surrogate objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}\big[\min\big(\rho_t(\theta) A_t,\, \text{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A_t\big)\big]$$

where $\rho_t(\theta) = \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) / \pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)$ is the importance sampling ratio and $A_t$ is the advantage estimate (here derived from the reward model).

**Conversion logic:** The clipping prevents destructively large policy updates, making RL fine-tuning stable enough to apply to multi-billion-parameter models.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: LLM Variants Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **The following analytical matrix is critical for KTU 2024 exam questions on "Compare and Contrast LLM architectures."**

| Property | BERT | GPT-3 | T5 | LLaMA-2 | Mistral-7B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | Encoder | Decoder | Enc-Dec | Decoder | Decoder |
| **Parameters** | 340M | 175B | 11B | 7B–70B | 7B |
| **Attention** | Bidirectional | Causal | Enc-Bidir + Dec-Causal | Causal + RoPE | Causal + RoPE + GQA |
| **Positional Enc.** | Learned | Learned | Relative (T5 bias) | Rotary (RoPE) | Rotary (RoPE) |
| **Objective** | MLM + NSP | CLM | Span corruption | CLM | CLM |
| **Context Length** | 512 | 2K | 512 | 4K | 8K (sliding window) |
| **Activation** | GELU | GELU | ReLU | SwiGLU | SwiGLU |
| **Pre-training Data** | BooksWiki 16GB | CommonCrawl + books 570GB | C4 750GB | OpenWeb 2T tokens | OpenWeb ~2T tokens |
| **Key Innovation** | Bidirectional context | In-context learning | Unified text-to-text | Open-source at scale | Sliding window attention + GQA |
| **Open Source?** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Primary Use** | Embeddings, classification | Generation, reasoning | Translation, summarization | Open foundation model | Efficient open model |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Emergent Capabilities by Model Scale

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix illustrates the **phase-transition** nature of LLM capabilities, a concept frequently tested under Bloom's *Analyze* level.

| Capability | 1B params | 10B params | 100B+ params |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fluent text generation | Partial | Yes | Yes |
| Basic in-context learning | Absent | Weak | Strong |
| Multi-step reasoning (CoT) | Absent | Absent | Emergent |
| Code generation | Absent | Basic | Strong (HumanEval >70%) |
| Instruction following | Poor | Moderate | Strong (post-SFT) |
| Factual recall (closed-book QA) | Weak | Moderate | Strong |
| Cross-lingual transfer | Absent | Weak | Strong |
| Theory of mind (ToM) | Absent | Absent | Partially emergent |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The LLM Training and Inference Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Web Corpora CommonCrawl Wikipedia Books Code] --> B[Data Cleaning Deduplication Filtering]
    B --> C[Tokenization BPE SentencePiece]
    C --> D[Pre-Training Causal LM on Trillion Tokens]
    D --> E[Base Model Foundation Model Raw Capabilities]
    E --> F[Supervised Fine-Tuning SFT on Instruction Datasets]
    F --> G[Instruction-Tuned Model Helpful Responses]
    G --> H[RLHF Reward Model PPO Optimization]
    H --> I[Aligned LLM Helpful Harmless Honest]
    I --> J[Deployment Inference Engine]
    J --> K[User Prompt]
    K --> L[Tokenize Encode]
    L --> M[Transformer Forward Pass Self-Attention FFN]
    M --> N[Logits over Vocabulary]
    N --> O[Decoding Strategy Greedy Beam Top-p Temperature]
    O --> P[Generated Token]
    P --> Q{Stop Condition EOS or Max Length}
    Q -->|No| M
    Q -->|Yes| R[Final Generated Text]
    I --> S[Safety Guardrails Content Filtering]
    J --> S
```

> **Caption:** This block diagram illustrates the end-to-end lifecycle of a modern LLM — from raw data ingestion through three training stages (pre-training, SFT, RLHF) to deployment with decoding and safety guardrails. Each block is a modular pipeline stage with well-defined inputs and outputs.

---

## 4.2 Transformer Block Architecture (Detailed Internal Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input Token Embeddings plus Positional Encoding] --> N1[Layer Norm 1]
    N1 --> MHA[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    MHA --> R1[Residual Add]
    R1 --> N2[Layer Norm 2]
    N2 --> FFN[Feed-Forward Network Two-Layer MLP GELU or SwiGLU]
    FFN --> R2[Residual Add]
    R2 --> OUT[Output to Next Block]
    MHA --> QKV[Project to Q K V Matrices]
    QKV --> ATT[Compute Scaled Dot-Product Attention softmax QK over sqrt d_k times V]
    ATT --> CON[Concat Heads and Project W O]
    CON --> MHA
    FFN --> EXP[Expand to 4 times d model]
    EXP --> ACT[Nonlinear Activation]
    ACT --> PROJ[Project Back to d model]
    PROJ --> FFN
```

> **Caption:** Internal anatomy of a single Transformer decoder block. Note the pre-normalization order (Layer Norm before attention/FFN) used in modern LLMs like LLaMA and GPT-3, and the residual pathways that preserve gradient flow across hundreds of stacked blocks.

---

## 4.3 LLM Architecture Family Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[What is your primary NLP task?] --> Q1{Need bidirectional context Understanding Embeddings}
    Q1 -->|Yes| ENC[Encoder-Only BERT RoBERTa]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need text-to-text generation Input and Output Sequences}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ED[Encoder-Decoder T5 BART FLAN-T5]
    Q2 -->|No| GEN[Decoder-Only GPT LLaMA Mistral]
    ENC --> ENCUSE[Classification NER Question Answering Sentiment Analysis]
    ED --> EDUSE[Translation Summarization Paraphrasing Structured Prediction]
    GEN --> GENUSE[Open-Ended Generation Chatbots Creative Writing Code Generation Reasoning]
```

> **Caption:** A decision-tree guide to selecting the appropriate LLM architectural family based on task requirements. Decoder-only models dominate modern applications due to their unified generative interface.

---

## 4.4 Attention Mechanism Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input X n times d model] --> WQ[Multiply by WQ]
    X --> WK[Multiply by WK]
    X --> WV[Multiply by WV]
    WQ --> Q[Query Matrix]
    WK --> K[Key Matrix]
    WV --> V[Value Matrix]
    Q --> MUL[Q times K Transpose]
    K --> MUL
    MUL --> SCALE[Divide by sqrt d k]
    SCALE --> MASK[Optional Causal Mask]
    MASK --> SOFT[Softmax Normalize Rows]
    SOFT --> WTV[Multiply by V]
    V --> WTV
    WTV --> OUT[Output Attention Weighted Sum]
```

> **Caption:** Computational flow of the scaled dot-product attention. The causal mask (used in decoder-only LLMs) sets the upper-triangular entries of the attention score matrix to $-\infty$ before softmax, preventing the model from "peeking" at future tokens.

---

## 4.5 RLHF Three-Stage Alignment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Prompts from Distribution] --> SFT[SFT Model pi SFT generates response]
    SFT --> SAMP[Sample Multiple Responses per Prompt]
    SAMP --> HUM[Human Ranks Responses by Preference]
    HUM --> RM[Train Reward Model r phi via Bradley-Terry Loss]
    RM --> PPO[PPO Update of LLM Policy pi theta]
    PPO --> KL[KL Divergence Constraint Stays Close to SFT]
    KL --> PPO
    PPO --> ALN[Aligned LLM Helpful Harmless Honest]
    PPO --> SAMP
    ALN --> DEPLOY[Deployment to End Users]
```

> **Caption:** The iterative RLHF loop. Note the feedback arrow from PPO back to sampling — the aligned model generates new responses for the next round of human preference annotation, creating a continuous improvement cycle.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define a Large Language Model. List any two distinguishing characteristics that differentiate LLMs from traditional NLP models.**

**Model Answer:**

A Large Language Model (LLM) is a deep neural network, built primarily on the Transformer architecture, trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised learning objectives, with parameter counts ranging from hundreds of millions to hundreds of billions.

**Two distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **General-purpose capability:** Unlike traditional task-specific NLP models (e.g., a POS tagger or NER model), a single LLM can perform translation, summarization, question-answering, code generation, and reasoning through a unified text-generation interface — no architectural retraining is required for new tasks.

2. **Emergent in-context learning:** LLMs can learn to perform new tasks at inference time purely from examples placed in the prompt, without any gradient updates — a capability absent in smaller, pre-Transformer NLP models.

3. *(Bonus point: Scale — parameter count and training data size are orders of magnitude larger.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Explain the concept of *in-context learning* in LLMs. How does it differ from conventional supervised learning?**

**Model Answer:**

**In-Context Learning (ICL)** is the ability of an LLM to perform a new task at inference time by conditioning on a few demonstration examples provided in the prompt, *without* updating its model weights.

**Key Differences from Conventional Supervised Learning:**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | In-Context Learning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Weight updates | Yes (gradient descent) | None (frozen weights) |
| Data requirement | Hundreds–thousands of labeled examples | A few (1–32) demonstrations |
| Adaptation speed | Hours to days of training | Instantaneous (single forward pass) |
| Generalization | Within training distribution | Cross-task generalization |

**Mechanism:** ICL is hypothesized to emerge because during pre-training, the model implicitly learns an *internal learning algorithm* — its attention mechanism over the demonstration tokens effectively implements a form of implicit Bayesian inference over the task.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Transformer decoder block** used in modern LLMs. Describe the role of **Multi-Head Self-Attention**, **Layer Normalization**, and **Residual Connections** in ensuring stable training of deep stacks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **scaled dot-product attention** formula from the basic self-attention mechanism. Explain mathematically why the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor is necessary. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transformer Decoder Block Architecture:**

A single Transformer decoder block consists of the following sub-layers (shown in the diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Layer Normalization (Pre-Norm):** Applied before the attention and FFN sub-layers. Normalizes activations across the feature dimension to have zero mean and unit variance, stabilizing training dynamics.

$$\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$$

2. **Multi-Head Self-Attention:** The core mechanism allowing each token to attend to all previous tokens (causal masking ensures no future leakage). It projects the input into $Q, K, V$ matrices, computes scaled dot-product attention, and concatenates $h$ parallel heads.

3. **Residual Connection:** Each sub-layer output is added back to its input:
$$x_{\text{out}} = x + \text{Sublayer}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$$
This creates a "gradient highway" that prevents vanishing gradients in very deep stacks (e.g., 96+ layers in GPT-3).

4. **Feed-Forward Network (FFN):** A two-layer MLP with activation (SwiGLU in modern LLMs), applied independently to each position.

**Role in training stability:**
- *Residual connections* preserve gradient magnitude across layers.
- *Layer normalization* controls activation scale, preventing explosion.
- Together, they enable training of 100+ layer models without divergence.

---

**(b) Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Attention:**

**Step 1 — Attention Score Computation:**
Given queries $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and keys $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, the raw attention scores are:

$$S = Q K^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 2 — Variance Analysis:**
Assume $Q$ and $K$ entries are independent random variables with mean $0$ and variance $1$. Then each entry of $S$ is a sum of $d_k$ products:

$$S_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{d_k} Q_{ik} K_{jk}$$

By the properties of variance for independent random variables:

$$\text{Var}(S_{ij}) = \sum_{k=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(Q_{ik} K_{jk}) = d_k \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = d_k$$

**Step 3 — The Saturation Problem:**
As $d_k$ grows large (e.g., $d_k = 128$), the dot-product values have variance $d_k$, so they take on large magnitudes. When passed through softmax, large values push the output distribution toward a one-hot vector:

$$\text{softmax}(x_i) \approx \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x_i = \max(x) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This causes **vanishing gradients** — the softmax output becomes saturated and the model cannot learn effectively.

**Step 4 — The Scaling Fix:**
Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ rescales the dot products to have unit variance:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{S_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1$$

Restoring the softmax to a well-conditioned regime with meaningful gradients.

**Final Formula:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **three-stage training pipeline** of a modern LLM: Pre-training, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). For each stage, state the data used, the objective, and the output artifact. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **emergent abilities** in LLMs? Discuss **scaling laws** as described by Kaplan et al. (2020) and the **Chinchilla optimal compute ratio** by Hoffmann et al. (2022). Why is the Chinchilla finding significant for practical model training? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Stage Training Pipeline:**

| Stage | Data | Objective | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Pre-Training** | Trillions of tokens from web (Common Crawl, Wikipedia, books, code) | Causal Language Modeling: maximize $P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ | **Base Model** — broad linguistic/world knowledge, but poor at following instructions |
| **2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** | Tens of thousands of curated (prompt, ideal response) pairs | Same CLM loss, restricted to high-quality demonstrations | **Instruction-Tuned Model** — responds helpfully to user queries in chat format |
| **3. RLHF** | Human preference rankings over multiple model outputs; trained reward model $r_\phi$ | PPO objective: maximize $E[r_\phi(x,y)]$ with KL constraint to SFT | **Aligned Model** — helpful, harmless, and honest |

**Mathematical formulation of RLHF objective:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RLHF}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{x,y \sim \pi_\theta}\big[r_\phi(x,y)\big] + \beta \, \text{KL}\big(\pi_\theta \,\|\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}\big)$$

The KL term prevents the policy from drifting too far from the SFT model, avoiding "reward hacking" where the model produces degenerate high-reward outputs.

---

**(b) Emergent Abilities and Scaling Laws:**

**Emergent Abilities:**
Emergent abilities are capabilities of LLMs that are *absent* in smaller models and *appear abruptly* once a critical scale threshold is crossed — analogous to phase transitions in physics. Documented examples include:

- In-context few-shot learning (emerges around ~10B parameters).
- Multi-step chain-of-thought reasoning (emerges around ~60B+ parameters).
- Code generation (HumanEval >50% emerges around ~10B+ parameters with instruction tuning).

**Kaplan et al. (2020) Scaling Law:**
Performance (cross-entropy loss) follows a power-law in model size, dataset size, and compute:

$$\mathcal{L}(N) \approx \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N}, \quad \alpha_N \approx 0.076$$

where $N$ is the number of parameters and $N_c$ is a constant.

**Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) — Compute-Optimal Frontier:**
For a given compute budget $C$, the optimal number of parameters $\mathcal{P}^*$ and training tokens $\mathcal{T}^*$ satisfy:

$$\mathcal{P}^* \approx \mathcal{T}^* / 20$$

**Significance of Chinchilla:**
The Kaplan scaling law suggested that *scaling parameters alone* (with fixed data) was most efficient. Chinchilla overturned this by showing that **parameters and data must scale in equal proportion** to be compute-optimal. Concretely:

- A 70B model trained on 1.4T tokens (LLaMA-style) is *more compute-efficient* than a 175B model trained on 300B tokens (GPT-3-style).
- This finding directly influenced the design of nearly all subsequent open-source LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon), all of which follow the $\mathcal{P} \approx \mathcal{T}/20$ rule.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mistakes students make on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing encoder-only with decoder-only architectures** — BERT is *not* generative; GPT is *not* bidirectional. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Stating "LLMs use RNNs" or "LLMs use LSTMs"** — All modern LLMs use Transformers. This is a *fatal* error.
> 3. **Writing $\sqrt{d_k}$ as "$\sqrt{d}$" or "$\sqrt{n}$"** — The $d_k$ is the *key dimension* per head, not the model dimension $d_{\text{model}}$ or sequence length $n$.
> 4. **Forgetting the KL-divergence term in RLHF** — Many students write only the reward maximization without the KL constraint, losing 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Stating that RLHF uses "supervised learning"** — RLHF is *reinforcement* learning (PPO), not supervised learning. This is a critical distinction.
> 6. **Confusing perplexity with accuracy** — Perplexity is *not* a percentage; it is the exponential of cross-entropy loss.
> 7. **Omitting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ justification** — Just writing the formula without explaining *why* scaling is needed loses marks under Bloom's *Analyze* level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Board Exam — Module 4: Introduction to LLMs**

- **Definition:** LLM = Transformer-based model trained on massive text with self-supervised objectives; parameters in the range $10^8$ to $10^{12}$.
- **Architectural Families:** Encoder-only (BERT), Decoder-only (GPT/LLaMA), Encoder-Decoder (T5). Modern dominance is decoder-only.
- **Transformer Core:** Scaled dot-product attention $\text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$; multi-head attention with $h$ parallel heads; residual + layer norm; FFN with GELU/SwiGLU.
- **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$:** Prevents softmax saturation by keeping dot-product variance at unity.
- **Positional Encoding:** Sinusoidal (original), Learned (BERT/GPT-2), RoPE (LLaMA/Mistral), ALiBi (BLOOM).
- **Pre-training Objective:** Causal LM (decoder) or MLM (encoder); cross-entropy loss = negative log-likelihood under one-hot targets.
- **Three-Stage Training:** Pre-train (CLM on web) → SFT (instruction data) → RLHF (PPO with reward model + KL constraint).
- **Emergent Abilities:** Capabilities appearing abruptly at scale — in-context learning, chain-of-thought, code generation.
- **Scaling Laws:** Power-law relationship: $\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{P}^{-\alpha}$. Chinchilla: $\mathcal{P}^* \approx \mathcal{T}^*/20$ for compute-optimal training.
- **In-Context Learning:** Task learning via prompt examples, *no* weight updates; zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot regimes.
- **Decoding Strategies:** Greedy (deterministic), Beam search (high-probability), Top-$k$ / Top-$p$ (diverse sampling), Temperature $\tau$ (sharpness control).
- **Tokenization:** BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece — sub-word units; vocabulary size $V$ typically 32K–256K.
- **Key Models to Know:** BERT (encoder, 2018), GPT-3 (decoder, 175B, 2020), T5 (enc-dec, 2020), LLaMA-2 (open decoder, 2023), Mistral (efficient open, 2023), GPT-4 (multimodal, 2023).
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Perplexity (lower = better), BLEU/ROUGE (generation quality), HumanEval (code), MMLU (knowledge), TruthfulQA (factuality).
- **Limitations:** Hallucinations (confident but wrong outputs), context-length limits, computational cost, bias, lack of true reasoning, data cutoff.
- **Safety & Alignment:** RLHF, Constitutional AI, red-teaming, content filters — all critical for deployment.
- **KTU Exam Buzzwords:** "Self-attention", "Causal masking", "Emergent abilities", "Compute-optimal", "Prompt engineering", "Fine-tuning", "RLHF", "Hallucination".
